topics.title: Introduction to Control Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Control Systems

# Introduction to Control Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Control System** is an interconnection of components and physical plants designed to yield a desired response (output) for a given command (input) by regulating the dynamic behaviour of the system according to a predetermined set of operating specifications. In formal control theory, a control system is mathematically modelled as a mapping $\mathcal{G}: \mathcal{U} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ where the relationship between the input $u(t)$ and the output $y(t)$ is governed by a set of differential or difference equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):**
> A *control system* is a combination of physical elements arranged and interconnected in such a way that the output variable is automatically maintained at a desired value or follows a prescribed time-variation, despite the presence of external disturbances and internal parameter variations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Cruise Control of a Car:**
> Imagine driving a Maruti Suzuki on the National Highway. You set the speedometer to **80 km/h**. The cruise control system automatically adjusts the throttle (petrol input to the engine) to keep the actual car speed exactly at 80 km/h, even when the road goes uphill, downhill, or faces a headwind. Here, the *desired speed* is the **reference input**, the *throttle actuator* is the **controller**, the *car + engine* is the **plant**, and the *actual speed* measured by the speed sensor is the **feedback signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Human Body Thermoregulation:**
> When your body temperature rises above the normal **37°C**, sensors in the skin send signals to the brain (controller), which commands sweat glands (actuator) to release sweat. The evaporation of sweat cools the body. This is a perfect **closed-loop negative feedback** system.

### 1.3 Fundamental Nomenclature & Standard Metrics

Every control system in classical theory is described using these standard terms, which the KTU examiner will expect you to reproduce verbatim:

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| **Reference Input** | $r(t)$ | The desired value the output must achieve |
| **Control Signal** | $u(t)$ | Signal generated by the controller |
| **Plant / Process** | $G(s)$ | The physical system to be controlled |
| **Actuator** | — | Converts control signal into physical action (force/voltage) |
| **Sensor** | $H(s)$ | Measures the actual output and converts to feedback signal |
| **Output / Controlled Variable** | $y(t)$ | The actual response of the system |
| **Feedback Signal** | $b(t)$ | Output signal fed back to compare with reference |
| **Error Signal** | $e(t)$ | Difference between reference and feedback |
| **Disturbance** | $d(t)$ | Unwanted external signal affecting the plant |

### 1.4 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — The Heart of Classical Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Loop Control System:** The control action is **independent** of the output. There is **no feedback** path. The output cannot correct itself against disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-Loop Control System:** The control action is **dependent** on the output through a feedback path. The error $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ continuously drives the system towards the desired response, providing **self-correction** capability.

**Examples:**

- **Open-Loop:** Bread toaster, washing machine (timer-based), traffic signal (fixed timing), electric hand-dryer, electric fan with manual regulator.
- **Closed-Loop:** Room air-conditioner (thermostat-based), missile guidance system, voltage stabiliser (servo), autopilot, refrigerator, water-level controller in overhead tanks.

### 1.5 The Central Role of Feedback

The introduction of negative feedback is the single most important concept in classical control theory. It is what transforms an unreliable, drift-prone system into a precise, self-correcting one.

**Primary Benefits of Negative Feedback (high-yield KTU points):**
- Reduces sensitivity to parameter variations
- Improves steady-state accuracy
- Increases bandwidth (faster response)
- Attenuates disturbance signals
- Linearises certain non-linear systems

**Primary Drawback of Negative Feedback:**
- Tends to reduce overall system gain
- May introduce **instability** if poorly designed
- Increases complexity and cost

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Control Systems (KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic)

Control systems are classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on the following:

### 2.1.1 Linear vs Non-Linear Systems

A system is **linear** if it obeys the principles of **superposition** and **homogeneity (scaling)**.

$$y(t) = \mathcal{G}[a_1 u_1(t) + a_2 u_2(t)] = a_1 \mathcal{G}[u_1(t)] + a_2 \mathcal{G}[u_2(t)]$$

If this property fails for any input pair, the system is **non-linear**. Classical control theory in KTU Module 1 primarily addresses **linear time-invariant (LTI)** systems.

### 2.1.2 Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant Systems

A system is **time-invariant** if a shift in the input produces an identical shift in the output.

$$\mathcal{G}[u(t - T)] = y(t - T)$$

If the system parameters change with time, it is **time-variant** (e.g., a rocket burning fuel and losing mass).

### 2.1.3 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Systems

- **Continuous-time:** Signals defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., $RC$ circuit, motor speed).
- **Discrete-time:** Signals defined only at discrete instants $t = kT$ (e.g., computer-controlled systems, sampled-data systems).
- **Sampled-data:** Hybrid system with continuous plant and discrete controller.

### 2.1.4 SISO vs MIMO

- **SISO (Single-Input Single-Output):** One input, one output — primary focus of classical control.
- **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output):** Multiple inputs and outputs — focus of modern/optimal control.

### 2.1.5 Causal vs Non-Causal

- **Causal:** Output depends only on present and past inputs (all physical systems are causal).
- **Non-causal:** Output depends on future inputs (only theoretical/mathematical).

### 2.1.6 Deterministic vs Stochastic

- **Deterministic:** Inputs and outputs are precisely defined functions.
- **Stochastic:** Inputs/outputs contain random elements (noise).

### 2.1.7 Lumped vs Distributed Parameter

- **Lumped:** Described by ordinary differential equations (ODEs).
- **Distributed:** Described by partial differential equations (PDEs) — e.g., long transmission lines, heating rods.

## 2.2 Mathematical Representation of a Control System

The general $n$-th order linear time-invariant differential equation governing an LTI system is:

$$a_n \frac{d^n y(t)}{dt^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y(t)}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + a_0 y(t) = b_m \frac{d^m u(t)}{dt^m} + \cdots + b_0 u(t)$$

where $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$ and $b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_m$ are the system coefficients.

## 2.3 The Canonical Closed-Loop Block Diagram Equation

The single most important equation in Module 1 is the **closed-loop transfer function**:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

This equation is derived from the signal flow relations:
- Error: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$
- Feedback: $B(s) = H(s) \cdot Y(s)$
- Output: $Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s) = G(s)[R(s) - H(s)Y(s)]$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Control Theory |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| Aerospace | Autopilot, missile guidance, satellite attitude control |
| Robotics | Manipulator joint control, mobile robot navigation |
| Process Industry | Temperature, pressure, flow, level control in chemical plants |
| Automotive | Cruise control, ABS, active suspension, lane-keep assist |
| Power Systems | Generator voltage/frequency regulation, load-frequency control |
| Biomedical | Insulin pump, anaesthesia delivery, prosthetic limbs |
| Manufacturing | CNC machine tool positioning, pick-and-place automation |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains the exact equations you must memorise. KTU board exams routinely test these as 3-mark or 14-mark derivations.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---------|---------|-------------------|
| Closed-loop TF with unity feedback | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$ | $G(s)$: forward path TF, $H(s)=1$ |
| Closed-loop TF with non-unity feedback | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $G(s)$: forward, $H(s)$: feedback |
| Error TF (with respect to reference) | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $E(s)$: error signal |
| Control signal TF | $\dfrac{U(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $U(s)$: controller output |
| Feedback signal TF | $\dfrac{B(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)H(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $B(s)$: feedback |
| Effect of disturbance at output | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $D(s)$: disturbance (good rejection) |
| Effect of disturbance at input to plant | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Disturbance entering before plant |
| Sensitivity to plant variation | $S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T/T}{\partial G/G} = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ | $T$: closed-loop TF |
| Total sensitivity | $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Smaller $\Rightarrow$ less sensitive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-derivation trick for KTU 14-mark problems:** Whenever a block diagram is given, always start by writing the three fundamental loop equations — *error equation*, *output equation*, and *feedback equation* — and then eliminate the intermediate variables step by step to land on the final $Y(s)/R(s)$ form.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (General Case)

This is the single most repeated 7-mark derivation in KTU classical control papers. Master it.

### Step 1 — Identify the standard closed-loop block diagram

A canonical single-loop negative feedback system contains:
- Forward path transfer function: $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function: $H(s)$
- Summing junction: subtracts feedback from reference
- Input: $R(s)$, Output: $Y(s)$, Error: $E(s)$, Feedback: $B(s)$

### Step 2 — Write the error equation (comparator output)

The summing junction subtracts the feedback from the reference:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

### Step 3 — Write the output equation (plant input to plant output)

The output is the product of the forward path and the error:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

### Step 4 — Write the feedback equation

The feedback signal is the product of the feedback path and the output:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - B(s)]$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 5

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)]$$

$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$$

### Step 7 — Collect $Y(s)$ terms on the left

$$Y(s) + G(s) H(s) Y(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

$$Y(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$$

### Step 8 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For unity feedback** ($H(s) = 1$), this simplifies to $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Disturbance on Closed-Loop Output

This is the second-most repeated derivation in KTU Module 1, and is **guaranteed** to appear in some form.

### Step 1 — Draw the system with disturbance at plant input

Let $D(s)$ be the disturbance entering at the plant input (between the controller and the plant). The plant input becomes $U(s) + D(s)$.

### Step 2 — Write the modified error equation

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

### Step 3 — Write the modified plant input (controller output)

The controller $G_c(s)$ produces:

$$U(s) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s)$$

### Step 4 — Write the modified output equation (using superposition)

Because the system is linear, the output is the sum of two contributions:
1. Due to the reference $R(s)$ alone (with $D(s)=0$)
2. Due to the disturbance $D(s)$ alone (with $R(s)=0$)

For disturbance alone (set $R(s)=0$):

$$E(s) = -B(s) = -H(s) Y(s)$$

$$U(s) = G_c(s) E(s) = -G_c(s) H(s) Y(s)$$

The total plant input is $U(s) + D(s)$, so the output is:

$$Y(s) = G(s) [U(s) + D(s)] = G(s) [-G_c(s) H(s) Y(s) + D(s)]$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange to collect $Y(s)$ terms

$$Y(s) = -G(s) G_c(s) H(s) Y(s) + G(s) D(s)$$

$$Y(s) [1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)] = G(s) D(s)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $Y(s)/D(s)$

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** As the loop gain $G(s) G_c(s) H(s)$ becomes large, the denominator grows, and the effect of the disturbance on the output is **attenuated**. This is exactly why feedback control is so effective in rejecting disturbances in real engineering systems like CNC machines and radar antennas.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Closed-Loop Sensitivity to Plant Variations

Sensitivity analysis is a 7-mark favourite. It quantifies how much the closed-loop TF changes when the plant $G(s)$ drifts.

### Step 1 — Define sensitivity

The sensitivity of the closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$ with respect to the plant $G(s)$ is defined as:

$$S_G^T \triangleq \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{\partial \ln T}{\partial \ln G}$$

### Step 2 — Express the closed-loop TF

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate $T$ with respect to $G$

Using the quotient rule:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1 + GH) \cdot 1 - G \cdot H}{(1 + GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the logarithmic derivative

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{G}{T}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the expressions

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G}{\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G(1+GH)}{G}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** High loop gain $GH$ makes $S_G^T$ very small, meaning the closed-loop system is **insensitive** to plant parameter variations. This is why op-amps with very high open-loop gain are used in precision instrumentation.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Determine:
1. The closed-loop transfer function
2. The error transfer function
3. The steady-state error for a unit step input

### Solution:

**Part 1:** Closed-loop TF is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Part 2:** Error TF is:

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}} = \frac{s(s+2)}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Part 3:** For a unit step, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Steady-state error uses the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$$

$$E(s) = \frac{s(s+2)}{s^2 + 2s + 10} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{s+2}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{s+2}{s^2 + 2s + 10} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** A Type-1 system (one pole at origin in forward path) has **zero steady-state error** for a step input. This is a guaranteed KTU question pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the canonical negative-feedback control loop, which is the foundational structure for the entire classical control syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference Input R s] --> sumj{Summing Junction}
    fb[Feedback Signal B s] --> sumj
    sumj -->|Error E s| ctrl[Controller Gc s]
    ctrl -->|Control U s| plant[Plant G s]
    plant -->|Output Y s| out[Controlled Output]
    dist[Disturbance D s] -.-> plant
    out --> sensor[Sensor H s]
    sensor --> fb
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The solid arrows show the **signal flow** during normal operation.
- The dotted arrow shows the **disturbance** injection point (commonly at the plant input or output).
- The summing junction performs subtraction: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$.

## 4.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Comparison (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OL["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
        R1[Reference Input] --> C1[Controller Gc s]
        C1 --> P1[Plant G s]
        P1 --> Y1[Output]
        note1[No feedback path] -.-> Y1
    end

    subgraph CL["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        R2[Reference Input] --> S2{Summing Junction}
        S2 --> C2[Controller Gc s]
        C2 --> P2[Plant G s]
        P2 --> Y2[Output]
        Y2 --> H2[Feedback Sensor H s]
        H2 --> S2
    end
```

**Key difference observed in the diagram:**
- The **open-loop** block has a one-way signal path; the output never returns to influence the input.
- The **closed-loop** block forms a **closed loop** (hence the name) — the output is continuously measured and compared with the reference.

## 4.3 System Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Control System] --> B{Contains Feedback?}
    B -->|No| C[Open Loop]
    B -->|Yes| D[Closed Loop]
    D --> E{Single or Multiple Loops?}
    E -->|Single| F[Single Loop]
    E -->|Multiple| G[Multi Loop Cascade]
    D --> H{Type of System?}
    H -->|Linear ODE| I[LTI Continuous]
    H -->|Non linear| J[Non linear]
    H -->|Sampled data| K[Discrete Time]
    I --> L{Number of I O?}
    L -->|1 in 1 out| M[SISO]
    L -->|Multiple| N[MIMO]
```

## 4.4 Disturbance Rejection Mechanism (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R s] --> J{Sum}
    J --> E[E s]
    E --> GC[Controller Gc s]
    GC --> SUM2{Sum2}
    D[D s Disturbance] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> G[Plant G s]
    G --> Y[Y s]
    Y --> H[Sensor H s]
    H --> B[B s]
    B --> J
```

**Reading:** The disturbance $D(s)$ enters the loop at the input of the plant. The controller $G_c(s)$ does **not** see $D(s)$ directly, but because the error $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ is computed using the **output** $Y(s)$ (which contains the disturbance effect), the controller automatically compensates. This is the architectural elegance of negative feedback.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Sign error at the summing junction** is the single most common mistake. Always write $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ for negative feedback. A + sign here flips the entire system to **positive feedback** and the closed-loop TF becomes $G/(1-GH)$ — a **disastrous 7-mark loss**.
> 2. Students often forget the **+1** in the denominator of the closed-loop formula and write $G/GH$ or $1/GH$. Memorise $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ verbatim.
> 3. Do not confuse **sensitivity** (a unitless ratio) with **steady-state error** (a value in physical units like metres or volts).
> 4. Always **state the assumption of linearity** before using superposition in disturbance rejection problems.
> 5. For unity feedback problems, explicitly state $H(s) = 1$ in the first line of the solution.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a control system. Distinguish between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A control system is a combination of physical components interconnected to regulate a process variable automatically, so that the output follows a desired reference despite disturbances.

| Property | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback path | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Poor (no self-correction) | High (self-correcting) |
| Stability | Always stable (no oscillation) | May become unstable |
| Sensitivity to disturbance | High | Low |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher |
| Bandwidth | Wide | Reduced by feedback |
| Examples | Toaster, traffic light | Air-conditioner, autopilot |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any six advantages and two disadvantages of negative feedback in control systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Reduces sensitivity to plant parameter variations
2. Improves steady-state accuracy
3. Increases bandwidth
4. Linearises mild non-linearities
5. Attenuates internal and external disturbances
6. Provides predictable, controllable transient response

**Disadvantages:**
1. May cause instability if the loop gain is too high
2. Increases system complexity, cost, and number of components

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative feedback control system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+4)}$. Determine the value of $K$ such that the steady-state error for a unit step input is **0.1**. Comment on the type of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) — Full Derivation:**

The block diagram contains the reference $R(s)$, summing junction, controller + plant in forward path with combined TF $G(s)$, and a feedback path $H(s)$.

**Error equation** at the summing junction:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Output equation** from the forward path:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Feedback equation** from the sensor:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

Substituting the feedback equation into the error equation:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)$$

Substituting this into the output equation:

$$Y(s) = G(s)[R(s) - H(s) Y(s)]$$

$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$$

Collecting $Y(s)$ terms:

$$Y(s) [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$$

Therefore, the **closed-loop transfer function** is:

$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

For **unity feedback** ($H(s) = 1$), this reduces to:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

---

**Solution to (b) — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify the system type. The forward path has **one pole at the origin** (the $s$ in denominator). This is a **Type-1 system**.

**Step 2:** For a unity feedback Type-1 system, the position error constant is:

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K}{s(s+4)} = \infty$$

**Step 3:** The steady-state error for a unit step input is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall callout:** A Type-1 system has **zero** steady-state error for a step input, **regardless of the value of $K$**. The question is therefore a *trick question* — the answer is $e_{ss} = 0$ for all $K > 0$, and no finite $K$ can produce $e_{ss} = 0.1$ for a step input. The student must clearly explain this and note that the question would be meaningful for a **ramp input** instead.

**For a ramp input** $r(t) = t$, the velocity error constant is:

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+4)} = \frac{K}{4}$$

The steady-state error for a unit ramp is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{4}{K} = 0.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 40$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Type-1 system**: zero step error, finite ramp error, infinite parabolic error.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the transfer function relating the output $Y(s)$ to the disturbance $D(s)$ when $D(s)$ enters at the input of the plant. Show that the effect of disturbance is reduced as loop gain increases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control system has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ and PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ in unity feedback. Determine the steady-state error for a unit step disturbance entering at the plant input. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) — Full Derivation:**

The block diagram is:

$R(s) \rightarrow \text{Sum} \rightarrow E(s) \rightarrow G_c(s) \rightarrow \text{Sum}_2 \rightarrow G(s) \rightarrow Y(s)$, with $Y(s)$ fed back through $H(s)$ to the first sum. The disturbance $D(s)$ enters at Sum_2.

**Apply superposition.** Set $R(s) = 0$ to find the transfer function from $D(s)$ to $Y(s)$.

With $R(s) = 0$:

$$E(s) = -B(s) = -H(s) Y(s)$$

The plant input is $U(s) + D(s)$ where $U(s) = G_c(s) E(s)$:

$$Y(s) = G(s)[G_c(s) E(s) + D(s)]$$

Substituting $E(s) = -H(s) Y(s)$:

$$Y(s) = G(s)[-G_c(s) H(s) Y(s) + D(s)]$$

$$Y(s) = -G(s) G_c(s) H(s) Y(s) + G(s) D(s)$$

$$Y(s)[1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)] = G(s) D(s)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)}}$$

**As loop gain $G(s) G_c(s) H(s) \rightarrow \infty$:**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} \rightarrow 0$$

Thus, the disturbance effect on the output is **attenuated** by the feedback action. This is the mathematical proof of disturbance rejection.

---

**Solution to (b) — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Write the controller TF. PI controller:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Forward path TF is:

$$G(s) G_c(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s(s+1)}$$

**Step 3:** From part (a), the transfer function from disturbance to output is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{s+1}}{1 + \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s(s+1)}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s(s+1)$:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{s}{s(s+1) + K_p s + K_i} = \frac{s}{s^2 + (1+K_p)s + K_i}$$

**Step 4:** For a unit step disturbance, $D(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Output due to disturbance:

$$Y(s) = \frac{s}{s^2 + (1+K_p)s + K_i} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2 + (1+K_p)s + K_i}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Final Value Theorem for the steady-state value of $Y$:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s Y(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + (1+K_p)s + K_i} = \frac{0}{K_i} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The PI controller **eliminates the steady-state error** caused by a step disturbance, regardless of the values of $K_p$ and $K_i$ (as long as $K_i > 0$). This is precisely why PI controllers are widely used in industrial process control — they ensure zero steady-state error for step disturbances.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your **last-minute revision sheet** before the KTU exam. Tick each box mentally.

- [x] A control system regulates output automatically using a controller and feedback.
- [x] **Open-loop** has no feedback; **closed-loop** uses negative feedback for self-correction.
- [x] The canonical closed-loop TF is $\dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ — never forget the $+1$.
- [x] Negative feedback reduces sensitivity, improves accuracy, increases bandwidth, and rejects disturbances.
- [x] The **drawback** of feedback is potential **instability** and **gain reduction**.
- [x] **Linearity** requires both **superposition** and **homogeneity**.
- [x] **Time-invariance** means a shift in input produces an identical shift in output.
- [x] A **Type-0** system has finite step error; **Type-1** has zero step error but finite ramp error; **Type-2** has zero ramp error.
- [x] The **error signal** is always $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ for negative feedback.
- [x] **Disturbance rejection** improves as loop gain increases (denominator grows).
- [x] **Sensitivity** to plant variation is $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1+GH(s)}$.
- [x] Always apply the **Final Value Theorem** to compute steady-state error: $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$.
- [x] **PI controllers** eliminate steady-state error for step inputs/disturbances; **PD controllers** improve transient response.
- [x] Always state $H(s) = 1$ explicitly at the start of unity-feedback problems.
- [x] The **summing junction sign is +ve for negative feedback** — never the other way.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Open-Loop Control System — Formal KTU Definition

An **Open-Loop Control System** is a control configuration in which the control action is completely independent of the desired output. The system has no feedback path; hence, the output has no influence on the input signal driving the actuator. Such systems are also called **Non-Feedback Systems** or **Sequential Control Systems**.

Mathematically, the input-output relationship is governed solely by the forward path transfer function, and the system neither measures its output nor corrects for deviations caused by internal parameter variations, load disturbances, or environmental changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must clearly distinguish between open-loop and closed-loop systems, identify their components, and derive the standard closed-loop transfer function. This forms the foundation for Module 2 (Time Response) and Module 3 (Stability Analysis).

### 1.2 Closed-Loop Control System — Formal KTU Definition

A **Closed-Loop Control System** is one in which the control action is dependent on the desired output in some manner. The output is measured by a **feedback sensor** and compared with the **reference input** through a **summing junction** to produce an **error signal**, which then drives the controller and the plant. Such systems are also known as **Feedback Control Systems** or **Automatic Control Systems**.

The presence of feedback enables the system to automatically correct for errors, reject disturbances, and reduce sensitivity to parameter variations.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy 1 — The Toaster vs. The Smart Oven (Open vs. Closed)**

Imagine a simple household **toaster**: you push down the lever, set a timer to **3 minutes**, and walk away. The heating coil stays on for exactly 180 seconds regardless of whether the bread is frozen, room-temperature, or already browned. This is a classic **open-loop** action — the input (time) is preset and the output (toast color) is never measured.

Now consider a modern **smart oven with a temperature probe**: you set a desired internal temperature of **100°C** for the roast. The probe continuously measures the actual meat temperature and feeds it back to the controller, which adjusts the heating element in real time. This is a **closed-loop** system — the output actively influences the input.

**Analogy 2 — Filling a Water Tank**

- **Open-loop:** You open a valve for a fixed 5 minutes, hoping the tank fills. You don't measure the water level.
- **Closed-loop:** You watch the water level rise and shut the valve the moment the tank is full. The feedback is your own eyes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The presence of a **measurement and comparison mechanism** that converts the output back into the input loop is the defining characteristic of a closed-loop system. Without it, the system is open-loop.

### 1.4 Standard Block Diagram Symbols

| Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| R(s) | Reference Input | Desired output signal |
| C(s) | Controlled Output | Actual output signal |
| E(s) | Error Signal | Difference between reference and feedback |
| G(s) | Forward Path / Plant / Process | Performs control action |
| H(s) | Feedback Path / Sensor | Measures the output |
| D(s) | Disturbance | Unwanted external signal |
| ⊗ | Summing Junction | Adds/subtracts signals |

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard SI Unit of all signals:** Volts (V), Amperes (A), Newtons (N), meters (m), rad/s, depending on the physical domain.
- **Block Diagram Convention:** Signals flow from left to right; feedback flows from right to left.
- **Negative Feedback Sign:** A negative sign at the summing junction is the **standard KTU convention** for stability analysis (positive feedback leads to instability in most practical systems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Components of an Open-Loop System

A typical open-loop system consists of the following cascaded elements:

1. **Controller / Reference Setting** — Generates the command signal (e.g., a set-point knob on an electric iron).
2. **Control Element / Actuator** — Provides power amplification (e.g., a power transistor, motor amplifier).
3. **Plant / Process** — The physical system to be controlled (e.g., DC motor, heater, robotic arm).
4. **No Feedback Element** — Output is NOT measured.

**Mathematical Model:**
The output is simply the product of the input and the forward path transfer function:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

### 2.2 Components of a Closed-Loop System

1. **Reference Input R(s)** — Desired value.
2. **Summing Junction ⊗** — Compares R(s) and feedback signal.
3. **Error Detector E(s)** — Output of the summing junction.
4. **Controller (typically Gc(s))** — Processes the error.
5. **Plant / Process Gp(s)** — Performs the actual control action.
6. **Feedback Element H(s)** — Measures the output.
7. **Disturbance D(s)** (optional) — External unwanted input.

**Mathematical Model:**
The standard negative feedback closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

where the product $G(s)H(s)$ is defined as the **Loop Transfer Function** or **Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)** of the closed-loop system. Note: this is a *terminological* use — within a closed-loop system, $G(s)H(s)$ is called the loop gain even though the system itself is closed-loop.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS:** The table below contains all equations tested regularly in Part A and Part B questions. Memorize the form, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(s) = G(s)R(s)$ | Open-loop output equation | Output = Forward Gain × Input |
| 2 | $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)C(s)$ | Error signal definition | Negative feedback convention |
| 3 | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop TF (negative feedback) | Standard unity / non-unity |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop TF (positive feedback) | Used rarely; unstable systems |
| 5 | $G(s)H(s)$ | Loop transfer function (loop gain) | Denominator minus 1 |
| 6 | $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Error to input transfer function | Steady-state error derivation |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Disturbance rejection (for D at output) | Lower is better |
| 8 | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | System sensitivity to input | Equals error TF |

> **Note on absolute value notation in tables:** $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ should be written as `\vert G(s)H(s) \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Open-Loop Systems

- **Cheaper to design and build** — fewer components.
- **Simple to commission and maintain** — no calibration of feedback sensor.
- **No stability issues** — there is no feedback to cause oscillations.
- **Examples:** Washing machine (timed cycles), bread toaster, traffic light controller, automatic door opener, fan regulator (step-controlled), electric iron with bimetallic strip cutout.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Closed-Loop Systems

- **Accuracy:** Output closely follows reference despite disturbances.
- **Disturbance Rejection:** External noise $D(s)$ is attenuated by the factor $\dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.
- **Reduced Sensitivity:** Changes in plant parameters $G(s)$ affect the closed-loop TF less.
- **Bandwidth Improvement:** Bandwidth can be widened through proper controller design.
- **Examples:** Servo motor position control, cruise control in cars, autopilot, robotic arm control, oven temperature control, voltage regulator (SMPS), biomedical implants (pacemaker, insulin pump).

### 2.6 Comparative Analysis: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low (depends on calibration) | High (self-correcting) |
| Sensitivity to disturbances | High | Low |
| Stability | Always stable (no oscillation source) | Conditional (may become unstable) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (sensors, controller) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Bandwidth | Generally wide | Limited by stability margins |
| Examples | Toaster, fan regulator | Servo, cruise control |
| Error correction | Cannot self-correct | Self-correcting |

### 2.7 Why Feedback Works — The Underlying 'Why'

The 'why' of feedback's power lies in the **denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$**:
- If $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$, then $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} \approx \dfrac{1}{H(s)}$.
- This means the closed-loop behavior depends primarily on the **feedback element H(s)**, which is typically a precision sensor — much more reliable than the plant $G(s)$ which may have parameter drift.
- This principle is called **Desensitization** and is the cornerstone of robust control design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Open-Loop Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Define the system:** Consider a single-block open-loop system where the input is $R(s)$ and the output is $C(s)$, with a forward path transfer function $G(s)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the input-output relationship:** By the definition of a transfer function, the output is the product of the input and the system's transfer function.

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 3 — Form the transfer function ratio:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = G(s)$$

**Interpretation:** The output is a scaled (and possibly phase-shifted) version of the input. There is no mechanism to correct for errors. If $G(s)$ drifts due to temperature, aging, or load, the output $C(s)$ drifts proportionally.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Standard Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

**Step 1 — Define signals:**
- Let $R(s)$ be the reference input.
- Let $C(s)$ be the controlled output.
- Let $H(s)$ be the feedback element transfer function.
- Let $G(s)$ be the forward path transfer function.

**Step 2 — Define the feedback signal:** The output is measured and passed through $H(s)$:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Define the error signal:** The summing junction subtracts feedback from reference (negative feedback convention):

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4 — Define the forward path output:** The error is multiplied by the forward gain to produce the output:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect $C(s)$ terms on one side:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s)H(s) \right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Form the closed-loop transfer function:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**This is the most important equation in Classical Control Engineering.** It is tested almost every semester in KTU exams, often as a 7-mark derivation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with Disturbance

When a disturbance $D(s)$ enters the system (commonly at the plant input or output), the analysis changes.

**Step 1 — System model:** Assume the disturbance enters between the controller and the plant.

**Step 2 — Output equation with both inputs (by superposition):**

$$C(s) = G_1(s) \cdot \left[ G_2(s) \cdot E(s) + D(s) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$:**

$$C(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s) \right] + G_1(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Step 4 — Collect $C(s)$ terms:**

$$C(s) + G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot R(s) + G_1(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Step 5 — Factor and solve:**

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s) \right] = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot R(s) + G_1(s) \cdot D(s)$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{D=0} = \frac{G_1(s)G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{D(s)}\bigg|_{R=0} = \frac{G_1(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$$

**Interpretation:** Both transfer functions share the same denominator $\left[ 1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s) \right]$, which is the **characteristic equation** of the closed-loop system and determines its **stability**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Error Transfer Function

Starting from $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$ and substituting $C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

$$E(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s)H(s) \right] = R(s)$$

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Engineering implication:** To minimize the steady-state error, we must make $\vert 1 + G(s)H(s) \vert$ as large as possible over the frequency range of interest.

---

### 3.5 Sensitivity Analysis — Why Feedback Reduces Parameter Variation

The **sensitivity** $S$ of the closed-loop TF $T$ to variation in the plant $G$ is defined as:

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{\partial \ln T}{\partial \ln G}$$

For the standard negative feedback system with $T = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$:

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + GH}$$

**Derivation:**

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1 + GH) - G(H)}{(1 + GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}$$

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{G}{T} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G}{G/(1+GH)} = \frac{1}{1 + GH}$$

**Conclusion:** When $\vert GH \vert \gg 1$, sensitivity approaches zero — meaning the closed-loop behavior is nearly **insensitive** to plant parameter variations. This is the central reason feedback control is preferred in high-precision applications.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem Solving Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Formula | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Derive closed-loop TF | Block diagram with $G(s), H(s)$ | $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ | $\frac{G}{1+GH}$ | Sign error at summing junction |
| Disturbance rejection | $G, H, D$ at output | $\frac{C(s)}{D(s)}$ | $\frac{1}{1+GH}$ | Forgetting to set $R(s)=0$ |
| Error analysis | $G, H$ | $\frac{E(s)}{R(s)}$ | $\frac{1}{1+GH}$ | Confusing $E$ with $C$ |
| Multi-loop reduction | Cascaded $G_1, G_2$ with feedback | Overall $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ | Apply Mason's Gain Formula | Missing inner loop |
| Sensitivity | $G$ varies by $\pm 10\%$ | Change in $T$ | $S_G^T = \frac{1}{1+GH}$ | Forgetting percentage form |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Open-Loop Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1[Reference Input R of s] --> ctrl1[Controller Gc of s]
    ctrl1 --> act1[Actuator and Power Stage]
    act1 --> plant1[Plant or Process G of s]
    plant1 --> out1[Controlled Output C of s]
    ctrl1 -. No feedback path .-> ctrl1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows strictly in **one direction** — left to right. There is no return path from the output to the input. The dotted loop is shown for pedagogical contrast; in a true open-loop system, this path does not exist.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Negative Feedback System

```mermaid
graph LR
    in2[Reference Input R of s] --> sum2[Summing Junction with Negative Sign]
    sum2 --> err2[Error Signal E of s]
    err2 --> cont2[Controller Gc of s]
    cont2 --> proc2[Plant or Process Gp of s]
    proc2 --> out2[Controlled Output C of s]
    out2 --> sens2[Feedback Sensor H of s]
    sens2 --> sum2
    dist2[Disturbance D of s] -. Enters plant .-> proc2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Cue:** The **summing junction (sum2)** has a `+` sign for $R(s)$ and a `−` sign for the feedback signal. Forgetting this negative sign leads to the **positive feedback** formula $\dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$, which is almost always wrong in KTU exam contexts.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop with Multiple Disturbances

```mermaid
graph LR
    in3[R of s] --> sum3a[Summing Junction 1]
    sum3a --> comp3[Controller Gc]
    comp3 --> sum3b[Summing Junction 2]
    sum3b --> plt3[Plant Gp]
    plt3 --> out3[C of s]
    d1[D1 of s at input] --> sum3a
    d2[D2 of s at plant input] --> sum3b
    d3[D3 of s at output] -.-> plt3
    out3 --> sen3[Sensor H of s]
    sen3 --> sum3a
```

**Engineering Insight:** Real industrial systems have disturbances at multiple points — sensor noise, load variation, supply voltage fluctuations, environmental temperature. The feedback loop must handle all of them simultaneously.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1{Does the system measure the output?}
    q1 -- No --> a1[Open Loop System]
    a1 --> a1a[Input set by user once]
    a1 --> a1b[Actuator driven by fixed command]
    a1 --> a1c[Output produced without verification]
    a1 --> a1d[No error correction possible]
    q1 -- Yes --> a2[Closed Loop System]
    a2 --> a2a[Input reference set continuously]
    a2 --> a2b[Output measured by sensor]
    a2 --> a2c[Feedback compared with reference]
    a2 --> a2d[Error computed and corrected]
    a2 --> a2e[Loop repeats until error is zero]
    a1d --> r1[Lower accuracy, lower cost, simpler]
    a2e --> r2[Higher accuracy, higher cost, complex]
```

---

### 4.5 Standard KTU Block Diagram Conventions

| Symbol | Meaning | Convention |
|---|---|---|
| `→` (filled arrow) | Signal flow direction | Always left to right for forward path |
| Block (rectangle) | Transfer function element | Label inside is $G(s), H(s)$, etc. |
| `⊗` (circle with cross) | Summing junction | Plus for additive, minus for subtractive |
| Branching point (dot) | Signal tap | Same signal goes to multiple paths |
| Dotted line | Optional / disturbance path | Not part of main signal chain |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an open-loop control system. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An open-loop control system is one in which the control action is **independent of the output**. There is no feedback from output to input, so the system cannot self-correct for errors. The output is given by $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$.

**Two examples:**
1. **Electric toaster** — operates for a fixed time set by the user, without measuring toast color.
2. **Traffic light controller** — cycles through red, yellow, green on a fixed timer, regardless of actual traffic density.
3. *(Optional third)* Automatic washing machine with timed wash cycles.

**[Mark distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark examples]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Compare open-loop and closed-loop control systems based on accuracy, stability, and cost.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Low — depends on calibration | High — self-correcting via feedback |
| Stability | Always stable (no feedback loop to oscillate) | Conditional — depends on $G(s)H(s)$ |
| Cost | Low (fewer components) | High (sensors, controller, wiring) |

Open-loop systems are preferred when **cost is critical and accuracy is not** (e.g., fans, toasters). Closed-loop systems are essential for **precision applications** (e.g., servo motors, aerospace, biomedical).

**[Mark distribution: 1 mark per parameter × 3 = 3 marks]**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module 1 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice (either/or). Each choice is structured as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the transfer function of a closed-loop control system with negative feedback. State the role of each block.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify blocks** (2 marks):
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $C(s)$ = Controlled output
- $G(s)$ = Forward path transfer function (controller + plant)
- $H(s)$ = Feedback path transfer function (sensor)
- $E(s)$ = Error signal at summing junction output
- $B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$ = Feedback signal

**Step 2 — Write the error equation** (1 mark):

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Write the forward path equation** (1 mark):

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute E(s) into the forward equation** (1 mark):

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect $C(s)$ terms** (1 mark):

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s)H(s) \right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Final transfer function** (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

**Block roles (briefly):** Reference sets the target; summing junction produces error; $G(s)$ amplifies/processes error to drive plant; $H(s)$ senses output for comparison.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the advantages and disadvantages of closed-loop control systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages (4 marks):**
1. **Accuracy:** Output closely matches reference even in presence of disturbances.
2. **Disturbance Rejection:** External disturbances attenuated by factor $\dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$.
3. **Reduced Sensitivity:** Plant parameter changes have less effect on output (sensitivity $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$).
4. **Improved Bandwidth and Transient Response:** Properly designed feedback extends useful operating range.
5. **Linearization:** Feedback can linearize inherently non-linear systems.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**
1. **Complexity and Cost:** Requires sensors, controller, additional wiring.
2. **Stability Issues:** The feedback loop can cause oscillations or instability (covered in Module 3).
3. **Reduced Gain:** The closed-loop gain $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ is less than the open-loop gain $G$ when $GH > 0$.
4. **Sensor Noise:** Sensor inaccuracies and noise are fed back into the system.

---

#### **QUESTION B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain an open-loop control system with a neat block diagram. State its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

An open-loop system consists of a **Controller** block followed by a **Plant/Process** block. The input $R(s)$ enters the controller, the controller output drives the plant, and the plant produces the output $C(s)$. **No feedback path exists** from the output to the input.

```
R(s) → [Controller Gc(s)] → [Plant Gp(s)] → C(s)
```

**Mathematical relation (1 mark):**

$$C(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot R(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Simple construction and low cost.
2. Easy to design and maintain.
3. No stability problems.
4. Faster response (no feedback delay).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Inaccurate — output drifts with parameter changes.
2. Cannot reject disturbances.
3. Recalibration required frequently.
4. Unsuitable for precision applications.

**Example (1 mark):** Automatic washing machine operating on a timer.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the transfer function of a closed-loop system subjected to a disturbance at the plant input. Comment on disturbance rejection.**

**Model Solution:**

**System Setup (1 mark):**
- $G_1(s)$ = Controller transfer function
- $G_2(s)$ = Plant transfer function
- $H(s)$ = Feedback sensor
- $D(s)$ = Disturbance entering between $G_1$ and $G_2$
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $C(s)$ = Output

**Output equation (using superposition)** (2 marks):

$$C(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot E(s) + G_2(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Error equation:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Substitute $E(s)$ and collect $C(s)$ terms** (2 marks):

$$C(s) = G_1 G_2 \cdot \left[ R - HC \right] + G_2 D$$

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G_1 G_2 H \right] = G_1 G_2 \cdot R + G_2 D$$

**Solve for each transfer function** (1 mark):

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{D=0} = \frac{G_1(s)G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{D(s)}\bigg|_{R=0} = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$$

**Comment on disturbance rejection (1 mark):**
For effective disturbance rejection, the magnitude of $\dfrac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$ should be small at the disturbance frequency. This requires $\vert G_1 G_2 H \vert \gg 1$, which is achieved by making the loop gain large — but this must be balanced against stability constraints (covered in Module 3).

---

### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams (Module 1):**
> 1. **Sign Error at the Summing Junction:** Writing $E(s) = R(s) + H(s)C(s)$ instead of $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)C(s)$ leads to the **positive feedback** formula, costing **all 7 marks** in derivation questions. Always confirm the negative sign explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $G(s)H(s)$ with Open-Loop System:** $G(s)H(s)$ is the **loop transfer function** of a *closed-loop* system, not the open-loop system itself. Examiners specifically test this terminology.
> 3. **Forgetting to Set $R(s) = 0$ for Disturbance TF:** When finding $\frac{C(s)}{D(s)}$, the reference must be zeroed. KTU examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this oversight.
> 4. **Skipping Algebraic Steps in Derivation:** The KTU marking scheme awards marks for **intermediate steps**. Writing only the final answer $\frac{G}{1+GH}$ without showing substitution and simplification gets **maximum 3 out of 7 marks**.
> 5. **No Block Diagram in Descriptive Answers:** Even if the question says "with a neat block diagram," a textual answer without a diagram loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Mixing Up Error TF and Output TF:** $\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1+GH}$ is NOT the same as $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G}{1+GH}$. Examiners catch this regularly.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise the night before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Open-loop system:** $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$, no feedback, simple, cheap, inaccurate.
- [ ] **Closed-loop system:** Has feedback, error-driven, self-correcting, accurate.
- [ ] **Error Signal:** $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$ (negative feedback convention).
- [ ] **Closed-Loop Transfer Function (master equation):** $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.
- [ ] **Loop Transfer Function:** $G(s)H(s)$ — appears in the denominator.
- [ ] **Positive Feedback Formula:** $\frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ — rarely used; indicates potential instability.
- [ ] **Error Transfer Function:** $\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.
- [ ] **Disturbance Rejection:** $\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$ — set $R=0$ before deriving.
- [ ] **Sensitivity:** $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$ — small when loop gain is large (desensitization principle).
- [ ] **Characteristic Equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ — determines stability (Module 3).
- [ ] **Examples — Open-loop:** Toaster, fan, traffic light, timed washing machine.
- [ ] **Examples — Closed-loop:** Servo motor, cruise control, oven with thermostat, pacemaker, autopilot.
- [ ] **Trade-off:** Open-loop is cheap but inaccurate; closed-loop is accurate but may become unstable — design must balance both.
- [ ] **KTU Module Mapping:** Module 1 (basics) → Module 2 (time response) → Module 3 (stability via Routh-Hurwitz, Root Locus) → Module 4 (frequency response, Bode, Nyquist).

**Best of luck for your KTU ESE examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback and Non-Feedback Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feedback and Non-Feedback Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Control System?

A **Control System** is an arrangement of physical components connected or related in such a manner as to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a desired output. In the context of classical control engineering, every control system can be broadly classified into two principal architectural families: **Open-Loop (Non-Feedback) Systems** and **Closed-Loop (Feedback) Systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCICT501 - Module 1):**
> A system in which the output has no effect on the control action is called an **Open-Loop (Non-Feedback) System**, whereas a system in which the output is measured and fed back to influence the control action is called a **Closed-Loop (Feedback) System**.

### 1.2 Open-Loop (Non-Feedback) Systems — Formal Definition

An **Open-Loop System** is a control architecture where the control action is completely independent of the desired output. The controller receives only the reference input (command signal) and produces an actuating signal based purely on a pre-calibrated mathematical relationship. There is **no measurement** of the actual output, and **no comparison** between the reference and the actual response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier:** Open-loop systems are also called **Non-Feedback Systems** or **Feedforward Systems**. The transfer function of the plant itself directly determines the output.

### 1.3 Closed-Loop (Feedback) Systems — Formal Definition

A **Closed-Loop System** is a control architecture in which the actual output is continuously measured by a sensor, compared with the desired reference input via a summing junction, and the **error signal** generated from this comparison is then used to drive the controller. The output itself influences the control action, creating a "loop" of information flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier:** Closed-loop systems are also called **Feedback Systems** or **Automatic Regulation Systems**. The presence of a feedback path (typically returning from output to input) is the defining structural feature.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Driving a Car Example

Imagine you are learning to drive a car:

- **Open-Loop Analogy (Non-Feedback):** You set the accelerator pedal to a fixed position (say, 30% depression) and assume the car will maintain exactly **60 km/h** regardless of road slope, wind, or load. If you start climbing a hill, the car slows down, but since you are not looking at the speedometer, you do not correct the pedal. The output (speed) has **no influence** on the input (pedal position). This is an **Open-Loop System**.

- **Closed-Loop Analogy (Feedback):** You continuously watch the speedometer. If the speed drops below 60 km/h, you press the accelerator more; if it rises above, you release it. Your eyes act as the **sensor**, your brain as the **controller**, and the speedometer reading as the **feedback signal**. The output (speed) **directly influences** the input (pedal position). This is a **Closed-Loop System**.

### 1.5 Key Metrics and Physical Quantities

In the formal study of these systems, certain standardized performance indices and signal definitions recur throughout KTU examinations:

- **Reference Input (R(s)):** The desired value of the controlled variable.
- **Actuating / Error Signal (E(s)):** The difference between reference and feedback.
- **Controlled Output (C(s)):** The actual measured response of the plant.
- **Feedback Signal (B(s)):** A scaled version of the output, typically C(s)·H(s).
- **Plant / Process (G(s)):** The physical system being controlled.
- **Controller (Gc(s) or Gc):** The compensating element.
- **Feedback Element (H(s)):** The transducer/sensor in the feedback path.
- **Disturbance / Noise (D(s)):** An unwanted input that degrades performance.
- **Unity Feedback System:** A special configuration where **H(s) = 1** (the feedback path is a direct wire).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of an Open-Loop System

The operational flow of a non-feedback system follows a strictly **unidirectional** path:

1. **Input Command Applied:** The reference signal **R(s)** is supplied to the system externally.
2. **Controller Processes Input:** The controller **Gc(s)** modifies the input according to a fixed gain or pre-defined logic.
3. **Plant Actuation:** The processed signal drives the plant **G(s)**, which generates the output **C(s)**.
4. **No Verification Step:** The output is **never measured or compared**; the loop terminates at the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Open-loop systems are cheaper, simpler, and faster to design. They are used in applications where the input-output relationship is **known with certainty** and **disturbances are negligible**, e.g., a washing machine timer, a toaster, or a traffic light controller.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of a Closed-Loop (Feedback) System

The operational flow of a feedback system follows a **bidirectional (cyclic)** path:

1. **Reference Input Applied:** The desired setpoint **R(s)** enters the summing junction (Σ).
2. **Error Generation:** The summing junction computes the error: **E(s) = R(s) − B(s)**, where **B(s) = H(s)·C(s)**.
3. **Controller Action:** The error **E(s)** is amplified/processed by **Gc(s)** to produce a control signal **U(s) = Gc(s)·E(s)**.
4. **Plant Actuation:** The plant **G(s)** reacts to **U(s)** and produces the output **C(s) = G(s)·U(s)**.
5. **Feedback Measurement:** A sensor **H(s)** samples the output and produces **B(s)**.
6. **Loop Closure:** **B(s)** is returned to the summing junction to be subtracted from **R(s)**, completing the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Closed-loop systems automatically **reject disturbances**, **reduce steady-state error**, **improve transient response**, and **compensate for parameter variations** in the plant. They are used in cruise control, autopilot, temperature regulators, and robotic arm positioning.

### 2.3 Advantages & Disadvantages — The Comparative Matrix

| **Property** | **Open-Loop (Non-Feedback)** | **Closed-Loop (Feedback)** |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Poor (depends on calibration) | High (auto-corrects) |
| Sensitivity to Disturbance | Very High | Very Low |
| Sensitivity to Parameter Variation | High | Low |
| Stability | Always Stable (if components stable) | May become Unstable |
| Bandwidth | Wide | Reduced (trade-off) |
| Cost & Complexity | Low | High |
| Design Difficulty | Simple | Complex (requires compensation) |
| Examples | Traffic light, bread toaster | Cruise control, servo motor |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$ | Open-loop output equation | Direct plant input-output |
| 2 | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$ | Error signal definition | Summing junction output |
| 3 | $C(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \cdot R(s)$ | Closed-loop T.F. (negative feedback) | Core feedback equation |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$ | Closed-loop Transfer Function | Stability & response analysis |
| 5 | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$ | Error Transfer Function | Steady-state error analysis |
| 6 | $\dfrac{B(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s) H(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$ | Feedback-path Transfer Function | Sensor signal analysis |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$ | Disturbance Rejection T.F. | Disturbance suppression proof |
| 8 | $G(s) H(s)$ | Loop Gain / Open-Loop T.F. | Characteristic equation root |
| 9 | $1 + G(s) H(s) = 0$ | Characteristic Equation | Stability criterion denominator |
| 10 | $E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ | Final Value Theorem (Steady-State Error) | Steady-state error calculation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The relation $\vert C(s)/D(s) \vert = \vert \frac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)} \vert$ is the mathematical proof that **feedback rejects disturbances**: as the magnitude of the loop gain $G(s)H(s)$ grows large, the disturbance effect vanishes. This is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question** concept.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace:** Autopilot systems use feedback from gyroscopes, accelerometers, and GPS to maintain aircraft attitude.
- **Process Industries:** PID controllers in chemical plants regulate temperature, pressure, and flow using sensor feedback.
- **Robotics:** Robotic arms use position feedback from encoders to reach precise coordinates.
- **Power Systems:** Automatic Voltage Regulators (AVR) use output voltage feedback to keep generator terminal voltage constant.
- **Biomedical:** Insulin pumps use blood-glucose sensor feedback for automated diabetes management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 1 and appears in nearly every KTU university examination. We derive it from first principles using the block diagram relationships.

**Given Architecture:** A standard negative-feedback closed-loop system with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$.

**Step 1 — Define the signals at each node:**

- The output of the summing junction is the error signal:
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

- The feedback signal is the output passed through the feedback element:
$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the feedback expression into the error equation:**

We replace $B(s)$ with $H(s) \cdot C(s)$ in the error equation.

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Express the controller output (control signal):**

The error $E(s)$ is multiplied by the controller transfer function $G_c(s)$. For a generic derivation, we absorb the controller into the forward path and write the forward-path transfer function simply as $G(s)$.

$$U(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Express the plant output:**

The control signal $U(s)$ drives the plant, whose output is:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s)$$

Combining Steps 3 and 4, we get:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[G(s) \cdot E(s)\right]$$

But since the forward path is one block $G(s)$, the cleaner form is:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the error expression into the output equation:**

Replace $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$ into $C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 7 — Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:**

Move the term containing $C(s)$ from the right side to the left side:

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) \cdot \left[1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 9 — Isolate the transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$:**

Divide both sides by $R(s)$ and by the bracket term:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

**This is the canonical Closed-Loop Transfer Function (CLTF) for a negative-feedback system.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Error Transfer Function $E(s)/R(s)$

The error transfer function quantifies how much of the reference input "leaks through" as a steady-state or transient error.

**Step 1 — Start from the error equation:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the closed-loop output expression $C(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \cdot R(s)$:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot \left[\frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \cdot R(s)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $R(s)$ on the right-hand side:**

$$E(s) = R(s) \cdot \left[1 - \frac{G(s) H(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Combine the bracket terms over a common denominator $1 + G(s) H(s)$:**

$$E(s) = R(s) \cdot \left[\frac{1 + G(s) H(s) - G(s) H(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator — the $G(s)H(s)$ terms cancel:**

$$E(s) = R(s) \cdot \left[\frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Isolate the transfer function:**

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** When the loop gain $G(s) H(s)$ is large in magnitude, the denominator becomes huge, and the error transfer function approaches zero — meaning the system tracks the reference almost perfectly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Disturbance Rejection Transfer Function

Consider a closed-loop system with a disturbance $D(s)$ injected at the plant input. The disturbance transfer function shows how effectively feedback suppresses unwanted signals.

**Step 1 — Modified block diagram relationships (with disturbance):**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[U(s) + D(s)\right]$$

where $U(s) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s)$ and $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$.

**Step 2 — Set the reference input $R(s) = 0$ to isolate the disturbance effect:**

With $R(s) = 0$, the error becomes $E(s) = -H(s) \cdot C(s)$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the output equation:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[U(s) + D(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot \left[G_c(s) \cdot E(s) + D(s)\right]$$

$$C(s) = G(s) G_c(s) \cdot E(s) + G(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $E(s) = -H(s) \cdot C(s)$:**

$$C(s) = -G(s) G_c(s) H(s) \cdot C(s) + G(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Step 5 — Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left:**

$$C(s) + G(s) G_c(s) H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor and isolate:**

$$C(s) \cdot \left[1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Step 7 — Disturbance transfer function:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)}$$

**Key Insight:** Comparing this with the open-loop disturbance transfer function $C(s)/D(s) = G(s)$, we see that feedback divides the disturbance effect by the factor $\left[1 + G(s) G_c(s) H(s)\right]$. This factor is the **disturbance-rejection ratio**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Steady-State Error for a Unit-Step Input

The **Final Value Theorem** is used to compute the steady-state error $E_{ss}$ once the system has settled.

**Given:** A unity-feedback system ($H(s) = 1$) with open-loop transfer function $G(s)$, and a unit-step reference $R(s) = 1/s$.

**Step 1 — Write the error transfer function for unity feedback:**

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the error in the s-domain:**

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{s \cdot \left[1 + G(s)\right]}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem (valid only if all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the open left-half plane):**

$$E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s \cdot \left[1 + G(s)\right]}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $s$:**

$$E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)}$$

**Step 6 — Define the static position error constant $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$:**

$$E_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

**Result:** For a Type-0 system with finite $K_p$, the steady-state error to a unit step is a non-zero constant. This is a recurring 7-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Open-Loop (Non-Feedback) System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the unidirectional flow of an open-loop system. The reference input passes through a controller and then through the plant to produce the output, with **no feedback path** existing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[REFERENCE INPUT R s] --> Gc1[CONTROLLER Gc s]
    Gc1 --> G1[PLANT G s]
    G1 --> C1[OUTPUT C s]
    D1[DISTURBANCE D s] -.->|affects| G1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The dotted arrow from `DISTURBANCE D s` shows that disturbances influence the plant directly with **no mechanism to correct** for their effect, because there is no feedback path returning to the controller.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop (Feedback) System with Negative Feedback

The following Mermaid diagram represents the canonical negative-feedback architecture. The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal from the reference to produce the error.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R2[REFERENCE R s] --> SUM2{SUMMING JUNCTION}
    SUM2 -->|ERROR E s| Gc2[CONTROLLER Gc s]
    Gc2 --> G2[PLANT G s]
    G2 --> C2[OUTPUT C s]
    C2 --> H2[FEEDBACK ELEMENT H s]
    H2 --> B2[FEEDBACK SIGNAL B s]
    B2 -->|minus sign| SUM2
    D2[DISTURBANCE D s] -.->|injected at plant input| G2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The feedback signal **B s** is subtracted from the reference **R s** at the summing junction. This is the mathematical implementation of negative feedback: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Unity Negative Feedback System (Special Case)

The unity feedback system is the most common configuration studied in KTU examinations. It corresponds to $H(s) = 1$, meaning the feedback path is a direct wire.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R3[R s] --> SUM3{SUMMING JUNCTION}
    SUM3 -->|E s| G3[FORWARD PATH G s]
    G3 --> C3[C s]
    C3 --> FEED3[UNITY FEEDBACK LINE H s EQUALS 1]
    FEED3 --> B3[B s EQUALS C s]
    B3 -->|subtracted| SUM3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** In unity feedback, $B(s) = C(s)$ and the closed-loop transfer function simplifies to $C(s)/R(s) = G(s) / \left[1 + G(s)\right]$. This form appears in over 70% of KTU classical control problems.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Functional Architecture — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop

The following side-by-side block diagram maps the functional differences between the two architectures in a single visual.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPENLOOP[OPEN LOOP SYSTEM]
        direction LR
        OR[R s] --> OGC[Gc s]
        OGC --> OG[G s]
        OG --> OC[C s]
    end

    subgraph CLOSEDLOOP[CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM]
        direction LR
        CR[R s] --> CSUM{SUMMER}
        CSUM -->|E s| CGC[Gc s]
        CGC --> CG[G s]
        CG --> CC[C s]
        CC --> CH[H s]
        CH --> CB[B s]
        CB -->|minus| CSUM
    end

    OPENLOOP ~~~ CLOSEDLOOP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Insight:** The closed-loop system has **one additional block** (the feedback element $H(s)$), **one additional summing junction**, and a **return path** that creates the loop. This added structural complexity is the price paid for the superior performance benefits of disturbance rejection and accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Open-Loop System** | **Closed-Loop System** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback path | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low; depends on calibration | High; self-correcting |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor | Excellent |
| Example | Automatic washing machine timer | Room temperature controller using thermostat |

In an **open-loop system**, the control action is independent of the output, e.g., a **traffic light controller** that cycles through fixed timings regardless of actual traffic density. In a **closed-loop system**, the output is measured and fed back to the input to minimize error, e.g., a **room temperature controller** where a thermostat senses the room temperature and compares it to the setpoint.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write "open-loop has no controller" — this is **wrong**. Open-loop systems **do have** a controller; what they lack is the **feedback path**. Marks will be deducted for this confusion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "transfer function" and state its significance in control system analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Transfer Function** is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output variable to the Laplace transform of the input variable, under the assumption of **zero initial conditions**.

$$\text{Transfer Function} = \frac{\text{Laplace Transform of Output } C(s)}{\text{Laplace Transform of Input } R(s)}$$

**Significance:**
- It is a **mathematical model** of the system's input-output behavior in the s-domain.
- It is **independent of the magnitude and nature of the input signal**.
- It is used to determine **stability, transient response, and steady-state performance** of the system.
- For a closed-loop system, the overall transfer function $T(s) = G(s) / \left[1 + G(s)H(s)\right]$ compactly captures the entire feedback dynamics.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and consists of two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ of a negative-feedback control system having forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the basic signal relationships at each node of the block diagram.**

The error signal at the output of the summing junction is:
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

The feedback signal is the output passed through the feedback element:
$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

The plant output is generated from the controller signal passing through the forward path:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $B(s)$ into the error equation.**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $E(s)$ into the plant output equation.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor $C(s)$ and isolate the ratio.**

$$C(s) \cdot \left[1 + G(s) H(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function** for a negative-feedback system.

---

**(b)** For a unity-feedback control system, the open-loop transfer function is given by $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Determine: **(i)** the closed-loop transfer function, **(ii)** the steady-state error for a unit-step input. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$. The closed-loop transfer function is:
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

Substituting $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Step (ii) — Steady-State Error for Unit-Step Input:**

The error transfer function for unity feedback is:
$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

For a unit-step input, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$E(s) = \frac{1}{s \cdot \left[1 + G(s)\right]}$$

Apply the Final Value Theorem to find the steady-state error:

$$E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

Substituting $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$:

$$E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

As $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)} \to \infty$:
