# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity Function from First Principles

### Problem Statement
Given the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ with unity feedback $H(s)=1$, derive the sensitivity of $T$ with respect to variations in $G(s)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write the closed-loop transfer function explicitly.**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

This expression shows $T$ as a function of the plant $G$.

**Step 2: Apply the definition of sensitivity with respect to $G$.**

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial G}$$

This is the standard Bode sensitivity formula.

**Step 3: Compute $\partial T / \partial G$ using the quotient rule.**

Let $N = G$ and $D = 1 + GH$. Then $T = N/D$, so:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1)\cdot D - G \cdot (H)}{D^2} = \frac{(1+GH) - GH}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}$$

The numerator $D - GH$ simplifies because the derivative of $1+GH$ with respect to $G$ is $H$ (since $H$ is treated as constant).

**Step 4: Substitute into the sensitivity formula.**

$$S_G^T = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}$$

**Step 5: Replace $T$ in the denominator with $G/(1+GH)$.**

$$S_G^T = G \cdot \frac{(1+GH)}{G} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}$$

**Step 6: Cancel common terms.**

$$S_G^T = \frac{G}{G} \cdot \frac{1+GH}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{1+GH}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** As $\vert GH \vert$ increases, the sensitivity decreases. For a high-gain system, $S_G^T \approx 1/(GH) \to 0$, meaning $T$ becomes independent of $G$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Percentage Change in Closed-Loop Gain

### Problem Statement
A plant has nominal gain $K = 10$ which varies by $\pm 20\%$ due to temperature. With unity feedback and a controller gain $K_c = 5$, compute the percentage change in the closed-loop DC gain.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the open-loop gain $L_0$ and DC gain $T(0)$.**

$$L_0 = K \cdot K_c = 10 \cdot 5 = 50$$

$$T(0) = \frac{L_0}{1+L_0} = \frac{50}{51} \approx 0.9804$$

**Step 2: Apply the sensitivity approximation formula.**

$$\frac{\Delta T}{T} \approx S_K^T \cdot \frac{\Delta K}{K}$$

**Step 3: Compute the sensitivity at DC.**

$$S_K^T = \frac{1}{1+L_0} = \frac{1}{51} \approx 0.0196$$

**Step 4: Compute the resulting percentage change in $T$.**

$$\frac{\Delta T}{T} \approx 0.0196 \times 20\% = 0.392\%$$

**Step 5: Compare with open-loop case.**

For open-loop, $T_{OL} = K$, so $\Delta T / T = 20\%$. The feedback reduces this variation by a factor of **51**.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta T}{T}\bigg|_{CL} = 0.392\% \ll 20\% = \frac{\Delta T}{T}\bigg|_{OL}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 20% plant gain variation is reduced to less than 0.4% in the closed-loop. This is the **desensitization** at work — high loop gain yields low sensitivity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Error Due to Step Disturbance

### Problem Statement
For a unity feedback system with forward path $G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)}$ and a step disturbance $D(s) = 1/s$ entering at the plant input, compute the steady-state error $e_{ss}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the disturbance transfer function.**

The disturbance enters before the plant, so it is multiplied by $G_2 = G(s)$ and divided by $(1+G(s))$:

$$T_d(s) = \frac{C(s)}{D(s)}\bigg|_{R=0} = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$$

**Step 2: Write $C(s)$ in response to the step disturbance.**

$$C(s) = T_d(s) \cdot D(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 3: Compute $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$.**

With $R(s) = 0$ (only disturbance acting):

$$E(s) = -C(s) = -\frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Final Value Theorem (assuming stability).**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = -\lim_{s \to 0} \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $G(s) = 10/[s(s+2)]$ and take the limit.**

$$G(0) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10}{s(s+2)} = \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = -\lim_{s \to 0} \frac{\infty}{1+\infty} = -1$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = -1 \text{ (in disturbance units)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The disturbance causes a steady-state error of magnitude equal to the disturbance itself in the *output*, but the **error signal** $e = R - C$ is finite because of the integrator in $G(s)$. In fact, since $G(s)$ has a pole at origin (Type 1), the disturbance is **completely rejected** at steady state when measured as the *controller output effort*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Closed-Loop Bandwidth Enhancement

### Problem Statement
A first-order plant $G(s) = \frac{5}{0.1s + 1}$ is placed in unity feedback. Compute the closed-loop bandwidth and compare with the open-loop bandwidth.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the open-loop bandwidth.**

For $G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$, the bandwidth is $\omega_{BW,ol} = 1/\tau = 1/0.1 = 10$ rad/s.

**Step 2: Write the closed-loop transfer function.**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \frac{5/(0.1s+1)}{1 + 5/(0.1s+1)} = \frac{5}{0.1s + 1 + 5} = \frac{5}{0.1s + 6}$$

**Step 3: Normalize to standard first-order form.**

$$T(s) = \frac{5/6}{0.1s/6 + 1} = \frac{0.833}{0.01667s + 1}$$

**Step 4: Identify the closed-loop time constant and bandwidth.**

$$\tau_{cl} = 0.01667 \text{ s}, \quad \omega_{BW,cl} = \frac{1}{0.01667} = 60 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute the bandwidth enhancement factor.**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{\omega_{BW,cl}}{\omega_{BW,ol}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 = 1 + K = 1 + 5$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\omega_{BW,cl} = 60 \text{ rad/s (6 times larger than open-loop)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop (Unity Feedback) | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Gain** | $K = 5$ | $K/(1+K) = 0.833$ | Reduced but predictable |
| **Bandwidth** | $10$ rad/s | $60$ rad/s | $\times 6$ |
| **Time constant** | $0.1$ s | $0.0167$ s | $\times 1/6$ |
| **Sensitivity to $K$** | $1.0$ (100%) | $1/(1+K) = 0.167$ (16.7%) | $\times 1/6$ |
| **Step disturbance $c_{ss}$** | $1$ (uncompensated) | $1/(1+K) = 0.167$ | $\times 1/6$ |
| **Steady-state error (Type 1)** | $\infty$ for ramp | $0$ for step | Infinite improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Magic Number:** All four improvements (bandwidth, sensitivity reduction, time constant reduction, disturbance rejection) are governed by the **single factor** $(1+K)$ for a first-order unity feedback system. This is the unifying principle of feedback.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Define the sensitivity of a control system. Derive the expression for sensitivity of closed-loop transfer function with respect to forward path transfer function."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Sensitivity is a measure of the effect of parameter variation on the system performance. It is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in the transfer function to the fractional change in the parameter.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
For a closed-loop system with unity feedback $H(s)=1$, $T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$.

$$S_G^T = \frac{dT/T}{dG/G} = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{dT}{dG} = \frac{G(1+G)}{G} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+G)^2} = \frac{1}{1+G(s)}$$

The sensitivity is **inversely proportional to the loop gain**, and as $G \to \infty$, $S \to 0$.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*"Explain how negative feedback helps in reducing the effect of disturbance signals. Mention the role of loop gain."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):** In a closed-loop system, the disturbance $D(s)$ affects the output, but the feedback loop senses this deviation and the controller generates a corrective signal. The disturbance transfer function is $T_d(s) = \frac{G_2(s)}{1+G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$.

**Role of loop gain (1 mark):** The denominator contains the loop gain $L(s) = G_1 G_2 H$. As $\vert L(s) \vert$ increases, the effect of disturbance on the output is suppressed by the factor $(1+L)$. Hence, **higher loop gain yields better disturbance rejection**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a) For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$, derive the sensitivity of the closed-loop transfer function with respect to the gain $K$. Show that feedback reduces sensitivity by a factor of $(1+K)$.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \frac{10}{s+5}$. A disturbance $D(s) = \frac{2}{s}$ enters at the input of the plant. Compute the steady-state error due to this disturbance and compare with the open-loop case.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Write the closed-loop transfer function.**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \frac{K/[s(\tau s + 1)]}{1 + K/[s(\tau s + 1)]} = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1) + K}$$

**Step 2: Apply the sensitivity formula.**

$$S_K^T = \frac{K}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial K}$$

**Step 3: Compute the partial derivative.**

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial K} = \frac{[s(\tau s+1) + K] - K[1]}{[s(\tau s+1) + K]^2} = \frac{s(\tau s+1)}{[s(\tau s+1) + K]^2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the sensitivity formula.**

$$S_K^T = \frac{K[s(\tau s+1) + K]}{K} \cdot \frac{s(\tau s+1)}{[s(\tau s+1) + K]^2} = \frac{s(\tau s+1)}{s(\tau s+1) + K}$$

**Step 5: Rewrite in terms of $G(s)$.**

Since $G(s) = K/[s(\tau s+1)]$, we have $s(\tau s+1) = K/G$:

$$S_K^T = \frac{K/G}{K/G + K} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{1+K/[s(\tau s+1)]}$$

**Step 6: Compare with open-loop sensitivity.**

For open-loop, $T_{OL} = G$, so $S_K^{OL} = 1$. The closed-loop sensitivity is reduced by the factor:

$$\frac{S_K^{OL}}{S_K^{CL}} = 1 + G(s) = 1 + \frac{K}{s(\tau s+1)}$$

At DC ($s=0$), the reduction factor is $(1 + \infty) = \infty$ (Type 1 system). At any non-zero frequency, the factor is $(1 + K/[s(\tau s+1)])$. **Result: Feedback reduces sensitivity by a factor of $(1+G)$, confirming desensitization.**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the system configuration.**

Unity feedback, plant $G(s) = \frac{10}{s+5}$, disturbance $D(s) = \frac{2}{s}$ at plant input.

**Step 2: Write the disturbance transfer function.**

With $G_1 = 1$ (no separate controller) and $G_2 = G$:

$$T_d(s) = \frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \frac{10/(s+5)}{1 + 10/(s+5)} = \frac{10}{s+15}$$

**Step 3: Compute the output due to disturbance.**

$$C_d(s) = T_d(s) \cdot D(s) = \frac{10}{s+15} \cdot \frac{2}{s} = \frac{20}{s(s+15)}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Final Value Theorem.**

$$c_{ss,d} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{20}{s(s+15)} = \frac{20}{15} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$$

**Step 5: Compute the open-loop disturbance effect.**

In open-loop, $C_{OL}(s) = G(s) D(s) = \frac{10 \cdot 2}{s(s+5)} = \frac{20}{s(s+5)}$:

$$c_{ss,OL} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{20}{s(s+5)} = \frac{20}{5} = 4$$

**Step 6: Compute the reduction factor.**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{c_{ss,OL}}{c_{ss,CL}} = \frac{4}{4/3} = 3 = 1 + G(0) = 1 + \frac{10}{5} = 3$$

**Result:** Feedback reduces the steady-state disturbance effect from **4** to **4/3**, an improvement factor of **3×**. The reduction factor equals $(1 + G(0)) = 3$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
*(a) With a block diagram, explain how feedback improves the transient response of a control system. Derive the relationship between closed-loop and open-loop bandwidth for a first-order system.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) A temperature control system has plant $G(s) = \frac{2}{(s+1)(s+3)}$ with unity feedback. A unit-step disturbance enters at the input. Compute:* **(7 marks)**
- *(i) The disturbance transfer function*
- *(ii) The steady-state value of the output due to disturbance*
- *(iii) The percentage reduction in disturbance effect compared to open-loop*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Block diagram explanation (3 marks).**

In a unity feedback system, the forward path is $G(s)$ and the feedback is $H(s) = 1$. The closed-loop transfer function is $T(s) = G/(1+G)$. The poles of $T(s)$ are the roots of $1 + G(s) = 0$, which can be relocated by adjusting the controller, thereby shaping the transient response.

**Step 2: First-order system derivation (4 marks).**

Let $G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ in unity feedback:

$$T(s) = \frac{K/(\tau s + 1)}{1 + K/(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1 + K} = \frac{K/(1+K)}{[\tau/(1+K)]s + 1}$$

This is a first-order system with effective time constant $\tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1+K}$ and DC gain $K_{cl} = \frac{K}{1+K}$.

**Bandwidth relationship:**

The open-loop bandwidth is $\omega_{BW,ol} = 1/\tau$.

The closed-loop bandwidth is $\omega_{BW,cl} = 1/\tau_{cl} = (1+K)/\tau$.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\omega_{BW,cl} = (1+K) \cdot \omega_{BW,ol}}$$

The closed-loop bandwidth is **larger by the factor $(1+K)$**, indicating faster transient response and wider operating range.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the disturbance transfer function (2 marks).**

With $G(s) = \frac{2}{(s+1)(s+3)}$ and unity feedback:

$$T_d(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \frac{2/(s^2+4s+3)}{1 + 2/(s^2+4s+3)} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 4s + 5}$$

**Step 2: Compute steady-state output for unit-step disturbance (2 marks).**

For $D(s) = 1/s$:

$$c_{ss,d} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot T_d(s) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = T_d(0) = \frac{2}{0+0+5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

**Step 3: Compute open-loop steady-state effect (2 marks).**

For open-loop, $C_{OL}(s) = G(s) D(s)$:

$$c_{ss,OL} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = G(0) = \frac{2}{(1)(3)} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 4: Compute percentage reduction (1 mark).**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{0.667 - 0.4}{0.667} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**Final Result:** Feedback reduces the disturbance effect by **40%**, with the reduction factor equal to $\frac{1}{1+G(0)} = \frac{1}{1+2/3} = 0.6$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sensitivity formula inversion (–2 marks):** Writing $S_G^T = 1 + GH$ instead of $S_G^T = 1/(1+GH)$. Always remember: sensitivity **decreases** with loop gain.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\partial \ln$ form (–1 mark):** The sensitivity is defined as $\frac{\partial \ln T}{\partial \ln \alpha}$, not $\frac{\partial T}{\partial \alpha}$. Many students lose marks by skipping the logarithmic differentiation.
>
> 3. **Disturbance vs Noise confusion (–2 marks):** Disturbance $D(s)$ is a low-frequency unwanted input; noise $N(s)$ is a high-frequency measurement error. They are affected differently by feedback — disturbance is rejected at low frequency, noise is rejected at high frequency.
>
> 4. **Stability assumption in FVT (–1 mark):** When applying the Final Value Theorem for steady-state error, you must check that the closed-loop system is stable. If $T_d(s)$ has poles in the right half-plane, the FVT gives a misleading result.
>
> 5. **Unit consistency (–1 mark):** Disturbance input and output have different physical units (e.g., Nm vs rad/s). The "disturbance transfer function" $T_d$ has units of [output unit] / [disturbance unit]. State this explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Feedback Characteristics**

- ✅ **Closed-loop TF:** $T(s) = G/(1+GH)$ — foundation of all feedback analysis.
- ✅ **Sensitivity formula:** $S_G^T = 1/(1+GH)$ — inversely proportional to loop gain.
- ✅ **Sensitivity to general parameter $\alpha$:** $S_\alpha^T = (\alpha/T)(\partial T/\partial \alpha)$.
- ✅ **Desensitization rule:** High loop gain $\vert GH \vert \gg 1 \Rightarrow S \to 0$.
- ✅ **Disturbance TF (after plant):** $T_d = G_2/(1+G_1G_2H)$.
- ✅ **Steady-state disturbance:** $c_{ss,d} = G_2(0)/(1+L_0)$.
- ✅ **Bandwidth enhancement:** $\omega_{BW,cl} = (1+L_0) \cdot \omega_{BW,ol}$.
- ✅ **Time constant reduction:** $\tau_{cl} = \tau/(1+L_0)$.
- ✅ **Identity:** $S(s) + T(s) = 1$ (waterbed effect).
- ✅ **Type-number rule:** Type $n$ system rejects polynomial disturbance up to degree $(n-1)$ in steady state.
- ✅ **Three feedback benefits:** (1) Reduce parameter sensitivity, (2) Control dynamics, (3) Reject disturbance.
- ✅ **Open-loop vs closed-loop trade-off:** Closed-loop reduces gain (by $1/(1+L_0)$) but improves all three benefits.
- ✅ **Final Value Theorem prerequisite:** System must be asymptotically stable before applying FVT.
- ✅ **Exam-tip formula to memorize:** $T = L/(1+L)$, $S = 1/(1+L)$, $T_d = G_2/(1+L)$.
- ✅ **Common numerical:** For $L_0 = 50$, sensitivity is $1/51 \approx 2\%$; disturbance is reduced by 51×.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Mathematical Modelling and Transfer Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Mathematical Modelling and Transfer Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mathematical Modelling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Modelling** is the process of deriving mathematical equations (typically differential equations) that describe the dynamic behaviour of a physical system by applying the fundamental laws of physics, such as Newton's Laws, Kirchhoff's Laws, and conservation principles.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Classical Control Engineering, mathematical modelling forms the **bedrock of Module 1**, because every subsequent concept — stability analysis, time-domain response, frequency response, and controller design — depends entirely on having an accurate mathematical representation of the plant.

The general workflow of mathematical modelling in control engineering involves:

1. **Identify the physical system** and its components.
2. **Apply the governing physical laws** (Newton's Law for mechanical, Kirchhoff's Voltage/Current Law for electrical).
3. **Derive the differential equation** relating input (forcing function) to output (response).
4. **Apply Laplace Transform** (with zero initial conditions) to obtain the Transfer Function.

## 1.2 Transfer Function — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Function** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace Transform of the output response to the Laplace Transform of the input excitation, assuming **all initial conditions are zero**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} \bigg|_{\text{zero initial conditions}}$$

where:
- $G(s)$ = Transfer Function of the system
- $Y(s)$ = Laplace Transform of the output signal $y(t)$
- $U(s)$ = Laplace Transform of the input signal $u(t)$
- $s$ = Complex Laplace variable, $s = \sigma + j\omega$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Ratio" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Water Tap and a Bucket**
> Imagine you are filling a bucket with water from a tap. The **input** is how far you turn the tap handle (in degrees). The **output** is how much water flows out (in litres per second). The **transfer function** is essentially the *constant ratio* that tells you: *"For every 1 degree you turn the handle, you get X litres per second of water."* This ratio does not depend on how much water is already in your pipes (zero initial conditions), and it stays the same regardless of when you open the tap (time-invariance). That constant, intrinsic ratio — that is your **transfer function**.

This analogy captures the three crucial properties of a transfer function:

| Property | Tap Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Zero Initial Conditions** | Pipes start empty | No stored energy in the system at $t = 0$ |
| **Linearity** | Twice the turn → twice the flow | Superposition principle holds |
| **Time-Invariance** | Same response in morning/evening | System parameters do not change with time |

## 1.4 Why Transfer Function Matters in KTU Curriculum

The transfer function is the **single most important mathematical object** in classical control engineering because:

- It **converts differential equations into algebraic equations** in the $s$-domain, making analysis far easier.
- It is the **direct input to all classical analysis tools**: root locus, Bode plot, Nyquist plot, and Routh-Hurwitz array.
- It allows the engineer to **predict system behaviour** (stability, speed, accuracy) without solving differential equations in the time domain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly requires students to derive the transfer function of at least one **electrical network**, one **translational mechanical system**, and one **rotational mechanical system**. These are the three compulsory derivations expected in the End Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steps in Mathematical Modelling — Structured Logic

The derivation of a transfer function follows a **rigorous six-step procedure** that KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate in full:

- **Step 1 — System Identification:** Recognise the physical domain (electrical / mechanical / electromechanical / thermal / hydraulic).
- **Step 2 — Element Selection:** Identify the energy-storing and energy-dissipating elements (e.g., for electrical: $R$, $L$, $C$; for mechanical: $M$, $D$, $K$).
- **Step 3 — Assumption Statement:** Explicitly state assumptions such as LTI behaviour, lumped parameters, and ideal elements.
- **Step 4 — Apply Governing Law:** Write the constitutive equations (e.g., $V = L\frac{di}{dt}$ for inductor, $f = Ma$ for mass).
- **Step 5 — Form Differential Equation:** Combine element equations using KVL/KCL or D'Alembert's principle.
- **Step 6 — Laplace Transform:** Convert the differential equation to the $s$-domain assuming **zero initial conditions**, and form the ratio $\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$.

## 2.2 Standard Physical Elements and Their Constitutive Laws

> [!NOTE]
> These six constitutive laws are the **fundamental building blocks** of every KTU Module 1 problem. Memorise them thoroughly.

| Domain | Element | Variable | Constitutive Equation (Time Domain) | Laplace Domain |
|-------|---------|----------|------------------------------------|----------------|
| **Electrical** | Resistor $R$ | Voltage $v$, Current $i$ | $v(t) = R \cdot i(t)$ | $V(s) = R \cdot I(s)$ |
| **Electrical** | Inductor $L$ | Voltage $v$, Current $i$ | $v(t) = L \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$ | $V(s) = L \cdot s \cdot I(s)$ |
| **Electrical** | Capacitor $C$ | Voltage $v$, Current $i$ | $i(t) = C \dfrac{dv(t)}{dt}$ | $V(s) = \dfrac{1}{Cs} \cdot I(s)$ |
| **Mechanical (Translational)** | Mass $M$ | Force $f$, Displacement $x$ | $f(t) = M \dfrac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$ | $F(s) = M s^{2} X(s)$ |
| **Mechanical (Translational)** | Damper $B$ | Force $f$, Velocity $v$ | $f(t) = B \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $F(s) = B s \cdot X(s)$ |
| **Mechanical (Translational)** | Spring $K$ | Force $f$, Displacement $x$ | $f(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ | $F(s) = K \cdot X(s)$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every standard formula** required for solving Module 1 problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Master this table and you can attempt 90% of expected questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Transfer Function | $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}\Big\vert_{\text{zero IC}}$ | Output-to-Input ratio in $s$-domain |
| 2 | Laplace of Derivative | $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = sX(s) - x(0^{-})$ | First derivative property |
| 3 | Laplace of Derivative (Zero IC) | $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = sX(s)$ | When $x(0^{-}) = 0$ |
| 4 | Laplace of Double Derivative | $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}\right\} = s^{2}X(s)$ | When all initial conditions zero |
| 5 | Impedance of Inductor | $Z_{L}(s) = Ls$ | Inductor in $s$-domain |
| 6 | Impedance of Capacitor | $Z_{C}(s) = \dfrac{1}{Cs}$ | Capacitor in $s$-domain |
| 7 | Mechanical Impedance (Mass) | $Z_{M}(s) = Ms^{2}$ | Force-Velocity ratio |
| 8 | Characteristic Equation | Denominator of $G(s) = 0$ | Roots give system poles |
| 9 | DC Gain | $K_{dc} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$ | Steady-state gain |
| 10 | Time Constant | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\vert p \vert}$ for first-order pole $p$ | Speed of response indicator |

## 2.4 Properties of Transfer Function — KTU Exam Favourite

- **Property 1:** The transfer function is defined **only for LTI systems** — non-linear or time-varying systems cannot be represented this way.
- **Property 2:** The transfer function is **independent of the magnitude and nature of the input** — it is an intrinsic property of the system.
- **Property 3:** If the highest power of $s$ in the denominator is $n$ and in the numerator is $m$, then the transfer function is of order $(n - m)$.
- **Property 4:** The roots of the numerator polynomial are called **zeros**; the roots of the denominator polynomial are called **poles**.
- **Property 5:** The transfer function gives the **complete description** of the system dynamics (only the steady-state output requires additional information).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robotics:** Mathematical models predict the precise joint torques needed for trajectory tracking.
- **Automotive:** Cruise control systems use transfer functions of engine + vehicle dynamics to maintain constant speed.
- **Aerospace:** Autopilot design relies on transfer functions of aircraft pitch, roll, and yaw dynamics.
- **Process Control:** Chemical plant temperature, pressure, and flow loops are all designed using transfer function models.
- **Power Systems:** Transfer functions characterise the dynamic response of generator voltage regulators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A transfer function is essentially the "fingerprint" of a system. Two systems with the same transfer function will behave identically, regardless of their physical construction. This is the principle of **analogous systems** — an electrical RLC circuit can be made to behave exactly like a mechanical mass-spring-damper, and this duality is widely exploited in control engineering education and in hardware-in-the-loop simulations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series RLC Electrical Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Consider a series RLC circuit driven by an input voltage $v_{in}(t)$. Derive the transfer function relating the output voltage across the capacitor $v_{out}(t) = v_{C}(t)$ to the input.

### Step 1 — Define Variables and Circuit

- Let $i(t)$ be the loop current.
- Let $v_{R}(t)$, $v_{L}(t)$, $v_{C}(t)$ be the voltages across $R$, $L$, $C$ respectively.
- Output: $v_{out}(t) = v_{C}(t)$.

### Step 2 — Write Element Equations (Time Domain)

For each element, write the voltage-current relation:

$$v_{R}(t) = R \cdot i(t)$$

$$v_{L}(t) = L \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

$$v_{C}(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i(\tau) d\tau$$

### Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)

Around the closed loop, the sum of voltage drops equals the input voltage:

$$v_{in}(t) = v_{R}(t) + v_{L}(t) + v_{C}(t)$$

Substituting the element equations:

$$v_{in}(t) = R \cdot i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i(\tau) d\tau$$

### Step 4 — Express Current in Terms of Capacitor Voltage

Since the output is the capacitor voltage, we differentiate and use $i = C \frac{dv_{C}}{dt}$:

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt}$$

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} = C \frac{d^{2}v_{C}(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into KVL Equation

$$v_{in}(t) = R \cdot C \frac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt} + L \cdot C \frac{d^{2}v_{C}(t)}{dt^{2}} + v_{C}(t)$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange into Standard Form

$$LC \frac{d^{2}v_{C}(t)}{dt^{2}} + RC \frac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt} + v_{C}(t) = v_{in}(t)$$

This is a **second-order linear constant-coefficient ordinary differential equation** (ODE).

### Step 7 — Apply Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)

Taking the Laplace transform of both sides, and using $\mathcal{L}\{v_{C}(t)\} = V_{out}(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{v_{in}(t)\} = V_{in}(s)$:

$$LC \cdot s^{2} V_{out}(s) + RC \cdot s \cdot V_{out}(s) + V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s)$$

### Step 8 — Factor Out $V_{out}(s)$

$$V_{out}(s) \left[ LC s^{2} + RC s + 1 \right] = V_{in}(s)$$

### Step 9 — Form the Transfer Function

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{LCs^{2} + RCs + 1}\,}$$

### Step 10 — Identify Standard Second-Order Parameters

Dividing numerator and denominator by $LC$:

$$G(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{LC}}{s^{2} + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

Comparing with the standard form $\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$:

$$\omega_{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}, \quad 2\zeta\omega_{n} = \frac{R}{L} \implies \zeta = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Spring-Damper Translational Mechanical System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A mass $M$ is connected to a fixed wall through a spring $K$ and a damper $B$ in parallel. An external force $f(t)$ is applied. Derive the transfer function $\frac{X(s)}{F(s)}$.

### Step 1 — Draw the Free Body Diagram Conceptually

The mass $M$ experiences four forces:
- Applied force $f(t)$ (to the right, taken positive)
- Inertial force $M\ddot{x}(t)$ (opposing acceleration, D'Alembert)
- Damping force $B\dot{x}(t)$ (opposing velocity)
- Spring force $Kx(t)$ (opposing displacement)

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law / D'Alembert's Principle

Sum of forces equals mass times acceleration:

$$f(t) = M \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + K x(t)$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Form

$$M \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + K x(t) = f(t)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{M \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}\right\} = M s^{2} X(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{B \frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = B s X(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{K x(t)\} = K X(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s)$$

Substituting:

$$Ms^{2} X(s) + Bs X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)$$

### Step 5 — Form the Transfer Function

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the System Order and Poles

This is a **second-order system** with two poles given by the roots of $Ms^{2} + Bs + K = 0$:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^{2} - 4MK}}{2M}$$

The system is **overdamped** if $B^{2} > 4MK$, **critically damped** if $B^{2} = 4MK$, and **underdamped** if $B^{2} < 4MK$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque-Driven Rotational Mechanical System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A rotational system has inertia $J$, viscous friction coefficient $B$, and torsional stiffness $K$, all in parallel, with an applied torque $T(t)$. Derive $\frac{\theta(s)}{T(s)}$.

### Step 1 — Write the Rotational Equation of Motion

By analogy with the translational system, replacing force by torque, displacement by angular displacement $\theta$, mass by inertia, and damper/spring coefficients by their rotational counterparts:

$$T(t) = J \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + K \theta(t)$$

### Step 2 — Apply Laplace Transform

$$Js^{2} \theta(s) + Bs \theta(s) + K \theta(s) = T(s)$$

### Step 3 — Form the Transfer Function

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\theta(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{1}{Js^{2} + Bs + K}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Force-Current Analogy:** Notice that the rotational mechanical transfer function has the **exact same form** as the translational mechanical transfer function. Similarly, the series RLC electrical transfer function has the same mathematical structure. This is the **principle of analogous systems** — a mass $M$ is analogous to an inductor $L$, a damper $B$ is analogous to a resistor $R$, and a spring $K$ is analogous to the inverse of a capacitor $1/C$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Mathematical Modelling Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end workflow** that an engineer follows to convert a physical system into its transfer function representation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical System] --> B[Identify Domain and Elements]
    B --> C[Apply Governing Physical Laws]
    C --> D[Derive Differential Equation in Time Domain]
    D --> E[Apply Laplace Transform with Zero Initial Conditions]
    E --> F[Form the Transfer Function Ratio Y_s over U_s]
    F --> G{Is the System LTI?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Valid Transfer Function G_s]
    G -->|No| I[Transfer Function Not Applicable]
    H --> J[Determine Poles and Zeros]
    J --> K[Identify System Order]
    K --> L[Module 1 Analysis Complete]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Generic Open-Loop Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input R_s] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[Controller Gc_s]
    C --> D[Actuator Ga_s]
    D --> E[Plant Gp_s]
    E --> F[Output Y_s]
    F --> G[Feedback Sensor H_s]
    G --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The signal flows from the reference input $R(s)$ on the left, through the controller, actuator, and plant in series, to produce the output $Y(s)$. The feedback path takes the output through a sensor $H(s)$ and returns it to the summing junction at the input. The closed-loop transfer function is given by $\frac{G_c G_a G_p}{1 + G_c G_a G_p H}$, which is derived in Module 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Translational Mechanical System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Force f_t] --> B[Force Summation Node]
    B --> C[Mass M produces Acceleration]
    C --> D[Double Integration yields Position x_t]
    D --> E[Spring K opposes Position]
    D --> F[Damper B opposes Velocity]
    E --> B
    F --> B
    D --> G[Output x_t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** The applied force $f(t)$ enters the summation node. The mass $M$ converts the net force into acceleration $\ddot{x}(t)$. A double integration transforms acceleration into displacement $x(t)$. The spring and damper elements generate restoring forces proportional to displacement and velocity respectively, and these forces are fed back to the summation node. This is the **closed-loop feedback structure inherent in every second-order mechanical system**, even though no controller is involved — the physics itself forms the loop.

## 4.4 Analogy Map Between Electrical and Mechanical Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inductor L] -->|Analogue| M[Mass M]
    B[Resistor R] -->|Analogue| N[Damper B]
    C[Capacitor 1 over C] -->|Analogue| O[Spring K]
    D[Voltage v_t] -->|Analogue| P[Force f_t]
    E[Current i_t] -->|Analogue| Q[Velocity v_t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Analogy Map:** The force-current analogy (also called the **Mobility analogy**) states that voltage is analogous to force, current is analogous to velocity, inductance is analogous to mass, resistance is analogous to damping coefficient, and the inverse of capacitance is analogous to spring stiffness. Using this map, an electrical engineer can mechanically simulate a control problem and vice versa — a powerful tool in KTU lab courses and in industrial practice.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Transfer Function and State Any Four of Its Properties

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Transfer Function** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace Transform of the output to the Laplace Transform of the input, assuming all initial conditions are zero.

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} \bigg|_{\text{zero initial conditions}}$$

**Four Properties of Transfer Function:**

1. It is defined **only for LTI systems** — non-linear and time-varying systems do not have a transfer function in the classical sense.
2. The transfer function is a **function of the complex variable $s$** (not the physical time $t$), making it an algebraic expression.
3. The transfer function is **independent of the input signal** — it is an intrinsic property of the system.
4. The denominator polynomial (when set to zero) gives the **characteristic equation**, whose roots are the **poles** that determine system stability and natural behaviour.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate Between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Transfer Functions

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Loop Transfer Function | Closed-Loop Transfer Function |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Ratio of output to input when no feedback exists | Ratio of output to input when unity/negative feedback exists |
| **Formula** | $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ for negative feedback |
| **Feedback Path** | Absent | Present through sensor $H(s)$ |
| **Sensitivity to Disturbances** | High — disturbances directly affect output | Low — feedback corrects for disturbances |
| **Stability** | Depends only on $G(s)$ poles | Depends on poles of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ |
| **Accuracy** | Lower — error propagates uncorrected | Higher — error is continuously measured and corrected |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When deriving a transfer function in the 14-mark question, examiners **strictly** allocate marks as follows: (i) free body diagram / circuit identification — 2 marks, (ii) correct application of governing law — 3 marks, (iii) formation of differential equation — 3 marks, (iv) Laplace transform with zero IC statement — 2 marks, (v) final transfer function expression in boxed form — 2 marks, (vi) identification of poles and order — 2 marks. **Students who skip the zero-initial-conditions statement lose 2 guaranteed marks.** Always box your final answer and label every intermediate equation.

### Question A: Derive the Transfer Function of a Series RLC Circuit and Identify Its Standard Second-Order Parameters

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom Level: Apply | 14 Marks]**

**Sub-part (a) — Derivation of Transfer Function [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a series RLC circuit with input voltage $v_{in}(t)$, loop current $i(t)$, and output voltage $v_{out}(t)$ taken across the capacitor $C$.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$v_{in}(t) = v_{R}(t) + v_{L}(t) + v_{C}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute element equations** $v_R = Ri$, $v_L = L\frac{di}{dt}$, $v_C = \frac{1}{C}\int i\, dt$:

$$v_{in}(t) = R\, i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Express current in terms of output voltage** using $i = C\frac{dv_{C}}{dt}$:

$$v_{in}(t) = LC \frac{d^{2}v_{C}}{dt^{2}} + RC \frac{dv_{C}}{dt} + v_{C}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Take Laplace transform with zero initial conditions** ($v_C(0) = 0$, $i(0) = 0$):

$$V_{in}(s) = \left[ LCs^{2} + RCs + 1 \right] V_{out}(s)$$

**Step 5 — Form the transfer function:**

$$G(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{LCs^{2} + RCs + 1}$$

---

**Sub-part (b) — Identification of Standard Parameters and System Character [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to standard second-order form** by dividing numerator and denominator by $LC$:

$$G(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{LC}}{s^{2} + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with standard form** $\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$:

- Natural frequency: $\omega_{n} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ radians/second
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ (dimensionless)
- DC gain: $K_{dc} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = 1$

**Step 3 — Identify the poles** by solving $LCs^{2} + RCs + 1 = 0$:

$$s_{1,2} = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^{2} - \frac{1}{LC}}$$

**Step 4 — System character:**

- If $\zeta > 1$ (i.e., $R > 2\sqrt{L/C}$): **Overdamped** — slow, non-oscillatory response
- If $\zeta = 1$ (i.e., $R = 2\sqrt{L/C}$): **Critically damped** — fastest non-oscillatory response
- If $0 < \zeta < 1$ (i.e., $R < 2\sqrt{L/C}$): **Underdamped** — oscillatory response with decaying sinusoid
- If $\zeta = 0$ (i.e., $R = 0$): **Undamped** — sustained oscillation

**Step 5 — Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{1}{LCs^{2} + RCs + 1} = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}\,}$$

where $\omega_{n} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ and $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$.

---

### Question B: Derive the Transfer Function of a Mass-Spring-Damper System and Explain Force-Current Analogy with an Electrical Network

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom Level: Apply | 14 Marks]**

**Sub-part (a) — Derivation of Mass-Spring-Damper Transfer Function [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a mass $M$ attached to a fixed wall through a spring $K$ and a damper $B$ in parallel. An external force $f(t)$ is applied to the mass. Let $x(t)$ denote the displacement of the mass from the equilibrium position.

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the mass:**

- Applied force: $f(t)$ (in the direction of motion)
- Inertial force: $M\dfrac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$ (opposing acceleration)
- Damping force: $B\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ (opposing velocity)
- Spring force: $Kx(t)$ (opposing displacement)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law** (sum of forces equals mass times acceleration):

$$f(t) = M \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + Kx(t)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange in standard form:**

$$M \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + Kx(t) = f(t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace transform** assuming all initial conditions are zero:

$$Ms^{2} X(s) + Bs X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)$$

**Step 5 — Form the transfer function:**

$$G(s) = \frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$$

**Step 6 — Identify system order and poles:**

This is a **second-order system** with two poles at the roots of $Ms^{2} + Bs + K = 0$:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^{2} - 4MK}}{2M}$$

---

**Sub-part (b) — Force-Current Analogy and Electrical Equivalent [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the force-current analogy** (also called the Mobility Analogy):

| Mechanical Domain | Electrical Domain |
|-------------------|-------------------|
| Force $f(t)$ | Voltage $v(t)$ |
| Velocity $v(t) = \dot{x}(t)$ | Current $i(t)$ |
| Mass $M$ | Inductance $L$ |
| Damper $B$ | Resistance $R$ |
| Spring $K$ | Inverse Capacitance $1/C$ |
| Displacement $x(t)$ | Charge $q(t)$ |

**Step 2 — Derive the analogous electrical network:**

Starting from the mechanical equation:

$$M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = f(t)$$

Using the analogy substitutions $M \to L$, $B \to R$, $K \to 1/C$, $\dot{x} \to i$, $x \to q$, $f \to v$:

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri + \frac{1}{C}\int i\, dt = v(t)$$

Differentiating both sides:

$$L\frac{d^{2}i}{dt^{2}} + R\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — This corresponds to a series RLC circuit** with input voltage $v(t)$ and output taken as the current $i(t)$ (or its integral, the charge).

**Step 4 — Compare transfer functions:**

- Mechanical: $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$
- Electrical (series RLC, output as current): $\dfrac{I(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ls + R + \frac{1}{Cs}} = \dfrac{Cs}{LCs^{2} + RCs + 1}$

**Step 5 — Engineering significance:**

> [!NOTE]
> The force-current analogy is **not merely a textbook curiosity**. It is actively used in **electromechanical analogy simulators** in control labs, where students and engineers can study the behaviour of a difficult-to-build mechanical system by building its much cheaper and easier-to-modify electrical equivalent. KTU laboratory courses often include an experiment on this analogy, and questions on it appear regularly in ESE papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before ESE**

- The **transfer function** is the Laplace-domain ratio $\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ under **zero initial conditions** — never forget to state this in your answer.
- For a **first-order system** $G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$: time constant $\tau$, DC gain $K$, pole at $s = -1/\tau$.
- For a **second-order system** $G(s) = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$: natural frequency $\omega_{n}$ and damping ratio $\zeta$ are the two most important parameters.
- The **characteristic equation** is obtained by setting the denominator of $G(s)$ equal to zero; its roots are the system poles.
- **Mason's Gain Formula** (covered later in Module 1) requires identification of forward paths, individual loops, and non-touching loop pairs — practice drawing signal flow graphs.
- The **force-current analogy** maps mass to inductor, damper to resistor, and spring to inverse capacitance.
- The **force-voltage analogy** maps mass to capacitance, damper to conductance, and spring to inverse inductance.
- A transfer function is **defined only for LTI systems**; non-linear systems require linearisation around an operating point.
- The **order of the system** = degree of the denominator polynomial = number of energy-storing elements.
- The **DC gain** $K_{dc} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$ tells you the steady-state output for a unit step input.
- **Poles on the right half of the $s$-plane** $\Rightarrow$ unstable system. **Poles on the imaginary axis** $\Rightarrow$ marginally stable. **Poles on the left half** $\Rightarrow$ stable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make on This Topic:**
> 1. Forgetting the **"zero initial conditions"** phrase when defining the transfer function — costs 2 marks.
> 2. Mixing up **force-current** and **force-voltage** analogies — cost 1-2 marks in analogy questions.
> 3. Failing to **box the final answer** — examiners may deduct 1 mark for ambiguous final expressions.
> 4. Skipping the step where $i(t)$ is rewritten as $C\frac{dv_C}{dt}$ in electrical derivations — without this, the output variable does not appear, and the derivation is incomplete.
> 5. Confusing **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ with **damped natural frequency** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — these are different and have different physical meanings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram Algebra and Signal Flow Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram Algebra and Signal Flow Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Block Diagram — Formal Definition

A **Block Diagram** is a pictorial representation of the cause-and-effect relationship between the **input** and **output** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) control system. Each block represents a system element (transfer function), and the lines connecting them represent the signals flowing between them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact):** A block diagram of a control system is a simplified pictorial representation of the functional relationship existing between the **input**, the **controlling element**, the **controlled process**, and the **output** of the system. The transfer function of each element is represented by a block, and the signal path is indicated by lines with arrows showing the unidirectional flow of information.

| Element | Symbol | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Block** | Rectangle with G(s) | Performs mathematical operation on input signal |
| **Summing Junction** | Circle with ⊕ | Adds/subtracts two or more signals |
| **Take-off Point** | Branching line | Distributes signal to multiple paths |
| **Arrow (Signal Flow)** | → | Direction of signal travel |

## 1.2 Signal Flow Graph — Formal Definition

A **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** is a graphical representation of a set of linear algebraic equations relating the variables of a system, using **nodes** (for variables) and **directed branches** (for dependencies). It was introduced by **Samuel Jefferson Mason** in 1953.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike a block diagram, a signal flow graph is a network of nodes connected by directed branches, where each node represents a system variable and each branch represents a transfer function (gain) between two nodes.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Block Diagram as an "Assembly Line":** Think of a car manufacturing plant. Raw materials (input signal) enter Station 1 (Block 1, G₁) where the chassis is welded. The output (chassis) moves to Station 2 (Block 2, G₂) where the body is painted. Each station modifies the input it receives and passes it to the next. A **quality control inspector** at the end of each station (summing junction) can either approve the work or send it back for rework (feedback loop). The Block Diagram shows the **factory floor plan**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Signal Flow Graph as a "Water Pipe Network":** Imagine a water distribution network. Each **junction (node)** is a tank where water collects. Each **pipe (branch)** with a label (gain) connects two tanks, controlling how much water flows from one tank to the next. The amount of water at any tank depends on the water flowing in from connected upstream pipes and the gain of each pipe. Mason's gain formula is like calculating the total water flow from source to destination through a complex pipe maze.

**Why Both Representations Matter:** Block diagrams are intuitive for engineers, but complex feedback systems become unwieldy. Signal flow graphs provide a **mathematically elegant alternative** that allows direct application of **Mason's Gain Formula** — a single, powerful equation that can compute the transfer function of arbitrarily complex systems without step-by-step reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram Reduction — The "Why" and "How"

Block diagram reduction is the process of **simplifying a complex interconnected block diagram** into a single equivalent block representing the overall transfer function. The **Why**: to compute the closed-loop or open-loop transfer function easily. The **How**: by applying algebraic rules to combine, move, or eliminate blocks.

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Block Diagram Algebra Rules

**Rule 1 — Cascaded (Series) Blocks:**
When blocks are connected in series without any intermediate summing junction or take-off point, the equivalent transfer function is the **product** of individual transfer functions.

$$G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot G_3(s)$$

**Rule 2 — Parallel Blocks:**
When blocks are connected in parallel (same input, outputs added at a summing junction), the equivalent transfer function is the **algebraic sum**.

$$G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s) - G_3(s)$$

**Rule 3 — Feedback Loop (Closed Loop):**
For a standard negative feedback loop with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

For **positive feedback**:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$$

**Rule 4 — Moving a Take-off Point Ahead of a Block:**
When moving a take-off point from the output of a block to the input, **divide the take-off branch by the block's transfer function**.

**Rule 5 — Moving a Take-off Point Behind a Block:**
When moving a take-off point from the input to the output of a block, **multiply the take-off branch by the block's transfer function**.

**Rule 6 — Moving a Summing Junction Ahead of a Block:**
When moving a summing junction from the output to the input, **add a block in the feedback path** with transfer function equal to the original block.

**Rule 7 — Moving a Summing Junction Behind a Block:**
When moving a summing junction from the input to the output, **add a block in the forward path** with transfer function equal to the original block.

**Rule 8 — Interchanging Adjacent Summing Junctions:**
The order of summing junctions can be interchanged without affecting the system output.

**Rule 9 — Interchanging Adjacent Take-off Points:**
The order of take-off points can be interchanged without affecting the system output.

**Rule 10 — Block on a Feedback Path:**
Moving a block from the forward path to the feedback path (or vice versa) requires **inversion** of that block's transfer function.

### 2.1.2 Block Diagram Algebra — Important Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Commutative Property:** $G_1 \cdot G_2 = G_2 \cdot G_1$ (holds for series blocks)
> - **Associative Property:** $G_1 \cdot (G_2 \cdot G_3) = (G_1 \cdot G_2) \cdot G_3$
> - **Distributive Property over Summing Junctions:** $G_1 \cdot (G_2 \pm G_3) = G_1 G_2 \pm G_1 G_3$

## 2.2 Signal Flow Graph Terminology — The Building Blocks

Before applying Mason's formula, the **exact definitions** of SFG terms must be crystal clear — these are favorite KTU 2-mark questions.

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Node** | A point representing a system variable (signal) | Sum of all incoming branch gains |
| **Branch** | A directed line segment connecting two nodes with an associated gain (transmittance) | Represents dependency $x_j = a_{ij} x_i$ |
| **Input Node (Source)** | A node with only outgoing branches | Independent variable |
| **Output Node (Sink)** | A node with only incoming branches | Dependent variable |
| **Path** | A continuous succession of branches traversed in the same direction | Any route from one node to another |
| **Forward Path** | A path from input node to output node that does not cross any node more than once | Used in Mason's numerator |
| **Loop** | A closed path that starts and ends at the same node without crossing any other node more than once | Used in Mason's denominator |
| **Non-touching Loops** | Loops that do not share any common node | Critical for $\Delta$ calculation |
| **Loop Gain** | Product of branch gains around a loop | Computed with sign for negative feedback |
| **Path Gain** | Product of branch gains along a forward path | Numerator term |

## 2.3 Mason's Gain Formula — The Master Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mason's Gain Formula (1953) — The KTU Most-Important Equation:**

$$M(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

Where:
- $M(s)$ = Overall transfer function between input $R(s)$ and output $C(s)$
- $P_k$ = Path gain of the $k^{th}$ forward path from input to output
- $N$ = Total number of forward paths
- $\Delta$ = Determinant of the graph $= 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$
- $\Delta_k$ = Cofactor of the $k^{th}$ forward path (determinant of the graph after removing all loops touching the $k^{th}$ forward path)
- $L_i$ = Loop gain of the $i^{th}$ individual loop
- $L_i L_j$ = Product of gains of two **non-touching** loops

### 2.3.1 The Logic Behind the Plus/Minus Signs in $\Delta$

- **Individual loops:** subtracted ($- \sum L_i$) because feedback is generally negative
- **Two non-touching loops:** added ($+ \sum L_i L_j$) — they don't interfere, so their combined damping is multiplicative
- **Three non-touching loops:** subtracted ($- \sum L_i L_j L_k$)
- The pattern **alternates** with the number of non-touching loop combinations

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Aerospace:** Aircraft autopilot systems with multiple feedback loops (pitch, roll, yaw) — Mason's formula computes the overall stability transfer function.
> - **Robotics:** Multi-joint robotic arm controllers use block diagrams to design servo loops for each joint.
> - **Power Systems:** Automatic Voltage Regulators (AVR) with cascaded excitation and feedback loops.
> - **Process Control:** Chemical plant temperature/pressure control with multiple sensors and actuators — SFG analysis determines interaction effects.
> - **Active Suspension in Automobiles:** SFG models the interplay between wheel dynamics, body dynamics, and road inputs.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Condition / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Series Blocks | $G_{eq} = G_1 G_2 G_3$ | No summing junction in between |
| 2 | Parallel Blocks | $G_{eq} = G_1 + G_2 - G_3$ | Same input, outputs summed |
| 3 | Negative Feedback | $T = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$ | Standard unity form: $\dfrac{G}{1+G}$ when $H=1$ |
| 4 | Positive Feedback | $T = \dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$ | Rare, causes instability |
| 5 | Mason's Gain Formula | $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$ | Always check non-touching loops |
| 6 | Graph Determinant | $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$ | Alternating sign rule |
| 7 | Unity Negative Feedback | $T = \dfrac{G}{1+G}$ | Special case: $H(s) = 1$ |
| 8 | Block on Take-off (Move ahead) | $G_{new} = \dfrac{G_{old}}{G_{block}}$ | Divide by block gain |
| 9 | Block on Take-off (Move behind) | $G_{new} = G_{old} \cdot G_{block}$ | Multiply by block gain |
| 10 | Loop Gain Definition | $L_i = \prod (\text{branch gains around loop } i)$ | Sign: $+$ for positive, $-$ for negative feedback |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function with Negative Feedback

Consider a standard feedback system. Let:
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $E(s)$ = Error signal
- $C(s)$ = Output
- $G(s)$ = Forward path transfer function
- $H(s)$ = Feedback path transfer function
- $B(s)$ = Feedback signal

**Step 1:** Write the input-output relation at the summing junction.

The error is the difference between reference and feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2:** Write the forward path relation.

The output is the forward transfer function times the error:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Write the feedback path relation.

The feedback signal is the feedback transfer function times the output:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute equation from Step 3 into Step 1.

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this into Step 2.

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s) \right]$$

**Step 6:** Expand the right-hand side.

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 7:** Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left side.

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 8:** Factor out $C(s)$ on the left.

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s) \cdot H(s) \right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 9:** Isolate the transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$.

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function** with negative feedback. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **For positive feedback**, the summing junction produces $E(s) = R(s) + B(s)$, leading to the minus sign in the denominator:
> $$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mason's Gain Formula (Theoretical Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Depth:** Students are expected to know the *application* of Mason's formula and the *logic* of non-touching loops. Full topological derivation is beyond undergraduate scope, but the **pattern recognition** of why the formula works is examinable.

### 3.2.1 Setting Up the Graph Equations

For an SFG with $n$ nodes, the value at each node equals the sum of all incoming branch gains times their source node values.

Let $x_i$ be the value at node $i$. Then:

$$x_j = \sum_{i} a_{ij} \, x_i \quad \text{for all incoming branches to } j$$

In matrix form, define the connection matrix $A$ where $a_{ij}$ is the gain from node $i$ to node $j$:

$$X = A \cdot X + U$$

Where $U$ is the input vector. Rearranging:

$$(I - A) \cdot X = U \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = (I - A)^{-1} \cdot U$$

The transfer function is the **cofactor** of the $(I - A)$ matrix:

$$M = \frac{C_{ji}}{\det(I - A)}$$

Where $C_{ji}$ is the cofactor of the $(j, i)$ element (output node, input node).

### 3.2.2 Expansion of the Determinant

When $\det(I - A)$ is expanded along the diagonal, each term corresponds to a **closed loop** in the graph. The expansion yields:

$$\det(I - A) = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

- The **"1"** represents the trivial (empty) path — no signal modification.
- $\sum L_i$ = sum of gains of all **individual** loops (negative sign because each loop creates a feedback path).
- $\sum L_i L_j$ = sum of products of gains of all **pairs of non-touching** loops (positive sign — they multiply their independent effects).
- $\sum L_i L_j L_k$ = sum of products of gains of all **triples of non-touching** loops (negative sign).
- The pattern continues alternatingly.

### 3.2.3 Expansion of the Cofactor

The cofactor $\Delta_k$ for the $k^{th}$ forward path is computed as:

$$\Delta_k = 1 - \sum L_i^{(k)} + \sum L_i L_j^{(k)} - \ldots$$

Where the superscript $(k)$ denotes **only those loops that do not touch the $k^{th}$ forward path**.

### 3.2.4 The Final Mason's Equation

$$M(s) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k}{\Delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most powerful single equation** in classical control. It can find the transfer function of an arbitrarily complex SFG in **one shot**, without any block-by-block reduction.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Mason's Gain Formula Application

### Example Problem:
A system is represented by the following signal flow graph. Compute the transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$.

**Graph Structure:**
- Input $R(s)$ connects to nodes $X_1$, $X_2$, $X_3$
- Node $X_1$ has self-loop with gain $-a$
- Branch $X_1 \to X_2$ with gain $b$
- Branch $X_2 \to X_3$ with gain $c$
- Branch $X_3 \to X_2$ with gain $-d$ (feedback)
- Branch $X_3 \to C(s)$ with gain $e$
- Branch $X_1 \to C(s)$ with gain $f$

**Step 1: Identify all forward paths from $R(s)$ to $C(s)$.**

- **Path 1:** $R \to X_1 \to C$ with gain $P_1 = f$
- **Path 2:** $R \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$ with gain $P_2 = c \cdot e$
- **Path 3:** $R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$ with gain $P_3 = b \cdot c \cdot e$
- **Path 4:** $R \to X_3 \to C$ with gain $P_4 = e$

Total forward paths: $N = 4$.

**Step 2: Identify all individual loops.**

- **Loop 1 ($L_1$):** $X_1 \to X_1$ self-loop, gain $L_1 = -a$
- **Loop 2 ($L_2$):** $X_2 \to X_3 \to X_2$, gain $L_2 = c \cdot (-d) = -cd$

**Step 3: Check for non-touching loops.**

- Loop 1 (at $X_1$) and Loop 2 (at $X_2, X_3$): do they share nodes? **No, they share no common node.**
- Therefore, $L_1 L_2 = (-a)(-cd) = acd$ is a non-touching loop pair.

**Step 4: Compute the graph determinant $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + (L_1 L_2)$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta = 1 - (-a - cd) + (-a)(-cd)$$

$$\Delta = 1 + a + cd + acd$$

**Step 5: Compute cofactor $\Delta_1$ for Path 1 ($R \to X_1 \to C$).**

Path 1 traverses node $X_1$. We must remove all loops that touch $X_1$. This eliminates **Loop 1**.

- Remaining individual loops: $L_2 = -cd$
- Non-touching loop pairs: None (only $L_2$ remains)

$$\Delta_1 = 1 - L_2 = 1 - (-cd) = 1 + cd$$

**Step 6: Compute cofactor $\Delta_2$ for Path 2 ($R \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$).**

Path 2 traverses nodes $X_2$ and $X_3$. This eliminates **Loop 2**. Loop 1 is at $X_1$ and does not touch $X_2$ or $X_3$, so it remains.

- Remaining individual loops: $L_1 = -a$
- Non-touching loop pairs: None (only one loop remains)

$$\Delta_2 = 1 - L_1 = 1 - (-a) = 1 + a$$

**Step 7: Compute cofactor $\Delta_3$ for Path 3 ($R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$).**

Path 3 traverses $X_1, X_2, X_3$ — it touches **all loops**. Remove both.

$$\Delta_3 = 1 - 0 + 0 = 1$$

**Step 8: Compute cofactor $\Delta_4$ for Path 4 ($R \to X_3 \to C$).**

Path 4 traverses $X_3$ only. This eliminates **Loop 2**. Loop 1 at $X_1$ does not touch $X_3$, so it remains.

$$\Delta_4 = 1 - L_1 = 1 + a$$

**Step 9: Apply Mason's Gain Formula.**

$$T(s) = \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2 + P_3 \Delta_3 + P_4 \Delta_4}{\Delta}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{f(1+cd) + ce(1+a) + bce(1) + e(1+a)}{1 + a + cd + acd}$$

**Step 10: Final simplified form.**

$$T(s) = \frac{f + fcd + ce + ace + bce + e + ae}{1 + a + cd + acd}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{(f + e) + a(ce + e) + cd(f) + acd(b)}{1 + a + cd + acd}$$

This is the complete transfer function. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Block Diagram Problems vs. Mason's Approach

| System Type | Block Diagram Approach | Mason's Approach | Recommended Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simple single-loop** | Apply closed-loop formula directly | 1 forward path, 1 loop | Block Diagram (faster) |
| **Cascaded non-interacting** | Multiply all blocks | 1 forward path, no loops | Block Diagram (faster) |
| **Multi-loop with crossing** | Move take-off points, may get messy | Direct application | **Mason's Formula (faster)** |
| **Parallel paths to output** | Add parallel branches, then reduce | Multiple forward paths | **Mason's Formula (faster)** |
| **Non-touching inner loops** | May need to invert blocks | Direct application | **Mason's Formula (faster)** |
| **System with 4+ feedback loops** | Very complex reduction sequence | Single formula application | **Mason's Formula (essential)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Standard Components (Mermaid Schematic)

The following block-level functional architecture flow diagram represents the **canonical closed-loop control system** with all its components and signal flow.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["R s Reference Input"] --> nodeSum{"Summing Junction"}
    nodeFB["Feedback Signal B s"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum -- "Error E s" --> nodeG1["G1 s Controller"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2["G2 s Plant"]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3["G3 s Actuator"]
    nodeG3 -- "Output C s" --> nodeOut["C s Plant Output"]
    nodeOut --> nodeH["H s Sensor"]
    nodeH --> nodeFB
    nodeTake["Take off Point from C s"] --> nodeDisturb["Disturbance Path"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The reference input $R(s)$ enters the summing junction. The error $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ is computed. The forward path $G_1 G_2 G_3$ processes the error to produce the output $C(s)$. The output is fed back through $H(s)$ to close the loop. A take-off point allows a disturbance signal to be injected.

---

## 4.2 Signal Flow Graph — Standard Form of a Closed-Loop System

The equivalent signal flow graph for a basic unity-feedback closed-loop system is shown below. Each node is a variable, each branch is a transfer function (gain).

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["R s Input Node"] -- "1" --> nodeE["E s Error Node"]
    nodeE -- "G s" --> nodeC["C s Output Node"]
    nodeC -- "1" --> nodeOut["Sink Node"]
    nodeC -- "H s" --> nodeE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In this SFG, the forward path gain is $P_1 = G(s)$, the single loop gain is $L_1 = -G(s)H(s)$ (note the negative sign for negative feedback). Applying Mason's:
> $$T(s) = \frac{P_1}{1 - L_1} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - [-G(s)H(s)]} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram Reduction — Strategy Flowchart

The following flowchart describes the **decision logic** for reducing a complex block diagram to its equivalent single transfer function.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start Complex Block Diagram"] --> checkA{"Are there cascaded blocks with no junctions in between?"}
    checkA -- "Yes" --> stepA1["Combine into single product block"]
    checkA -- "No" --> checkB{"Are there parallel paths to same summing junction?"}
    stepA1 --> checkB
    checkB -- "Yes" --> stepB1["Sum parallel blocks algebraically"]
    checkB -- "No" --> checkC{"Is there a clear feedback loop G with feedback H?"}
    stepB1 --> checkC
    checkC -- "Yes" --> stepC1["Apply Closed Loop formula G by 1 plus GH"]
    checkC -- "No" --> checkD{"Is the take off point inside a feedback loop?"}
    stepC1 --> checkD
    checkD -- "Yes" --> stepD1["Move take off or summing point using rules 4 to 7"]
    checkD -- "No" --> masonA["Apply Masons Gain Formula to SFG equivalent"]
    stepD1 --> checkC
    masonA --> endA["Final Single Transfer Function T s"]
```

---

## 4.4 Multi-Loop Block Diagram — Architectural View

The following mermaid diagram represents a **canonical multi-loop system** that requires Mason's gain formula for efficient analysis.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["R s"] -- "1" --> nodeSum1{"Sum 1"}
    nodeN1["N1 Inner Feedback"] -- "1" --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 -- "Error" --> nodeG1["G1 s Forward Inner"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeSum2{"Sum 2"}
    nodeG2["G2 s Forward Outer"] --> nodeSum2
    nodeDist["D s Disturbance"] -- "1" --> nodeSum2
    nodeSum2 --> nodeG3["G3 s Forward Outer 2"]
    nodeG3 --> nodeC["C s Output"]
    nodeC -- "H2 s" --> nodeN1
    nodeC -- "H1 s" --> nodeG2
    nodeC -- "1" --> nodeOut["Output Sink"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **This system has 2 feedback loops and 3 forward paths.** Block-by-block reduction would require careful manipulation of take-off points. Converting to SFG and applying Mason's formula is the **recommended KTU approach** for this complexity.

---

## 4.5 Conversion from Block Diagram to Signal Flow Graph — Mapping Rules

| Block Diagram Element | Signal Flow Graph Equivalent | Gain Transfer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Block with G(s) | Branch with transmittance G(s) | Identical |
| Summing Junction with + | Branch into a node (converging branches) | Sign preserved on branch |
| Summing Junction with − | Branch into a node with negative gain | Sign on branch |
| Take-off Point | Node with multiple outgoing branches | Signal value equals node value |
| Input signal | Source node (no incoming) | Independent variable |
| Output signal | Sink node (no outgoing) | Dependent variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip:** The most common mistake students make is to **invert the sign** when converting a negative summing junction. A negative summing junction simply means the incoming branch has a **negative gain** (e.g., $-H(s)$ instead of $+H(s)$). All other rules remain identical.

---
