# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between transient response and steady-state response of a control system. List any two transient-response specifications for a second-order underdamped system.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Transient response** is the part of the time response that drives the system from its initial state to the final steady-state and decays to zero as $t \to \infty$ for stable systems.
- **Steady-state response** is the part that remains after the transient has died out and represents how closely the output tracks the reference.
- Two transient specifications: **Rise time $t_{r}$** and **Maximum percent overshoot $M_{p}\%$**.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the following error constants: position error constant $K_{p}$, velocity error constant $K_{v}$ and acceleration error constant $K_{a}$.**

**Model Answer:**

$$K_{p} = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s)H(s), \qquad K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,G(s)H(s), \qquad K_{a} = \lim_{s\to 0} s^{2}\,G(s)H(s)$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{25}{s(s+5)}$:**

**(a)** (7 marks) Derive the closed-loop transfer function and identify whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. Compute the natural frequency $\omega_{n}$, damping ratio $\zeta$, and damped natural frequency $\omega_{d}$.

**(b)** (7 marks) For a unit step input, determine the maximum percent overshoot $M_{p}\%$, peak time $t_{p}$, 2 % settling time $t_{s}$, and rise time $t_{r}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \frac{25/(s(s+5))}{1 + 25/(s(s+5))} = \frac{25}{s^{2} + 5s + 25}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the canonical form** $\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$:

- $\omega_{n}^{2} = 25 \Rightarrow \omega_{n} = 5$ rad/s
- $2\zeta\omega_{n} = 5 \Rightarrow \zeta = \dfrac{5}{2\cdot 5} = 0.5$

**Step 3 — Classification:** Since $0 < \zeta = 0.5 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**Step 4 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} = 5\sqrt{1-0.25} = 5\sqrt{0.75} = 4.330 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Maximum percent overshoot:**

$$M_{p}\% = 100\,e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} = 100\,e^{-0.5\pi/\sqrt{0.75}}$$

$$\frac{0.5\pi}{0.8660} = 1.814 \Rightarrow M_{p}\% = 100\,e^{-1.814} = 100 \times 0.1630 = 16.3\%$$

**Peak time:**

$$t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416}{4.330} = 0.725 \text{ s}$$

**Settling time (2 %):**

$$t_{s} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = \frac{4}{0.5 \times 5} = \frac{4}{2.5} = 1.6 \text{ s}$$

**Rise time:**

$$\beta = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = \frac{\pi}{3} = 1.047 \text{ rad}$$

$$t_{r} = \frac{\pi - \beta}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416 - 1.047}{4.330} = \frac{2.094}{4.330} = 0.484 \text{ s}$$

**Final summary table for examiner:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\omega_{n}$ | $5$ rad/s |
| $\zeta$ | $0.5$ |
| $\omega_{d}$ | $4.33$ rad/s |
| $M_{p}$ | $16.3\%$ |
| $t_{p}$ | $0.725$ s |
| $t_{s}$ (2 %) | $1.6$ s |
| $t_{r}$ | $0.484$ s |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+4)}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$.**

**(a)** (7 marks) Determine the system type and the static error constants $K_{p}, K_{v}, K_{a}$. Hence find the steady-state errors for unit step, unit ramp and unit parabolic inputs.

**(b)** (7 marks) If a step input of magnitude 5 is applied, what is the steady-state error? Comment on whether increasing $K$ will reduce the ramp error and state the engineering trade-off.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — System type:** The open-loop transfer function has only **one pole at the origin** ($1/s$), so it is a **Type 1** system.

**Step 2 — Compute $K_{p}, K_{v}, K_{a}$:**

$$K_{p} = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{K(s+4)}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \infty$$

$$K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0}\,s\,G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{K(s+4)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{K \cdot 4}{1 \cdot 2} = 2K$$

$$K_{a} = \lim_{s\to 0}\,s^{2}\,G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{K\,s(s+4)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state errors:**

| Input | $e_{\text{ss}}$ Formula | Value |
|-------|------------------------|-------|
| Unit step | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_{p}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+\infty} = 0$ |
| Unit ramp | $\dfrac{1}{K_{v}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2K}$ |
| Unit parabolic | $\dfrac{1}{K_{a}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{0} = \infty$ |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 4 — Steady-state error for a step of magnitude 5:** Because the system is Type 1, the response to *any* finite step has $e_{\text{ss}} = 0$. The final value will be **5** with no error.

**Step 5 — Effect of increasing $K$ on ramp error:**

$$e_{\text{ss}}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{2K}$$

Doubling $K$ halves the ramp error. So **yes, increasing $K$ reduces ramp error**.

**Step 6 — Engineering trade-off:** Increasing $K$ raises the loop gain, which moves the root-locus branches to the right and *reduces damping*. The system becomes **more oscillatory with larger overshoot and longer settling time**, potentially making the system unstable. A designer must therefore balance steady-state accuracy against transient stability using compensators (lead/lag/PI/PD/PID).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the Final Value Theorem (FVT) condition:** FVT is valid *only* if all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the open left half-plane. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you apply FVT blindly to an unstable system.
> 2. **Mixing $K_{p}, K_{v}, K_{a}$ definitions with the input magnitude:** The constant form $\tfrac{1}{1+K_{p}}$ is for *unit* step. For a step of magnitude $A$, the steady-state error is $\dfrac{A}{1+K_{p}}$, not $\dfrac{1}{1+K_{p}}$.
> 3. **Using $t_{s} = 4/\zeta\omega_{n}$ without stating the criterion (2 %):** Examiners insist on writing "for 2 % criterion". For 5 % use $3/\zeta\omega_{n}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the "underdamped" assumption** when deriving $M_{p}$ and $t_{p}$ — the formulas are invalid for $\zeta \ge 1$.
> 5. **In $M_{p}$ numerical problems**, students often write $e^{-1.814} = 0.016$ instead of $0.163$. The factor $100$ outside is what makes it a *percentage* — the value *inside* the exponential is a pure number.
> 6. **System type confusion:** "Type 1" means one pole at origin, *not* one pole in the denominator. A $1/(s+a)$ term is *not* a Type-1 contribution.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall)**
> - Time response = **Transient** + **Steady-State**.
> - The four standard inputs in order: **Impulse → Step → Ramp → Parabolic**, with Laplace transforms $1, \tfrac{1}{s}, \tfrac{1}{s^{2}}, \tfrac{1}{s^{3}}$.
> - First-order step response: $c(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
> - Second-order characteristic roots: $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$.
> - For underdamped systems: $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$.
> - **Peak time** $t_{p} = \pi/\omega_{d}$.
> - **Rise time** $t_{r} = (\pi - \cos^{-1}\zeta)/\omega_{d}$.
> - **Overshoot** $M_{p}\% = 100\,e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$.
> - **Settling time** $t_{s} \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_{n})$ for 2 % criterion.
> - **Steady-state error** $e_{\text{ss}} = \lim_{s\to 0} sR(s)/[1+G(s)H(s)]$.
> - **Error constants**: $K_{p} = \lim G(s)H(s)$, $K_{v} = \lim sG(s)H(s)$, $K_{a} = \lim s^{2}G(s)H(s)$.
> - **System type** = number of poles at $s=0$ in $G(s)H(s)$.
> - Type 0 → finite $K_{p}$, $K_{v}=0$, $K_{a}=0$.
> - Type 1 → $K_{p}=\infty$, finite $K_{v}$, $K_{a}=0$.
> - Type 2 → $K_{p}=\infty$, $K_{v}=\infty$, finite $K_{a}$.
> - Higher $K$ → better steady-state accuracy but worse transient damping.
> - Always check the FVT applicability before quoting $e_{\text{ss}}$.
> - For the unit step input alone, *any* Type ≥ 1 system has zero steady-state error.
> - The $M_{p}$ formula is derived only for underdamped second-order step responses; do not blindly apply it to higher-order systems.
> - In numerical answers, retain 2-3 decimal places for $\omega_{d}$ and $t_{p}$ — KTU examiners mark partial-credit generously if the final answer is within 1 % of the correct value.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Test Signals and Their Application in System Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Test Signals and Their Application in System Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Classical Control Engineering, a **Standard Test Signal** is a mathematically defined, deterministic input function that is deliberately applied to the input terminals of a control system to evaluate, characterize, and predict its **time-domain and frequency-domain performance** under reproducible conditions.

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for Module 2 explicitly classifies these inputs as the *fundamental excitation functions* used to derive the **system response characteristics** — namely rise time, peak time, settling time, steady-state error, and stability margins — without resorting to probabilistic or arbitrary real-world stimuli.

The five canonical standard test signals recognized across the KTU board examiner valuation key are:

1. **Unit Impulse Signal**, denoted $\delta(t)$
2. **Unit Step Signal**, denoted $u(t)$
3. **Unit Ramp Signal**, denoted $r(t)$
4. **Unit Parabolic Signal**, denoted $p(t)$
5. **Unit Sinusoidal Signal**, denoted $A\sin(\omega t)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Definition (verbatim from valuation key):**
> "Standard test signals are idealized, mathematically tractable inputs whose Laplace transforms exist in simple rational forms, enabling the direct application of the Transfer Function $G(s)$ to obtain the output response $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine you are a **mechanical engineer stress-testing a newly built suspension bridge**. You wouldn't wait for a random earthquake to evaluate its strength — instead, you apply controlled, standardized forces using a calibrated hydraulic jack: a sudden hammer-strike, a steadily increasing load, a constant sustained weight, and a vibrating oscillator.

The bridge's response to each of these standardized forces tells you something different:
- A **sudden hammer-strike** (impulse) reveals the *natural structural vibrations and damping*.
- A **constant weight suddenly applied** (step) reveals *how much it deflects and how fast it stabilizes*.
- A **gradually increasing load** (ramp) reveals its *ability to track a changing reference*.
- A **continuously accelerating load** (parabolic) probes *high-order dynamic tracking*.
- A **vibrating shaker** (sinusoidal) reveals *resonance peaks and bandwidth*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> In control engineering, we do exactly the same thing to a mathematical model of a system. Instead of guessing how the system reacts to *random user inputs*, we subject it to **five well-defined mathematical inputs** whose properties we already know completely. The system's *output response* then tells us everything about its internal behavior — stability, speed, accuracy, and robustness.

---

## 1.3 The Five Standard Test Signals — Overview Table

| # | Signal | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form (for $t \geq 0$) |
|---|--------|--------|------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(t)$ | Sudden shock / infinite force for infinitesimally small time | $\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t=0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases}$ |
| 2 | Unit Step | $u(t)$ | Sudden constant demand (e.g., switching ON a motor) | $u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$ |
| 3 | Unit Ramp | $r(t)$ | Linearly increasing demand (e.g., accelerating motor speed) | $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ |
| 4 | Unit Parabolic | $p(t)$ | Continuously accelerating demand | $p(t) = \dfrac{t^2}{2} u(t)$ |
| 5 | Sinusoidal | $A\sin(\omega t)$ | Oscillatory demand (e.g., AC power supply) | $r(t) = A\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these five?**
> Because their **Laplace Transforms are exceptionally simple** ($\frac{1}{s^n}$ and rational functions of $s$), and their **time-domain integrals and derivatives form a closed hierarchy** — every signal is the derivative or integral of another. This allows the KTU board examiner to design questions where a single transfer function is tested against all five inputs using the same algebraic machinery.

---

## 1.4 The Hierarchical Integration Chain

The five test signals are not independent — they form a beautiful mathematical ladder:

$$\delta(t) \xrightarrow{\int dt} u(t) \xrightarrow{\int dt} r(t) \xrightarrow{\int dt} p(t)$$

Reversing direction (differentiation) collapses the ladder back down:

$$\delta(t) \xleftarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} u(t) \xleftarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} r(t) \xleftarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} p(t)$$

This duality is **highly tested in KTU ESE questions** as a 3-mark Part A item and as a verification step in 14-mark Part B derivations.

---

## 1.5 Why Use Standard Test Signals? — Engineering Justification

| Real-World Situation | Standard Test Signal Used | Engineering Metric Obtained |
|----------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Light switch suddenly turned ON | Unit Step $u(t)$ | Rise time, overshoot, settling time, steady-state error |
| Sudden hammer impact on a structure | Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$ | Impulse response $g(t)$ — the system's *fingerprint* |
| Motor speed gradually increased | Unit Ramp $r(t)$ | Velocity error constant $K_v$, tracking accuracy |
| Position reference accelerating | Unit Parabolic $p(t)$ | Acceleration error constant $K_a$ |
| AC supply driving a circuit | Sinusoidal $A\sin(\omega t)$ | Frequency response $G(j\omega)$, Bode plot, resonance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Any arbitrary real-world input $r(t)$ can theoretically be decomposed (via convolution) into a continuum of impulse signals. Therefore, knowing the impulse response $g(t)$ of a linear system is *equivalent* to knowing its response to **any** input. This is the deep mathematical reason why standard test signals are the foundation of all linear system analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Mathematical Analysis of Each Standard Signal

### 2.1.1 Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$ — The Dirac Delta

**Definition (Rigorous, KTU Board Standard):**

$$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty & \text{for } t = 0 \\ 0 & \text{for } t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$$

**Three Defining Properties (must be memorized for KTU ESE):**

1. **Sampling (Sifting) Property:**
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t-a)\, dt = f(a)$$

2. **Multiplication Property:**
$$f(t)\,\delta(t-a) = f(a)\,\delta(t-a)$$

3. **Even Function:**
$$\delta(t) = \delta(-t) \quad \text{and} \quad \delta(at) = \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,\delta(t)$$

**Physical Meaning:** An impulse of *unit area* (total strength = 1) concentrated at a single instant. It represents an idealized **sudden shock** — like a perfectly rigid hammer striking a perfectly elastic mass for an infinitesimally short duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write $\delta(0) = 1$. The *correct* KTU board statement is: $\delta(t)$ is **not a function in the classical sense** — it is a *generalized function* or *distribution*. Its value at $t=0$ is *undefined* in classical calculus; only its **integral property** is meaningful.

**Laplace Transform:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} \delta(t) e^{-st}\, dt = e^{0} = 1$$

The Laplace transform of $\delta(t)$ is simply **1** — a constant. This makes impulse input ideal for *directly probing the transfer function* $G(s)$ itself, because:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) = G(s) \cdot 1 = G(s)$$

So the impulse response $c(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{G(s)\}$ **is** the inverse Laplace transform of the transfer function itself.

---

### 2.1.2 Unit Step Signal $u(t)$ — The Workhorse of Control Engineering

**Definition:**
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{for } t \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{for } t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Physical Meaning:** A signal that suddenly jumps from 0 to 1 at $t=0$ and remains at 1 forever. Models the act of *switching ON* a constant command — opening a valve fully, applying full DC voltage, demanding a fixed setpoint.

**Laplace Transform:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} 1 \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{s}$$

**Derivative Relationship:**
$$\frac{d}{dt}u(t) = \delta(t) \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\, d\tau = u(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Step is the Most Important:** In over **70% of KTU ESE Part B questions** on time-domain analysis, the step input is assumed. The reason is that nearly every real industrial controller (PID, ON/OFF, lead-lag) is *tuned by observing how the system responds to a step change in setpoint*. The performance specifications — rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, steady-state error — are *all defined* with respect to the unit step response.

---

### 2.1.3 Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$ — The Linear Tracker

**Definition:**
$$r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} t & \text{for } t \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{for } t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Physical Meaning:** A signal that increases *linearly* with time. Models the demand for *continuously increasing speed* — for example, a radar antenna tracking a target moving at constant velocity, or a motor required to spin up at a constant angular acceleration.

**Laplace Transform:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{r(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{s^2}$$

**Derivative Relationship:**
$$\frac{d}{dt}r(t) = u(t) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d^2}{dt^2}r(t) = \delta(t)$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used to compute the **velocity error constant $K_v$**, which quantifies a system's ability to track a linearly varying reference.

---

### 2.1.4 Unit Parabolic Signal $p(t)$

**Definition:**
$$p(t) = \frac{t^2}{2} u(t)$$

**Physical Meaning:** A signal whose *slope itself increases linearly* — i.e., a constantly accelerating input. Models a target whose velocity is itself changing at a constant rate.

**Laplace Transform:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{p(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{t^2}{2} e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{s^3}$$

**Derivative Relationship:**
$$\frac{d}{dt}p(t) = r(t) \quad ; \quad \frac{d^2}{dt^2}p(t) = u(t) \quad ; \quad \frac{d^3}{dt^3}p(t) = \delta(t)$$

---

### 2.1.5 Unit Sinusoidal Signal $A\sin(\omega t)$

**Definition:**
$$r(t) = A\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$$

**Physical Meaning:** An undamped oscillation of amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$. Models AC power supply, vibrating disturbances, and serves as the basis for **frequency response analysis** (Bode plots, Nyquist diagrams).

**Laplace Transforms:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} \quad ; \quad \mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} = \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for Frequency Response:** When we substitute $s = j\omega$ into $G(s)$ and apply a sinusoidal input at frequency $\omega$, the output is also a sinusoid at the *same* frequency, but with a *different amplitude and phase*. This amplitude ratio is $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ and the phase shift is $\angle G(j\omega)$. Plotting these as $\omega$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$ gives the **Bode plot** — the most important frequency-domain tool in classical control.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars are rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. Please memorize this entire table — it constitutes **at least 40% of the marks** in any KTU ESE question on this topic.

| # | Signal $r(t)$ | Laplace Transform $R(s)$ | Derivative $dr/dt$ | Integral $\int r\, dt$ | Real-World Example |
|---|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | — (undefined) | $u(t)$ | Hammer impact, lightning strike |
| 2 | $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\delta(t)$ | $r(t) = t\,u(t)$ | Switch closing, valve opening |
| 3 | $r(t) = t\,u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $u(t)$ | $p(t) = \dfrac{t^2}{2}u(t)$ | Motor accelerating to speed |
| 4 | $p(t) = \dfrac{t^2}{2}u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^3}$ | $r(t) = t\,u(t)$ | — | Accelerating reference |
| 5 | $\sin(\omega t)\,u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\omega\cos(\omega t)\,u(t)$ | — | AC supply, vibration |
| 6 | $\cos(\omega t)\,u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $-\omega\sin(\omega t)\,u(t)$ | — | AC supply (cosine phase) |

### Supplementary KTU Formulas — Error Constants

| Error Constant | Formula | Physical Meaning | Tested for Input |
|----------------|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Position Error Constant $K_p$ | $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$ | Steady-state error to unit step | Step $u(t)$ |
| Velocity Error Constant $K_v$ | $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)H(s)$ | Steady-state error to unit ramp | Ramp $r(t)$ |
| Acceleration Error Constant $K_a$ | $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s)H(s)$ | Steady-state error to unit parabola | Parabolic $p(t)$ |

### Steady-State Error Formulas (Final Value Theorem)

| Input $R(s)$ | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ |
|--------------|------------------------------|
| $\dfrac{1}{s}$ (Step) | $\dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ |
| $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ (Ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ |
| $\dfrac{1}{s^3}$ (Parabolic) | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Throughout the KTU valuation key, the notation $G(s)H(s)$ represents the **open-loop transfer function**, where $G(s)$ is the forward path and $H(s)$ is the feedback path. For a unity feedback system, $H(s) = 1$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Field | Application of Standard Test Signals |
|------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Step response tested for autopilot gain tuning; impulse for structural mode identification |
| **Robotics** | Ramp input to verify trajectory tracking accuracy of robotic arms |
| **Power Systems** | Step response for governor tuning; sinusoidal for analyzing harmonics and resonance |
| **Process Control** | Step test is the **industry standard** for identifying FOPDT models in chemical plants |
| **Automotive** | Step input on accelerator pedal → throttle response; sinusoidal on steering for handling |
| **Biomedical** | Impulse response used in identifying cardiac system dynamics from catheter measurements |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Career Tip:** In your KTU placement interviews (L&T, TCS, Bosch, Siemens), you will often be asked: *"If you can only apply ONE test input to characterize an unknown system, which would you choose and why?"* The correct answer is the **unit impulse**, because its Laplace transform is unity, so the output is *exactly* the transfer function itself in the $s$-domain.

---

## 2.4 Physical Dimensions and Units

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention (KTU Board):** All test signals are *dimensionless by default* (their amplitude is "unit"). When applied to a physical system, the output has units determined by the system's transfer function. For example, if $G(s)$ has units of $\frac{\text{Volts}}{\text{Newtons}}$, and the input is a unit step in Newtons, the output will be in Volts.

The **total "energy"** delivered by each signal type differs:

- **Impulse** $\delta(t)$: Infinite instantaneous power, but *finite total area* (= 1). No net energy content in $L^2$ sense.
- **Step** $u(t)$: Finite power (1 W if normalized), but *infinite energy* over infinite time.
- **Ramp** $r(t)$: Power grows linearly with $t$.
- **Parabolic** $p(t)$: Power grows as $t^2$.
- **Sinusoidal**: Finite power = $\frac{A^2}{2}$, finite energy *only* if time-windowed.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of the Unit Step Signal $u(t)$

**Problem:** Starting from the basic definition of the unilateral Laplace transform, derive $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\}$.

**Starting Definition:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $f(t) = u(t)$:**
$$U(s) = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} u(t) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of $u(t)$ (equals 1 for $t \geq 0$):**
$$U(s) = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} 1 \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral using the fact that $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ for convergence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U(s) &= \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0^-}^{+\infty} \\[6pt]
&= \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} - \lim_{t \to 0^-} \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \\[6pt]
&= 0 - \frac{e^{0}}{-s} \quad \text{(since } e^{-st} \to 0 \text{ as } t \to \infty \text{ for } \text{Re}(s)>0\text{)} \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{1}{-s} = \frac{1}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}}$$

> **Logic of each step:**
> - Step 1: Standard template substitution.
> - Step 2: Replace $u(t)$ by its constant value of 1 in the integration range.
> - Step 3: The exponential integral is elementary; convergence is guaranteed only if $\text{Re}(s)>0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Laplace Transform of the Unit Ramp Signal $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$

**Starting Definition:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1 — Use integration by parts.** Let:
$$u_{\text{ibp}} = t \quad ; \quad dv_{\text{ibp}} = e^{-st}\, dt$$
$$du_{\text{ibp}} = dt \quad ; \quad v_{\text{ibp}} = -\frac{e^{-st}}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the integration by parts formula $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0^-}^{+\infty} t\, e^{-st}\, dt &= \left[ t \cdot \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) \right]_{0^-}^{+\infty} - \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) \cdot 1\, dt \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{t\, e^{-st}}{s}\bigg\vert_{0^-}^{+\infty} + \frac{1}{s}\int_{0^-}^{+\infty} e^{-st}\, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term.** For $\text{Re}(s) > 0$:
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{t}{s\, e^{st}} = 0 \quad \text{(exponential dominates polynomial)}$$
$$\lim_{t \to 0^-} \frac{t\, e^{-st}}{s} = \frac{0}{s} = 0$$

So the boundary term is $0 - 0 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Recognize the remaining integral as $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}$:**
$$\frac{1}{s} \cdot \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Laplace Transform of the Unit Parabolic Signal $p(t) = \frac{t^2}{2} u(t)$

**Starting Definition:**
$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^2}{2} u(t)\right\} = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} \frac{t^2}{2}\, e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0^-}^{+\infty} t^2\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1 — Apply the general Laplace formula (derivable by repeated integration by parts):**
$$\mathcal{L}\{t^n\} = \frac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$$

For $n = 2$:
$$\mathcal{L}\{t^2\} = \frac{2!}{s^{3}} = \frac{2}{s^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the constant factor $\frac{1}{2}$:**
$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^2}{2}\right\} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{s^3} = \frac{1}{s^3}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^2}{2} u(t)\right\} = \frac{1}{s^3}}$$

> **Verification via differentiation property:** Since $p(t) = \int r(t)\, dt$ and $\mathcal{L}\{\int f\}\, dt = \frac{F(s)}{s}$, we have $\mathcal{L}\{p(t)\} = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2} = \frac{1}{s^3}$. ✓ Consistent.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Laplace Transform of $\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$

**Starting Definition:**
$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} \sin(\omega t) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1 — Use Euler's identity:** $\sin(\omega t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} &= \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2j}\int_{0^-}^{+\infty} \left(e^{-(s - j\omega)t} - e^{-(s + j\omega)t}\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate each exponential:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{1}{2j}\left[ \frac{e^{-(s-j\omega)t}}{-(s-j\omega)} - \frac{e^{-(s+j\omega)t}}{-(s+j\omega)} \right]_{0^-}^{+\infty} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2j}\left[ 0 - \frac{1}{-(s-j\omega)} - 0 + \frac{1}{-(s+j\omega)} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2j}\left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine fractions over a common denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\, u(t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: System Response to Unit Impulse — Direct Recovery of Transfer Function

**Problem:** Show that the impulse response of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is the inverse Laplace transform of its transfer function.

**Given:**
- Transfer function: $G(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$
- Input: $r(t) = \delta(t) \implies R(s) = 1$

**Step 1 — Apply the input-output relation:**
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) = G(s) \cdot 1 = G(s)$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse Laplace transform of both sides:**
$$c(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{G(s)\}$$

**Step 3 — Define $c(t)$ as the impulse response, conventionally denoted $g(t)$:**
$$\boxed{g(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{G(s)\}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE most important derivation in Module 2.** The KTU board examiner frequently tests this as a Part A (3-mark) or as a sub-part (a) of a Part B (14-mark) question. If you can reproduce this 3-line proof, you secure **at least 3 marks instantly**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Steady-State Error to Unit Step Input Using Final Value Theorem

**Problem:** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)$, derive the steady-state error to a unit step input.

**Given:**
- Closed-loop transfer function: $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$
- Error: $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$
- Input: $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$

**Step 1 — Express the error signal in $s$-domain:**
$$E(s) = R(s) - C(s) = R(s)\left[1 - \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}\right] = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:**
$$E(s) = \frac{1/s}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{s[1 + G(s)]}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem:**
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s[1 + G(s)]} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Position Error Constant:**
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic summary:** The final value theorem requires that all poles of $sE(s)$ lie strictly in the left half-plane (i.e., the system must be stable). KTU board examiners frequently include this stability precondition in follow-up questions.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Steady-State Error to Unit Ramp Input

**Given:** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the error formula:**
$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{s^2[1 + G(s)]}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Final Value Theorem:**
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s[1 + G(s)]}$$

**Step 3 — For a finite, non-infinite $e_{ss}$, the denominator must approach a finite non-zero value. The only way is for $G(s)$ to have at least one integrator (a $1/s$ factor):**
$$G(s) = \frac{K(1 + \tau_1 s + \dots)}{s^N (1 + \tau_a s + \dots)}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Velocity Error Constant:**
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s + sG(s)} = \frac{1}{K_v}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = \frac{1}{K_v}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A Type-0 system (no integrator) gives $K_v = 0$ and hence $e_{ss} = \infty$ for ramp input. The KTU board examiner loves asking: *"Can a Type-0 system track a ramp input?"* Correct answer: *No — the error grows unbounded.*

---

## 3.8 Analytical Master Matrix — Step Response Characteristics vs System Type

| System Type | $K_p$ | $K_v$ | $K_a$ | $e_{ss}$ Step | $e_{ss}$ Ramp | $e_{ss}$ Parabolic |
|-------------|-------|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Type-0** ($N=0$) | $K$ (finite) | $0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+K}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| **Type-1** ($N=1$) | $\infty$ | $K/T$ (finite) | $0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $\infty$ |
| **Type-2** ($N=2$) | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $K/T_1 T_2$ (finite) | $0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |
| **Type-N** ($N \geq 3$) | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

> **Where $K$ is the open-loop DC gain and $T_i$ are time constants.** This matrix is **THE most heavily tested table** in KTU ESE Part B questions on steady-state error analysis.

---

## 3.9 Worked Example — Full KTU Board Style Problem

**Problem:** A unity feedback control system has open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+5)}$$

**Determine the steady-state errors for input:** (i) Unit step, (ii) Unit ramp, (iii) Unit parabolic.

---

**Solution (i) — Unit Step:**

**Step 1 — Identify system type:** $G(s)$ has one pole at $s = 0$, so it is **Type-1**.

**Step 2 — Compute $K_p$:**
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+5)} = \frac{10 \cdot 2}{0 \cdot 5} \to \infty$$

**Step 3 — Apply step error formula:**
$$e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

---

**Solution (ii) — Unit Ramp:**

**Step 1 — Compute $K_v$:**
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+5)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s+2)}{s+5} = \frac{10 \cdot 2}{5} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Apply ramp error formula:**
$$e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

---

**Solution (iii) — Unit Parabolic:**

**Step 1 — Compute $K_a$:**
$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+5)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10\,s(s+2)}{s+5} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply parabolic error formula:**
$$e_{ss}^{\text{parabolic}} = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary:** $e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = 0$, $e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = 0.25$, $e_{ss}^{\text{parabolic}} = \infty$. This is a classic Type-1 system result: zero error to step, finite non-zero error to ramp, infinite error to parabolic input.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Relationship Between Standard Test Signals

This diagram captures the **integration and differentiation chain** that links the five standard test signals into a single coherent mathematical structure. This is the **diagram most frequently drawn on the KTU board examination answer sheet** by the examiner herself while explaining the topic.

```mermaid

    node4["IMPULSE delta(t)"]:::impulse
    node5["SINE A·sin(ωt)·u(t)"]:::sine

    node1 -- "d/dt" --> node2
    node2 -- "d/dt" --> node3
    node3 -- "d/dt" --> node4

    node4 -- "integral dt" --> node3
    node3 -- "integral dt" --> node2
    node2 -- "integral dt" --> node1

    node5 -. "Independent family, frequency domain analysis" .-> node3

    classDef parabolic fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef ramp fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100

    classDef sine fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
```

> **Reading Guide:** The blue arrows with `d/dt` represent differentiation; the orange arrows with `integral dt` represent integration. The dashed purple line indicates that the sinusoidal signal is *not* part of the differentiation hierarchy — it is an independent input used specifically for **frequency-domain analysis**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Test Signal Application

This block diagram shows the **complete workflow** that a control engineer follows when applying a standard test signal to a system, from the choice of input through to the extraction of performance metrics. This is the most common **functional schematic** tested in KTU Module 2 questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["STANDARD TEST INPUT r(t)"]:::input
    src["Step OR Ramp OR Impulse OR Parabolic OR Sine"]:::src
    sys["CONTROL SYSTEM G(s)"]:::sys
    out["OUTPUT RESPONSE c(t)"]:::output
    ana["ANALYSIS MODULE"]:::ana
    spec["PERFORMANCE SPECIFICATIONS"]:::spec

    inp -- "Select based on objective" --> src
    src -- "Apply via input port" --> sys
    sys -- "Generate" --> out
    out -- "Feed into" --> ana
    ana -- "Extract" --> spec

    spec --> s1["Rise Time tr"]:::metric
    spec --> s2["Peak Time tp"]:::metric
    spec --> s3["Settling Time ts"]:::metric
    spec --> s4["Peak Overshoot Mp percent"]:::metric
    spec --> s5["Steady-State Error ess"]:::metric

    classDef input fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef src fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef sys fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef output fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#880E4F
    classDef ana fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#5E35B1,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92
    classDef spec fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#004D40
    classDef metric fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:1px,color:#263238
```

> **Reading Guide:** A test signal is selected from the **input library** based on the engineering objective (step for transient analysis, ramp for tracking accuracy, impulse for system identification, sine for frequency response). It is fed into the plant $G(s)$, the response $c(t)$ is recorded, and then analyzed to extract **five canonical performance metrics** which completely characterize the system's dynamic behavior.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Mapping Standard Test Signals to Laplace-Domain Analysis Pipeline

This diagram captures the **Laplace transform pipeline** — the exact sequence of operations a KTU student must perform when solving a system response problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1["TIME DOMAIN INPUT r(t)"]:::td
    a2["Step u(t)"]:::form1
    a3["Ramp t·u(t)"]:::form2
    a4["Impulse delta(t)"]:::form3
    a5["Sine sin(ωt)·u(t)"]:::form4

    b1["LAPLACE DOMAIN R(s)"]:::ls
    b2["R(s) = 1/s"]:::lsf1
    b3["R(s) = 1/s squared"]:::lsf2
    b4["R(s) = 1"]:::lsf3
    b5["R(s) = ω divided by s² + ω²"]:::lsf4

    c1["OUTPUT C(s) = G(s)·R(s)"]:::out

    d1["PARTIAL FRACTION EXPANSION"]:::pfe
    e1["INVERSE LAPLACE TRANSFORM"]:::inv
    f1["TIME DOMAIN RESPONSE c(t)"]:::final

    a1 --> a2
    a1 --> a3
    a1 --> a4
    a1 --> a5

    a2 --> b2
    a3 --> b3
    a4 --> b4
    a5 --> b5

    b2 --> b1
    b3 --> b1
    b4 --> b1
    b5 --> b1

    b1 -- "Multiply by G(s)" --> c1
    c1 -- "Decompose" --> d1
    d1 -- "Apply standard table" --> e1
    e1 -- "Obtain" --> f1

    classDef td fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef form1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef form2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#E65100
    classDef form3 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:1px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef form4 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1px,color:#4A148C
    classDef ls fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef lsf1 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lsf2 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lsf3 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lsf4 fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#5E35B1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#004D40
    classDef pfe fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#880E4F
    classDef inv fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef final fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
```

> **Reading Guide:** Every KTU ESE system-response question follows this pipeline — start with the time-domain input, transform to $s$-domain, multiply by $G(s)$, do partial fractions, invert to get $c(t)$. Master this pipeline and you can solve any problem of this type.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Error Constant Definition Map

This diagram provides a **visual decision tree** for selecting the correct error constant based on the input signal type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q{"WHAT IS THE INPUT?"}:::question
    a["STEP u(t)"]:::choice
    b["RAMP t·u(t)"]:::choice
    c["PARABOLIC t²/2 · u(t)"]:::choice
    d["SINE sin(ωt)·u(t)"]:::choice

    e["Use Kp = lim s→0 G(s)"]:::kp
    f["Use Kv = lim s→0 s·G(s)"]:::kv
    g["Use Ka = lim s→0 s²·G(s)"]:::ka
    h["Steady-state error is zero for stable system"]:::sine

    i["ess = 1 divided by 1 plus Kp"]:::kpans
    j["ess = 1 divided by Kv"]:::kvans
    k["ess = 1 divided by Ka"]:::kaans

    q --> a
    q --> b
    q --> c
    q --> d

    a -- "Compute" --> e
    b -- "Compute" --> f
    c -- "Compute" --> g
    d -- "Note" --> h

    e --> i
    f --> j
    g --> k

    classDef question fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef choice fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B

    classDef kv fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef ka fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef sine fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef kpans fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef kvans fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef kaans fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
```

> **Reading Guide:** Follow the arrow from the input type to the correct error constant definition, then to the corresponding steady-state error formula. This decision tree eliminates the most common KTU student error — applying the wrong error constant to the wrong input.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: List any five standard test signals used in control system analysis and write their Laplace transforms.**

**Model Answer (Board-Expected, 5 points):**

| # | Signal | Time-Domain $r(t)$ | Laplace $R(s)$ |
|---|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(t)$ | $1$ |
| 2 | Unit Step | $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ |
| 3 | Unit Ramp | $t\,u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ |
| 4 | Unit Parabolic | $\dfrac{t^2}{2}u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^3}$ |
| 5 | Unit Sinusoid | $\sin(\omega t)\,u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}$ |

> **Board Valuation Tip:** All five entries must be listed — partial marks (1 per correct entry) are awarded even if the student only remembers three.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Show that the impulse response of a linear time-invariant system is the inverse Laplace transform of its transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** The output of an LTI system in the Laplace domain is:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 2:** For impulse input, $r(t) = \delta(t)$, hence:
$$R(s) = \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot 1 = G(s)$$

**Step 4:** Taking inverse Laplace transform:
$$\boxed{c(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{G(s)\} = g(t)}$$

Hence, the impulse response $g(t)$ is mathematically identical to the inverse Laplace transform of the transfer function. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Rule:** In Part B, every question offers **two options (A and B)**. You must answer ONLY ONE. Both options are of equal marks and difficulty. Choose the one in which you are most confident.

---

### **PART B — OPTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Question A:** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

**Determine:**
**(a)** The steady-state error to a unit step input. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The steady-state error to a unit ramp input, and comment on the system's ability to track a parabolic input. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the system type:**
$G(s)$ has one pole at the origin ($s = 0$) and no other poles at origin, so the system is **Type-1**.

**Step 2 — Compute the Position Error Constant $K_p$:**
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

As $s \to 0$, the numerator $\to 20 \cdot 1 = 20$, while the denominator $\to 0 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 0$.

Hence $K_p = \infty$.

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state error formula for unit step input:**
$$e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

**Final Answer (a):** $e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = 0$ **(2 marks for the formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for the limit computation, 2 marks for stating the conclusion with justification)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the Velocity Error Constant $K_v$:**
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

Cancelling the common factor $s$:
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{20(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)}$$

Substituting $s = 0$:
$$K_v = \frac{20(0+1)}{(0+2)(0+5)} = \frac{20}{10} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state error formula for unit ramp input:**
$$e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Acceleration Error Constant $K_a$ for parabolic input comment:**
$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot \frac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{20\,s(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{20 \cdot 0 \cdot 1}{2 \cdot 5} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state error for parabolic input:**
$$e_{ss}^{\text{parabolic}} = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

**Comment:** Since $K_a = 0$ and $e_{ss}^{\text{parabolic}} = \infty$, the system **cannot track a parabolic input** in the steady state — the error grows unbounded with time. This is consistent with the property of Type-1 systems. **(1 mark for the comment)**

**Final Answer (b):** $e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = 0.5$, $e_{ss}^{\text{parabolic}} = \infty$ — system cannot track parabolic input.

---

### **PART B — OPTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Question B:** For a linear time-invariant control system with closed-loop transfer function:
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 3s + 10}$$

**Determine:**
**(a)** The impulse response $g(t)$ of the system and identify whether the system is stable. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The step response $c(t)$ and compute the steady-state value $c(\infty)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Express $C(s)$ for impulse input:**

For impulse input, $R(s) = 1$, so:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) = \frac{10}{s^2 + 3s + 10} \cdot 1 = \frac{10}{s^2 + 3s + 10}$$

**Step 2 — Convert denominator to standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:**

Comparing $s^2 + 3s + 10$ with $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:
$$\omega_n^2 = 10 \implies \omega_n = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$2\zeta\omega_n = 3 \implies \zeta = \frac{3}{2\omega_n} = \frac{3}{2\sqrt{10}} \approx 0.474$$

**Step 3 — Compute the damped natural frequency:**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = \sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{1 - 0.225} = \sqrt{10 \cdot 0.775} = \sqrt{7.75} \approx 2.784 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Express $C(s)$ in standard underdamped form:**
$$C(s) = \frac{10}{(s+\zeta\omega_n)^2 + \omega_d^2} = \frac{10}{(s + 1.5)^2 + 7.75}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the inverse Laplace formula:**
$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{\omega_d}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_d^2}\right\} = e^{-at}\sin(\omega_d t)$$

Rearranging:
$$C(s) = \frac{10}{\omega_d} \cdot \frac{\omega_d}{(s+1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2}$$

So:
$$g(t) = c(t) = \frac{10}{\omega_d} e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t) = \frac{10}{2.784} e^{-1.5 t}\sin(2.784 t)$$

$$\boxed{g(t) \approx 3.59\, e^{-1.5 t}\sin(2.784 t)\, u(t)}$$

**Step 6 — Stability check:**

Poles of $G(s)$ are roots of $s^2 + 3s + 10 = 0$:
$$s = \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{9 - 40}}{2} = \frac{-3 \pm j\sqrt{31}}{2} = -1.5 \pm j\,2.784$$

Both poles have negative real parts ($=-1.5$). The poles lie in the **left half of the $s$-plane**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable**. The impulse response decays exponentially to zero as $t \to \infty$ (since the envelope $e^{-1.5t}$ decays).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Express $C(s)$ for unit step input:**

For unit step input, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) = \frac{10}{s(s^2 + 3s + 10)}$$

**Step 2 — Partial Fraction Expansion:**
$$C(s) = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{Bs + C}{s^2 + 3s + 10}$$

Multiplying both sides by $s(s^2 + 3s + 10)$:
$$10 = A(s^2 + 3s + 10) + (Bs + C)(s)$$

Substituting $s = 0$:
$$10 = A(10) + 0 \implies A = 1$$

Equating coefficients of $s^2$:
$$0 = A + B \implies B = -A = -1$$

Equating coefficients of $s$:
$$0 = 3A + C \implies C = -3A = -3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**
$$C(s) = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{-s - 3}{s^2 + 3s + 10} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{s + 3}{(s + 1.5)^2 + 7.75}$$

**Step 4 — Decompose the second term to fit standard inverse Laplace pairs:**

$$\frac{s + 3}{(s + 1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2} = \frac{(s + 1.5) + 1.5}{(s + 1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2} = \frac{(s+1.5)}{(s+1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2} + \frac{1.5}{(s+1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2}$$

Rewriting with $\omega_d = 2.784$:

$$\frac{s+3}{(s+1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2} = \frac{s+1.5}{(s+1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2} + \frac{1.5}{\omega_d} \cdot \frac{\omega_d}{(s+1.5)^2 + \omega_d^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply standard inverse Laplace transforms:**

Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2+\omega_d^2}\right\} = e^{-at}\cos(\omega_d t)$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{\omega_d}{(s+a)^2+\omega_d^2}\right\} = e^{-at}\sin(\omega_d t)$:

$$c(t) = 1 - e^{-1.5t}\cos(2.784 t) - \frac{1.5}{2.784} e^{-1.5t}\sin(2.784 t)$$

$$\boxed{c(t) = 1 - e^{-1.5t}\left[\cos(2.784 t) + 0.539\,\sin(2.784 t)\right] u(t)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute steady-state value:**

As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term $e^{-1.5t} \to 0$, so:
$$c(\infty) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

**Verification using Final Value Theorem:**
$$c(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot C(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{10}{s(s^2+3s+10)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10}{s^2+3s+10} = \frac{10}{10} = 1 \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC — KTU Board Examiner's Debrief:**
>
> **1. Forgetting the stability prerequisite for the Final Value Theorem.**
> Students blindly apply $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ without checking that all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the LHP. For unstable systems, the FVT gives **wrong, misleading results** (e.g., a finite value for an actually-diverging error). Always check stability first using Routh-Hurwitz or pole locations. **Penalty: 2–3 marks lost.**
>
> **2. Confusing $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$ definitions with their formulas.**
> Many students write $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)$ (which is actually $K_v$). Memorize:
> - $K_p \to G(s)$ (no $s$ factor)
> - $K_v \to sG(s)$ (one $s$ factor)
> - $K_a \to s^2 G(s)$ (two $s$ factors)
> **Penalty: Full 7 marks lost on the sub-part.**
>
> **3. Writing $\delta(t) = 1$ at $t=0$.**
> The impulse function is a *distribution*, not a classical function. Its value at $t=0$ is undefined; only the integral $\int \delta(t) dt = 1$ is meaningful. **Penalty: 1 mark lost** (and examiner's red pen marks it).
>
> **4. Incorrectly applying the unit ramp formula for a unit step.**
> Some students write $e_{ss} = 1/K_v$ even for step input. The error formulas are *strictly input-specific*. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> **5. Skipping the time-domain form of the output.**
> When asked to find the impulse/step response, students often leave the answer in the $s$-domain and forget to apply inverse Laplace transform. **Penalty: 3 marks lost** (the final time-domain expression carries full marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT THIS SECTION. READ IT 30 MINUTES BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL.**

### A. Signal Hierarchy (must be memorized verbatim)

$$\boxed{\delta(t) \xrightleftharpoons[\text{d/dt}]{\int dt} u(t) \xrightleftharpoons[\text{d/dt}]{\int dt} t \cdot u(t) \xrightleftharpoons[\text{d/dt}]{\int dt} \frac{t^2}{2} \cdot u(t)}$$

### B. Laplace Transform Table (must be memorized verbatim)

| Signal | Laplace Transform |
|--------|-------------------|
| $\delta(t)$ | $1$ |
| $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ |
| $t \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ |
| $\dfrac{t^2}{2} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^3}$ |
| $\sin(\omega t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ |
| $\cos(\omega t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ |

### C. Error Constant Definitions (must be memorized verbatim)

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) \quad ; \quad K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) \quad ; \quad K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s)$$

### D. Steady-State Error Formulas (must be memorized verbatim)

$$e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = \frac{1}{1+K_p} \quad ; \quad e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = \frac{1}{K_v} \quad ; \quad e_{ss}^{\text{parabolic}} = \frac{1}{K_a}$$

### E. Type-N System Behavior (master matrix)

| Type | Step Error | Ramp Error | Parabolic Error |
|------|------------|------------|------------------|
| 0 | $\dfrac{1}{1+K}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | $0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |
| $\geq 3$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

### F. Three Imperial Properties of $\delta(t)$

1. **Sampling:** $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t)\delta(t-a)\,dt = f(a)$
2. **Multiplication:** $f(t)\delta(t-a) = f(a)\delta(t-a)$
3. **Evenness & Scaling:** $\delta(-t) = \delta(t)$; $\delta(at) = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert}\delta(t)$

### G. Two Imperial Properties of $u(t)$

1. **Step response is the integral of impulse response:**
$$c_{\text{step}}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} g(\tau)\, d\tau$$

2. **Impulse response is the derivative of step response:**
$$g(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\, c_{\text{step}}(t)$$

### H. The "Golden Rule" of KTU Control Exams

> **"If a transfer function $G(s)$ is given, always check three things FIRST before solving anything:**
> 1. **Poles** (location, multiplicity, real/imaginary)
> 2. **Zeros** (location)
> 3. **System Type** (number of pure integrators = number of poles at $s=0$)
>
> These three checks give you 70% of the marks for free."

### I. Numerical Constants to Remember

| Constant | Value | Used In |
|----------|-------|---------|
| $\pi$ | $3.14159$ | Period calculations |
| $\sqrt{2}$ | $1.414$ | Peak overshoot formula |
| $\ln$ | $\approx 0.434$ per dB | Bode plots (next module) |
| $\zeta = 0.707$ | Butterworth damping | Optimal second-order |

---

## 5.5 Final Word from the Examiner

> [!TIP]
> **Closing Note for KTU 2024 Scheme Students:**
>
> Module 2 is the *gateway module* of Classical Control Engineering — every subsequent module (time-domain specs, Routh-Hurwitz, root locus, Bode, Nyquist, compensator design) is built directly on the foundation of standard test signals and their Laplace transforms. If you master the **fourteen formulas** in this notes file (5 signal transforms + 5 signal definitions + 3 error constants + 1 FVT statement), you will find Modules 3 through 5 remarkably easier. Treat this topic as a **prerequisite pillar**, not just a syllabus item.
>
> **Recommended study order for the ESE:**
> 1. Memorize the Signal Hierarchy diagram (Section 4.1)
> 2. Memorize the Laplace Table (Section 5.4 B)
> 3. Practice deriving each Laplace transform from scratch (Section 3)
> 4. Solve the worked example in Section 3.9 by hand, without looking
> 5. Attempt both Part B options in Section 5.2 under timed conditions (1 hour each)
>
> **Target score in ESE for this topic alone: 12 out of 14 marks minimum.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Transfer Functions, Type and Order of Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Transfer Functions, Type and Order of Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Open-Loop Control System

An **Open-Loop Control System** is a control configuration in which the control action is completely independent of the desired output. The system has **no feedback path** from the output to the input, meaning the controller has no knowledge of whether the actual output matches the desired reference input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *An open-loop system is one in which the control action is generated solely based on the input command, without using any information about the actual output. The transfer function relating input to output is simply the cascade of all forward path elements.*

The mathematical model is straightforward:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

where $G(s)$ is the forward path transfer function and $R(s)$ is the reference input.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Toaster

Imagine a **pop-up toaster** from the 1990s. You set a timer (say, 2 minutes) and push the lever down. The toaster will heat the coil for exactly 2 minutes regardless of whether the bread is lightly browned, perfectly toasted, or burnt to a crisp. There is **no sensor** checking the actual color of the bread. The input is "2 minutes," the output is "bread color," and there is **no feedback**. This is a classic open-loop system — accurate only if the model of the heating process is perfect and no disturbances occur.

## 1.2 Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control System

A **Closed-Loop Control System** continuously monitors the actual output and compares it with the desired reference input. The difference (the **error signal**) drives the controller, which adjusts the control action to minimize this error. This is the foundational principle of **automatic control theory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A closed-loop (feedback) control system is one in which the output is measured and fed back to a summing junction, where it is compared with the reference input to produce an actuating error signal that drives the forward path. The closed-loop transfer function is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input with the feedback loop closed.*

The defining transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

For a **unity feedback system** ($H(s) = 1$):

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

### Conceptual Analogy: The Cruise Control

Consider a **car's cruise control system**. You set the desired speed at 80 km/h. A **speed sensor** continuously measures the actual wheel speed. The control unit computes the error (desired − actual). If the car climbs a hill, the speed drops, the error becomes positive, and the controller **increases the throttle opening** automatically. The system corrects itself in real time. The key insight: **disturbances (hills, wind) are rejected** because the feedback loop detects and corrects deviations.

## 1.3 Type of a Control System

The **Type** of a control system is defined as the **number of pure integrations (poles at the origin, i.e., factors of $1/s$)** present in the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Type matter?**
> The Type number directly determines the system's ability to track standard test inputs (step, ramp, parabolic) **with zero steady-state error**. This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU examinations.

| System Type | Number of Integrators | Pole Multiplicity at Origin |
|:-----------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Type 0 | 0 | No pole at $s = 0$ |
| Type 1 | 1 | One pole at $s = 0$ |
| Type 2 | 2 | Double pole at $s = 0$ |
| Type n | n | $n$ poles at $s = 0$ |

## 1.4 Order of a Control System

The **Order** of a system is the **highest power of the complex variable $s$** appearing in the denominator polynomial of the **closed-loop transfer function** (after reduction to standard form). Equivalently, it is the **total number of poles** of the closed-loop transfer function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction — Type vs. Order:**
> - **Type** is determined by the **open-loop** transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ and counts integrators.
> - **Order** is determined by the **closed-loop** transfer function $T(s)$ and counts total poles.
> - These two metrics describe **different things** and a student must never confuse them.

A third-order system has a closed-loop transfer function of the form:

$$T(s) = \frac{b_0}{a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0}$$

The characteristic equation is $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$, yielding **three poles**.

### Conceptual Analogy: Type and Order

Think of **Type** as the *fuel type* of a vehicle (petrol, diesel, electric) — it tells you the *qualitative capability* (what kind of journey the car can sustain without error). Think of **Order** as the *engine size* (number of cylinders) — it tells you the *quantitative complexity* (how detailed the dynamic response will be, how many energy storage elements are present).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)

In a feedback configuration, the **Open-Loop Transfer Function** is the product of all transfer function elements in the loop, taken by **breaking the feedback path at the summing junction**. Mathematically:

$$\text{OLTF} = G(s)H(s)$$

For a unity feedback system, this simplifies to $G(s)$. The OLTF is **not** the transfer function of the physical open-loop system; rather, it is a **mathematical construct** obtained by severing the feedback loop and tracing the signal path from the error point around and back to the same error point.

### Properties of OLTF
- It is used to determine **system stability** via the Nyquist criterion and Bode plots.
- It is used to determine the **Type** of the system.
- It is the basis for computing **error constants** ($K_p, K_v, K_a$).
- It does **not** represent the actual input-output relationship in normal operation.

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function (CLTF)

For the **standard negative feedback** block diagram, applying Mason's gain formula or direct block reduction:

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

This is the **primary CLTF**. The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation** of the system, whose roots are the **closed-loop poles**.

### Auxiliary Transfer Functions in a Feedback System

For the standard configuration with $R(s)$ as reference, $E(s)$ as error, $C(s)$ as output, and no disturbance:

**Error Transfer Function (with respect to reference):**

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Control Signal Transfer Function (output of forward path):**

$$\frac{U(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Feedback Signal Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{B(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)H(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

## 2.3 Steady-State Error

The **steady-state error** is the difference between the desired reference and the actual output as $t \to \infty$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$$

using the **Final Value Theorem** (valid only if all closed-loop poles lie in the open left-half plane, i.e., the system is stable).

For the standard unity feedback system with reference $R(s)$:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

## 2.4 Static Error Constants

The **static error constants** are the limiting values of $G(s)$ and its integrations, evaluated at $s = 0$. They parameterize the system's tracking ability for each standard test input.

| Error Constant | Definition | Test Input Applied | Steady-State Error |
|:--------------:|:----------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| $K_p$ (Position) | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$ | Step $R(s) = 1/s$ | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ |
| $K_v$ (Velocity) | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)H(s)$ | Ramp $R(s) = 1/s^2$ | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ |
| $K_a$ (Acceleration) | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)H(s)$ | Parabolic $R(s) = 1/s^3$ | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For a **unity feedback system**, substitute $H(s) = 1$ in all the above formulas.

## 2.5 Type vs. Steady-State Error — The Master Relationship

This is the single most important relationship in this module. For a **unity negative feedback system**, the table below gives the steady-state error for all combinations of system Type and test input:

| System Type | Step Input $\frac{1}{s}$ | Ramp Input $\frac{1}{s^2}$ | Parabolic Input $\frac{1}{s^3}$ |
|:-----------:|:------------------------:|:--------------------------:|:-------------------------------:|
| **Type 0** | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| **Type 1** | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $\infty$ |
| **Type 2** | $0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |
| **Type 3** | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition (Critical for KTU):** For an input of order $k$ (step = order 0, ramp = order 1, parabolic = order 2):
> - If system Type $> k$ : $e_{ss} = 0$
> - If system Type $= k$ : $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{\text{appropriate error constant}}$
> - If system Type $< k$ : $e_{ss} = \infty$

## 2.6 Order of the System

The order is read from the **closed-loop transfer function denominator** after all block diagram reduction is complete. The order of the system has the following implications:

- **First-order systems:** Single energy storage element. Step response is exponential, monotonic.
- **Second-order systems:** Two energy storage elements. Step response can be overdamped, underdamped, critically damped, or undamped.
- **Higher-order systems:** Multiple storage elements; response combines modes.

The order of a system is **fixed by the physical plant** and cannot be reduced by changing the controller alone, although controller poles can increase the effective order.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for this module. Master this — it is the heart of Part A and Part B questions.**

| \# | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:-------------------|
| 1 | OLTF | $G(s)H(s)$ | Loop broken at summing junction |
| 2 | CLTF (negative feedback) | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Standard form |
| 3 | CLTF (positive feedback) | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1-G(s)H(s)}$ | Rarely used; sign error prone |
| 4 | Error TF | $\dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | For unity feedback $H(s)=1$ |
| 5 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Roots = closed-loop poles |
| 6 | Steady-state error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ | Requires system stability |
| 7 | Position constant | $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$ | For step input |
| 8 | Velocity constant | $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)H(s)$ | For ramp input |
| 9 | Acceleration constant | $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2G(s)H(s)$ | For parabolic input |
| 10 | Type | Number of $1/s$ in $G(s)H(s)$ | Open-loop metric |
| 11 | Order | Highest power of $s$ in $\text{den}[T(s)]$ | Closed-loop metric |
| 12 | Step error | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | Type 0 only; zero for Type $\geq 1$ |
| 13 | Ramp error | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | Type 1 only; $\infty$ for Type 0; zero for Type $\geq 2$ |
| 14 | Parabolic error | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$ | Type 2 only; $\infty$ for Type $<2$; zero for Type $\geq 3$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why OLTF/CLTF/Type Matters |
|:-------------------|:---------------------------|
| **Robotics** | Determining how accurately a robot arm can follow a commanded trajectory (ramp/velocity input) requires Type $\geq 1$ design. |
| **Antenna Positioning Systems** | To track a moving satellite (parabolic input), the system must be at least Type 2 to avoid accumulating error. |
| **Servo Motor Drives** | Position control (step input) is satisfied with Type 1; velocity control needs Type 1 or higher with finite $K_v$. |
| **Spacecraft Attitude Control** | Disturbance torques are step-like, but orbital mechanics generate ramp-like drift; Type 1 with adequate $K_v$ is standard. |
| **Hard Disk Drive Read Heads** | Track-seeking is a step input; settling to within micro-inches demands high $K_p$ in a Type 1 system. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Consider the canonical **single-loop negative feedback** block diagram with reference input $R(s)$, error $E(s)$, forward path $G(s)$, output $C(s)$, feedback element $H(s)$, and feedback signal $B(s)$.

### Step 1 — Identify the error signal
The error is formed at the summing junction as the difference between reference and feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

### Step 2 — Express the feedback signal in terms of the error
The forward path transfer function $G(s)$ takes the error and produces the output:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

The feedback element processes the output:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute (Step 2) into (Step 1)

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

But from Step 2, $C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$, so:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

### Step 4 — Collect $E(s)$ terms

$$E(s) + G(s)H(s) \cdot E(s) = R(s)$$

$$E(s) \cdot [1 + G(s)H(s)] = R(s)$$

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

This is the **error transfer function** — Eq. (i).

### Step 5 — Derive the closed-loop transfer function
Since $C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$ and $E(s) = R(s)/[1+G(s)H(s)]$:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function** $T(s)$ — the most important equation in the entire classical control course. (End of derivation.)

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Error for a Unit Step Input

Given $R(s) = 1/s$ (unit step) and unity feedback $H(s) = 1$.

### Step 1 — Write the error transform
From the error transfer function derived above:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + G(s)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem
For a stable system, the steady-state value of the error is:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the position error constant
Recognizing that $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$ for unity feedback:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

**Verification of Type-dependence:**
- If $G(s) = \dfrac{K(1+\tau_1 s)(1+\tau_2 s)\cdots}{(\tau_a s + 1)(\tau_b s + 1)\cdots}$ (Type 0, no integrators):

$$K_p = G(0) = K \cdot \text{(product of DC gains)} = K_0$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_0} \quad \text{(nonzero finite error)}$$

- If $G(s) = \dfrac{K(1+\tau_1 s)\cdots}{s(\tau_a s + 1)\cdots}$ (Type 1, one integrator):

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K}{s \cdot (\tau_a s + 1)} = \infty$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

This **proves** that a Type 1 or higher system has zero steady-state error for a step input. (End of derivation.)

## 3.3 Derivation of Steady-State Error for a Unit Ramp Input

Given $R(s) = 1/s^2$ (unit ramp) and unity feedback.

### Step 1 — Write the error transform

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1/s^2}{1 + G(s)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s[1 + G(s)]} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s + sG(s)}$$

### Step 3 — Factor and recognize the velocity error constant
Multiplying numerator and denominator by $1/s$ in the inner product:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s \cdot [1 + G(s)]}$$

To handle the indeterminate form, multiply and divide by $s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1/s}{1/s + G(s)/1} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Substituting $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)$:

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v}}$$

**Type-dependence verification:**
- **Type 0 system:** $G(s)$ has no $1/s$ factor, so $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s) = 0$, giving $e_{ss} = 1/0 = \infty$.
- **Type 1 system:** $G(s) = K/[s(\tau s+1)\cdots]$, so $K_v = K/1 = K$, giving $e_{ss} = 1/K$ (finite).
- **Type 2 system:** $G(s) = K/[s^2(\tau s+1)\cdots]$, so $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot K/[s^2(\cdots)] = \lim_{s \to 0} K/[s(\cdots)] = \infty$, giving $e_{ss} = 0$.

(End of derivation.)

## 3.4 Worked Example: Determining Type, Order, and Steady-State Errors

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:
> $$G(s) = \frac{50(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$
> Determine: (i) Type and Order, (ii) error constants, (iii) steady-state errors for step, ramp, and parabolic inputs.

### Step 1 — Identify the system Type
Look for pure $1/s$ factors in $G(s)$. The numerator has $(s+2)$, the denominator has $s \cdot (s+1) \cdot (s+3)$. There is **exactly one** $1/s$ factor.

$$\text{Type} = 1$$

### Step 2 — Identify the system Order
Find the closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{50(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3) + 50(s+2)}$$

Expand the denominator:

$$s(s+1)(s+3) = s(s^2 + 4s + 3) = s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s$$

$$50(s+2) = 50s + 100$$

$$\text{Denominator} = s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s + 50s + 100 = s^3 + 4s^2 + 53s + 100$$

The highest power of $s$ in the denominator is **3**.

$$\text{Order} = 3$$

### Step 3 — Compute the static error constants

**Position constant $K_p$:**

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

As $s \to 0$, the denominator $\to 0$ because of the $1/s$ factor, while the numerator $\to 100$. So:

$$K_p = \infty$$

**Velocity constant $K_v$:**

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)} = \frac{50 \cdot 2}{1 \cdot 3} = \frac{100}{3} \approx 33.33$$

**Acceleration constant $K_a$:**

$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50s(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Compute steady-state errors

**Step input** $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p} = \frac{1}{1+\infty} = 0$$

**Ramp input** $R(s) = 1/s^2$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{100/3} = \frac{3}{100} = 0.03$$

**Parabolic input** $R(s) = 1/s^3$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check using the Type rule:** Type 1 system. Step = Type > input order (0), so $e_{ss} = 0$ ✓. Ramp = Type = input order (1), so $e_{ss} = 1/K_v$ (finite) ✓. Parabolic = Type < input order (2), so $e_{ss} = \infty$ ✓. All consistent.

## 3.5 Order Determination from Block Diagram Reductions

When the closed-loop system is described by a complex block diagram, the order must be computed **only after all block reduction** (cascade, parallel, feedback) is complete and a single equivalent forward transfer function is obtained.

### Example: Multi-loop Reduction

Suppose the system is described by:

$$G_1(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}, \quad G_2(s) = \frac{5}{s+2}, \quad H_1(s) = \frac{1}{s+3}, \quad H_2(s) = 2$$

**Step 1 — Inner loop reduction (around $G_2$ with feedback $H_2$):**

$$G_{2,eq} = \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_2} = \frac{5/(s+2)}{1 + 10/(s+2)} = \frac{5/(s+2)}{(s+2+10)/(s+2)} = \frac{5}{s+12}$$

**Step 2 — Series combination with $G_1$:**

$$G_{12}(s) = G_1 \cdot G_{2,eq} = \frac{1}{s+1} \cdot \frac{5}{s+12} = \frac{5}{(s+1)(s+12)}$$

**Step 3 — Outer loop closure with $H_1$:**

$$T(s) = \frac{G_{12}}{1 + G_{12} H_1} = \frac{5/[(s+1)(s+12)]}{1 + 5/[(s+1)(s+12)(s+3)]}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(s+1)(s+12)(s+3)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{5(s+3)}{(s+1)(s+12)(s+3) + 5}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$(s+1)(s+12) = s^2 + 13s + 12$$

$$(s^2 + 13s + 12)(s+3) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 13s^2 + 39s + 12s + 36 = s^3 + 16s^2 + 51s + 36$$

$$\text{Denominator} = s^3 + 16s^2 + 51s + 36 + 5 = s^3 + 16s^2 + 51s + 41$$

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{5(s+3)}{s^3 + 16s^2 + 51s + 41}}$$

**Order of the system = 3.**

**Type of the system** = Number of $1/s$ factors in $G_1(s) G_2(s) H_1(s) = \dfrac{5}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$ = **Type 0**.

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing Type and Order

| Feature | Type | Order |
|:--------|:----:|:-----:|
| Source transfer function | $G(s)H(s)$ (open-loop) | $T(s)$ (closed-loop) |
| Definition | Number of $1/s$ factors | Highest power of $s$ in denominator of $T(s)$ |
| What it controls | Steady-state tracking accuracy | Dynamic response complexity |
| Effect of adding a controller | Adding a $1/s$ increases Type by 1 | Adding a pole increases Order |
| For $G(s) = \frac{K}{s^n}$ | Type = $n$ | (Depends on feedback) |
| Max meaningful | Practically up to Type 2 in industry | Theoretically unbounded |
