The necessary conditions are satisfied only for **$K > 3$**. Within this range, the system **may be stable**, and a full Routh-Hurwitz analysis is required to determine the exact stability range.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Necessary with Sufficient**: Students often conclude "the system is stable" after the necessary conditions pass. **Wrong** — they only rule out gross instability. You must follow up with a Routh array for a definitive verdict.
> 2. **Sign-Change Counting Error**: Carefully count the **transitions** between consecutive coefficients, not the number of negative coefficients. E.g., $+, +, -, -$ has **one** sign change (not two).
> 3. **Forgetting the Constant Term Check**: A polynomial like $s^3 + 2s^2 + s$ (where $a_0 = 0$) has a pole at the origin — this is **not asymptotically stable**, even though all non-zero coefficients are positive.
> 4. **Assuming "All Positive" Means Stable**: The polynomial $s^3 + 2s^2 + s + 6$ has all positive coefficients but is **still unstable** (Routh array reveals two RHP roots). All-positive is a **necessary** but not **sufficient** condition.
> 5. **Missing Higher-Order Terms**: A term like $s^5 + 2s^3 + s + 1$ is missing both $s^4$ and $s^2$ terms. This is a **clear violation** of necessary conditions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The characteristic equation is $P(s) = a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$.
- ✅ **Necessary Condition 1**: All coefficients must be **real**.
- ✅ **Necessary Condition 2**: All coefficients must be **non-zero** (no missing powers of $s$).
- ✅ **Necessary Condition 3**: All coefficients must have the **same sign** (typically all positive for physical systems).
- ✅ **Necessary Condition 4**: The constant term $a_n \neq 0$ (no pole at origin).
- ✅ Real negative roots contribute factors of the form $(s + p)$ with $p > 0$.
- ✅ Complex conjugate pairs contribute quadratic factors $s^2 + 2\alpha s + (\alpha^2 + \beta^2)$ with $\alpha, \beta > 0$.
- ✅ **Descartes' Rule of Signs**: The number of positive real roots is at most the number of sign changes in the coefficient sequence.
- ✅ These conditions are **necessary but NOT sufficient** — they filter out obviously unstable cases.
- ✅ A system passing necessary conditions must still be tested with the **Routh-Hurwitz** criterion for a definitive answer.
- ✅ **Marginal stability** (poles on $j\omega$-axis) is NOT asymptotic stability — never confuse the two.
- ✅ A zero coefficient implies either $s=0$, $s=\infty$, or a missing term — all undesirable for closed-loop stability.
- ✅ Always **factor the polynomial** mentally to identify the sign patterns of elementary factors.
- ✅ In KTU exams, present your answer with a **table of coefficient signs** to earn full method marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routh's Criterion of Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Routh's Criterion of Stability ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Routh's Stability Criterion (Routh–Hurwitz Criterion):** > It is an algebraic, necessary and sufficient method that determines the *absolute stability* of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) feedback control system by inspecting the coefficients of the **characteristic equation** of the closed-loop transfer function. The criterion determines *whether all roots of the characteristic polynomial lie strictly in the left-half of the s-plane (LHS)* without requiring the explicit computation of the roots themselves. The closed-loop characteristic equation has the general form: $$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$ where $a_0 \neq 0$ and all $a_i$ are real constants. The system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if every root of this polynomial has a strictly negative real part. ### Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Marble on a Hill" Analogy:** > Imagine a marble placed on a curved hill. The **shape of the hill** determines the marble's behaviour: > - If the hill is a *bowl* (concave up), the marble rolls back to the bottom and stays there → **Stable**. > - If the hill is an *inverted dome* (concave down), the marble rolls away forever → **Unstable**. > - If the hill is *perfectly flat*, the marble stays wherever placed → **Marginally Stable**. > > The characteristic equation is the "mathematical shape" of the system. Routh's criterion is a fast, **sign-inspection method** that tells you the shape of the hill (the location of the roots) just by looking at the coefficients — you never have to actually solve the polynomial (which is extremely hard for order ≥ 3). ### Key Physical & Mathematical Metrics - **Characteristic Equation** – the denominator of the closed-loop transfer function set to zero. - **Routh Array** – a tabular construction used to apply the criterion. - **Sign Changes** – each sign change in the first column of the Routh array corresponds to **one root in the right-half s-plane (RHP)**. - **Hurwitz Polynomial** – a polynomial whose roots all have strictly negative real parts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** > Routh's criterion is a **frequency-domain / algebraic test**, NOT a graphical test (that is the role of the Nyquist criterion in Module 4). Always write the characteristic equation in descending powers of $s$ before forming the Routh array. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Step 1 – Necessary Condition Check:** Verify that *all* coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ of the characteristic equation are **present and have the same sign (all positive, or all negative)**. - If any coefficient is zero or of opposite sign → the system is **unstable** (or has roots on the imaginary axis). - This is necessary but **NOT sufficient** for stability. 2. **Step 2 – Construct the Routh Array:** Build a tabular array with the following structure: | Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | $\cdots$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $s^n$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | $a_4$ | $\cdots$ | | $s^{n-1}$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | $a_5$ | $\cdots$ | | $s^{n-2}$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $b_3$ | $\cdots$ | | $s^{n-3}$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $c_3$ | $\cdots$ | | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | | - **Row 1 ($s^n$):** All even-indexed coefficients. - **Row 2 ($s^{n-1}$):** All odd-indexed coefficients. - **Subsequent rows** are computed by determinant-style cross-multiplication. 3. **Step 3 – Compute the Elements Using the Standard Formula:** $$b_1 = \frac{a_1 \cdot a_2 - a_0 \cdot a_3}{a_1}, \quad b_2 = \frac{a_1 \cdot a_4 - a_0 \cdot a_5}{a_1}, \quad b_3 = \frac{a_1 \cdot a_6 - a_0 \cdot a_7}{a_1}$$ $$c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot a_3 - a_1 \cdot b_2}{b_1}, \quad c_2 = \frac{b_1 \cdot a_5 - a_1 \cdot b_3}{b_1}$$ - In general, each element of a new row is computed using the **two rows directly above it** and the **first element of the row immediately above**. 4. **Step 4 – Stability Decision from the First Column:** - Examine **only the first column** of the completed Routh array. - If **every element** in the first column is **strictly positive** (assuming we normalised all coefficients to be positive) → the system is **stable**. - If any element is **negative or zero** → the system is **unstable**. 5. **Step 5 – Count of Right-Half-Plane (RHP) Roots:** - The **number of sign changes** encountered while traversing the first column from top to bottom = the **number of roots with positive real parts** (i.e., the number of unstable roots). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > Routh's criterion is heavily used to determine the **range of a system parameter (e.g., gain $K$)** for which the closed-loop system remains stable. It is also a quick design-verification tool for control engineers in industries ranging from aerospace to process control. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Characteristic equation | $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ | Real coefficients, $a_0 \neq 0$ | | First row of array ($s^n$) | $a_0, a_2, a_4, \ldots$ | Even-indexed coefficients | | Second row of array ($s^{n-1}$) | $a_1, a_3, a_5, \ldots$ | Odd-indexed coefficients | | General row element | $R_{ij} = \dfrac{R_{i-1,0} \cdot R_{i-2,j+1} - R_{i-2,0} \cdot R_{i-1,j+1}}{R_{i-1,0}}$ | Determinant-based cross-product | | Stability condition (sufficient) | All entries in **1st column** $> 0$ | After normalising to positive leading coeff. | | Necessary condition | All coefficients must be present & same sign | Otherwise, definitely unstable | | RHP root count | $=$ (number of sign changes in 1st column) | KTU board asks this *very* often | | Marginal stability | Entire row of zeros appears | Roots exist on $j\omega$-axis (symmetric) | | Zero-in-first-column fix | Replace zero by small $\varepsilon > 0$ and take $\varepsilon \to 0$ | Only when rest of row is non-zero | --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix ### Worked Example 1 — Direct Stability Check (3rd Order) **Problem:** Determine the stability of a closed-loop system with characteristic equation: $$s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + 4 = 0$$ **Step 1 — Form the Routh Array Header (first two rows):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Row } s^3 &: \quad 1, \quad 3, \quad 0 \\ \text{Row } s^2 &: \quad 2, \quad 4, \quad 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the $s^1$ row:** $$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$ $$b_2 = \frac{(2)(0) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{0}{2} = 0$$ Hence: Row $s^1$ = $1, \; 0$ **Step 3 — Compute the $s^0$ row:** $$c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(0)}{1} = \frac{4 - 0}{1} = 4$$ Hence: Row $s^0$ = $4$ **Step 4 — Inspect the first column:** | Row | First Column | | :---: | :---: | | $s^3$ | $1$ | | $s^2$ | $2$ | | $s^1$ | $1$ | | $s^0$ | $4$ | All four entries are **strictly positive** → **No sign change** → **Zero RHP roots** → **System is STABLE**. --- ### Worked Example 2 — Sign-Change / RHP-Root Counting (4th Order) **Problem:** Investigate the stability of: $$s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$ **Step 1 — Header rows (even & odd coefficients):** $$\text{Row } s^4: \; 1, \; 3, \; 5, \; 0$$ $$\text{Row } s^3: \; 2, \; 4, \; 0, \; 0$$ **Step 2 — $s^2$ row:** $$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = 1$$ $$b_2 = \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$ Hence: Row $s^2$ = $1, \; 5, \; 0$ **Step 3 — $s^1$ row:** $$c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = 4 - 10 = -6$$ $$c_2 = \frac{(1)(0) - (2)(0)}{1} = 0$$ Hence: Row $s^1$ = $-6, \; 0$ **Step 4 — $s^0$ row:** $$d_1 = \frac{(-6)(5) - (1)(0)}{-6} = \frac{-30}{-6} = 5$$ Hence: Row $s^0$ = $5$ **Step 5 — First-Column Analysis:** | Row | First Column | Sign | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $s^4$ | $1$ | $+$ | | $s^3$ | $2$ | $+$ | | $s^2$ | $1$ | $+$ | | $s^1$ | $-6$ | $-$ | | $s^0$ | $5$ | $+$ | **Sign-change count:** $(+ \to -) = 1$ and $(- \to +) = 1$. **Total = 2 sign changes.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The system has **2 roots in the right-half s-plane (RHP)** → the system is **UNSTABLE**. --- ### Worked Example 3 — Range of Parameter $K$ (Most Common KTU Pattern) **Problem:** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is: $$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$ Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. **Step 1 — Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation:** $$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = 0$$ $$s(s+1)(s+2) + K = 0$$ Expand the cubic: $$s(s^2 + 3s + 2) + K = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K = 0$$ **Step 2 — Routh Array:** | Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $s^3$ | $1$ | $2$ | | $s^2$ | $3$ | $K$ | | $s^1$ | $\dfrac{(3)(2) - (1)(K)}{3}$ | $0$ | | $s^0$ | $K$ | | Compute the $s^1$ entry: $$b_1 = \frac{3 \cdot 2 - 1 \cdot K}{3} = \frac{6 - K}{3}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Stability Condition (all first-column entries $> 0$):** $$1 > 0 \quad (\text{always true})$$ $$3 > 0 \quad (\text{always true})$$ $$\frac{6 - K}{3} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 6$$ $$K > 0$$ **Step 4 — Combine the inequalities:** $$\boxed{\,0 < K < 6\,}$$ > [!TIP] > **Boundary behaviour:** > - At $K = 0$: the system has a pole at the origin → **marginally stable**. > - At $K = 6$: the auxiliary equation $3s^2 + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{2}$ → **sustained oscillations** (marginally stable). > - For $K > 6$: the system becomes **unstable**. --- ### Special Case — Zero in the First Column (Auxiliary $\varepsilon$ Method) If a single element of the first column becomes zero (with the rest of that row non-zero), substitute a small positive number $\varepsilon \to 0^+$ in its place and continue construction. The system is unstable if a sign change is introduced as $\varepsilon \to 0$. ### Special Case — Entire Row of Zeros If a complete row of the Routh array is zero, the characteristic equation has **symmetrical roots** about the origin (e.g., $\pm j\omega$ or $\pm \sigma$). The procedure is to form an **auxiliary polynomial** from the row immediately above the zero row, differentiate it with respect to $s$, and use the coefficients of this derivative to replace the zero row. **Marginal stability** is indicated. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Mermaid Diagram 1 — Stability Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Closed-Loop
Characteristic Equation"] --> B{"Step 1: All coefficients
present and same sign?"} B -- "NO" --> C["UNSTABLE
(Necessary condition
violated)"] B -- "YES" --> D["Step 2: Construct
Routh Array"] D --> E{"Step 3: Check for
special cases"} E -- "Entire row of zeros" --> F["Use Auxiliary
Polynomial Method"] E -- "Single zero in col 1" --> G["Replace by epsilon,
take limit e -> 0"] E -- "No special case" --> H["Step 4: Inspect
1st Column"] F --> H G --> H H --> I{"All 1st column
entries > 0?"} I -- "YES" --> J["STABLE
0 RHP roots"] I -- "NO" --> K["UNSTABLE
Count sign changes
= RHP roots"] ``` ### Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture for Stability Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Reference
Input R(s)"] --> S1((+)) S1 --> C["Controller
Gc(s)"] C --> P["Plant
Gp(s)"] P --> H["Sensor / Feedback
H(s) = 1"] H --> S2((-)) S2 --> S1 P --> OUT["Output
Y(s)"] CE["Characteristic
Equation
1 + Gc Gp H = 0"] -.-> RARR["Routh Array
Construction"] RARR --> DEC["Stability
Decision
Block"] DEC --> R1["Case 1:
All +ve ->
STABLE"] DEC --> R2["Case 2:
Sign changes ->
UNSTABLE"] DEC --> R3["Case 3:
j-omega roots ->
MARGINALLY STABLE"] ``` ### Mermaid Diagram 3 — Routh Array Cross-Multiplication Pattern ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["Row s^n:
a0 a2 a4"] --> B1 A2["Row s^(n-1):
a1 a3 a5"] --> B1 B1["Compute:
b1 = (a1*a2 - a0*a3)/a1"] --> C1 B2["b2 = (a1*a4 - a0*a5)/a1"] --> C1 B3["b3 = (a1*a6 - a0*a7)/a1"] --> C1 C1["Row s^(n-2):
b1 b2 b3"] --> D1 A2 --> D1 D1["Compute:
c1 = (b1*a3 - a1*b2)/b1"] --> E1 D2["c2 = (b1*a5 - a1*b3)/b1"] --> E1 E1["Row s^(n-3):
c1 c2 c3"] --> F1["Continue until
s^0 row is reached"] F1 --> G1["Inspect
First Column"] G1 --> H1{"All elements
strictly positive?"} H1 -- "YES" --> I1["SYSTEM
IS STABLE"] H1 -- "NO" --> J1["Count sign changes
= RHP roots
SYSTEM UNSTABLE"] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What are the necessary conditions for stability as per this criterion?* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion is an algebraic procedure that determines whether all the roots of the characteristic equation of an LTI system lie in the left-half of the s-plane, without actually solving for the roots. > The **necessary conditions** are: > 1. The characteristic equation must have all real coefficients. > 2. All coefficients of the characteristic equation $a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ must be **present** (no missing terms) and must have the **same sign** (either all positive or all negative). > 3. If any coefficient is zero or of opposite sign, the system is definitely unstable or at best marginally stable. These are necessary but not sufficient conditions; sufficiency is confirmed by the Routh array. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the significance of the first column of the Routh array? How are the number of roots in the right-half s-plane determined from it?* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The first column of the Routh array is the **only column that needs to be inspected** to determine absolute stability. It is significant because: > 1. If **all entries** in the first column have the **same sign** (preferably all positive), then the system is **absolutely stable**, i.e., no characteristic roots exist in the right-half s-plane. > 2. The **number of sign changes** encountered while traversing the first column from top to bottom is **exactly equal to the number of roots lying in the right-half of the s-plane (RHP roots)**. For instance, two sign changes mean two unstable roots. --- ### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** > - Students often **forget to write the characteristic equation** in descending powers of $s$ — this is the single most common reason for an entire-row shift that leads to wrong answers. *(−2 marks)* > - The **auxiliary equation** is required when an *entire row* is zero, **NOT** when a single element is zero — confusing these is a frequent error. *(−1 to −2 marks)* > - When asked for "the range of $K$", always state the boundary cases $K = K_{critical}$ explicitly and comment on the **type of motion** (sustained oscillation, marginal stability). *(−1 mark if omitted)* --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — *"Stability Determination & Parameter Range"* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback control system is given by: $$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+5)}$$ **(a)** *(7 marks)* Form the Routh array for the closed-loop characteristic equation and determine the range of $K$ for which the system is stable. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Find the value of $K$ that will cause sustained oscillations in the system, and determine the corresponding oscillation frequency. **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** The closed-loop characteristic equation is: $$1 + G(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+5)} = 0$$ $$s(s+4)(s+5) + K = 0$$ Expand: $$s(s^2 + 9s + 20) + K = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^3 + 9s^2 + 20s + K = 0$$ Routh array: $$\begin{array}{c|cc} s^3 & 1 & 20 \\ s^2 & 9 & K \\ s^1 & \dfrac{9 \cdot 20 - 1 \cdot K}{9} & 0 \\ s^0 & K & \\ \end{array}$$ Simplify the $s^1$ entry: $$b_1 = \frac{180 - K}{9}$$ For stability, **every** element in the first column must be positive: $$1 > 0 \quad \text{(satisfied)}$$ $$9 > 0 \quad \text{(satisfied)}$$ $$\frac{180 - K}{9} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 180$$ $$K > 0$$ $$\boxed{\text{Stability range: } 0 < K < 180}$$ **Part (b):** Sustained oscillations occur at the **boundary of stability**, i.e., when the $s^1$ row entry becomes zero (auxiliary equation of the $s^2$ row has purely imaginary roots). Set $b_1 = 0$: $$\frac{180 - K}{9} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K = 180$$ The **auxiliary equation** is formed from the row immediately above (the $s^2$ row): $$A(s) = 9s^2 + K = 9s^2 + 180 = 0$$ $$s^2 = -20 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j\sqrt{20} = \pm j 2\sqrt{5}$$ The oscillation frequency is: $$\omega_n = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{K_{\text{crit}} = 180, \quad \omega_{\text{osc}} = 2\sqrt{5} \text{ rad/s}}$$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *"Sign-Change Counting & RHP Roots"* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* The characteristic equation of a closed-loop system is: $$s^5 + 2s^4 + 3s^3 + 6s^2 + 2s + 1 = 0$$ Determine the number of roots that lie in the right-half s-plane, the left-half s-plane, and on the imaginary axis. Is the system stable? **(b)** *(7 marks)* For the system in part (a), if the characteristic equation is modified to: $$s^5 + 3s^4 + 5s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s + 2 = 0$$ verify whether the modification improves stability or not. **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** Form the Routh array. $$\begin{array}{c|ccc} s^5 & 1 & 3 & 2 \\ s^4 & 2 & 6 & 1 \\ \end{array}$$ Compute the $s^3$ row: $$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(6)}{2} = \frac{6 - 6}{2} = 0$$ Since $b_1 = 0$ and the rest of the row is non-zero, **replace 0 by $\varepsilon \to 0^+$** and continue. $$b_2 = \frac{(2)(2) - (1)(1)}{2} = \frac{4 - 1}{2} = \frac{3}{2}$$ Row $s^3$ becomes: $\varepsilon, \; 3/2$ Now compute the $s^2$ row: $$c_1 = \frac{(\varepsilon)(6) - (2)(3/2)}{\varepsilon} = \frac{6\varepsilon - 3}{\varepsilon} = 6 - \frac{3}{\varepsilon}$$ As $\varepsilon \to 0^+$, $c_1 \to -\infty$ (large negative). $$c_2 = \frac{(\varepsilon)(1) - (2)(0)}{\varepsilon} = 1$$ Row $s^2$: $6 - 3/\varepsilon, \; 1$ Compute the $s^1$ row: $$d_1 = \frac{(6 - 3/\varepsilon)(3/2) - (\varepsilon)(1)}{(6 - 3/\varepsilon)}$$ As $\varepsilon \to 0^+$, $d_1 \to 3/2$ (positive). Compute the $s^0$ row: $$e_1 = \frac{d_1 \cdot 1 - (6 - 3/\varepsilon) \cdot 0}{d_1} = 1$$ Row $s^0$: $1$ **First column (limit as $\varepsilon \to 0^+$):** $$\begin{aligned} s^5 &: \quad +1 \\ s^4 &: \quad +2 \\ s^3 &: \quad +\varepsilon \to 0^+ \\ s^2 &: \quad -(3/\varepsilon) \to -\infty \\ s^1 &: \quad +3/2 \\ s^0 &: \quad +1 \end{aligned}$$ **Sign changes:** $(+ \to +)$ none, $(+ \to +)$ none, $(+ \to -)$ one, $( - \to +)$ two, $(+ \to +)$ none. **Total = 2 sign changes** → **2 roots in the RHP**. Since the polynomial is of order 5, the remaining 3 roots are in the LHP (or on the $j\omega$-axis). Substituting $s = j\omega$ into the characteristic equation and separating real and imaginary parts: Real: $\omega^4 - 6\omega^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \omega^2 = 3 \pm 2\sqrt{2}$ Imag: $2\omega^4 - 3\omega = 0 \Rightarrow \omega(\omega^2)(2\omega) - 3\omega$... *(formal check shows no common root)* → **no roots on the $j\omega$-axis**. Hence: **2 roots in RHP, 3 roots in LHP, 0 on $j\omega$-axis** → **System is UNSTABLE**. **Part (b):** Modified equation: $s^5 + 3s^4 + 5s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s + 2 = 0$ Routh array: $$\begin{array}{c|ccc} s^5 & 1 & 5 & 4 \\ s^4 & 3 & 6 & 2 \\ \end{array}$$ $s^3$ row: $$b_1 = \frac{(3)(5) - (1)(6)}{3} = \frac{15 - 6}{3} = 3$$ $$b_2 = \frac{(3)(4) - (1)(2)}{3} = \frac{12 - 2}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$$ $s^2$ row: $$c_1 = \frac{(3)(6) - (3)(10/3)}{3} = \frac{18 - 10}{3} = \frac{8}{3}$$ $$c_2 = \frac{(3)(2) - (3)(0)}{3} = 2$$ $s^1$ row: $$d_1 = \frac{(8/3)(10/3) - (3)(2)}{8/3} = \frac{80/9 - 6}{8/3} = \frac{80/9 - 54/9}{8/3} = \frac{26/9}{8/3} = \frac{26}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{8} = \frac{26}{24} = \frac{13}{12}$$ $s^0$ row: $$e_1 = \frac{(13/12)(2) - (8/3)(0)}{13/12} = 2$$ **First column:** $1, \; 3, \; 3, \; 8/3, \; 13/12, \; 2$ — **all positive** → **Zero sign changes** → **Zero RHP roots**. **Conclusion:** The modification **improves stability** — the system is now **completely stable** with all 5 roots in the LHP. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **One-Page Rapid Revision — Print This Before the Exam!** - ✅ **Routh's criterion** is a *purely algebraic* method; it does **not** require root-finding. - ✅ Always write the characteristic equation in the form $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ with $a_0 > 0$. - ✅ **Necessary condition:** all coefficients must be present, real, and have the **same sign**. - ✅ **Sufficient condition:** all entries in the **first column** of the Routh array are **strictly positive**. - ✅ **Number of sign changes in the first column = number of roots in the RHP (right-half plane)**. - ✅ **First two rows** of the Routh array are simply the even-indexed and odd-indexed coefficients respectively. - ✅ **Subsequent rows** are computed by the cross-multiplication determinant formula — always use the *first element of the row above* as the denominator. - ✅ **Special case 1 — Single zero in 1st column:** substitute $\varepsilon \to 0^+$ in place of zero and continue. - ✅ **Special case 2 — Entire row of zeros:** form the *auxiliary polynomial* from the row above, differentiate w.r.t. $s$, and use its coefficients to replace the zero row. This indicates **symmetric roots about the origin** (marginal stability). - ✅ **$K$-range problems** are the most common KTU question pattern — always state boundary values and the oscillation frequency at the critical gain. - ✅ **Auxiliary equation at the boundary of stability** gives the frequency of sustained oscillations: solve $A(s) = 0$ for $s$ to obtain $s = \pm j\omega$. - ✅ Routh's criterion works for polynomials with **real coefficients only** (most LTI physical systems). - ✅ Routh's criterion is a **necessary and sufficient** test for the *absolute stability* of systems described by linear constant-coefficient ODEs. - ✅ For KTU's 14-mark questions, always show the **full Routh array** in tabulated form — this alone earns 2–3 marks even if the rest is incomplete. - ✅ **Common pitfall:** confusing "sign change" with "sign difference between two arbitrary rows" — only the **first column** matters. ---

topics.title: The Root Locus Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Root Locus Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Classical Control Engineering*, the **Root Locus** is defined as the graphical trajectory (path) traced by the **closed-loop poles** of a feedback control system in the **complex s-plane** as a specific system parameter — most commonly the **open-loop gain K** — is varied over a continuous range from **0 → +∞**.

Mathematically, for a unity negative feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$, the root locus consists of all points in the s-plane that satisfy the **characteristic equation**:

$$1 + K\,G(s)H(s) = 0$$

The concept was introduced by **Walter R. Evans in 1948** as a systematic graphical method for analyzing and designing linear control systems. It is the most frequently tested topic in KTU Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim recall):**
> *"The root locus is the locus of all possible locations of the closed-loop poles (roots of the characteristic equation) as the gain K varies from zero to infinity, under the constraint that the angle of $KG(s)H(s)$ is an odd multiple of 180°."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship Captain's Rudder" Intuition

Imagine you are steering a giant ship across the ocean. The ship has a **destination** (desired closed-loop response: stable, fast, no oscillation). However, the ship's actual **course** depends on:

- **Engine power (Gain K)**: How hard the propellers push.
- **Ship design (Poles of $G(s)H(s)$)**: The natural tendencies of the hull.

As you increase engine power **K** from 0 (engine off) to very high values (full throttle), the ship's actual course curves and bends across the ocean. If you could plot every single position of the ship on a chart for every engine setting, the resulting curved path is the **Root Locus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> - At **K = 0**, the closed-loop poles start exactly at the **open-loop poles** of $G(s)H(s)$.
> - At **K = ∞**, the closed-loop poles end at the **zeros** of $G(s)H(s)$ (or escape to infinity along asymptotes).
> - In between, they trace a smooth continuous curve — this curve **IS** the root locus.

---

## 1.3 Why Root Locus Matters in KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Significance in Control Engineering |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Stability Analysis** | Instantly shows whether poles cross into the **RHP** (unstable region). |
| **Transient Response** | Pole locations directly dictate **damping ratio ($\zeta$)** and **natural frequency ($\omega_n$)**. |
| **Gain Design** | Find the exact **range of K** for stability without solving polynomials repeatedly. |
| **Compensator Design** | Lead/Lag compensators are designed by reshaping the root locus. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Sketch the root locus for..."* or *"Find the range of K for stability"*, you are expected to follow the **9-step Evans' Procedure** rigorously. Marks are awarded for each rule correctly applied.

---

## 1.4 Pre-Requisite Concepts (Must Know)

Before attempting root locus problems, the student must be fluent in:

1. **Poles and Zeros** of $G(s)H(s)$ in the s-plane.
2. **Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)** form $K \cdot \dfrac{N(s)}{D(s)}$.
3. **Characteristic Equation**: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.
4. **Basic Complex Number Geometry**: angles and magnitudes in polar form.
5. **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** (to find marginal stability / imaginary axis crossing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Conditions of Root Locus

The characteristic equation $1 + K G(s)H(s) = 0$ can be rewritten as:

$$K G(s) H(s) = -1$$

A complex number equals $-1$ only if it satisfies **both** magnitude and angle criteria simultaneously.

### Condition 1 — Magnitude Condition

$$\boxed{\;\vert K\, G(s) H(s) \vert = 1\;}$$

Since **K is real and positive**, this reduces to:

$$K = \frac{1}{\vert G(s) H(s) \vert} \quad \text{for } K \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The magnitude condition is used **after** sketching the locus to compute the value of K at any specific point on the locus.

### Condition 2 — Angle Condition

$$\boxed{\;\angle G(s) H(s) = \pm 180°\,(2q + 1), \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\;}$$

Equivalently: $\angle G(s)H(s) = 180°, 540°, -180°, \ldots$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Angle Condition is the SOLE criterion for a point to lie on the root locus.** The magnitude condition is used only to compute the K value once a point is known to be on the locus.

### Why only these conditions?

- **Magnitude = 1** ensures the magnitude of $KG(s)H(s)$ exactly cancels $+1$ (in vector form, $-1$ has unit magnitude).
- **Angle = ±180°** ensures the vector $KG(s)H(s)$ points exactly opposite to the positive real axis, summing to zero with $+1$.

---

## 2.2 Symmetry Property

Because all coefficients of the characteristic polynomial (with **real** coefficients) are real, **complex roots must occur in conjugate pairs**. Therefore:

> The root locus is always **symmetric about the real axis** of the s-plane.

This single fact saves enormous time in KTU exams — you only need to sketch the upper half; the lower half is its mirror image.

---

## 2.3 The 9 Rules of Evans' Procedure (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Rule | Formula / Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **Locate Open-Loop Poles & Zeros** on the s-plane using $\times$ (poles) and $\bigcirc$ (zeros). | Factorize $D(s)$ and $N(s)$. |
| **2** | **Number of Branches** = Number of open-loop poles ($n$) = number of root locus branches. | Each pole traces one branch as K → ∞. |
| **3** | **Real Axis Segments**: A point on the real axis lies on the root locus if the total number of real poles + zeros to its **right** is **odd**. | Count finite poles/zeros only. |
| **4** | **Start ($K=0$) and End ($K=\infty$) points**: Branches start at open-loop **poles** and end at open-loop **zeros** (or $\infty$). | When $n > m$ (more poles than zeros), $n-m$ branches go to infinity. |
| **5** | **Asymptote Angles**: $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1)180°}{n-m}$, for $q = 0, 1, \ldots, (n-m-1)$. | There are $n - m$ asymptotes. |
| **6** | **Centroid (Asymptote Intersection on Real Axis)**: $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros}}{n - m}$. | Both finite AND infinite poles/zeros considered. |
| **7** | **Breakaway / Break-in Points**: Roots of $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$, equivalently where multiple roots occur. | Valid points must lie on root locus. |
| **8** | **Imaginary Axis Crossing**: Find K at which locus crosses $j\omega$-axis using **Routh-Hurwitz**. | The auxiliary equation from Routh array gives $\omega$ crossing frequency. |
| **9** | **Angle of Departure from Complex Pole**: $\phi_d = 180° + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$ where angles are measured **from** all other poles and zeros **to** the pole in question. | Similarly for **Angle of Arrival** at complex zeros. |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Root Locus

In modern control engineering practice, root locus is the **bridge between time-domain specifications and frequency-domain design**:

- **Stabilizing a Drone**: Determine maximum PID gain before oscillations grow.
- **Cruise Control Design**: Adjust forward-path gain for desired settling time.
- **Robotic Arm**: Position controller gains to avoid overshoot.
- **Power System Stabilizers**: Tune excitation gain to damp electromechanical oscillations.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"What is the maximum value of K for the system to remain stable?"* — Use **Rule 8 (Routh-Hurwitz)** for this.

---

## 2.5 Relationship Between K, $\zeta$, and $\omega_n$

Once the root locus is drawn, for any point on it:
- The **damping ratio** $\zeta$ corresponds to a constant line from origin: $\cos\theta = \zeta$, where $\theta$ is the angle of the point from the negative real axis.
- The **natural frequency** $\omega_n = \vert s \vert$ (radial distance from origin).
- $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$ at that point.

This is the foundation of **Root Locus-Based Controller Design** taught later in the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Angle Condition

**Starting point:** The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + K G(s) H(s) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K G(s) H(s) = -1$$

The complex number $-1$ in polar form is:

$$-1 = 1 \cdot e^{j(\pi + 2\pi q)}, \quad q \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Since $K$ is real and positive, the angle contributed by $K$ is **zero**. Therefore:

$$\angle G(s) H(s) = \pi + 2\pi q \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\angle G(s) H(s) = \pm 180°\,(2q + 1)$$

For the principal root locus, we use odd multiples of $180°$: $\pm 180°, \pm 540°, \pm 900°, \ldots$

> **Physical Interpretation:** A point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ lies on the root locus if, when you draw vectors from every open-loop pole and zero to that point, the sum of the angles from the zeros minus the sum of the angles from the poles equals an **odd multiple of $180°$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Asymptote Angles

Consider the OLTF in pole-zero form:

$$G(s) H(s) = \frac{K \prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(s - p_j)}, \quad n > m$$

For very large $s$ (i.e., as we approach $\infty$), each factor $(s - p_j) \approx s$ and $(s - z_i) \approx s$. The transfer function asymptotically behaves as:

$$G(s) H(s) \approx \frac{K}{s^{n-m}}$$

Applying the **Angle Condition** at infinity:

$$\angle G(s) H(s) = -(n - m) \cdot \angle(s) = \pm 180°(2q + 1)$$

For points at infinity, the argument of $s$ is the asymptote angle $\phi_A$:

$$\boxed{\;\phi_A = \frac{\pm 180°(2q + 1)}{n - m}\;}$$

Since there are $(n-m)$ asymptotes, we take $q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, (n-m-1)$.

**Example:** For $n - m = 3$, the three asymptote angles are:
- $q=0$: $\phi_A = +60°$
- $q=1$: $\phi_A = +180°$
- $q=2$: $\phi_A = +300°$ (or $-60°$)

These three lines are spaced at $60°$ apart — confirming the **$120°$ symmetry** for $(n-m) = 3$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Centroid $\sigma_A$

The asymptotic approximation yields:

$$G(s) H(s) \approx \frac{K}{s^{n-m}} = \frac{K}{(s - \sigma_A)^{n-m}}$$

using the centroid as the pole of the asymptotic equivalent. Expanding both sides around $s \to \infty$ and equating coefficients of $s^{n-m-1}$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j - \sum_{i=1}^{m} z_i = (n - m)\sigma_A$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_A = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j - \sum_{i=1}^{m} z_i}{n - m}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** The pole sum is **negative** of the coefficient of $s^{n-1}$ in $D(s)$. So if $D(s) = s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \ldots$, then $\sum p_j = -a_{n-1}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Breakaway Point Condition

The closed-loop characteristic equation rearranged as:

$$1 + K \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = -\frac{D(s)}{N(s)}$$

A **breakaway point** is where two root locus branches meet on the real axis (or complex path). Mathematically, it is a point where the characteristic equation has a **double root**. This occurs when:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = 0$$

Computing the derivative:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -\frac{D'(s) N(s) - D(s) N'(s)}{[N(s)]^2} = 0$$

Hence the breakaway condition:

$$\boxed{\;D'(s) N(s) - D(s) N'(s) = 0\;}$$

Or equivalently, using the implicit function form:

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{D(s)}{N(s)}\right] = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad D'(s) N(s) - D(s) N'(s) = 0$$

The real roots of this equation that lie **on the root locus segments** of the real axis are the valid breakaway/break-in points.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Complete Root Locus Construction

**Problem:** Sketch the root locus for the unity feedback system with:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}, \quad H(s) = 1$$

Find (a) the range of K for stability, (b) the value of K at the breakaway point, and (c) the frequency of oscillation at marginal stability.

---

### Step 1 — Open-Loop Poles & Zeros

Poles: $p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = -2, \; p_3 = -4 \quad \Rightarrow n = 3$
Zeros: None $\Rightarrow m = 0$

Number of branches = **3**.

### Step 2 — Real Axis Segments

Apply Rule 3. Count real poles + zeros to the right of test points:

| Test Point | Poles/Zeros to Right | Count | On Locus? |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $s = -1$ | $-2, -4$ | 2 | No (even) |
| $s = -3$ | $-4$ | 1 | **Yes (odd)** |
| $s = -5$ | None | 0 | No |

Real axis segments: $[-2, 0]$ and $[-\infty, -4]$.

### Step 3 — Asymptotes

Number of asymptotes = $n - m = 3 - 0 = 3$.

Angles:
$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2$$

- $q = 0$: $\phi_A = 60°$
- $q = 1$: $\phi_A = 180°$
- $q = 2$: $\phi_A = 300°$ (or $-60°$)

Centroid:
$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0 + (-2) + (-4)) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

So three asymptotes emanate from $\sigma_A = -2$ at $60°, 180°, -60°$.

### Step 4 — Breakaway Point

Solve $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$:

Characteristic equation: $1 + \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0 \Rightarrow K = -s(s+2)(s+4)$

$$K = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s)$$

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 12s + 8) = 0$$

$$3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0$$

$$s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6}$$

$$s = \frac{-12 \pm 6.928}{6}$$

So $s_1 = -0.845$ and $s_2 = -3.155$.

**Validity Check:** The real axis segment with locus is $[-2, 0]$, so $s_1 = -0.845$ lies in this segment → **Valid breakaway point**.

The point $s_2 = -3.155$ lies between $-4$ and $-2$, **not** on a locus segment → Reject.

### Step 5 — Imaginary Axis Crossing (Routh-Hurwitz)

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$

Routh Array:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{48 - K}{6} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0
\end{array}
$$

For marginal stability (all first column entries $\geq 0$):

$$K \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{48 - K}{6} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K \leq 48$$

**Range of K for stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 48}$

At $K = 48$ (marginal), the auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row:

$$6s^2 + 48 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 = -8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$$

**Frequency of oscillation at marginal stability:** $\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$ rad/s.

### Step 6 — Sketch Summary

The complete root locus consists of:

- A branch from $p_1 = 0$ moving **leftward** along the real axis, breaking away at $s = -0.845$.
- A branch from $p_2 = -2$ moving **rightward**, meeting the first branch at $-0.845$ (breakaway), then both branches curve into the complex plane and cross the imaginary axis at $\pm j 2\sqrt{2}$ when $K = 48$.
- A branch from $p_3 = -4$ moves **rightward** on $[-\infty, -4]$ and joins the asymptotes.
- All three branches escape to infinity along the $60°, 180°, -60°$ asymptotes centered at $\sigma_A = -2$.

---

### Step 7 — Value of K at Breakaway Point

At $s = -0.845$:
$$K = -(-0.845)((-0.845) + 2)((-0.845) + 4)$$
$$K = -(-0.845)(1.155)(3.155)$$
$$K \approx 3.08$$

**Value of K at breakaway:** $K \approx 3.08$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Root Locus in a Closed-Loop System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **role of root locus** within a unity-feedback control loop and how each rule maps to a specific system parameter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["Reference Input R s"] --> nodeSum["Summing Junction"]
    nodeSum --> nodeF["Forward Path G s"]
    nodeF --> nodePlant["Plant with Gain K"]
    nodePlant --> nodeC["Output C s"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Feedback Path H s equals 1"]
    nodeH --> nodeSum
    nodeRL["Root Locus Analysis Sweep K from 0 to infinity"] -.-> nodeF
    nodeCE["Characteristic Equation 1 plus K G s H s equals 0"] -.-> nodeRL
    nodeMag["Magnitude Condition K equals 1 over G s H s"] -.-> nodeRL
    nodeAng["Angle Condition Angle G s H s equals plus minus 180 deg"] -.-> nodeRL
    nodeStab["Stability Range of K Identified"] -.-> nodeRL
    nodeStab --> nodeResp["Transient Response Damping and Frequency"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — The 9-Step Evans' Procedure

This is the **exact sequence** a student must follow to construct a root locus — KTU examiners reward methodical steps.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1 Identify Open Loop Poles and Zeros"] --> stepB["Step 2 Determine Number of Branches equals n"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3 Mark Real Axis Locus Segments Odd Count to Right"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 4 Plot Start K equals 0 and End K equals infinity Points"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5 Calculate Asymptote Angles Phi A equals 180 times 2q plus 1 over n minus m"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6 Find Centroid Sigma A"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 7 Solve Breakaway Equation dK by ds equals 0"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 8 Find Imaginary Axis Crossing via Routh Hurwitz"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 9 Compute Angle of Departure and Arrival for Complex Poles Zeros"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Step 10 Sketch Final Root Locus with Symmetry About Real Axis"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sketch — Annotated Root Locus (Upper Half)

The diagram below shows the **qualitative shape** of a typical root locus with three poles and no zeros (as in our worked example).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisY["Imaginary Axis j omega"] --- axisX["Real Axis sigma"]
    p1["Pole at 0"] --- seg1["Real axis segment from minus 2 to 0"]
    p2["Pole at minus 2"] --- seg1
    p3["Pole at minus 4"] --- seg2["Real axis segment from minus infinity to minus 4"]
    brk["Breakaway at s equals minus 0.845"] --- seg1
    cen["Centroid Sigma A equals minus 2"]
    asy1["Asymptote at 60 degrees"] --- cen
    asy2["Asymptote at 180 degrees"] --- cen
    asy3["Asymptote at minus 60 degrees"] --- cen
    img["Imaginary crossing at plus minus j 2 sqrt 2 when K equals 48"] --- axisY
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Angle of Departure from a Complex Pole

For a system with complex-conjugate poles $p_{1,2} = \alpha \pm j\beta$, the angle at which the root locus **leaves** the complex pole is computed using vectors from all other poles/zeros to this pole.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    otherP["Other Open Loop Poles and Zeros"] -- vector angles phi p --> cp["Complex Pole p1 equals alpha plus j beta"]
    otherP2["Open Loop Zeros"] -- vector angles phi z --> cp
    cp -- "Angle of Departure phi d equals 180 plus sum phi z minus sum phi p" --> depart["Direction of root locus branch leaving p1"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define root locus. State the angle and magnitude conditions. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The root locus is the path traced by the closed-loop poles in the s-plane as the loop gain K is varied from **0 to ∞**.

- **Angle Condition:** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$, where $q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- **Magnitude Condition:** $\vert K G(s) H(s) \vert = 1$

The angle condition determines whether a point lies on the root locus, while the magnitude condition is used to compute K at that point.

---

### Question 2: What are asymptotes? Why are they needed in root locus sketching? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Asymptotes are straight lines along which the root locus branches approach infinity as K → ∞. They are needed because:

1. With $n > m$, there are $n - m$ branches that escape to infinity.
2. Asymptotes provide the **direction** of these escaping branches.
3. They are determined by the **centroid** $\sigma_A$ and **angles** $\phi_A$, computed as:

$$\phi_A = \frac{\pm 180°(2q+1)}{n-m}, \quad \sigma_A = \frac{\sum p_j - \sum z_i}{n - m}$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Sketch the complete root locus for the system

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

Find (a) the range of K for stability, (b) the frequency of marginal stability, (c) the breakaway point and corresponding K.

---

#### Part (a) — Real Axis Segments, Asymptotes & Centroid `[7 Marks]`

**Poles:** $p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = -1, \; p_3 = -3$ (so $n = 3$)
**Zeros:** None (so $m = 0$)

**Real Axis Segments** (Rule 3): Odd number of finite poles to the right.
- Test $s = -0.5$: Poles $-1, -3$ to the right $\Rightarrow$ count = 2 (even) → NOT on locus.
- Test $s = -2$: Only pole $-3$ to the right $\Rightarrow$ count = 1 (odd) → **On locus**.
- Test $s = -4$: No poles to right → NOT on locus.

**Locus segments:** $[-1, 0]$ and $[-\infty, -3]$.

**Asymptote Angles:**
$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3} \Rightarrow 60°, \; 180°, \; -60° \; (300°)$$

**Centroid:**
$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0 - 1 - 3) - 0}{3 - 0} = \frac{-4}{3} \approx -1.333$$

---

#### Part (b) — Breakaway Point `[4 Marks]`

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s + K = 0$
$$K = -(s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s)$$
$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 8s + 3) = 0$$

Solving: $s = \dfrac{-8 \pm \sqrt{64 - 36}}{6} = \dfrac{-8 \pm \sqrt{28}}{6}$

$$s_1 = -0.451, \quad s_2 = -2.215$$

**Validity:** $s_1 = -0.451$ lies in $[-1, 0]$ → **Valid breakaway**.
$s_2 = -2.215$ lies in $[-3, -1]$, **not on locus** → Reject.

**K at breakaway:**
$$K = -((-0.451)^3 + 4(-0.451)^2 + 3(-0.451))$$
$$K = -(-0.0917 + 0.8136 - 1.353) = -(-0.631) \approx 0.631$$

---

#### Part (c) — Range of K and Marginal Frequency `[3 Marks]`

Routh Array for $s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s + K = 0$:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 3 \\
s^2 & 4 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{12 - K}{4} & 0 \\
s^0 & K &
\end{array}
$$

For stability: $K > 0$ and $\dfrac{12 - K}{4} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 12$.

**Range of K for stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 12}$

**Auxiliary equation** at $K = 12$: $4s^2 + 12 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -3 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{3}$

**Marginal frequency:** $\omega = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ rad/s.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): For the system

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

(a) Sketch the root locus and comment on stability. (b) Find the angle of departure from the complex poles if any.

*Note: This system has a real zero at $s = -2$ which lies between poles, hence the root locus has only one breakaway segment. The detailed construction follows the same 9 steps with $m = 1$ modifying the asymptote count to $n - m = 2$.*

The two asymptote angles are $\phi_A = \pm 90°$ and centroid $\sigma_A = \dfrac{(0 - 1 - 3) - (-2)}{2} = \dfrac{-2}{2} = -1$.

Since no complex poles exist, the angle of departure question is not applicable.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Centroid Formula:** Students often forget that pole sums are **negative** of the polynomial coefficients. If $D(s) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K$, the sum of poles is $-6$, NOT $+6$.
>
> 2. **Asymptote Angle Calculation:** Using even multiples of $180°$ instead of odd multiples. The correct formula has the $(2q+1)$ term — never forget this.
>
> 3. **Breakaway Point Validity:** Solving $dK/ds = 0$ gives candidate points, but **only those lying on real-axis locus segments are valid**. Always state the validity check.
>
> 4. **Routh Array Boundary Value:** Marginal K value is the value that **makes the first column zero**, not just any value less than the limit.
>
> 5. **Confusing Breakaway with Break-in:** Breakaway = locus **leaves** the real axis. Break-in = locus **returns** to the real axis. Check whether K increases or decreases at the point.
>
> 6. **Magnitude Condition Use:** NEVER use the magnitude condition to find points on the locus. Use it **only after** confirming a point lies on the locus (via the angle condition) to compute K.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Root locus** = trajectory of closed-loop poles as K varies from 0 to ∞.
- **Angle condition** decides locus membership; **magnitude condition** gives K value.
- The locus is **always symmetric** about the real axis.
- Branches **start** at open-loop poles ($K = 0$) and **end** at open-loop zeros or **infinity** ($K = \infty$).
- Number of branches going to infinity = $(n - m)$.
- **Asymptote angles:** $\phi_A = \pm 180°(2q+1)/(n-m)$.
- **Centroid:** $\sigma_A = (\sum p_j - \sum z_i)/(n-m)$.
- **Real axis segments** = where total real poles + zeros to the right is **odd**.
- **Breakaway points** = roots of $dK/ds = 0$ that lie on locus segments.
- **Marginal stability** = found via Routh-Hurwitz; locus crosses imaginary axis at this K.
- **Angle of departure** from a complex pole = $180° + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$.
- The procedure is **systematic** — always follow the 9-step Evans' rule order.
- Use **graph paper** with proper scaling; label all critical points, asymptotes, and the imaginary crossing frequency.

---

### 📘 Quick-Reference Summary Card

| Concept | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| Angle Condition | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$ |
| Magnitude Condition | $K = 1/\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ |
| Asymptote Angles | $\phi_A = (2q+1)180°/(n-m)$ |
| Centroid | $\sigma_A = (\sum p_j - \sum z_i)/(n-m)$ |
| Breakaway Condition | $dK/ds = 0$ |
| Marginal Stability | First column of Routh array = 0 |
| Angle of Departure | $180° + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$ |
| Symmetry | About real axis (always) |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evan's Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evan's Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evan's Criteria (Root Locus Method):** A graphical technique developed by **Walter R. Evans (1948)** for locating the trajectories (loci) of the closed-loop poles of a feedback control system in the **s-plane** when a single system parameter (typically the open-loop gain **K**) is varied from **0 to +∞**. It provides a complete stability map of the system without explicitly solving the characteristic equation for each value of K.

The **characteristic equation** governing the closed-loop dynamics is:

$$1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$$

which can be equivalently written as the **vector equation**:

$$KG(s)H(s) = -1$$

This single vector equation splits into two fundamental scalar conditions that form the foundation of Evan's criteria:

1. **Angle Condition** → $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1), \quad q = 0,1,2,\dots$
2. **Magnitude Condition** → $\vert KG(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$

> [!NOTE]
> The **angle condition alone is sufficient to plot the root locus**. The magnitude condition is used *afterwards* to find the exact value of K at any point on the locus.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car on a winding mountain road** at night with your headlights illuminating only a small patch of the road ahead. You cannot see the entire path, but you know the road's general direction (rules of the road), and you feel the steering wheel pulling as you approach each bend.

**The root locus is exactly this road**, and the **headlights are the angle condition**:

- You don't need to know K (the speed) to know the road's path.
- The **path is determined by the geometry** of the open-loop poles and zeros (the road's bends and turns).
- The **steering wheel feedback** (whether the curve bends left or right) is governed by how many poles/zeros lie on the real axis to the right of the test point.
- When you reach a particular point on the road, the **speedometer (K)** can be calculated from the magnitude condition.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important in KTU exams?** Almost every Classical Control problem (stability, transient response, design of compensators) starts with sketching a root locus. Mastery of Evan's criteria = mastery of Module 3.

---

## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

Evan's criteria are foundational in:

| Engineering Domain | Application of Root Locus |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Designing autopilot gain schedules for aircraft pitch/yaw control |
| **Robotics** | Tuning PID gains for manipulator joint servos |
| **Process Control** | Determining safe proportional gain ranges in chemical reactors |
| **Power Systems** | Analyzing damping of electromechanical oscillations in grids |
| **Automotive** | Cruise control and active suspension tuning |
| **Servo Drives** | Position control loop design in CNC machines |

---

## 1.4 Key Symbol Conventions (KTU Board Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **P** | Number of finite open-loop **poles** of $G(s)H(s)$ |
| **Z** | Number of finite open-loop **zeros** of $G(s)H(s)$ |
| **K** | Open-loop gain variable (0 → +∞) |
| **σₐ** | Centroid (asymptote intersection) on real axis |
| **θ** | Angle of asymptote(s) |
| **φd** | Angle of departure from a complex pole |
| **φa** | Angle of arrival at a complex zero |
| **jb** | Breakaway / break-in point on real axis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

Starting from the closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

The **poles of T(s)** are the roots of the characteristic polynomial:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G(s)H(s) = -1$$

A complex number $-1$ has:
- **Magnitude** = 1
- **Angle** = $\pm 180°(2q+1)$ for $q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

A trial point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ lies on the **root locus** if and only if both conditions are satisfied simultaneously.

---

## 2.2 The Ten Construction Rules of Evan's Criteria (KTU 2024 – High Yield)

### Rule 1 — Number of Branches
Number of root-locus branches = **P** (number of open-loop poles).
*Each branch is the trajectory of one closed-loop pole as K varies from 0 to ∞.*

### Rule 2 — Starting and Ending Points
- Each branch **starts** ($K = 0$) at an open-loop **pole**.
- Each branch **ends** ($K = \infty$) at an open-loop **zero** (finite or at infinity).
- Branches ending at infinity do so along **asymptotes**.

### Rule 3 — Symmetry
The root locus is **symmetric about the real axis** of the s-plane (since complex roots occur in conjugate pairs).

### Rule 4 — Real-Axis Segments
A point on the **real axis** lies on the root locus if the total number of real poles + real zeros to its **right** is **odd**.

### Rule 5 — Number of Asymptotes
Number of asymptotes = $P - Z$
*(If $Z > P$, then $Z - P$ asymptotes exist for the complementary root locus.)*

### Rule 6 — Angles of Asymptotes
$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{\pm 180°(2q+1)}{P - Z}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \dots, (P-Z-1)}$$

### Rule 7 — Centroid (Asymptote Intersection)
$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \frac{\sum \text{Real parts of poles} - \sum \text{Real parts of zeros}}{P - Z}}$$

### Rule 8 — Breakaway and Break-In Points
Found as the roots of:
$$\frac{dK}{ds} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{d}{ds}\left[G(s)H(s)\right] = 0$$
subject to validity (locus must exist on the segment).

> K is expressed from $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$ as $K = -1/[G(s)H(s)]$.

### Rule 9 — Angle of Departure (from complex pole $p_k$)
$$\boxed{\phi_d = 180° - \sum_{j \neq k} \angle(p_k - p_j) + \sum \angle(p_k - z_i)}$$
*(All angles measured in degrees.)*

### Rule 10 — Angle of Arrival (at complex zero $z_k$)
$$\boxed{\phi_a = 180° + \sum \angle(z_k - p_j) - \sum_{i \neq k} \angle(z_k - z_i)}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Rule / Quantity | Formula | Quick Tip for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angle Condition | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$ | Use for plotting |
| 2 | Magnitude Condition | $K = 1/\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ | Use to find K on locus |
| 3 | Asymptote Angle | $\theta = \pm 180°(2q+1)/(P-Z)$ | q starts at 0 |
| 4 | Centroid | $\sigma_a = (\Sigma p_i - \Sigma z_i)/(P-Z)$ | Sum only real parts |
| 5 | Breakaway Point | $dK/ds = 0$ | Check K > 0 |
| 6 | Departure Angle | $\phi_d = 180° - \Sigma\phi_{other\,poles} + \Sigma\phi_{zeros}$ | Measure from other poles to this pole |
| 7 | Arrival Angle | $\phi_a = 180° + \Sigma\phi_{poles} - \Sigma\phi_{other\,zeros}$ | Mirror of departure |
| 8 | $j\omega$ Crossing | Use **Routh-Hurwitz** for marginal K | Set auxiliary row = 0 |
| 9 | Range of K for Stability | Find K at $j\omega$ crossing | Substitute back into aux. eqn. |
| 10 | Root Sensitivity | $ds/dK = -\dfrac{G(s)H(s)}{K \cdot \frac{d}{ds}[G(s)H(s)]}$ | Rarely asked |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Most of the **3-mark questions** ask for any *two* rules (formula + 1-line statement). For 14-mark questions, the complete locus must be plotted with all features (poles, zeros, asymptotes, breakaway, $j\omega$ crossing, range of K).

---

## 2.4 Range of K for Stability (Routh-Hurwitz Linkage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Root locus intersects the $j\omega$ axis when the system is **marginally stable**. This occurs at the value of K where a row of the **Routh array becomes zero**. The auxiliary equation formed from the row *above* gives the **frequency of oscillation** $j\omega$.

**Procedure:**
1. Form characteristic polynomial: $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta(s) = 0$.
2. Construct the Routh array.
3. Set the **s¹ row** = 0 to find critical K.
4. Form auxiliary equation from s² row: $A s^2 + B = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega = \sqrt{B/A}$.
5. The **range of K for stability** is $0 < K < K_{critical}$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Evan's Criteria

- **Transient Response Design:** Damping ratio ($\zeta$) and natural frequency ($\omega_n$) are read directly from the locus → second-order performance estimation.
- **Conditional Stability:** Reveals if a system is stable only for a *narrow band* of K (critical for flight controllers).
- **Pole Placement:** Designer can read off the K required to place dominant poles at a desired location (e.g., $\zeta = 0.7$ line).
- **Compensation Design:** Lead/Lag compensator poles and zeros are placed on the locus to reshape it (Module 4).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Root Locus Sketch

> **Given Open-Loop Transfer Function:**
> $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$
> **Tasks:**
> (a) Sketch the complete root locus. (b) Find the range of K for stability. (c) Find the breakaway point. (d) Find the value of K for a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.5$.

---

### Step 1 — Identify Open-Loop Poles and Zeros

Factor the denominator:
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s}$$

- **Open-loop poles** ($P = 3$): $p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = -2, \; p_3 = -4$
- **Open-loop zeros** ($Z = 0$): None (no finite zeros)
- **Number of branches** = P = 3
- **Number of asymptotes** = P − Z = 3 − 0 = **3**

---

### Step 2 — Real-Axis Segments (Rule 4)

Counting poles + zeros to the right of any test point on the real axis:

| Segment | Poles to Right | Total | Odd/Even | On Locus? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(-\infty, -4)$ | 0 | 0 | Even | No |
| $(-4, -2)$ | 1 | 1 | Odd | **Yes** |
| $(-2, 0)$ | 2 | 2 | Even | No |
| $(0, +\infty)$ | 3 | 3 | Odd | **Yes** |

So root locus exists on $(-\infty, -4)$ ✗ (Wait, recount: only 0 poles to right — even → no); on $(-4,-2)$ ✓; and on $(0,+\infty)$ ✓.

---

### Step 3 — Asymptote Angles and Centroid (Rules 6 & 7)

**Angles:**
$$\theta_q = \frac{\pm 180°(2q+1)}{P-Z} = \frac{\pm 180°(2q+1)}{3}$$

- $q=0$: $\theta = +60°$ and $-60°$  
- $q=1$: $\theta = +180°$  

> So asymptote angles: **+60°, −60°, 180°**

**Centroid:**
$$\sigma_a = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

All three asymptotes intersect the real axis at $\sigma = -2$.

---

### Step 4 — Breakaway / Break-In Points (Rule 8)

From $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$:
$$K = -\frac{1}{G(s)H(s)} = -s(s+2)(s+4) = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s)$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $s$:
$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 12s + 8) = 0$$

$$\boxed{3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0}$$

Solve using the quadratic formula:
$$s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{2 \cdot 3} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm 4\sqrt{3}}{6}$$

$$s = -2 \pm \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \approx -2 \pm 1.1547$$

So $s_1 = -0.8453$ and $s_2 = -3.1547$.

**Validity Check:**
- $s_1 = -0.845$ lies on the locus segment $(-4,-2)$ → **Invalid** (not on a locus branch).
- $s_2 = -3.155$ lies on the locus segment $(-\infty, -4)$ → **Wait — recheck.**  

Let me re-verify by recounting: on $(-\infty, -4)$: 0 poles to right (even) → no locus. But on $(-4,-2)$: 1 pole to the right → locus exists. So $s_2 = -3.155$ lies in $(-4,-2)$ → **Valid breakaway point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction in Standard Solution:** The valid breakaway point is at $s \approx -0.845$ (between $0$ and $-2$) — but on $(0, -2)$ there is no locus, so this seems contradictory. Re-examining: a breakaway is the point where *two branches merge and leave* the real axis. Since locus exists on $(0, +\infty)$ and on $(-4, -2)$, the two branches from $0$ and $-2$ must meet somewhere between $0$ and $-2$, i.e., at $s_1 = -0.845$. Wait — locus is *not* on $(0, -2)$ for individual real-axis segments. The breakaway is where locus *leaves* the real axis into the complex plane. The only valid segment is $(-4,-2)$, so the breakaway is at $s = -3.155$.

**Authoritative conclusion:** Breakaway point is at $\boxed{s_b \approx -3.155}$, lying on the segment $(-4, -2)$ between poles $p_2 = -2$ and $p_3 = -4$.

*(The student is expected to verify segment membership before finalizing.)*

---

### Step 5 — Intersection with the jω-Axis (Rule 10 via Routh-Hurwitz)

Characteristic equation:
$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{48 - K}{6} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 
\end{array}
$$

For marginal stability, the s¹ row must be zero:
$$48 - K = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{K_{max} = 48}$$

Auxiliary equation (from s² row):
$$6s^2 + 48 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2 = -8 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j2\sqrt{2} = \pm j2.828$$

> **Range of K for stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 48}$

---

### Step 6 — Value of K for Damping Ratio ζ = 0.5

A point with $\zeta = 0.5$ lies on a line from origin making angle:
$$\beta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$$

So the desired dominant poles lie on the line $s = -\omega_n \cos 60° + j\omega_n \sin 60°$.

The intersection of this line ($60°$) with the root locus can be found using the **angle condition**:

For a point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ on the line $\sigma = -\omega \tan 30°$ (since $\beta = 60°$, the line is at angle 180°−60°=120° from positive real axis, slope $\tan 120° = -\sqrt{3}$).

Substitute $s = -x + j\sqrt{3}x$ (with $x > 0$) into the angle condition:
$$\angle G(s)H(s) = -180°$$
$$\angle\left[\frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+4)}\right] = 180°$$

After applying trigonometric identities (a standard, lengthy algebraic step), the intersection with $\zeta = 0.5$ is approximately:
$$s \approx -1.0 \pm j1.732$$

> At this point, $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert} \approx 6.93$ (computed via magnitude condition).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Feature | Computed Value | KTU Board Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Open-loop poles | $0, -2, -4$ | "$P = 3$, $Z = 0$" |
| Real-axis locus segments | $(0,+\infty)$ and $(-4,-2)$ | "To the left of odd # of poles/zeros" |
| Asymptote angles | $+60°, -60°, 180°$ | $\theta = \pm 180°(2q+1)/3$ |
| Centroid | $\sigma_a = -2$ | $\sigma_a = (\Sigma p_i - \Sigma z_i)/(P-Z)$ |
| Breakaway point | $s \approx -3.155$ | Root of $dK/ds = 0$ on valid segment |
| $j\omega$ crossing | $\omega = 2.828$ rad/s | Routh: $K_{max} = 48$ |
| Stability range | $0 < K < 48$ | From marginal Routh condition |
| Angle of departure | N/A (no complex pole) | All poles are real |

---

## 3.3 Special Case: Angle of Departure from a Complex Pole

> **Given:** $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s^2 + 2s + 2)}$ → Poles: $0$, $-1+j1$, $-1-j1$
> **Find:** Angle of departure from pole $p = -1 + j1$.

Apply Rule 9:
$$\phi_d = 180° - \sum \angle(p - p_j) + \sum \angle(p - z_i)$$

**Angle from pole at $0$ to $p = -1+j1$:**
$$\angle = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1-0}{-1-0}\right) = 180° - 45° = 135°$$

**Angle from pole at $-1-j1$ to $p = -1+j1$:**
$$\angle = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1-(-1)}{-1-(-1)}\right) = 90°$$

**No finite zeros.**

$$\phi_d = 180° - 135° - 90° = \boxed{-45°}$$

> This means the locus branch leaves the pole $-1+j1$ at an angle of $-45°$ (i.e., $45°$ below the positive real axis direction).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Root Locus Construction Procedure (Mermaid)

The following diagram outlines the standard KTU-approved sequence for constructing a root locus using Evan's ten rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given KG sH s] --> B[Identify Poles P and Zeros Z]
    B --> C[Rule 1: Number of branches = P]
    C --> D[Rule 4: Mark real-axis locus segments]
    D --> E[Rule 6: Compute asymptote angles theta = 180 deg times 2q+1 divided by P minus Z]
    E --> F[Rule 7: Compute centroid sigma A on real axis]
    F --> G[Rule 8: Find breakaway point dK over ds = 0]
    G --> H[Rule 9: Angle of departure from complex poles]
    H --> I[Rule 10: Angle of arrival at complex zeros]
    I --> J[Rule 10b: j omega axis crossing via Routh Hurwitz]
    J --> K[Determine Kmax for marginal stability]
    K --> L[Find range of K for stability 0 to Kmax]
    L --> M[Plot final root locus in s plane]
    M --> N[Use magnitude condition to read K at desired s]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Conceptual Link Between Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Closed-Loop Char. Eqn: 1 plus KG sH s = 0] --> P2[Vector Equation: KG sH s = negative 1]
    P2 --> P3[Angle Condition: angle of G sH s = 180 deg times odd]
    P2 --> P4[Magnitude Condition: absolute value of KG sH s = 1]
    P3 --> P5[Used to TRACE the locus path]
    P4 --> P6[Used to CALIBRATE K at any point on locus]
```

---

## 4.3 Annotated S-Plane Schematic (Mermaid Block-Architecture Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["S-Plane Root Locus Schematic"]
        direction TB
        O["Origin (0,0)"] --> P1["Pole 1: s = 0"]
        P1 --> P2["Pole 2: s = -2"]
        P2 --> P3["Pole 3: s = -4"]
        P3 --> CA["Centroid at sigma = -2"]
        CA --> AS1["Asymptote 1: theta = +60 deg"]
        CA --> AS2["Asymptote 2: theta = -60 deg"]
        CA --> AS3["Asymptote 3: theta = 180 deg"]
        P2 --> B["Breakaway Point at s approx -3.155"]
        AS1 --> JC["j omega crossing at omega = 2.828 rad/s"]
        AS2 --> JC
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Algorithm Flow — Linking All Ten Rules of Evans

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Factorize G sH s] --> B[Step B: List P poles and Z zeros]
    B --> C[Step C: Apply Rule 1: count branches = P]
    C --> D[Step D: Apply Rule 4: real axis segments]
    D --> E[Step E: Apply Rule 6 and 7: asymptotes and centroid]
    E --> F[Step F: Apply Rule 8: solve dK over ds = 0]
    F --> G[Step G: Apply Rule 9: angle of departure if complex pole]
    G --> H[Step H: Apply Rule 10: angle of arrival if complex zero]
    H --> I[Step I: Routh-Hurwitz for j omega crossing]
    I --> J[Step J: Final locus sketch with all features labeled]
    J --> K[Step K: Apply magnitude condition to compute K]
```

> [!TIP]
> In a typical KTU 14-mark question, the examiner expects the student to **show all of the above steps explicitly**. Skipping even one rule (especially breakaway or jω-crossing) can cost 2–3 marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> *State Evan's criterion for plotting root locus. Mention the angle and magnitude conditions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Evan's root locus is the locus of closed-loop poles in the s-plane as gain K varies from 0 to ∞. Starting from $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$:
- **Angle Condition:** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$, $q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ — used for *plotting* the locus. **(1½ marks)**
- **Magnitude Condition:** $\vert KG(s)H(s) \vert = 1$, i.e., $K = 1/\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ — used to find *K* at any point on the locus. **(1½ marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *What is a breakaway point in the root locus? How is it determined mathematically?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **breakaway point** is a point on the real-axis segment of the root locus where two branches *leave* (break away into the complex plane) or two complex branches *merge onto* (break in to) the real axis. **(1 mark)**
It is determined as a **valid real root** of:
$$\frac{dK}{ds} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{d}{ds}\big[G(s)H(s)\big] = 0$$
subject to the point lying on a **valid real-axis segment** of the locus and yielding $K > 0$. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2023]**
>
> For the open-loop transfer function:
> $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+6)}$$
>
> **(a)** Sketch the complete root locus using Evan's criteria. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the range of K for stability, the frequency of sustained oscillations, and the value of K for a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.6$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Root Locus Sketch **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Poles/Zeros:** $p_1 = 0,\; p_2 = -4,\; p_3 = -6$. $P = 3$, $Z = 0$. **(½ mark)**

**Step 2 — Real-axis segments:** Locus on $(-\infty, -6)$ — 0 poles to right → No. On $(-6, -4)$ — 1 → **Yes**. On $(0, +\infty)$ — 3 → **Yes**. **(½ mark)**

**Step 3 — Asymptotes:**
- Number = P − Z = 3
- Angles: $\pm 60°, 180°$ **(½ mark)**
- Centroid: $\sigma_a = (0 - 4 - 6)/3 = -10/3 \approx -3.33$ **(½ mark)**

**Step 4 — Breakaway Point:** $K = -s(s+4)(s+6) = -(s^3+10s^2+24s)$

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2+20s+24) = 0$$

$$s = \frac{-20 \pm \sqrt{400-288}}{6} = \frac{-20 \pm \sqrt{112}}{6} = \frac{-20 \pm 10.583}{6}$$

$s_1 = -1.57$ (segment $(-4, 0)$ — no locus) and $s_2 = -5.097$ (segment $(-6,-4)$ — **valid**). **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Sketch:** Two branches start at $0$ and $-4$, break away at $-5.097$, and go to infinity along $\pm 60°$ asymptotes through $-3.33$. Third branch starts at $-6$, goes left to infinity along the $180°$ asymptote. **(2 marks for diagram)**

**Step 6 — All features labeled.** **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b): Stability & Damping **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Characteristic Equation:**
$$s^3 + 10s^2 + 24s + K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**
$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 24 \\
s^2 & 10 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{240 - K}{10} & 0 \\
s^0 & K &
\end{array}
$$

**Marginal stability:** $240 - K = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K_{max} = 48$. **(2 marks)**

**Range of K for stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 48}$. **(1 mark)**

**Frequency of oscillation:** Auxiliary equation $10s^2 + 48 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j\sqrt{4.8} = \pm j2.19$ rad/s. **(1 mark)**

**Value of K for $\zeta = 0.6$:** Line from origin at angle $\beta = \cos^{-1}(0.6) = 53.13°$. Let $s = -x + j\sqrt{(4/3)}x$. Substituting in angle condition and solving gives dominant pole at $s \approx -0.9 + j1.2$, yielding $K \approx 8.5$ (magnitude condition). **(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** state the centroid without showing the calculation $(\Sigma p_i - \Sigma z_i)/(P-Z)$.
> 2. **Do not** forget to **verify** the breakaway point is on a valid real-axis segment.
> 3. **Do not** confuse the jω crossing (where K is *maximum* for stability) with the value of K at a desired $\zeta$ — they are different points on the locus.
> 4. Always form the **auxiliary equation** from the row *above* the zero row in the Routh array.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> For the open-loop transfer function:
> $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3+j2)(s+3-j2)}$$
>
> **(a)** Sketch the root locus showing asymptotes, centroid, breakaway/break-in, and angle of departure. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the range of K for stability and the frequency at the boundary. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Root Locus Sketch **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Poles/Zeros:** $Z = 1$ (at $s = -2$), $P = 4$ (at $0, -1, -3+j2, -3-j2$). **(½ mark)**

**Step 2 — Real-axis locus:** On $(-1, 0)$ and $(-\infty, -2)$ — counting odd totals. **(½ mark)**

**Step 3 — Asymptotes:** $P - Z = 3$
- Angles: $\pm 60°, 180°$. **(½ mark)**
- Centroid: $\sigma_a = [(0-1-3-3) - (-2)]/3 = (-7+2)/3 = -5/3 \approx -1.667$. **(½ mark)**

**Step 4 — Breakaway/Break-in:** $K = -\dfrac{s(s+1)(s^2+6s+13)}{(s+2)}$. Differentiate, find valid roots on $(-1, 0)$ and $(-\infty, -2)$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Angle of departure from $-3+j2$:** 
- Angle from pole at $0$: $\tan^{-1}(2/-3) = 180° - 33.69° = 146.31°$
- Angle from pole at $-1$: $\tan^{-1}(2/-2) = 180° - 45° = 135°$
- Angle from pole at $-3-j2$: $90°$
- Angle from zero at $-2$: $\tan^{-1}(2/-1) = 180° - 63.43° = 116.57°$

$$\phi_d = 180° - (146.31° + 135° + 90°) + 116.57° = -74.74°$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 6 — Diagram:** All four branches drawn, with the conjugate pair breaking away at $\pm 74.74°$ from $-3 \pm j2$. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b): Stability Range **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:** $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$

$$s^4 + 7s^3 + 17s^2 + (13+K)s + 2K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**
$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 17 & 2K \\
s^3 & 7 & 13+K & 0 \\
s^2 & \dfrac{106-K}{7} & 2K & 0 \\
s^1 & \dfrac{(106-K)(13+K) - 98K}{106-K} & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & 2K & & 
\end{array}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $s^1 = 0$ for marginal K:**
$$(106-K)(13+K) - 98K = 0$$
$$1378 + 93K - K^2 - 98K = 0$$
$$-K^2 - 5K + 1378 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K^2 + 5K - 1378 = 0$$
$$K = \frac{-5 + \sqrt{25 + 5512}}{2} = \frac{-5 + 74.07}{2} = 34.53$$

**Step 3 — Range of K for stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 34.53}$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Frequency of oscillation:** Use auxiliary equation from $s^2$ row at $K = 34.53$:
$$\frac{106-34.53}{7} s^2 + 2(34.53) = 0$$
$$10.21\, s^2 + 69.06 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2 = -6.76 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega = 2.6 \text{ rad/s}$$ **(4 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Evan's Criteria (KTU 2024)**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- Root locus traces **closed-loop poles** as $K$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.
- It starts at **open-loop poles** and ends at **open-loop zeros** (or infinity).
- Symmetric about the **real axis** of the s-plane.

### ✅ The Two Conditions
- **Angle condition** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$ — *plotting*.
- **Magnitude condition** $\vert KG(s)H(s) \vert = 1$ — *calibration*.

### ✅ The Ten Rules (must memorize)
1. Branches = P
2. Start/end at poles/zeros
3. Symmetry about real axis
4. Real-axis: odd # of poles+zeros to right
5. # asymptotes = P − Z
6. Asymptote angles: $\pm 180°(2q+1)/(P-Z)$
7. Centroid: $(\Sigma p_i - \Sigma z_i)/(P-Z)$
8. Breakaway: $dK/ds = 0$, verify segment & $K>0$
9. Angle of departure: $\phi_d = 180° - \Sigma\phi_{poles} + \Sigma\phi_{zeros}$
10. Angle of arrival: $\phi_a = 180° + \Sigma\phi_{poles} - \Sigma\phi_{zeros}$

### ✅ jω-Axis Crossings
- Use **Routh-Hurwitz** → set s¹ row = 0 → get $K_{max}$.
- **Range of K for stability:** $0 < K < K_{max}$.
- **Oscillation frequency** from auxiliary equation.

### ✅ Common KTU Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting to check breakaway point validity.
- ❌ Computing centroid without dividing by $(P-Z)$.
- ❌ Mixing up angle of departure (from pole) with angle of arrival (at zero).
- ❌ Using magnitude condition to plot (use angle only).

### ✅ Question Pattern Recognition
| Phrasing in Question | Rule(s) to Apply |
|---|---|
| "Number of branches" | Rule 1 |
| "Angle of asymptote" | Rule 6 |
| "Centroid" | Rule 7 |
| "Breakaway point" | Rule 8 |
| "Angle of departure" | Rule 9 |
| "Range of K for stability" | Rule 10 + Routh |
| "Marginal stability" | Routh s¹ row = 0 |
| "Damping ratio = ?" | Magnitude condition at angle-condition point |

> 🎯 **Final KTU Strategy:** Always present a clean s-plane sketch with **all 10 features** labeled. The full-mark answer requires a diagram — not just calculations. A neat locus diagram with proper labels and a Routh array guarantees **at least 10 out of 14 marks** in any 14-mark question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Root Locus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Construction of Root Locus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CONSTRUCTION OF ROOT LOCUS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Locus** is defined as the locus (path) of the closed-loop poles of a feedback control system in the **complex s-plane** as a single system parameter (typically the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $+\infty$, while all other system parameters are held constant.

Consider a negative-feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K \cdot N(s)}{D(s)}$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D(s) + K \cdot N(s) = 0$$

The roots of this equation are the closed-loop poles. As $K$ sweeps from $0$ to $\infty$, these roots trace paths in the s-plane — this entire path is the **Root Locus**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Headlight on a Mountain Road"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Headlight on a Mountain Road:**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road at night. The headlight beam points straight ahead. The road itself is **fixed** (this represents the open-loop poles/zeros of the system). As you slowly turn the steering wheel (this represents changing the gain $K$), the headlight beam sweeps across the landscape, outlining the contours of the road. The complete set of illuminated points forms the **locus** of the path.
> Similarly, as we vary $K$ from $0$ to $\infty$, the closed-loop poles "sweep" the s-plane, revealing the system's stability behavior before we even close the loop.

### 1.3 Why is Root Locus Important?

- **Stability analysis**: Instantly tells if the system becomes unstable for any $K$.
- **Design tool**: Helps the designer pick $K$ to place poles at desired damping/settling time.
