- **Time-domain spec tuning**: Damping ratio $\zeta$ and natural frequency $\omega_n$ can be enforced.
- **No solving required for the full polynomial** at every value of $K$.

### 1.4 The Two Fundamental Conditions

Any point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ lies on the root locus if and only if it satisfies:

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Angle Condition** | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1),\ \ q = 0,1,2,\dots$ | Pure phase criterion — defines the **shape** |
| **Magnitude Condition** | $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \ \Rightarrow\ K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$ | Defines the **value of K** at any point on the locus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The **angle condition alone** determines the root locus. The **magnitude condition** is used *afterward* to compute the value of $K$ at a given test point on the locus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Angle Condition — Derivation of the Master Equation

From $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, we get $G(s)H(s) = -1$. Converting to polar form:

$$G(s)H(s) = \vert G(s)H(s) \vert \angle G(s)H(s) = 1 \angle \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)$$

Equating angles yields the **Angle Condition**:

$$\boxed{\sum \angle(s - z_i) - \sum \angle(s - p_j) = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)}$$

where $z_i$ are the open-loop zeros and $p_j$ are the open-loop poles.

### 2.2 Magnitude Condition — The K-Finder

$$\boxed{K = \frac{\prod_{j=1}^{n} \vert s - p_j \vert}{\prod_{i=1}^{m} \vert s - z_i \vert}}$$

This is extensively used in KTU problems to find the gain $K$ corresponding to a specific damping ratio or dominant pole location.

### 2.3 The 9 Golden Rules of Root Locus Construction (KTU High-Yield)

Let $n$ = number of open-loop poles, $m$ = number of open-loop zeros, and $n - m$ = number of asymptotes.

| # | Rule | Formula / Logic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of branches** | Equals the number of open-loop poles $n$ |
| 2 | **Symmetry** | Root locus is symmetric about the real axis (complex roots occur in conjugate pairs) |
| 3 | **Starting points ($K=0$)** | Each branch starts at an open-loop pole |
| 4 | **Ending points ($K \to \infty$)** | $m$ branches terminate at open-loop zeros; the remaining $n-m$ branches go to infinity |
| 5 | **Real-axis segments** | A point on the real axis lies on the root locus if the total number of poles + zeros to its **right** is **odd** |
| 6 | **Asymptote angles** | $\phi_a = \dfrac{\pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)}{n - m},\ \ q = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n-m-1$ |
| 7 | **Asymptote centroid ($\sigma_a$)** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum \text{Re}(p_j) - \sum \text{Re}(z_i)}{n - m}$ |
| 8 | **Breakaway / Break-in points** | Roots of $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ on the real axis (valid only if the resulting $K > 0$) |
| 9 | **j$\omega$-axis crossing** | Found by applying **Routh-Hurwitz** to the characteristic polynomial and finding the $K$ that yields purely imaginary roots $s = j\omega$ |

### 2.4 Angle of Departure (from complex pole $p_k$)

$$\boxed{\phi_d = 180^{\circ} + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \angle(p_k - z_i) - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq k}}^{n} \angle(p_k - p_j)}$$

### 2.5 Angle of Arrival (at complex zero $z_k$)

$$\boxed{\phi_a^{arr} = 180^{\circ} - \sum_{\substack{i=1 \\ i \neq k}}^{m} \angle(z_k - z_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \angle(z_k - p_j)}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

| Quantity | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Angle Condition | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)$ | Test if a point lies on locus |
| Magnitude Condition | $K = 1 \big/ \vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ | Calculate $K$ at a known point |
| Asymptote Angles | $\phi_a = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1) / (n-m)$ | Sketch asymptotes |
| Centroid | $\sigma_a = \left[ \sum p_j - \sum z_i \right] / (n-m)$ | Locate intersection of asymptotes |
| Breakaway points | Roots of $dK/ds = 0$ | Where branches leave real axis |
| Angle of departure | $\phi_d = 180^{\circ} + \sum \angle(p-z) - \sum \angle(p-p)$ | Direction at complex pole |
| $j\omega$ crossing | Routh array — auxiliary eqn. $\to \omega_c$ | Find marginal stability gain |
| Damping ratio line | $\zeta = \cos \theta$, where $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ | Locate desired dominant pole |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In all KTU exams, use the **degree symbol °** with a leading space (e.g., $90^{\circ}$), not a superscript zero. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or `\vert` for absolute value — never the literal pipe `$\vert$` inside a markdown table.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Asymptote Centroid

Let the closed-loop characteristic equation be $D(s) + K N(s) = 0$. For very large $s$:

$$s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + K(s^m + b_1 s^{m-1} + \cdots) = 0$$

Dividing by $s^m$ and applying the binomial approximation for large $s$ (where $s^n$ dominates):

$$s^{n-m} \left[1 + \frac{(a_1 - K b_1)}{s} + \cdots \right] \to 0$$

The asymptotic behavior is governed by:

$$s^{n-m} + (\sum p_j - \sum z_i) \cdot s^{n-m-1} + \cdots = 0$$

For large $s$, the centroid is where the asymptotes intersect the real axis. Substituting $s = \sigma_a + j\omega$ and equating the linear term in $s^{n-m-1}$ to the case where all $(n-m)$ asymptotes meet gives:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j - \sum_{i=1}^{m} z_i}{n - m}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Root Locus Construction (KTU Model Problem)

**Problem:** For the unity feedback system with

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

**Sketch the root locus and find:**
(a) Asymptotes, centroid, and j$\omega$-axis crossing.
(b) Range of $K$ for stability.
(c) Value of $K$ for a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.5$.

#### Step 1 — Identify Poles and Zeros

Open-loop poles: $p_1 = 0,\ p_2 = -2,\ p_3 = -4$ (so $n = 3$)
Open-loop zeros: None (so $m = 0$)
Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 3$

#### Step 2 — Asymptote Angles

$$\phi_a = \frac{\pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)}{3},\quad q = 0, 1, 2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q = 0: \quad \phi_a &= +60^{\circ} \\
q = 1: \quad \phi_a &= +180^{\circ} \\
q = 2: \quad \phi_a &= -60^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Centroid

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - 0}{3 - 0} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2
$$

#### Step 4 — Real-Axis Segments (Rule 5)

To the right of $0$: 3 (poles) → **odd** → on locus
Between $0$ and $-2$: 2 (poles to the right) → **even** → **not on locus**
Between $-2$ and $-4$: 1 (pole to the right) → **odd** → on locus
Left of $-4$: 0 → **even** → **not on locus**

#### Step 5 — Breakaway Point

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0 \Rightarrow K = -s^3 - 6s^2 - 8s$

$$
\frac{dK}{ds} = -3s^2 - 12s - 8 = 0
$$

$$
3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6}
$$

$$
s = \frac{-12 \pm 6.928}{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_1 = -0.845,\quad s_2 = -3.155
$$

Valid breakaway point lies **between** the poles $0$ and $-2$ (where the locus exists on the real axis), so $s_{br} = -0.845$. The other root is a break-in candidate but lies outside the valid real-axis segment.

#### Step 6 — j$\omega$-Axis Crossing (Routh-Hurwitz)

Characteristic polynomial: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$

**Routh Array:**

| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

For marginal stability: $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} = 0 \Rightarrow K = 48$

Auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row:

$$6s^2 + K = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6s^2 + 48 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 = -8$$

$$s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2} \approx \pm j 2.828$$

#### Step 7 — Range of K for Stability

For stability, all first-column entries must be positive: $0 < K < 48$.

#### Step 8 — Damping Ratio $\zeta = 0.5$

$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60^{\circ}$. Draw a line from origin at $60^{\circ}$ to real axis.

Locate point $s_1$ on this line that also lies on the root locus branch. Using geometry and the angle condition, solving yields:

$$s_1 \approx -1.0 + j1.732$$

Apply magnitude condition at $s_1$:

$$K = \vert s_1 \vert \cdot \vert s_1 + 2 \vert \cdot \vert s_1 + 4 \vert$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert s_1 \vert &= \sqrt{1.0^2 + 1.732^2} = \sqrt{1 + 3} = 2 \\
\vert s_1 + 2 \vert &= \sqrt{1.0^2 + 1.732^2} = 2 \\
\vert s_1 + 4 \vert &= \sqrt{3.0^2 + 1.732^2} = \sqrt{9 + 3} = \sqrt{12} = 3.464
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
K = 2 \times 2 \times 3.464 \approx 13.86
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Scheme — Magnitude Condition:** Always show *all three* factors in the product (one per open-loop pole). Even a 0.5 mark is lost if you skip a term in the $K$ calculation.

### 3.3 Summary Matrix of the Worked Example

| Feature | Value |
|---|---|
| Asymptote angles | $\pm 60^{\circ},\ +180^{\circ}$ |
| Centroid $\sigma_a$ | $-2$ |
| Breakaway point | $s \approx -0.845$ |
| j$\omega$-axis crossing | $s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$ |
| Critical gain $K_{max}$ | $48$ |
| Gain at $\zeta = 0.5$ | $\approx 13.86$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Systematic Root Locus Construction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: G open-loop TF] --> B[Identify poles p1 pn and zeros z1 zm]
    B --> C[Compute n-m asymptotes]
    C --> D[Calculate asymptote angles: phi_a = ±180 deg 2q+1 / n-m]
    D --> E[Calculate centroid sigma_a]
    E --> F[Mark real-axis locus segments rule of odd count]
    F --> G{Complex poles?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute angle of departure phi_d]
    G -- No --> I[Skip to breakaway points]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Find roots of dK/ds = 0]
    J --> K[Apply Routh-Hurwitz to char. eqn.]
    K --> L[Find j omega crossing and K_max]
    L --> M[Sketch complete root locus]
    M --> N[Apply magnitude condition to find K at any desired point]
    N --> O[End: Final root locus + K values]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Open-Loop to Closed-Loop Pole Migration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputPlane["S-Plane Mapping for Root Locus"]
        P1[Open-loop pole p1] -- K = 0 --> M1
        P2[Open-loop pole p2] -- K = 0 --> M2
        P3[Open-loop pole p3] -- K = 0 --> M3
        Z1[Open-loop zero z1] -- K = infinity --> M1
        M1[Migrating closed-loop pole]
        M2[Migrating closed-loop pole]
        M3[Migrating closed-loop pole]
    end
    M1 --> R[Final Root Locus Path]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Angle and Magnitude Condition Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Choose test point s in s-plane] --> AC[Apply Angle Condition]
    AC --> Q1{Angle = ±180 deg 2q+1 ?}
    Q1 -- No --> X[Point NOT on locus]
    Q1 -- Yes --> MC[Apply Magnitude Condition]
    MC --> K[Compute K = 1 / |G s H s|]
    K --> OUT[Point s on locus with gain K]
    X --> NP[Try next test point]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip:** The angle condition is the *gatekeeper* (binary yes/no), while the magnitude condition is the *valuator* (assigns a numerical $K$). This two-step process is what makes root locus such an elegant design tool.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**State and explain the angle and magnitude conditions for a point to lie on the root locus.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Angle Condition:** A point $s$ in the s-plane lies on the root locus if the total angle of $G(s)H(s)$ at that point equals an odd multiple of $180^{\circ}$:
> $$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1),\ q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$
>
> **Magnitude Condition:** The value of the gain $K$ at any point on the root locus is given by the reciprocal of the magnitude of $G(s)H(s)$ evaluated at that point (with $K$ factored out):
> $$K = \frac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert} = \frac{\prod_{j=1}^{n} \vert s - p_j \vert}{\prod_{i=1}^{m} \vert s - z_i \vert}$$
>
> The angle condition determines the **shape** of the locus, while the magnitude condition determines the **value of $K$** at any point already on the locus.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**List any six rules for the construction of root locus.**

**Model Answer:**

The six rules are:

1. The number of branches of the root locus equals the number of open-loop poles $n$.
2. The root locus is symmetric with respect to the real axis.
3. Each branch starts at an open-loop pole (when $K = 0$) and terminates either at a finite open-loop zero or at infinity along an asymptote.
4. The asymptote angles are $\phi_a = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1) / (n-m)$ for $q = 0, 1, \dots, n-m-1$.
5. The asymptote centroid is $\sigma_a = [\sum p_j - \sum z_i] / (n-m)$.
6. A real-axis segment belongs to the root locus if the total number of real poles and real zeros to its right is odd.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Question A (Choice 1):**

For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)(s+5)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the root locus showing all asymptotes, centroid, real-axis segments, breakaway points, and j$\omega$-axis crossings.

**(b)** Find the range of $K$ for which the system is stable and the value of $K$ at the breakaway point.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Poles and Zeros**

Open-loop poles: $p_1 = 0,\ p_2 = -1,\ p_3 = -3,\ p_4 = -5$ (so $n = 4$)
Open-loop zeros: $z_1 = -2$ (so $m = 1$)
Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 3$

**Step 2 — Asymptote Angles**

$$
\phi_a = \frac{\pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)}{3},\quad q = 0, 1, 2
$$

$$
q = 0: \phi_a = +60^{\circ};\quad q = 1: \phi_a = +180^{\circ};\quad q = 2: \phi_a = -60^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Centroid**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{[(0) + (-1) + (-3) + (-5)] - [(-2)]}{4 - 1} = \frac{-9 + 2}{3} = \frac{-7}{3} \approx -2.33
$$

**Step 4 — Real-Axis Segments**

Counting poles + zeros to the right of a test point:
- Right of $0$: 4 (all poles) → odd → on locus
- Between $0$ and $-1$: 3 → odd → on locus
- Between $-1$ and $-2$: 2 → even → **not** on locus
- Between $-2$ and $-3$: 1 (zero) → odd → on locus
- Between $-3$ and $-5$: 2 → even → **not** on locus
- Left of $-5$: 0 → even → **not** on locus

**Step 5 — Breakaway Point**

Characteristic equation: $s(s+1)(s+3)(s+5) + K(s+2) = 0$

Expanding: $s^4 + 9s^3 + 23s^2 + 15s + K(s+2) = 0$

$$s^4 + 9s^3 + 23s^2 + (15 + K)s + 2K = 0$$

$$K = \frac{-s^4 - 9s^3 - 23s^2 - 15s}{s + 2}$$

Setting $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ yields the breakaway point on the segment $[-1, 0]$ (the only valid segment with two poles bounding it). Solving gives:

$$s_{br} \approx -0.45,\quad K_{br} \approx 0.62$$

**Step 6 — j$\omega$-Axis Crossing (Routh-Hurwitz)**

Applying the Routh criterion to $s^4 + 9s^3 + 23s^2 + (15+K)s + 2K = 0$:

**Routh Array:**

| $s^4$ | $1$ | $23$ | $2K$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $9$ | $15+K$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{192 - K}{9}$ | $2K$ | |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{(192-K)(15+K) - 162K}{9(192-K)/9}$ | | |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | | |

For stability, $K > 0$ and $K < 192$. The auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row gives the j$\omega$-axis crossing at:

$$K = 192 \Rightarrow \omega_c = \sqrt{23} \approx 4.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 7 — Range of K for Stability**

$$0 < K < 192$$

---

**Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Q3):**

For the unity feedback system

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s^2 + 4s + 20)}$$

**(a)** Determine the angle of departure from the complex poles and the asymptote angles.

**(b)** Find the range of $K$ for stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Poles and Zeros**

Poles: $p_1 = 0,\ p_2 = -4,\ p_{3,4} = -2 \pm j4$ (so $n = 4$)
Zeros: None (so $m = 0$)
Asymptotes: $n - m = 4$

**Step 2 — Asymptote Angles**

$$
\phi_a = \frac{\pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1)}{4},\quad q = 0, 1, 2, 3
$$

$$
\phi_a = +45^{\circ},\ +135^{\circ},\ -45^{\circ},\ -135^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Centroid**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{0 + (-4) + (-2) + (-2)}{4} = \frac{-8}{4} = -2
$$

**Step 4 — Angle of Departure from $p_3 = -2 + j4$**

Apply the departure angle formula with reference to $p_3$:

$$
\phi_d = 180^{\circ} + \sum \angle(p_3 - z_i) - \sum_{\text{others}} \angle(p_3 - p_j)
$$

With $z_i$ being none, the first sum is zero.

Angles from $p_3$ to other poles:
- To $p_1 = 0$: vector $= (0 - (-2)) + j(0 - 4) = 2 - j4$

$$\angle(p_3 - p_1) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-4}{2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-2) = -63.43^{\circ}$$

- To $p_2 = -4$: vector $= (-4 - (-2)) + j(0 - 4) = -2 - j4$

$$\angle(p_3 - p_2) = -180^{\circ} + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{2}\right) = -180^{\circ} + 63.43^{\circ} = -116.57^{\circ}$$

- To $p_4 = -2 - j4$ (conjugate): vector $= 0 - j8$

$$\angle(p_3 - p_4) = -90^{\circ}$$

$$
\phi_d = 180^{\circ} - [(-63.43^{\circ}) + (-116.57^{\circ}) + (-90^{\circ})]
$$

$$
\phi_d = 180^{\circ} + 63.43^{\circ} + 116.57^{\circ} + 90^{\circ} = 450^{\circ}
$$

Subtracting $360^{\circ}$:

$$
\boxed{\phi_d = +90^{\circ}}
$$

By symmetry, the departure angle from the conjugate pole $p_4$ is $-90^{\circ}$.

**Step 5 — Routh-Hurwitz for Stability**

Characteristic equation: $s(s+4)(s^2 + 4s + 20) + K = 0$

Expanding:

$$
s^4 + 8s^3 + 36s^2 + 80s + K = 0
$$

**Routh Array:**

| $s^4$ | $1$ | $36$ | $K$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $8$ | $80$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $26$ | $K$ | |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{2080 - 8K}{26}$ | | |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | | |

For stability, all first-column entries must be positive:

$$K > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad 2080 - 8K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 260$$

$$
\boxed{0 < K < 260}
$$

At $K = 260$, the auxiliary equation $26s^2 + 260 = 0$ gives $s = \pm j\sqrt{10} \approx \pm j3.162$ — the j$\omega$-axis crossing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in angle of departure** — Most frequent mistake. Remember the master formula is $\phi_d = 180^{\circ} + \sum \angle(p - z) - \sum \angle(p - p)$. Skipping the leading $180^{\circ}$ costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong asymptote angle indexing** — Students often compute only $q = 0$ asymptote. KTU requires **all** $n - m$ asymptote angles.
> 3. **Magnitude condition products** — Always show *every* factor $\vert s - p_j \vert$ and $\vert s - z_i \vert$ explicitly. A skipped term = lost 1 mark.
> 4. **Routh array arithmetic** — The $s^1$ row entry must be computed correctly. A common error is inverting the column structure; always verify the determinant form: $\frac{b_1 c_1 - b_2 c_0}{b_1}$.
> 5. **Real-axis rule** — A real-axis segment is on the locus if the count of (poles + zeros) to its **right** is odd. Confusing "left" and "right" is a recurring 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Breakaway point validation** — Always check $K > 0$ and that the point lies on a valid real-axis segment.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Root Locus Construction**
> - [ ] **Step 1:** Identify open-loop poles ($n$) and zeros ($m$).
> - [ ] **Step 2:** Count asymptotes = $n - m$.
> - [ ] **Step 3:** Compute asymptote angles using $\phi_a = \pm 180^{\circ}(2q+1) / (n-m)$.
> - [ ] **Step 4:** Compute centroid $\sigma_a = [\sum p_j - \sum z_i] / (n-m)$.
> - [ ] **Step 5:** Mark real-axis segments (rule of odd count to the right).
> - [ ] **Step 6:** Compute breakaway/break-in points from $dK/ds = 0$.
> - [ ] **Step 7:** Compute angle of departure for complex poles.
> - [ ] **Step 8:** Compute angle of arrival for complex zeros.
> - [ ] **Step 9:** Use Routh-Hurwitz to find j$\omega$-axis crossing and stability range.
> - [ ] **Step 10:** Use magnitude condition $K = 1/\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ at any point on the locus to find $K$.
>
> **One-line memory hooks:**
> - *Angle condition defines shape, magnitude condition defines K.*
> - *Asymptotes angle: (odd) × 180° divided by (n-m).*
> - *Centroid: sum of poles minus sum of zeros, all divided by (n-m).*
> - *Real axis: odd number of real poles + zeros to the right.*
> - *jω crossing: Routh gives K, auxiliary equation gives ω.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Roots from Root Locus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Determination of Roots from Root Locus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Classical Control Engineering (PCICT501)*, the **Root Locus** is formally defined as the **locus (path) traced in the $s$-plane by the closed-loop poles of a feedback control system as a specific system parameter — typically the open-loop gain $K$ — is varied from $0$ to $+\infty$**, while all other system parameters are held constant. The **determination of roots from the root locus** refers to the inverse problem: *given a particular value of $K$, extract (compute) the exact closed-loop pole locations* that lie on the root locus for that gain. These poles are precisely the roots of the closed-loop **characteristic equation**: $$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** The root locus satisfies two fundamental conditions derived from the complex variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$: > - **Angle Condition:** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ (2k+1), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ > - **Magnitude Condition:** $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}\bigg\vert_{s=\text{point on locus}}$ > > The angle condition identifies *candidate points*, while the magnitude condition quantifies the *gain* required to place a closed-loop pole at that exact location. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are sailing a small boat across a wide lake (the **$s$-plane**). You have **three anchors** tied to the boat (the open-loop poles at $s = 0,\; -1,\; -5$). As you pull the rope (increase $K$), the boat — your **closed-loop pole** — does not stay still; it is dragged along a curved path until it eventually settles downstream toward one of the original anchor points (asymptotes heading to infinity). Now, the practical question is: *"If I tighten the rope to exactly $K = 4.2$, where exactly is my boat?"* That is precisely what **determination of roots from the root locus** answers — it is the **reverse-engineering** of pole positions for a chosen gain. ### 1.3 Key Physical Insight | Term | Real-world Engineering Meaning | |------|--------------------------------| | Pole on the locus | A natural response mode of the system | | Crossing of $j\omega$-axis | Boundary of **stability** | | Breakaway point | Where two real poles leave the real axis together | | Roots determined for $K$ | Closed-loop dynamic behavior under that gain | > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters industrially:** Determining the roots for a given $K$ allows engineers to predict settling time, overshoot, oscillation frequency, and stability — the *four* most critical performance specs in any feedback design (e.g., cruise control, robotic arm positioning, DC motor speed control). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Foundational Conditions (Why & How) The closed-loop characteristic equation $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$ can be rewritten as: $$KG(s)H(s) = -1$$ This single complex equation imposes **two real constraints** on the complex number $s = \sigma + j\omega$: **WHY the angle condition arises:** A complex number equals $-1$ if and only if its **magnitude is unity** *and* its **angle is an odd multiple of $180^\circ$**. Therefore, separating the real and imaginary parts of $KG(s)H(s) = -1$ gives the two conditions. 1. **Angle Condition** — *Locates the locus (geometry independent of $K$)* - It filters $s$-plane points that *qualify* to be on the root locus. - Independent of $K$, so the locus shape is fixed once $G(s)H(s)$ is given. 2. **Magnitude Condition** — *Quantifies $K$ for any chosen point* - Once a point $s_0$ is identified on the locus, $|K| = \dfrac{1}{|G(s_0)H(s_0)|}$. - This is the workhorse for *determining the roots when $K$ is known*. ### 2.2 Procedure: Determination of Roots for a Given $K$ When the value of $K$ is prescribed (say $K = K_0$), the closed-loop characteristic polynomial is fully known numerically: $$1 + K_0 \, G(s)H(s) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$$ The roots of this polynomial are the **closed-loop poles**. There are three common analytical strategies: - **Strategy A — Direct Numerical Factoring** (synthetic division / Newton-Raphson / MATLAB `roots`) - **Strategy B — Trial-and-error + Polynomial Long Division** (preferred for hand-written KTU exams) - **Strategy C — Graphical Root Locus Read-off + Magnitude Condition** (used to *verify* the locus plot) > [!TIP] > **Strategy C verification step:** Once you find a root $s_0$ from Strategy A/B, *always* verify it lies on the root locus by checking whether the **angle condition** holds at that point. This is a common KTU examiner's test of conceptual depth. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Condition | Symbolic Expression | Purpose in KTU Problems | |---|---------------------|--------------------|-------------------------| | 1 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Defines closed-loop poles | | 2 | Angle Condition | $\sum \angle(s - z_i) - \sum \angle(s - p_j) = \pm(2k+1)180^\circ$ | Locates root locus | | 3 | Magnitude Condition | $K = \dfrac{\prod \vert s - p_j \vert}{\prod \vert s - z_i \vert}$ | Computes $K$ at a point | | 4 | Gain Formula (poles only) | $K = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \vert s - p_j \vert$ | Most used form (no zeros) | | 5 | Breakaway/Break-in Equation | $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ | Locates real-axis transitions | | 6 | Centroid of Asymptotes | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum p_j - \sum z_i}{n - m}$ | Direction of locus at infinity | | 7 | Asymptote Angles | $\theta_a = \dfrac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{n - m}$ | Spread of asymptotes | | 8 | $j\omega$-axis Crossing | $s = j\omega \Rightarrow$ equating imaginary parts to zero | Stability boundary for $K$ | | 9 | Routh–Hurwitz Stability | All first column entries $> 0$ | Range of $K$ for stability | | 10 | Polynomial for Given $K$ | Substitute $K$ and solve | Direct root determination | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Determining the roots from the root locus is the **bridge between graphical analysis and time-domain performance**. In practical systems: - **DC Motor Speed Control:** Knowing the pole locations for a chosen $K$ tells the designer whether the motor will overshoot, settle within 2 seconds, or oscillate uncontrollably. - **Aircraft Autopilot Design:** Each closed-loop pole represents a flight mode (pitch, roll, yaw). The locus root determination guarantees no mode becomes unstable. - **Oscillator Design:** The exact point where poles cross the $j\omega$-axis gives the **sustained oscillation frequency** — exploited in signal generators and self-oscillating converters. > [!NOTE] > The closed-loop **damping ratio** $\zeta$ and **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ are read directly from the polar coordinates of the root $s = \sigma + j\omega$ as: > $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \omega^2}, \qquad \zeta = \dfrac{-\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \omega^2}}$$ > These translate directly into time-domain specs: $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta \omega_n)$ (2% settling time) and $\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ (peak overshoot). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Highly Recommended for KTU) **Problem:** For the unity-feedback system whose open-loop transfer function is $$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$ determine the closed-loop roots when **(a)** $K = 1.128$ (the breakaway value) and **(b)** $K = 30$. --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (a): $K = 1.128$ **Step 1 — Write the characteristic equation.** From $1 + G(s) = 0$: $$1 + \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s(s+1)(s+5) + K = 0$$ Expand the product: $$s(s+1)(s+5) = s(s^2 + 6s + 5) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s$$ So the characteristic polynomial is: $$s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + K = 0$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given value of $K = 1.128$.** $$s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + 1.128 = 0$$ **Step 3 — Locate the breakaway point analytically (so we can predict the double root).** Express $K$ from the characteristic equation: $$K(s) = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s)$$ Apply the breakaway condition $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$: $$\dfrac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 12s + 5) = 0$$ Solve the quadratic: $$\begin{aligned} 3s^2 + 12s + 5 &= 0 \\[4pt] s &= \dfrac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 60}}{6} \\[4pt] s &= \dfrac{-12 \pm \sqrt{84}}{6} \\[4pt] s &= \dfrac{-12 \pm 9.165}{6} \\[4pt] s_1 &= -0.4725, \quad s_2 = -3.5275 \end{aligned}$$ The valid breakaway on the segment $[-1,\; 0]$ is: $$s_{\text{br}} = -0.4725$$ **Step 4 — Compute $K$ at the breakaway point.** $$\begin{aligned} K_{\text{br}} &= -[(-0.4725)^3 + 6(-0.4725)^2 + 5(-0.4725)] \\[4pt] &= -[-0.1055 + 6(0.2233) - 2.3625] \\[4pt] &= -[-0.1055 + 1.3398 - 2.3625] \\[4pt] &= -[-1.1282] \\[4pt] K_{\text{br}} &\approx 1.128 \end{aligned}$$ This matches our prescribed $K$, confirming the root has **multiplicity 2** at $s = -0.4725$. **Step 5 — Factor the cubic by polynomial division knowing one root.** Since $s_1 = -0.4725$ is a **double root**, factor out $(s + 0.4725)^2$: $$s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + 1.128 = (s + 0.4725)^2 (s + \alpha)$$ Expand the square: $$(s + 0.4725)^2 = s^2 + 0.9450\,s + 0.2233$$ Multiply by $(s + \alpha)$: $$\begin{aligned} (s^2 + 0.9450\,s + 0.2233)(s + \alpha) &= s^3 + \alpha s^2 + 0.9450\,s^2 + 0.9450\alpha s + 0.2233\,s + 0.2233\alpha \\[4pt] &= s^3 + (\alpha + 0.9450)s^2 + (0.9450\alpha + 0.2233)s + 0.2233\alpha \end{aligned}$$ Match coefficients with $s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + 1.128$: $$\begin{aligned} \alpha + 0.9450 &= 6 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \alpha = 5.055 \\[4pt] 0.9450\alpha + 0.2233 &\approx 5.000 \quad \text{(verified: } 4.777 + 0.223 = 5.000\text{)} \\[4pt] 0.2233 \times 5.055 &\approx 1.128 \quad \text{(verified)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Write the final closed-loop roots.** $$\boxed{\,s_{1,2} = -0.4725 \pm j0 \;\;(\text{double root}), \qquad s_3 = -5.055\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** A double real pole produces a critically-damped-like response — no oscillation, but a slower, sluggish build-up because the system "lingers" at that critical gain. --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (b): $K = 30$ **Step 1 — Substitute $K = 30$ into the characteristic polynomial.** $$s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + 30 = 0$$ **Step 2 — Use the Rational Root Theorem to test integer candidates.** Possible rational roots: $\pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \pm 5, \pm 6, \pm 10, \pm 15, \pm 30$. Test $s = -3$: $$(-3)^3 + 6(-3)^2 + 5(-3) + 30 = -27 + 54 - 15 + 30 = 42 \neq 0$$ Test $s = -5$: $$(-5)^3 + 6(-5)^2 + 5(-5) + 30 = -125 + 150 - 25 + 30 = 30 \neq 0$$ Test $s = -6$: $$(-6)^3 + 6(-6)^2 + 5(-6) + 30 = -216 + 216 - 30 + 30 = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$ So $s_1 = -6$ is an exact root. **Step 3 — Polynomial long division: divide by $(s + 6)$.** $$\begin{array}{r|rrrr} -6 & 1 & 6 & 5 & 30 \\ & & -6 & 0 & -30 \\ \hline & 1 & 0 & 5 & 0 \end{array}$$ The quotient polynomial is $s^2 + 5$, so: $$s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + 30 = (s + 6)(s^2 + 5) = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve the quadratic factor.** $$s^2 + 5 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s = \pm j\sqrt{5} = \pm j\,2.236$$ **Step 5 — Final closed-loop roots for $K = 30$:** $$\boxed{\,s_1 = -6, \qquad s_{2,3} = \pm j\,2.236\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL OBSERVATION:** The roots $s_{2,3} = \pm j\,2.236$ lie **exactly on the imaginary axis**. This means $K = 30$ is the **marginal stability** value — the system will sustain **undamped oscillations at $\omega = 2.236$ rad/s forever**. Any $K > 30$ makes the system unstable. This boundary matches the Routh array result: for $s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + K = 0$, the $s^1$ row gives $\dfrac{30 - K}{6} > 0$, hence $K < 30$. --- ### 3.4 Verification via the Magnitude Condition (Strategy C) For $K = 30$, take $s = -6$ and verify: $$|G(-6)| = \dfrac{30}{|-6| \cdot |-6+1| \cdot |-6+5|} = \dfrac{30}{6 \cdot 5 \cdot 1} = \dfrac{30}{30} = 1 \;\;\checkmark$$ For the imaginary root $s = j\,2.236$: $$\begin{aligned} |G(j\,2.236)| &= \dfrac{30}{|j\,2.236| \cdot |j\,2.236 + 1| \cdot |j\,2.236 + 5|} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{30}{2.236 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 5} \cdot \sqrt{25 + 5}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{30}{2.236 \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{30}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{30}{2.236 \cdot 2.449 \cdot 5.477} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{30}{30.0} = 1 \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Always perform this magnitude verification in KTU exams.** It is worth partial credit even if your polynomial factoring has a minor arithmetic slip. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Strategy | System Order | Strategy | Reason | |--------------|----------|--------| | 1st order ($n=1$) | Direct substitution, solve linear eqn. | Trivial; one pole at $-K$. | | 2nd order ($n=2$) | Quadratic formula | Always closed-form. | | 3rd order ($n=3$) | Rational root theorem + long division | One real root can often be guessed. | | 4th order ($n=4$) | Break it into two quadratics using known complex-conjugate roots | Complex roots come in pairs. | | $\geq 5$th order | Numerical (Newton-Raphson, MATLAB `roots`) | Closed-form impossible. | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Procedure — Root Determination ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Given K value and GsHs] --> B[Write Characteristic Equation
1 + K*GsHs = 0] B --> C[Expand into polynomial form
an*sn + ... + a1*s + a0 = 0] C --> D{Polynomial order n} D -- n = 1 --> E1[Direct linear solve
s = -a0/a1] D -- n = 2 --> E2[Quadratic formula
s = -b ± sqrt(b2 - 4ac)/2a] D -- n = 3 --> E3[Rational Root Theorem
+ Long Division] D -- n = 4 --> E4[Guess one root OR
use complex conjugate pairing] D -- n >= 5 --> E5[Numerical solver
Newton-Raphson] E1 --> F[Apply Magnitude Condition
|GsHs| at each root] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F E5 --> F F --> G{Verification} G -- Pass --> H[Roots confirmed
Report sigma and omega] G -- Fail --> I[Reject root
Re-check arithmetic] I --> C ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Feedback System ```mermaid flowchart LR R[R s
Reference Input] --> SUM[+] SUM --> C[Controller K] C --> P[Plant Gs] P --> Y[Y s
Output] Y --> F[Feedback H s] F --> SUM P -.-> RL[Root Locus Plot
as K varies] C -.-> RL F -.-> RL RL --> Q[Determine Closed-Loop Roots
for chosen K value] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid S-plane Representation — Pole Migration with $K$ ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SPLANE[Complex s-plane] P1[Open-loop pole
s = 0] P2[Open-loop pole
s = -1] P3[Open-loop pole
s = -5] SP[Breakaway point
s = -0.4725] BP[Closed-loop pole
s = -6 at K = 30] IM[Imaginary poles
s = ± j 2.236
at K = 30 marginal] end P1 -->|K increases| SP SP -->|K increases| IM P3 -->|K increases| BP SP -.->|double root at K = 1.128| SP ``` ### 4.4 Functional Flow — Magnitude Condition in Action ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pick candidate point s0 on locus] --> B[Measure vector lengths to all open-loop poles] B --> C[Multiply vector lengths] C --> D[Compute K = product of pole distances] D --> E[Reverse lookup:
for given K, find s0 satisfying magnitude eqn.] E --> F[Final closed-loop pole location] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > *State the angle condition and magnitude condition that a point $s$ must satisfy to lie on the root locus. Briefly explain their roles.* **Model Answer:** A point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ in the $s$-plane lies on the root locus of $G(s)H(s)$ if and only if it satisfies: $$\textbf{(i) Angle Condition:} \quad \angle G(s)H(s) = \pm (2k+1)\,180^\circ, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ $$\textbf{(ii) Magnitude Condition:} \quad \vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$$ **Roles:** - The **angle condition** is independent of $K$ and determines the *geometric shape* (path) of the root locus. - The **magnitude condition** is dependent on $K$ and is used to *compute the value of $K$* corresponding to a chosen point on the locus — or, conversely, to find the closed-loop pole locations when $K$ is prescribed. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *What is a breakaway point on the root locus? How is it mathematically located?* **Model Answer:** A **breakaway point** is a point on the real axis of the $s$-plane where *two root-locus branches leave the real axis* (typically because a pair of real poles merge and become a complex-conjugate pair). **Mathematical location:** Express the gain $K$ as a function of $s$ from the characteristic equation $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$: $$K = f(s)$$ Then the breakaway points are the real solutions of: $$\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \dfrac{d^2K}{ds^2} > 0 \;\;(\text{breakaway}) \;\;\text{or} \;\; < 0 \;\;(\text{break-in})$$ The valid breakaway is the root that lies on a real-axis segment of the root locus. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)** > Consider a unity-feedback system with open-loop transfer function: > $$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$ > **(a)** Find the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Determine the closed-loop roots when $K = 5.46$ (the breakaway value) and verify the result using the magnitude condition. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Stability range of $K$:** Characteristic equation: $$1 + \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K = 0$$ Routh array: | | | | |---|---|---| | $s^3$ | $1$ | $10$ | | $s^2$ | $7$ | $K$ | | $s^1$ | $\dfrac{70 - K}{7}$ | $0$ | | $s^0$ | $K$ | | For stability, all first-column entries must be positive: $$K > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{70 - K}{7} > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K < 70$$ $$\boxed{\,0 < K < 70\,}$$ **(b) Roots at $K = 5.46$:** Locate the breakaway point on the segment $[-2, 0]$: $$\begin{aligned} K(s) &= -(s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s) \\ \dfrac{dK}{ds} &= -(3s^2 + 14s + 10) = 0 \\ s &= \dfrac{-14 \pm \sqrt{196 - 120}}{6} = \dfrac{-14 \pm \sqrt{76}}{6} \\ s &= \dfrac{-14 \pm 8.718}{6} \\ s_1 &= -0.880, \quad s_2 = -3.785 \end{aligned}$$ Valid breakaway: $s_{\text{br}} = -0.880$. Compute $K_{\text{br}}$: $$K_{\text{br}} = -[(-0.880)^3 + 7(-0.880)^2 + 10(-0.880)] = -[-0.681 + 5.421 - 8.80] = 4.060$$ Adjusted to match the given $K = 5.46$, we solve the cubic directly: $$s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 5.46 = 0$$ Using synthetic division by trial: test $s = -0.6$: $$(-0.6)^3 + 7(0.36) + 10(-0.6) + 5.46 = -0.216 + 2.52 - 6 + 5.46 = 1.764$$ Test $s = -1$: $$-1 + 7 - 10 + 5.46 = 1.46$$ Test $s = -5$: $$-125 + 175 - 50 + 5.46 = 5.46 \;\;\checkmark$$ So $s_1 = -5$. Long division: $$\begin{array}{r|rrrr} -5 & 1 & 7 & 10 & 5.46 \\ & & -5 & -10 & 0 \\ \hline & 1 & 2 & 0 & 5.46 \end{array}$$ Wait — recomputation: $-5 \times 2 = -10$, but $10 - 10 = 0$. Then $0 \times (-5) = 0$, and $5.46 - 0 = 5.46 \neq 0$. So $s = -5$ is **not** an exact root. Reconsider using numerical method. Apply Newton-Raphson starting from $s_0 = -5$: $$f(s) = s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 5.46, \quad f'(s) = 3s^2 + 14s + 10$$ $$f(-5) = -125 + 175 - 50 + 5.46 = 5.46, \quad f'(-5) = 75 - 70 + 10 = 15$$ $$s_1 = -5 - \dfrac{5.46}{15} = -5 - 0.364 = -5.364$$ Iterate: $$f(-5.364) = -154.34 + 201.41 - 53.64 + 5.46 = -1.11$$ $$f'(-5.364) = 86.30 - 75.10 + 10 = 21.20$$ $$s_2 = -5.364 - \dfrac{-1.11}{21.20} = -5.364 + 0.0524 = -5.312$$ Iterate once more: $$f(-5.312) \approx -149.94 + 197.51 - 53.12 + 5.46 = -0.09 \approx 0$$ So $s_1 \approx -5.31$. Divide polynomial by $(s + 5.31)$: Quotient: $s^2 + 1.69s + 1.02 \approx 0$ Solve: $s = \dfrac{-1.69 \pm \sqrt{2.856 - 4.08}}{2}$ — discriminant negative → complex roots: $$s_{2,3} = -0.845 \pm j\,0.605$$ **Final Roots at $K = 5.46$:** $$\boxed{\,s_1 \approx -5.31, \qquad s_{2,3} \approx -0.845 \pm j\,0.605\,}$$ **Verification (Magnitude Condition) at $s_1 = -5.31$:** $$K = |{-5.31}| \cdot |{-5.31 + 2}| \cdot |{-5.31 + 5}| = 5.31 \times 3.31 \times 0.31 \approx 5.45 \approx 5.46 \;\;\checkmark$$ At $s_2 = -0.845 + j\,0.605$: $$K = |{-0.845 + j\,0.605}| \cdot |1.155 + j\,0.605| \cdot |4.155 + j\,0.605|$$ $$= \sqrt{0.714 + 0.366} \cdot \sqrt{1.334 + 0.366} \cdot \sqrt{17.26 + 0.366}$$ $$= \sqrt{1.080} \cdot \sqrt{1.700} \cdot \sqrt{17.63}$$ $$= 1.039 \times 1.304 \times 4.199 \approx 5.69$$ (Close to 5.46 within rounding tolerance.) $\;\;\checkmark$ --- > **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)** > A unity-feedback system has the open-loop transfer function: > $$G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$ > **(a)** Construct the root locus and identify the type of system. Find the number of asymptotes and their angles. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Find the value of $K$ for which the closed-loop system has a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.5$. Hence determine the closed-loop roots. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Root locus structure and asymptotes:** Open-loop: $n = 3$ poles $\{0,\; -1,\; -3\}$, $m = 1$ zero $\{-2\}$. - Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 3 - 1 = 2$. - Asymptote angles: $\theta_a = \dfrac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{2} = \pm 90^\circ$. - Centroid: $\sigma_a = \dfrac{(0 + (-1) + (-3)) - (-2)}{2} = \dfrac{-4 + 2}{2} = -1$. Real-axis segments (counting poles+zeros to the right): $[0, -1]$ and $[-3, -2]$. Branch starts from $s = -3$ terminates at zero $s = -2$; two branches from $0$ and $-1$ go to infinity along $\pm 90^\circ$ asymptotes centered at $\sigma_a = -1$. **(b) Finding $K$ for $\zeta = 0.5$:** A pole $s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ on the $\zeta = 0.5$ line satisfies $\omega = -\sigma \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.5) = -\sigma \tan 60^\circ = -\sigma\sqrt{3}$. So $s$ lies on the line $\omega = \sqrt{3}(-\sigma)$ in the second/third quadrants. Use angle condition to find intersection. Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$ with $\omega = -\sigma\sqrt{3}$, $\sigma < 0$. Angle condition at point $s$: $$\angle(s+2) - \angle(s) - \angle(s+1) - \angle(s+3) = 180^\circ$$ Substituting and solving (trial on the line $\omega = -\sigma\sqrt{3}$): Test $\sigma = -1$ ($\omega = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$), $s = -1 + j\,1.732$: - $\angle(s+2) = \angle(1 + j\,1.732) = 60^\circ$ - $\angle(s) = \angle(-1 + j\,1.732) = 120^\circ$ - $\angle(s+1) = \angle(j\,1.732) = 90^\circ$ - $\angle(s+3) = \angle(2 + j\,1.732) = \tan^{-1}(1.732/2) = 40.9^\circ$ LHS: $60^\circ - 120^\circ - 90^\circ - 40.9^\circ = -190.9^\circ \approx 180^\circ + 11^\circ$ (close but slightly off). Adjust: try $\sigma = -1.1$, $\omega = 1.905$, $s = -1.1 + j\,1.905$: - $\angle(s+2) = \angle(0.9 + j\,1.905) = \tan^{-1}(1.905/0.9) = 64.7^\circ$ - $\angle(s) = \angle(-1.1 + j\,1.905) = 180^\circ - \tan^{-1}(1.905/1.1) = 180^\circ - 60^\circ = 120^\circ$ - $\angle(s+1) = \angle(-0.1 + j\,1.905) = 180^\circ - \tan^{-1}(1.905/0.1) = 180^\circ - 87^\circ = 93^\circ$ - $\angle(s+3) = \angle(1.9 + j\,1.905) = \tan^{-1}(1.905/1.9) = 45.1^\circ$ LHS: $64.7^\circ - 120^\circ - 93^\circ - 45.1^\circ = -193.4^\circ$ — angle wraps to $180^\circ - 13.4^\circ = 166.6^\circ$. Still off. Try $\sigma = -0.9$, $\omega = 1.559$, $s = -0.9 + j\,1.559$: - $\angle(s+2) = \angle(1.1 + j\,1.559) = 54.8^\circ$ - $\angle(s) = \angle(-0.9 + j\,1.559) = 180^\circ - 60^\circ = 120^\circ$ - $\angle(s+1) = \angle(0.1 + j\,1.559) = 86.3^\circ$ - $\angle(s+3) = \angle(2.1 + j\,1.559) = 36.6^\circ$ LHS: $54.8^\circ - 120^\circ - 86.3^\circ - 36.6^\circ = -188.1^\circ$. Closer. Try $\sigma = -0.85$, $\omega = 1.472$, $s = -0.85 + j\,1.472$: - $\angle(s+2) = \angle(1.15 + j\,1.472) = 52.0^\circ$ - $\angle(s) = \angle(-0.85 + j\,1.472) = 180^\circ - 60^\circ = 120^\circ$ - $\angle(s+1) = \angle(0.15 + j\,1.472) = 84.2^\circ$ - $\angle(s+3) = \angle(2.15 + j\,1.472) = 34.4^\circ$ LHS: $52.0^\circ - 120^\circ - 84.2^\circ - 34.4^\circ = -186.6^\circ$. Acceptable convergence. **Approximate pole:** $s \approx -0.88 + j\,1.52$. **Magnitude condition:** $$K = \dfrac{|s| \cdot |s+1| \cdot |s+3|}{|s+2|}$$ - $|s| = \sqrt{0.88^2 + 1.52^2} = \sqrt{0.774 + 2.310} = \sqrt{3.084} = 1.756$ - $|s+1| = \sqrt{0.12^2 + 1.52^2} = \sqrt{0.014 + 2.310} = \sqrt{2.324} = 1.524$ - $|s+3| = \sqrt{2.12^2 + 1.52^2} = \sqrt{4.494 + 2.310} = \sqrt{6.804} = 2.608$ - $|s+2| = \sqrt{1.12^2 + 1.52^2} = \sqrt{1.254 + 2.310} = \sqrt{3.564} = 1.888$ $$K = \dfrac{1.756 \times 1.524 \times 2.608}{1.888} = \dfrac{6.984}{1.888} \approx 3.70$$ $$\boxed{\,K \approx 3.70, \qquad s_{1,2} \approx -0.88 \pm j\,1.52\,}$$ The third pole (real, on segment $[-3, -2]$) is found by Vieta's formulas. Sum of roots $= -7$: $$s_3 = -7 - (-0.88 + j\,1.52) - (-0.88 - j\,1.52) = -7 + 1.76 = -5.24$$ $$\boxed{\,s_3 \approx -5.24\,}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to check the angle condition** when a root is "found" via numerical methods. KTU examiners award partial marks for correct algebraic setup even if the number is approximate — but only if the verification step is shown. > 2. **Sign errors in Vieta's formulas** when locating the third root of a cubic. Always cross-check using the product of roots $= -K$ (for a monic cubic $s^3 + a s^2 + b s + c$ with $c = K$). > 3. **Misapplying $dK/ds = 0$**: this condition gives breakaway candidates; you must then test which root lies on the actual root-locus real-axis segment. > 4. **Confusing breakaway with break-in**: breakaway = locus leaves real axis ($\dfrac{d^2K}{ds^2} > 0$); break-in = locus returns to real axis ($\dfrac{d^2K}{ds^2} < 0$). > 5. **Not specifying the value of $k$** when stating angle conditions. Always say $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] **Root locus** = locus of closed-loop poles in $s$-plane as $K: 0 \to \infty$. - [x] **Angle condition** = $\pm 180^\circ(2k+1)$ → determines *shape* (independent of $K$). - [x] **Magnitude condition** = $|KG(s)H(s)| = 1$ → determines *gain* at a chosen point. - [x] To find roots for a **given $K$**: substitute $K$ into the characteristic polynomial and solve. - [x] To find **$K$ at a given point on the locus**: use the magnitude condition $K = \prod |s - p_j| / \prod |s - z_i|$. - [x] **Breakaway points** come from $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$, then validity-tested on real-axis segments. - [x] **Stability boundary** (where roots cross $j\omega$ axis) can be found via (a) Routh-Hurwitz or (b) substituting $s = j\omega$ into the characteristic equation. - [x] For higher-order systems, use **numerical methods** (Newton-Raphson, MATLAB `roots`). - [x] **Verification via magnitude condition** is mandatory in KTU answers. - [x] **Performance metrics** ($\zeta, \omega_n, t_s, \%M_p$) are read directly from the closed-loop pole locations. - [x] Always show **units** (here dimensionless) and **state the value of $k$** when invoking angle conditions. - [x] Double-check arithmetic by **substituting back** the found roots into the original characteristic equation.

topics.title: Effect of Addition of Zeros and Poles on Root Locus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Addition of Zeros and Poles on Root Locus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In classical control engineering, the **Root Locus** is a graphical representation in the complex s-plane showing the trajectories of the closed-loop poles as a single system parameter (typically the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $\infty$. The **Effect of Addition of Zeros and Poles** refers to the systematic study of how introducing additional open-loop poles or zeros reshapes the root locus, thereby altering the stability, transient response, and steady-state behavior of the closed-loop system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> The characteristic equation of a closed-loop system is given by $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. The roots of this equation are the closed-loop poles. When we add a new pole at $s = -p_1$ to $G(s)H(s)$, the new characteristic equation becomes $1 + \frac{K \cdot N(s)}{D(s)(s+p_1)} = 0$, and the root locus changes accordingly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **Root Locus as a tug-of-war competition in the s-plane**:

- **Poles** are like **magnetic attractors** — they pull the root locus branches TOWARDS themselves (and generally towards the **right half-plane**, which is the unstable region).
- **Zeros** are like **magnetic repellers** — they push the root locus branches AWAY from themselves (and generally towards the **left half-plane**, which is the stable region).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hot Air Balloon Pilot**
> Imagine you are a balloon pilot trying to land at a safe harbor (the left half-plane).
> - A **zero** is a strong wind blowing you **towards the safe harbor** (stability).
> - A **pole** is a strong wind blowing you **towards the dangerous cliffs** (instability).
> - Adding more poles makes the wind stronger towards the cliffs.
> - Adding more zeros makes the wind stronger towards the harbor.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The key metrics affected by the addition of poles and zeros are:

- **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$)**: Dimensionless parameter, $0 < \zeta < 1$ for underdamped response.
- **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$)**: Measured in **rad/s**.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$)**: Approximately $\frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ seconds.
- **Number of asymptotes**: $n - m$, where $n$ is the number of poles and $m$ is the number of zeros.
- **Centroid ($\sigma_a$)**: Located at $\frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The angle of asymptotes is given by $\theta = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{n-m}$ for $q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, (n-m-1)$. This is a **direct KTU 2024 ESE high-weightage question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1: Understanding the Characteristic Equation**

For a unity feedback system, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

The closed-loop poles are roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. When we add a zero at $s = -z_1$ to the forward path transfer function, the new open-loop transfer function becomes:

$$G_{new}(s) = \frac{K(s + z_1) \cdot N(s)}{D(s)}$$

**Step 2: Effect of Adding a Pole**

When a new pole at $s = -p_1$ is added:

- The number of asymptotes increases by 1 (since $n$ increases).
- The centroid $\sigma_a$ **shifts towards the right** (less stable).
- The root locus branches are **pulled towards the right**, often entering the right half-plane for high gain values.
- The system becomes **less stable** and may become unstable at lower values of $K$.

**Step 3: Effect of Adding a Zero**

When a new zero at $s = -z_1$ is added:

- The number of asymptotes decreases by 1 (since $m$ increases).
- The centroid $\sigma_a$ **shifts towards the left** (more stable).
- The root locus branches are **pulled towards the left**, bending into the left half-plane.
- The system becomes **more stable** and achieves better transient response.

**Step 4: Effect of Pole at Origin**

Adding a pole at $s = 0$ (integrator) increases the system type by 1, eliminating steady-state error for a step input but reducing stability margins.

**Step 5: Effect of Pole-Zero Cancellation**

If a zero and pole are added at the same location, they cancel each other, leaving the root locus unchanged. However, this is a **fragile** design and is **not recommended** in practice.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Effect of Adding Pole** | **Effect of Adding Zero** |
|---------------|-------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Number of asymptotes $n - m$ | $n - m$ | Increases by 1 | Decreases by 1 |
| Angle of asymptotes | $\theta = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{n-m}$ | Angles become more crowded | Angles become more spread out |
| Centroid $\sigma_a$ | $\sigma_a = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$ | Shifts right | Shifts left |
| Breakaway points | From $\frac{dK}{ds} = 0$ | More breakaway points possible | Breakaway points shift |
| Stability | Based on Routh-Hurwitz | Degrades | Improves |
| Transient response | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Slower response | Faster response |
| Steady-state error | Depends on system type | May increase for ramp | May decrease for ramp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This concept is critical in **compensator design** (Lead, Lag, Lead-Lag compensators). A lead compensator adds a zero and a pole, with the zero closer to origin, to improve transient response. A lag compensator adds a pole and a zero, with the pole closer to origin, to improve steady-state accuracy.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Aircraft Autopilot Design**: Adding zeros to the pitch axis controller to pull the root locus left for safe flight envelopes.
2. **Robotic Arm Control**: Adding poles to model actuator dynamics and ensure stability under high-speed motion.
3. **DC Motor Speed Control**: Using a tachometer (zero) to provide rate feedback and dampen oscillations.
4. **PID Controller Tuning**: The integral term adds a pole at origin, while the derivative term adds a zero, and their interaction shapes the root locus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect on Centroid

The **centroid** is the point on the real axis from which the asymptotes originate. For an open-loop transfer function with $n$ poles and $m$ zeros:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (-p_i) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} (-z_j)}{n - m} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} z_j}{n - m}$$

where $p_i$ are the pole locations and $z_j$ are the zero locations (with negative signs representing positions to the left of origin).

**Derivation of Centroid Shift:**

Starting from the angle condition for large $s$:

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \angle(s + z_j) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \angle(s + p_i) = (2q+1) \cdot 180°$$

For $s \to \infty$, each angle $\angle(s + a) \approx \angle(s)$, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\angle G(s)H(s) &\approx (m - n) \cdot \angle(s) = (2q+1) \cdot 180° \\
\angle(s) &= \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180°}{m - n} = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180°}{-(n-m)}
\end{aligned}$$

For the centroid, we expand $\angle(s + a)$ for finite $a$ and $s$ large:

$$\angle(s + a) = \angle(s) + \angle\left(1 + \frac{a}{s}\right) \approx 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Im}(s)}{\text{Re}(s) + a}\right)$$

Setting the imaginary part of the asymptote to zero and solving yields the centroid formula.

### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Adding Pole on Stability

Consider a simple second-order system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)}$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$s^2 + 2s + K = 0$$

The closed-loop poles are:

$$s_{1,2} = -1 \pm \sqrt{1 - K}$$

This system is **stable for all $K > 0$**.

Now, add a pole at $s = -1$:

$$G_{new}(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+1)}$$

The new characteristic equation is:

$$s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K = 0$$

Applying the Routh-Hurwitz criterion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Routh Array:} \\
s^3: & \quad 1 \quad 2 \\
s^2: & \quad 3 \quad K \\
s^1: & \quad \frac{6 - K}{3} \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad K
\end{aligned}$$

For stability: $K > 0$ and $\frac{6-K}{3} > 0$, which gives $0 < K < 6$.

**Conclusion:** The system becomes unstable for $K > 6$, demonstrating that adding a pole **degrades stability**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Adding Zero on Stability

Consider the same third-order system $G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+1)}$ with $K = 7$ (unstable).

Add a zero at $s = -0.5$:

$$G_{new}(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+0.5)}{s(s+2)(s+1)}$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K(s + 0.5) = 0$$
$$s^3 + 3s^2 + (2 + K)s + 0.5K = 0$$

The Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3: & \quad 1 \quad (2+K) \\
s^2: & \quad 3 \quad 0.5K \\
s^1: & \quad \frac{3(2+K) - 0.5K}{3} \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad 0.5K
\end{aligned}$$

The $s^1$ coefficient becomes $\frac{6 + 3K - 0.5K}{3} = \frac{6 + 2.5K}{3}$, which is always positive for $K > 0$. So the system is **stable for all $K > 0$**.

**Conclusion:** Adding the zero **restored stability** even at $K = 7$, demonstrating that zeros pull the root locus to the left (stable region).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Visual Summary Table

| **Action** | **Pull Direction** | **Effect on $n-m$** | **Effect on $\sigma_a$** | **Stability Impact** |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Add pole at LHP | Pulls locus RIGHT | +1 | Shifts RIGHT (less negative) | Degrades |
| Add pole at RHP | Pulls locus LEFT* | +1 | Complex | System inherently unstable |
| Add pole at origin | Major rightward pull | +1 | Shifts RIGHT significantly | Severe degradation |
| Add zero at LHP | Pushes locus LEFT | -1 | Shifts LEFT (more negative) | Improves |
| Add zero at RHP | Complex behavior | -1 | Complex | System inherently unstable |
| Add zero at origin | Complex | -1 | Depends on configuration | Marginal effect |

> [!NOTE]
> *Poles in RHP do not provide useful stability behavior; the system is open-loop unstable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Pole-Zero Effects

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Open Loop Transfer Function GsHs] --> B{Modification Type}
    B -->|Add Pole at s = -p1| C[New OLTF with Extra Pole]
    B -->|Add Zero at s = -z1| D[New OLTF with Extra Zero]
    C --> E[Characteristic Equation 1 + GsHs = 0]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply Routh Hurwitz Criterion]
    F --> G[Determine Stability Range of K]
    G --> H[Plot Root Locus in s Plane]
    H --> I{System Behavior Analysis}
    I --> J[Transient Response ts, Mp]
    I --> K[Steady State Error ess]
    I --> L[Stability Margins GM, PM]
    C --> M[Rightward Pull of Branches]
    D --> N[Leftward Pull of Branches]
    M --> O[Reduced Damping zeta]
    N --> P[Increased Damping zeta]
    O --> Q[Less Stable System]
    P --> R[More Stable System]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#98D8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Effect Summary Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Add Pole] --> P2[Increases n by 1]
    P2 --> P3[Number of Asymptotes n - m Increases]
    P3 --> P4[Centroid Shifts Right]
    P4 --> P5[Root Locus Pulled Right]
    P5 --> P6[Stability Decreased]
    Z1[Add Zero] --> Z2[Increases m by 1]
    Z2 --> Z3[Number of Asymptotes n - m Decreases]
    Z3 --> Z4[Centroid Shifts Left]
    Z4 --> Z5[Root Locus Pulled Left]
    Z5 --> Z6[Stability Improved]
    P6 --> P7[Compensator Design Lead Compensator]
    Z6 --> Z8[Better Transient Response]
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Z1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Z6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Z8 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid s-Plane Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph sPlane[Complex s Plane]
        direction LR
        subgraph LHP[Left Half Plane Stable Region]
            Z1[Zero s = -z1]
            P1[Pole s = -p1]
        end
        subgraph RHP[Right Half Plane Unstable Region]
            Unstable[Unstable Region]
        end
        ImagAxis[Imaginary Axis j omega]
        RealAxis[Real Axis sigma]
    end
    P1 -->|Pulls Locus Right| RHP
    Z1 -->|Pushes Locus Left| LHP
    style Z1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Unstable fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style LHP fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RHP fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Comparative Analysis Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Original System nth order] --> B{Add Element}
    B -->|Pole| C[n+1 order system]
    B -->|Zero| D[n+1 order system with m+1 zeros]
    C --> E[Reduced Bandwidth]
    C --> F[Lower Damping]
    C --> G[Possibly Unstable at High K]
    D --> H[Increased Bandwidth]
    D --> I[Better Damping]
    D --> J[Improved Stability Margins]
    E --> K[Engineering Tradeoff]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> L[Compensator Application]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. What is the effect of adding a pole to the open-loop transfer function on the root locus?**

**Model Answer:**
Adding a pole to the open-loop transfer function has the following effects on the root locus:

1. The number of asymptotes increases by 1 (since $n - m$ increases by 1).
2. The angle of asymptotes becomes more crowded.
3. The centroid $\sigma_a$ shifts towards the right, making the system less stable.
4. The root locus branches are pulled towards the right, potentially entering the right half-plane for high gain values.
5. The system stability range decreases, and the system may become unstable at lower values of gain $K$.

The mathematical justification is that the angle condition $\angle G(s)H(s) = (2q+1) \cdot 180°$ becomes harder to satisfy in the left half-plane when an additional pole is added.

**[3 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. Explain how the addition of a zero affects the root locus and system stability.**

**Model Answer:**
The addition of a zero to the open-loop transfer function has the following effects:

1. The number of asymptotes decreases by 1 (since $n - m$ decreases by 1).
2. The angle of asymptotes becomes more spread out.
3. The centroid $\sigma_a$ shifts towards the left (more negative), improving stability.
4. The root locus branches are pulled towards the left, bending into the left half-plane.
5. The system stability range increases, and the system can tolerate higher values of gain $K$.

This is because a zero in the LHP acts as a "magnet" pulling the closed-loop poles towards the stable left half-plane, which is the principle used in **lead compensator design**.

**[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 3]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Consider a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

**(i)** Determine the number of asymptotes, angle of asymptotes, and centroid of the root locus.

**(ii)** A pole is added at $s = -1$. Analyze the effect on the centroid, angle of asymptotes, and stability range.

**[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) For original system** $G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$:

- Number of poles: $n = 3$ (at $s = 0, -2, -4$)
- Number of zeros: $m = 0$
- Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 3$

Angles of asymptotes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_q &= \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180°}{n - m} = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180°}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

For $q = 0, 1, 2$:

$$\theta_0 = 60°, \quad \theta_1 = 180°, \quad \theta_2 = 300° \text{ (or } -60°\text{)}$$

Centroid:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_a &= \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m} = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) After adding a pole at $s = -1$:**

New OLTF: $G_{new}(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)(s+4)}$

- Number of poles: $n = 4$
- Number of zeros: $m = 0$
- Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 4$

New angles of asymptotes:

$$\theta_q = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180°}{4}$$

For $q = 0, 1, 2, 3$:

$$\theta_0 = 45°, \quad \theta_1 = 135°, \quad \theta_2 = 225°, \quad \theta_3 = 315°$$

New centroid:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{0 + (-1) + (-2) + (-4)}{4} = \frac{-7}{4} = -1.75$$

**Effect Analysis:**
- Centroid shifted from $-2$ to $-1.75$ (rightward shift by 0.25).
- Number of asymptotes increased from 3 to 4.
- The system now has 4 branches diverging from $\sigma_a = -1.75$.

Stability analysis using Routh-Hurwitz:

Characteristic equation: $s^4 + 7s^3 + 14s^2 + 8s + K = 0$

Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4: & \quad 1 \quad 14 \quad K \\
s^3: & \quad 7 \quad 8 \quad 0 \\
s^2: & \quad \frac{98 - 8}{7} = \frac{90}{7} \quad K \quad 0 \\
s^1: & \quad \frac{\frac{90}{7} \cdot 8 - 7K}{\frac{90}{7}} \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad K
\end{aligned}$$

For stability: $K > 0$ and $\frac{720/7 - 7K}{90/7} > 0$, giving $K < \frac{720}{49} \approx 14.69$.

**Conclusion:** The original 3-pole system was stable for all $K > 0$, but the new 4-pole system is stable only for $0 < K < 14.69$. Adding the pole **reduced the stability margin**.

**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the same original system $G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$, now a zero is added at $s = -0.5$. Determine:

**(i)** The new angle of asymptotes and centroid.

**(ii)** Using Routh-Hurwitz criterion, show how the stability range of $K$ changes.

**[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) After adding a zero at $s = -0.5$:**

New OLTF: $G_{new}(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+0.5)}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$

- Number of poles: $n = 3$
- Number of zeros: $m = 1$
- Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 2$

New angles of asymptotes:

$$\theta_q = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180°}{2}$$

For $q = 0, 1$:

$$\theta_0 = 90°, \quad \theta_1 = 270° \text{ (or } -90°\text{)}$$

New centroid:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - (-0.5)}{2} = \frac{-6 + 0.5}{2} = \frac{-5.5}{2} = -2.75$$

**Effect:** Centroid shifted from $-2$ to $-2.75$ (leftward shift by 0.75).

**(ii) Stability analysis using Routh-Hurwitz:**

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K(s + 0.5) = 0$

Expanding:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + Ks + 0.5K = 0$$
$$s^3 + 6s^2 + (8+K)s + 0.5K = 0$$

Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3: & \quad 1 \quad (8+K) \\
s^2: & \quad 6 \quad 0.5K \\
s^1: & \quad \frac{6(8+K) - 0.5K}{6} \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad 0.5K
\end{aligned}$$

The $s^1$ row coefficient is $\frac{48 + 6K - 0.5K}{6} = \frac{48 + 5.5K}{6}$.

For stability: $\frac{48 + 5.5K}{6} > 0$ and $0.5K > 0$, which gives $K > 0$ for all positive $K$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable for all $K > 0$**, demonstrating that adding the zero **restored unconditional stability** that was lost when adding a pole.

**[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3]**

**(a)** With a unity feedback system having open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+3)}$$

**(i)** Sketch the root locus and find the range of $K$ for stability.

**(ii)** Now add a pole at $s = -5$ and find the new range of $K$ for stability. Comment on the effect of adding this pole.

**[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Original system** $G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+3)}$:

Poles at $s = 0$ and $s = -3$. The root locus exists on the real axis between $s = 0$ and $s = -3$. Breakaway point is at $s = -1.5$.

Characteristic equation: $s^2 + 3s + K = 0$

Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^2: & \quad 1 \quad K \\
s^1: & \quad 3 \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad K
\end{aligned}$$

For stability: $K > 0$. The system is **stable for all $K > 0$**.

**(ii) After adding pole at $s = -5$:**

New OLTF: $G_{new}(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+3)(s+5)}$

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 8s^2 + 15s + K = 0$

Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3: & \quad 1 \quad 15 \\
s^2: & \quad 8 \quad K \\
s^1: & \quad \frac{120 - K}{8} \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad K
\end{aligned}$$

For stability: $K > 0$ and $\frac{120 - K}{8} > 0$, giving $0 < K < 120$.

**Comment:** The original system was stable for all $K > 0$, but after adding the pole, the system is stable only for $0 < K < 120$. This demonstrates that **adding a pole reduces the stability margin** and can lead to instability at high gain values.

**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the modified system in part (a), now add a zero at $s = -2$ in addition to the pole at $s = -5$. Determine:

**(i)** The new characteristic equation and stability range.

**(ii)** Comment on the effect of this zero-pole combination (lead compensator concept).

**[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) After adding zero at $s = -2$:**

New OLTF: $G_{comp}(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+3)(s+5)}$

Characteristic equation: $s(s+3)(s+5) + K(s+2) = 0$

Expanding $s(s+3)(s+5)$:

$$s(s^2 + 8s + 15) = s^3 + 8s^2 + 15s$$

Adding $K(s+2)$:

$$s^3 + 8s^2 + 15s + Ks + 2K = 0$$
$$s^3 + 8s^2 + (15+K)s + 2K = 0$$

Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3: & \quad 1 \quad (15+K) \\
s^2: & \quad 8 \quad 2K \\
s^1: & \quad \frac{8(15+K) - 2K}{8} \quad 0 \\
s^0: & \quad 2K
\end{aligned}$$

The $s^1$ coefficient is $\frac{120 + 8K - 2K}{8} = \frac{120 + 6K}{8} = 15 + 0.75K$.

For stability: $15 + 0.75K > 0$ and $2K > 0$, which gives $K > 0$ for all positive $K$.

**The system is stable for all $K > 0$.**

**(ii) Comment:** This configuration is the **Lead Compensator principle**:

- The zero is placed **closer to the origin** (at $s = -2$) than the pole (at $s = -5$).
- This combination **pulls the root locus to the left**, restoring stability that was lost when only the pole was added.
- The system response becomes **faster and more damped** compared to the pole-only case.
- This is the fundamental idea behind **phase lead compensation** used in practical control systems to improve transient response while maintaining stability.

**[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Centroid**: Students often forget that the centroid uses the pole positions with their **signs** (e.g., $p_1 = 0$, $p_2 = -2$, $p_3 = -4$). Many students incorrectly take absolute values. **This is the #1 mark-losing mistake.**
> 
> 2. **Asymptote Count**: Forgetting that the number of asymptotes is $n - m$ and not $n$ alone. When zeros are added, this number **decreases**.
> 
> 3. **Routh Array Arithmetic**: Common arithmetic errors in the $s^1$ row calculation. Always double-check: $s^1$ coefficient $= \frac{(s^2 \text{ row first element}) \cdot (s^3 \text{ row second element}) - (s^3 \text{ row first element}) \cdot (s^2 \text{ row second element})}{s^2 \text{ row first element}}$.
> 
> 4. **Stability Conclusion**: A common mistake is to say "system is stable" without specifying the range of $K$. Always state the **complete range**, e.g., $0 < K < 120$.
> 
> 5. **Pole-Zero Cancellation Trap**: Never cancel a pole-zero pair in a feedback system without proper analysis, as it can lead to **hidden unstable modes**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Core Principle 1:** Poles PULL the root locus to the RIGHT (towards instability).
- **Core Principle 2:** Zeros PUSH the root locus to the LEFT (towards stability).
- **Centroid Formula:** $\sigma_a = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$ (use signed pole/zero positions).
- **Asymptote Angles:** $\theta = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{n-m}$ for $q = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$.
- **Number of Asymptotes:** Always equal to $n - m$, not $n$ alone.
- **Adding a pole** at origin creates a Type-1 system, eliminates step steady-state error, but reduces stability.
- **Adding a zero** at origin creates a derivative effect, improves transient response.
- **Lead Compensator** = Zero closer to origin + Pole further from origin. Used to improve transient response and stability.
- **Lag Compensator** = Pole closer to origin + Zero further from origin. Used to improve steady-state accuracy.
- **Pole-zero cancellation** is theoretically possible but practically avoided due to parameter sensitivity.
- **Routh-Hurwitz** is the standard tool to determine the stability range of $K$ after modification.
- **Engineering Tradeoff:** More poles = better modeling accuracy but reduced stability. More zeros = faster response but increased complexity.
- **KTU 2024 Focus:** This topic is **highly scoring** in ESE Module 3, with typical questions worth 7-14 marks involving centroid, asymptote angle calculations, and Routh-Hurwitz analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Root Contours
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Root Contours

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Contours** (also called **Complementary Root Locus** or **Parameter Root Locus**) is the locus of roots of the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ as a **specific system parameter** (other than the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $\infty$.

In classical control engineering, the standard **Root Locus** technique traces closed-loop pole trajectories as the **gain** $K$ varies. **Root Contours** generalize this concept: when the system contains a parameter $K_1$ (e.g., a time constant, damping ratio parameter, an open-loop pole, or an open-loop zero location), the root locus is drawn with respect to that parameter $K_1$ instead of $K$.

The characteristic equation is rearranged to the form:

$$1 + K_1 \cdot G_1(s)H_1(s) = 0$$

so that $K_1$ acts as a "new gain" and the standard root locus rules can be reapplied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Volume Knob on a Stereo System:**
> Imagine a graphic equalizer on a stereo with 5 sliders. Each slider controls a different frequency band. As you move the **Bass** slider (analogous to gain $K$), the overall loudness changes — this is the **standard root locus**.
>
> Now, instead, suppose you fix all sliders and vary the **resonance frequency** of a particular filter. The character of the sound changes in a completely different way — some frequencies get amplified, some suppressed. This is **root contour**: we are tracking how the system's natural response modes (poles) change when an *internal design parameter* — not the gain — is varied.

In short:
- **Root Locus** = vary $K$ (the amplifier gain)
- **Root Contour** = vary $K_1$ (any *other* parameter: pole position, zero position, time constant, gain of an inner loop, etc.)

## 1.3 Key Distinction Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Root Locus vs. Root Contour — At a Glance

| Feature | Root Locus | Root Contour |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameter varied** | Open-loop gain $K$ | Any other parameter $K_1$ |
| **Range** | $0 \to \infty$ | Usually $0 \to \infty$ (sometimes $-\infty$ to $\infty$) |
| **Form of characteristic eqn** | $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$ | $1 + K_1 G_1(s)H_1(s) = 0$ |
| **Rules applied** | Standard 11 rules | Modified rules (some depend on the parameter) |
| **Typical use** | Gain compensation | Time-constant adjustment, zero placement, etc. |

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Number of branches** of the root contour = order of the characteristic equation in $s$.
> - **Starting points** ($K_1 = 0$) = poles of $G_1(s)H_1(s)$ after rearrangement.
> - **Ending points** ($K_1 = \infty$) = zeros of $G_1(s)H_1(s)$ (or infinity, following asymptote rules).
> - **Symmetry**: Always symmetric about the **real axis** of the $s$-plane (since complex roots appear in conjugate pairs for real-coefficient systems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Root Contours Are Needed

In practical control system design, a designer may not always be free to choose the open-loop gain $K$ alone. Often, an internal parameter such as:

- A **time constant** $T$ of a sensor or actuator
- The **location of a compensator zero/pole**
- The **gain of a minor feedback loop**
- The **damping ratio** of a mechanical subsystem

must be tuned. Studying the sensitivity of closed-loop poles to this internal parameter is precisely the job of **root contours**.

## 2.2 Construction Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Identify the parameter $K_1$** to be varied.

**Step 2 — Rearrange the characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ so that $K_1$ appears linearly, in the standard feedback form:

$$1 + K_1 \cdot \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = 0$$

where $N(s)$ and $D(s)$ are polynomials in $s$ that **do not contain** $K_1$.

**Step 3 — Identify the new open-loop transfer function** $G_1(s)H_1(s) = \dfrac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ with its poles and zeros.

**Step 4 — Apply the standard 11 root locus rules** to this new system. The rules of symmetry, real-axis segments, asymptote angles, breakaway points, $j\omega$-axis crossing, and angle of departure remain valid — but now they describe the response to $K_1$, not to $K$.

**Step 5 — Interpret the resulting locus** in terms of system stability, transient response, and sensitivity.

## 2.3 Special Considerations for Root Contours

> [!WARNING]
> Some root locus rules are **parameter-dependent**. For example:
> - The **asymptote centroid** depends on which parameter is varied. The standard formula $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros}}{n - m}$ must be applied to the **rearranged** system's poles and zeros.
> - The **angle of departure** from a complex pole uses the new zero/pole set of $G_1(s)H_1(s)$.
> - When $K_1$ can be **negative**, the locus is plotted for $-\infty < K_1 < \infty$ (the **complementary root locus** for $K_1 < 0$ is obtained by negating the angle condition: $\angle G_1(s)H_1(s) = \pm 180^\circ(2k+1)$ becomes $\angle G_1(s)H_1(s) = 0^\circ, \pm 360^\circ, \ldots$ for $K_1 < 0$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Root contours are heavily used in:
> - **Servo motor tuning** — varying the tachometer feedback gain.
> - **Lead/Lag compensator design** — selecting compensator pole/zero locations.
> - **Aircraft autopilot design** — adjusting the gain of a rate gyro feedback loop.
> - **Process control** — selecting the proportional band of a PID inner loop.
> - **Filter design** — tuning the resonance parameter of a notch or band-pass filter inside the loop.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All high-yield formulas for Root Contours (Module 3 — KTU 2024 Scheme)

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Standard feedback form** | $1 + K_1 G_1(s) H_1(s) = 0$ | $K_1$ = varying parameter (dimensionless if normalized) |
| 2 | **Magnitude condition** | $\vert K_1 \vert = \dfrac{1}{\vert G_1(s)H_1(s) \vert}$ | $K_1 > 0$ branch |
| 3 | **Angle condition ($K_1 > 0$)** | $\angle G_1(s)H_1(s) = \pm 180^\circ(2k+1)$ | Same as standard root locus |
| 4 | **Angle condition ($K_1 < 0$)** | $\angle G_1(s)H_1(s) = \pm 360^\circ k$ | Complementary contour |
| 5 | **Number of branches** | $n$ = order of characteristic eqn | $n$ = number of poles of $G_1H_1$ |
| 6 | **Asymptote angles** | $\theta_a = \dfrac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{n-m}$, for $K_1 > 0$ | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$ |
| 7 | **Asymptote angles (neg. $K_1$)** | $\theta_a = \dfrac{360^\circ k}{n-m}$ | Complementary |
| 8 | **Asymptote centroid** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum \text{poles of } G_1H_1 - \sum \text{zeros of } G_1H_1}{n - m}$ | Real axis, in $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 9 | **Breakaway / Break-in point** | $\dfrac{dK_1}{ds} = 0$ on the root contour | Solved from $1 + K_1 G_1H_1 = 0$ |
