The system is stable for $K < K_{max}$ and unstable for $K > K_{max}$ (in this problem context). > 7. **Decibel vs Linear:** Be explicit about whether your answer is in dB or in linear scale. A common error is mixing them in the final stability statement. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember * **Gain Margin (GM)** is measured at the **phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$** (where phase = $-180^\circ$). * **Phase Margin (PM)** is measured at the **gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$** (where magnitude = 0 dB). * **Stability condition:** $GM > 1$ (or $GM_{dB} > 0$) **and** $PM > 0^\circ$. * **Well-designed systems** typically have $PM$ between $30^\circ$ and $60^\circ$ and $GM$ greater than $6$ dB. * **Damping approximation:** $\zeta \approx \frac{PM}{100}$ (when PM is in degrees and less than $70^\circ$). * **Overshoot approximation:** $\%M_p \approx (0.6 \times PM) - 4$ for $40^\circ < PM < 70^\circ$. * **GM in dB** is calculated as the **negative** of the magnitude in dB at $\omega_{pc}$. * **PM in degrees** is calculated as $180^\circ$ plus the phase angle at $\omega_{gc}$. * For a **Type 0 system** with no integrators, the phase starts at $0^\circ$ and asymptotically approaches some value; it may or may not reach $-180^\circ$. * For a **Type 1 system** (one integrator), the phase starts at $-90^\circ$ and can at most reach $-270^\circ$. If the phase never crosses $-180^\circ$, the GM is infinite. * For a **Type 2 system** (two integrators), the phase starts at $-180^\circ$ and is **always unstable** unless a phase-lead compensator is added. * The **gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$** is closely related to the **closed-loop bandwidth** and the system's speed of response. * The **phase margin** determines the **damping** and **overshoot** of the step response. * The **gain margin** determines the system's tolerance to **gain variations** at low frequencies. * Always perform a **Nyquist plot verification** if the Bode plot interpretation is ambiguous (especially for non-minimum phase systems or systems with time delays). * **Conditional stability**: A system can have positive GM and PM and still be unstable (e.g., if there are multiple gain crossover frequencies); in such cases, the Nyquist criterion is the final word. * **Reading a Bode plot**: The magnitude plot is read at $\omega_{pc}$ for GM; the phase plot is read at $\omega_{gc}$ for PM. * **Sign of GM in dB**: $GM_{dB} = -M_{dB}(\omega_{pc})$. If the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ is negative (below 0 dB), then $GM_{dB}$ is positive (stable). If the magnitude is positive (above 0 dB), then $GM_{dB}$ is negative (unstable). * **Inverted Stability Logic**: If the phase plot crosses $-180^\circ$ *before* the magnitude plot crosses 0 dB (i.e., $\omega_{pc} < \omega_{gc}$), the closed-loop system is **unstable** for positive gain values. * **Maximum Stable Gain**: For a system with $GM_{linear} = N$, the maximum gain multiplier before instability is $N$. Any additional gain $K_{new} > N \times K_{old}$ causes instability.

topics.title: Stability from Bode Plots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stability from Bode Plots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability from Bode Plots (Bode's Stability Criterion):** It is a frequency-domain method to determine the **closed-loop stability** of a negative-feedback control system directly from the **open-loop Bode magnitude and phase plots**, by computing two quantitative metrics — the **Gain Margin (GM)** and the **Phase Margin (PM)**. A system is **stable** in the Nyquist/Bode sense if *both* margins are **positive** when measured at their respective crossover frequencies.

The two foundational metrics are:

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain Crossover Frequency** | $\omega_{gc}$ | The frequency at which the open-loop magnitude plot crosses **0 dB**, i.e., $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ |
| **Phase Crossover Frequency** | $\omega_{pc}$ | The frequency at which the open-loop phase plot crosses **$-180^\circ$** (or equivalently $-360^\circ$, etc.) |
| **Gain Margin** | $GM$ | The vertical distance (in dB) of the magnitude curve from the **0 dB line** measured at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| **Phase Margin** | $\gamma$ (or $PM$) | The horizontal distance (in degrees) of the phase curve from the **$-180^\circ$ line** measured at $\omega_{gc}$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Drunk Driver on a Winding Road"** 🚗
> 
> Imagine a car (the closed-loop system) trying to follow a road. The road has two kinds of trouble spots:
> - **Potholes** (representing *phase* errors — delays): The driver lags behind the steering command. The more lag before reaching the "danger point" ($-180^\circ$ phase), the safer the car is.
> - **Uphill climbs** (representing *gain* errors — amplification): The road tilts up, and the car's response could grow uncontrollably. The lower the magnitude at the "phase danger point," the safer.
> 
> **Gain Margin** = How much *extra uphill* (gain) the road can tolerate before the car loses control at the phase-danger point.  
> **Phase Margin** = How much *extra delay* (phase lag) the driver can tolerate before the car loses control at the unity-gain point.  
> 
> If both are **positive**, the road is safe. If either is **negative**, the car will spin out (oscillate / become unstable).

### 1.3 Why Bode Stability?

- **Stability** is a property of the *closed-loop* system, but Bode plots work with the *open-loop* transfer function $G(s)H(s)$.
- The link is provided by the **Nyquist stability criterion**, which states that for a stable open-loop system, the closed-loop system is stable if and only if the **$-1 + j0$ point is *not encircled* by the Nyquist plot** of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$.
- On a Bode plot, the $-1$ point corresponds to **0 dB magnitude** and **$-180^\circ$ phase** — the two "crossover" axes. This makes Bode stability analysis remarkably **graphical and intuitive**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Bode stability is the *most frequently tested* frequency-domain stability method in KTU ESE papers. Always pair your analysis with the **Polar/Nyquist plot** interpretation for full marks, as examiners award bonus credit for cross-plot reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bode Stability Decision Logic

The decision is made by evaluating the two margins at the crossover frequencies:

**Step 1 — Locate $\omega_{pc}$ (Phase Crossover Frequency):**
Find the frequency where:
$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Compute Gain Margin (GM):**
$$GM = -20 \log_{10} \left[ \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \right] \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

Equivalently in linear (absolute) form:
$$GM_{\text{linear}} = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Locate $\omega_{gc}$ (Gain Crossover Frequency):**
Find the frequency where:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; 20 \log \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Phase Margin (PM):**
$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Stability Rule:**
- If $GM > 0$ (i.e., magnitude is **below** 0 dB at $\omega_{pc}$) **AND** $\gamma > 0$ (i.e., phase is **above** $-180^\circ$ at $\omega_{gc}$) → **Stable**.
- If either margin is **negative** → **Unstable**.
- If either margin is **zero** → **Marginally Stable** (sustained oscillations).
- If the *sign* of the slope near crossover is wrong → **Conditionally Stable** (special case).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA BOX — memorize these for KTU ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_{pc}$ | $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ$ | Solve for $\omega$ |
| 2 | $\omega_{gc}$ | $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ | Solve for $\omega$ |
| 3 | $GM$ (dB) | $GM = -20 \log \vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Vertical dB-distance at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| 4 | $GM$ (linear) | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | Reciprocal of magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| 5 | $PM = \gamma$ | $\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle GH(j\omega_{gc})$ | Horizontal degree-distance at $\omega_{gc}$ |
| 6 | Stability rule | $GM > 0$ **AND** $\gamma > 0$ | Both must be positive |
| 7 | Marginal stability | $GM = 0$ or $\gamma = 0$ | Sustained oscillation |
| 8 | Instability | $GM < 0$ or $\gamma < 0$ | Either margin negative |
| 9 | Conditionally stable | Multiple $\omega_{pc}$ and $\omega_{gc}$ | Both must be positive for stability |
| 10 | Slope at $\omega_{gc}$ for stability | Net slope $> -40$ dB/decade | Ensures $\gamma > 0$ |

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Servo motors and robotic arms** require $PM \ge 45^\circ$ for acceptable transient overshoot.
- **Aerospace flight controllers** demand $GM \ge 6$ dB and $PM \ge 30^\circ$ for robustness against parameter drift.
- **Power-electronics SMPS** loops are designed with $PM \approx 60^\circ$ to balance bandwidth vs. ringing.
- **Audio amplifiers** avoid low $PM$ to prevent high-frequency oscillation ("motor-boating").
- **Process control (chemical plants)** use Bode margins to tune PID loops where analytical root-finding is impossible.

### 2.4 Conditional Stability — The Tricky Case

> [!WARNING]
> A system can be stable but lose stability if gain is **reduced**! This is called **Conditional Stability** and is a favorite KTU trick question.

It occurs when the magnitude plot crosses 0 dB **multiple times** (e.g., twice), creating two gain-crossover frequencies, and the phase plot crosses $-180^\circ$ **multiple times** (e.g., twice), creating two phase-crossover frequencies. The stability condition becomes:
$$GM_1 > 0,\; GM_2 > 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \gamma_1 > 0,\; \gamma_2 > 0$$
for **all** crossover pairs simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: GM and PM from First Principles (Nyquist Connection)

The Nyquist criterion states that a closed-loop system is stable if the **$-1+j0$ point is NOT encircled** by the polar plot of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$.

Now, in the $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ plane, the point $-1+j0$ has:
- **Modulus** $= 1$
- **Angle** $= -180^\circ$ (or $\pm 180^\circ$)

When we map this to a Bode plot, the **log-magnitude axis** uses $20\log_{10}(\text{modulus})$ and the **phase axis** uses the angle directly. Hence:

- The **0 dB** line on the magnitude plot corresponds to a unit circle of radius 1.
- The **$-180^\circ$** line on the phase plot corresponds to the negative real axis.

If the open-loop polar plot **encircles** $-1$, then on the Bode plot:
- The magnitude is **above 0 dB** at the frequency where phase is $-180^\circ$, giving $GM < 0$ (unstable).
- The phase is **below $-180^\circ$** at the frequency where magnitude is 0 dB, giving $PM < 0$ (unstable).

Mathematically, with $N =$ number of encirclements of $-1$ and $P =$ number of open-loop RHP poles:
$$Z = N + P$$

where $Z$ is the number of closed-loop RHP poles. Stability demands $Z = 0$.

**Define the encirclement count** $N$ in terms of Bode margins:
- Each *positive* crossing of $-180^\circ$ (phase increasing through $-180^\circ$ as $\omega$ increases) **adds** $+1$ to $N$.
- Each *negative* crossing of $-180^\circ$ (phase decreasing through $-180^\circ$) **subtracts** $-1$ from $N$.

Hence:
$$N = \frac{1}{2}\big[(\text{positive crossings of } -180^\circ) - (\text{negative crossings})\big]$$

**Critical transition — "if there is no encirclement" means $N = 0$**, which (for $P = 0$) demands $Z = 0$, i.e., **stability**. The graphical translation of "$N = 0$" with $P = 0$ is:

$$GM > 0 \;\;\text{AND}\;\; PM > 0$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Analytical Derivation of GM and PM

**Problem.** Given
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.1s)}$$

Find $\omega_{pc}$, $\omega_{gc}$, $GM$, $PM$, and comment on stability.

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**
$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(1 + 0.5j\omega)(1 + 0.1j\omega)}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude in dB form:**
$$\vert GH(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log 10 - 20\log \omega - 20\log\sqrt{1 + (0.5\omega)^2} - 20\log\sqrt{1 + (0.1\omega)^2}$$

$$\vert GH(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 - 20\log\omega - 10\log[1 + 0.25\omega^2] - 10\log[1 + 0.01\omega^2]$$

**Step 3 — Phase form:**
$$\angle GH(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega)$$

**Step 4 — Find $\omega_{pc}$ from the phase condition:**
$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) = 90^\circ$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1}A + \tan^{-1}B = 90^\circ \Rightarrow AB = 1$:
$$(0.5\omega_{pc})(0.1\omega_{pc}) = 1$$
$$0.05\,\omega_{pc}^2 = 1$$
$$\omega_{pc}^2 = 20 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ to get $GM$:**
$$\vert GH(j4.472) \vert = \frac{10}{4.472 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.25(20)} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.01(20)}}$$

$$= \frac{10}{4.472 \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{1.2}}$$

$$= \frac{10}{4.472 \cdot 2.449 \cdot 1.095}$$

$$= \frac{10}{11.99} \approx 0.834$$

$$GM = -20\log(0.834) = -20 \times (-0.079) = 1.58 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $GM = +1.58$ dB → positive, marginal.

**Step 6 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ from the magnitude condition (asymptotic approximation):**
For $\omega > 2$ rad/s (corner 1), the $-20$ dB/dec slope from the integrator plus the additional $-20$ dB/dec from the $0.5$s corner gives net $-40$ dB/dec. Using straight-line approximation in the relevant band:

$$20 - 20\log\omega_{gc} - 20\log(0.5\omega_{gc}) - 20\log(0.1\omega_{gc}) = 0$$

$$20 - 20\log\omega_{gc} - 20[\log 0.5 + \log\omega_{gc}] - 20[\log 0.1 + \log\omega_{gc}] = 0$$

$$20 - 20\log\omega_{gc} - 20(-0.301) - 20\log\omega_{gc} - 20(-1) - 20\log\omega_{gc} = 0$$

$$20 + 6.02 + 20 - 60\log\omega_{gc} = 0$$

$$46.02 = 60\log\omega_{gc}$$

$$\log\omega_{gc} = 0.767 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{gc} \approx 5.85 \text{ rad/s}$$

(Refining numerically with exact magnitudes yields $\omega_{gc} \approx 5.5$ rad/s.)

**Step 7 — Compute $PM$ at $\omega_{gc} = 5.5$ rad/s:**
$$\angle GH(j5.5) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \times 5.5) - \tan^{-1}(0.1 \times 5.5)$$
$$= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(2.75) - \tan^{-1}(0.55)$$
$$= -90^\circ - 70.0^\circ - 28.8^\circ$$
$$= -188.8^\circ$$

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + (-188.8^\circ) = -8.8^\circ$$

> [!WARNING]
> $PM = -8.8^\circ$ → **negative** → system is **UNSTABLE** in practice.

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**
| Metric | Value | Sign | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| $GM$ | $+1.58$ dB | Positive (barely) | Marginal |
| $PM$ | $-8.8^\circ$ | Negative | Unstable |
| **Overall** | — | — | **UNSTABLE — design compensation required** |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stability Classification Table

| $\omega_{pc}$ exists? | $GM$ sign | $\omega_{gc}$ exists? | $PM$ sign | Stability Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yes | $> 0$ | Yes | $> 0$ | **Stable** ✓ |
| Yes | $= 0$ | Yes | $= 0$ | Marginally stable (oscillatory) |
| Yes | $< 0$ | Yes | $< 0$ | **Unstable** ✗ |
| No (no $-180^\circ$ crossing) | $\infty$ | Yes | $> 0$ | **Stable** (no phase danger) |
| Multiple crossings | Mixed | Multiple | Mixed | **Conditionally stable** (check all pairs) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bode Stability Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open Loop GHs given] --> B[Plot Bode Magnitude and Phase]
    B --> C{Phase crosses 180 deg?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Mark frequency as wpc]
    D --> E[Read magnitude at wpc in dB]
    E --> F[GM = negative of that magnitude]
    C -- No --> G[GM is infinite]
    G --> H[System has no phase danger]
    B --> I{Magnitude crosses 0 dB?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Mark frequency as wgc]
    J --> K[Read phase at wgc]
    K --> L[PM = 180 plus phase at wgc]
    I -- No --> M[PM is infinite]
    M --> N[Check GM only]
    F --> O{GM positive?}
    L --> P{PM positive?}
    O -- Yes --> Q[Stable with gain margin]
    O -- No --> R[Unstable]
    P -- Yes --> S[Stable with phase margin]
    P -- No --> R
    Q --> T[Final Decision]
    S --> T
    N --> O
    H --> T
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Block Diagram Indicating Stability Margin Measurement Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R] --> SM[Comparator]
    SM --> CTRL[Controller Gc]
    CTRL --> PLANT[Plant Gp]
    PLANT --> Y[Output Y]
    Y --> FB[Feedback H]
    FB --> SM
    BODE[Bode Plot of Open Loop Gc Gp H] -.-> GM[GM at wpc]
    BODE -.-> PM[PM at wgc]
    GM --> STAB[Stability Verdict]
    PM --> STAB
```

### 4.3 Graphical Interpretation of GM and PM on Bode Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MAG[ Magnitude Plot ]
        M0[Low frequency region 20 dB] --> M1[Slope minus 20 dB per decade]
        M1 --> M2[Corner at 2 rad per s]
        M2 --> M3[Slope minus 40 dB per decade]
        M3 --> M4[Crosses 0 dB at wgc]
        M4 --> M5[Continues to wpc]
        M5 --> M6[Magnitude at wpc is negative GM in dB]
    end
    subgraph PHA[ Phase Plot ]
        P0[Starts at minus 90 deg] --> P1[Slope decreasing]
        P1 --> P2[Crosses minus 180 deg at wpc]
        P2 --> P3[Phase at wgc gives PM]
    end
    M6 --> RESULT[GM positive implies safe at phase danger]
    P3 --> RESULT2[PM positive implies safe at gain danger]
    RESULT --> FINAL[System Stable]
    RESULT2 --> FINAL
```

### 4.4 Conditional Stability Architecture (Multiple Crossovers)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Crossover pair 1: wgc1 and wpc1] --> D1{PM1 positive AND GM1 positive}
    D1 -- Yes --> C2[Proceed to next pair]
    D1 -- No --> UNSTABLE[System Unstable]
    C2 --> C3[Crossover pair 2: wgc2 and wpc2]
    C3 --> D2{PM2 positive AND GM2 positive}
    D2 -- Yes --> STABLE[Conditionally Stable]
    D2 -- No --> UNSTABLE
```

### 4.5 GM-PM Relationship Schematic (Stability Region)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AX[Plot axes: GM in dB vs PM in deg] --> Q1[Quadrant 1: GM greater than 0 and PM greater than 0]
    Q1 --> OK[STABLE]
    AX --> Q2[Quadrant 2: GM less than 0 and PM greater than 0]
    Q2 --> BAD1[UNSTABLE due to GM]
    AX --> Q3[Quadrant 3: GM less than 0 and PM less than 0]
    Q3 --> BAD2[UNSTABLE both]
    AX --> Q4[Quadrant 4: GM greater than 0 and PM less than 0]
    Q4 --> BAD3[UNSTABLE due to PM]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **Gain Margin** and **Phase Margin**. State the conditions for stability based on these margins in a Bode plot.

**Model Answer:**
- **Gain Margin (GM):** The factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. Measured (in dB) at the **phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$** where $\angle GH(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$.
$$GM \text{ (dB)} = -20 \log \vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$
- **Phase Margin (PM):** The additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be tolerated at the **gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$** (where $\vert GH(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$) before instability.
$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle GH(j\omega_{gc})$$
- **Stability condition:** The closed-loop system is stable if and only if $GM > 0$ (dB) **and** $\gamma > 0$ (degrees).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is meant by **conditional stability** in frequency response analysis? How is it identified from a Bode plot?

**Model Answer:**
**Conditional stability** is a phenomenon in which a feedback system is stable for the *nominal* gain but becomes unstable if the gain is either **increased** or **decreased** within a certain range. It is identified on a Bode plot by the existence of **multiple gain-crossover frequencies** ($\omega_{gc}$) and/or **multiple phase-crossover frequencies** ($\omega_{pc}$). For stability, *every* GM and *every* PM associated with the multiple crossover pairs must be positive simultaneously. A reduction in gain (which ordinarily helps stability) can take the system into the unstable region between two positive-magnitude crossings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** compute GM at $\omega_{gc}$ — it is computed at $\omega_{pc}$.
> - **Do not** forget the negative sign in $GM = -20\log(\text{magnitude at } \omega_{pc})$.
> - **Always** specify units (dB for GM, degrees for PM).
> - **Always** state the stability *conclusion* explicitly, not just numerical values.
> - For compound expressions, the Bode phase is **additive** (sum of individual phase contributions), while the magnitude in dB is also additive.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(1 + 0.2s)(1 + 0.02s)}$$

**(a)** Find the value of $K$ for which the system has a **gain margin of 12 dB**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the value of $K$ found in (a), determine the **phase margin** and comment on the stability. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Finding $K$:**

The open-loop transfer function is $G(s)H(s) = G(s)$ (unity feedback, so $H = 1$).

Substitute $s = j\omega$:
$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(1 + 0.2j\omega)(1 + 0.02j\omega)}$$

**Phase:**
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.02\omega)$$

**Step 1:** Find $\omega_{pc}$ by setting $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$:
$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.02\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$
$$\tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.02\omega_{pc}) = 90^\circ$$

For two arctangents to sum to $90^\circ$, the product of their arguments must equal 1:
$$(0.2\omega_{pc})(0.02\omega_{pc}) = 1$$
$$0.004\,\omega_{pc}^2 = 1$$
$$\omega_{pc}^2 = 250 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{K}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{1 + (0.2\omega_{pc})^2}\sqrt{1 + (0.02\omega_{pc})^2}}$$

$$\omega_{pc} = 15.81 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.2\omega_{pc} = 3.162 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.02\omega_{pc} = 0.3162$$

$$\sqrt{1 + (3.162)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 10} = \sqrt{11} = 3.317$$

$$\sqrt{1 + (0.3162)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.1} = \sqrt{1.1} = 1.049$$

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{K}{15.81 \times 3.317 \times 1.049} = \frac{K}{54.99}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $GM = 12$ dB:
$$GM = -20\log \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = -20\log\left(\frac{K}{54.99}\right) = 12$$

$$20\log(54.99) - 20\log K = 12$$

$$20\log K = 20\log(54.99) - 12 = 34.80 - 12 = 22.80$$

$$\log K = 1.14 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K = 10^{1.14} \approx 13.80$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 13.8}$$

**Part (b) — Phase Margin at $K = 13.8$:**

**Step 1:** Find $\omega_{gc}$ by setting $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:
$$\frac{13.8}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{1 + 0.04\omega_{gc}^2}\sqrt{1 + 0.0004\omega_{gc}^2}} = 1$$

Solving numerically by trial:
- Try $\omega_{gc} = 7$: $\vert G(j7) \vert = \dfrac{13.8}{7 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 1.96}\cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.0196}} = \dfrac{13.8}{7 \cdot 1.72 \cdot 1.0098} = \dfrac{13.8}{12.16} = 1.135$ → magnitude too high.
- Try $\omega_{gc} = 8$: $\vert G(j8) \vert = \dfrac{13.8}{8 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 2.56}\cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.0256}} = \dfrac{13.8}{8 \cdot 1.886 \cdot 1.0127} = \dfrac{13.8}{15.28} = 0.903$ → magnitude too low.
- Try $\omega_{gc} = 7.5$: $\vert G(j7.5) \vert \approx 1.008$ → very close to 1.

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 7.5$ rad/s.

**Step 2:** Compute phase at $\omega_{gc} = 7.5$ rad/s:
$$\angle G(j7.5) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.2 \times 7.5) - \tan^{-1}(0.02 \times 7.5)$$
$$= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(1.5) - \tan^{-1}(0.15)$$
$$= -90^\circ - 56.31^\circ - 8.53^\circ$$
$$= -154.84^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Phase Margin:
$$\gamma = 180^\circ + (-154.84^\circ) = 25.16^\circ \approx 25.2^\circ$$

**Conclusion:** $GM = 12$ dB (positive) and $PM \approx 25.2^\circ$ (positive) → **System is STABLE**. The phase margin of $\sim 25^\circ$ indicates acceptable but not excellent relative stability; the system will have moderate overshoot. To improve transient response, $K$ should be reduced to increase $PM$ further.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

A unity feedback system has the open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{20}{s(1 + 0.5s)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Bode magnitude and phase plots. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the **Gain Margin** and **Phase Margin** from the plots and hence comment on the closed-loop stability. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Bode Plot Construction:**

The transfer function is:
$$G(j\omega) = \frac{20}{j\omega(1 + 0.5j\omega)}$$

**Magnitude in dB:**
$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log 20 - 20\log\omega - 20\log\sqrt{1 + 0.25\omega^2}$$
$$= 26.02 - 20\log\omega - 10\log(1 + 0.25\omega^2)$$

**Asymptotes:**
- For $\omega \ll 2$ rad/s: slope $-20$ dB/decade (integrator dominates). At $\omega = 1$: $\vert G \vert_{dB} = 26.02$ dB.
- Corner frequency at $\omega = 2$ rad/s (where $0.5\omega = 1$).
- For $\omega \gg 2$ rad/s: slope $-40$ dB/decade.

**Asymptotic magnitude table:**

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | 0.1 | 0.5 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 50 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert G \vert_{dB}$ (asymp) | 46.0 | 32.0 | 26.0 | 20.0 | 8.0 | $-2.0$ | $-14.0$ | $-34.0$ |
| $\vert G \vert_{dB}$ (exact) | 46.0 | 32.04 | 26.02 | 19.97 | 6.99 | $-3.52$ | $-15.74$ | $-35.44$ |

**Phase:**
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega)$$

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | 0.1 | 0.5 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 | $\infty$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\angle G$ (deg) | $-92.86$ | $-104.04$ | $-116.57$ | $-135.00$ | $-158.20$ | $-168.69$ | $-174.29$ | $-180.00$ |

**Plot description:**
- **Magnitude plot:** Starts at $+\infty$ (theoretically) at low $\omega$, passes through $\sim 26$ dB at $\omega = 1$, descends at $-20$ dB/dec until $\omega = 2$, then steepens to $-40$ dB/dec.
- **Phase plot:** Starts at $-90^\circ$ at $\omega = 0$, decreases monotonically, and asymptotically approaches $-180^\circ$ as $\omega \to \infty$. The phase *reaches* $-180^\circ$ only at $\omega = \infty$.

**Part (b) — GM and PM:**

**Step 1: Find $\omega_{gc}$** (magnitude = 0 dB):
$$26.02 - 20\log\omega_{gc} - 10\log(1 + 0.25\omega_{gc}^2) = 0$$

By trial:
- $\omega = 10$: $-2 - 10\log(1 + 25) = -2 - 14.77 = -16.77$ dB (below 0).
- $\omega = 8$: $26.02 - 18.06 - 10\log(17) = 26.02 - 18.06 - 12.30 = -4.34$ dB.
- $\omega = 5$: $26.02 - 13.98 - 10\log(7.25) = 26.02 - 13.98 - 8.60 = 3.44$ dB (above 0).
- $\omega = 6$: $26.02 - 15.56 - 10\log(10) = 26.02 - 15.56 - 10 = 0.46$ dB.
- $\omega = 6.2$: $\approx 0$ dB.

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 6.2$ rad/s.

**Step 2: Find $\omega_{pc}$** (phase = $-180^\circ$):
The phase approaches $-180^\circ$ only as $\omega \to \infty$. In strict sense, $\omega_{pc} = \infty$, and the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ is $-\infty$ dB.

$$GM = -20\log \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = -20\log(0) = \infty \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical interpretation:** Since the phase *asymptotically* approaches $-180^\circ$ and the magnitude drops below 0 dB well before that, the system has effectively **infinite gain margin** for all practical purposes. However, in some textbooks $\omega_{pc}$ is taken as the value where phase is just slightly above $-180^\circ$ (e.g., $-178^\circ$) and a *finite* GM is reported.

**Step 3: Compute PM at $\omega_{gc} = 6.2$ rad/s:**
$$\angle G(j6.2) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \times 6.2) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(3.1)$$
$$= -90^\circ - 72.08^\circ = -162.08^\circ$$

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + (-162.08^\circ) = 17.92^\circ \approx 17.9^\circ$$

**Stability conclusion:**

| Metric | Value | Sign |
|---|---|---|
| $GM$ | $\infty$ dB (theoretical) | Strongly positive |
| $PM$ | $\approx 17.9^\circ$ | Positive |

The closed-loop system is **STABLE** but with a **low phase margin of $\sim 18^\circ$**. The system will exhibit **significant overshoot and ringing** in its step response. To improve transient performance, gain $K$ should be **decreased** (which raises $PM$ at the expense of bandwidth). A **lead compensator** is typically recommended to raise $PM$ to $\ge 45^\circ$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Bode Stability:** When the phase plot **only asymptotically** approaches $-180^\circ$ without crossing it, $GM = \infty$ and the system is stable provided $PM > 0$. Such systems are called **absolutely stable** or **stable for all positive gains** (Type-1 with at most one additional pole, satisfying the Bode integral constraints).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Bode Stability — One-Page Revision Bullets**

- **Two crossovers:** $\omega_{pc}$ (phase hits $-180^\circ$) and $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude hits 0 dB).
- **Gain Margin** is measured on the *magnitude* axis (in dB) at $\omega_{pc}$, with **negative sign**: $GM = -20\log \vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- **Phase Margin** is measured on the *phase* axis (in degrees) at $\omega_{gc}$: $\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle GH(j\omega_{gc})$.
- **Stability rule:** $GM > 0$ **AND** $\gamma > 0$ — both must hold.
- **Rule of thumb design targets:** $GM \ge 6$ dB (gain robustness), $PM \ge 45^\circ$ (transient quality).
- **Slope at $\omega_{gc}$ matters:** Net slope must be $> -40$ dB/decade for $\gamma > 0$. A slope of $-20$ dB/decade gives $\gamma \approx 90^\circ$.
- **For Type-0 systems** (no integrator): low-frequency magnitude = $20\log K_d$ (position constant).
- **For Type-1 systems** (one integrator): low-frequency magnitude slope is $-20$ dB/decade, starting from $20\log K_v - 20\log\omega$.
- **Arctan sum to $90^\circ$ trick:** If $\tan^{-1}A + \tan^{-1}B = 90^\circ$, then $AB = 1$ — use this to find $\omega_{pc}$ quickly for factorized forms.
- **Conditional stability** arises with multiple $\omega_{pc}$ and $\omega_{gc}$ — *all* margins must be checked.
- **Infinite GM** when phase never reaches $-180^\circ$ — check $PM$ only.
- **Increasing gain $K$** raises magnitude plot (by $20\log K$ dB) without changing phase — *decreases* $PM$ and *decreases* $GM$.
- **Decreasing gain $K$** lowers magnitude plot — *increases* $PM$ and *increases* $GM$, but reduces bandwidth.
- **Compensation choice:** If $PM$ too low → add **lead compensator**; if steady-state error too high → add **lag compensator**; both needed → **lead-lag compensator**.
- **Connection to Nyquist:** $GM > 0$ ⇔ $-1$ point not encircled (when open-loop is minimum phase stable); equivalently, number of encirclements $N = 0$ with $P = 0$.
- **KTU-favorite pitfalls:** mixing up GM and PM frequencies, missing the negative sign in GM, ignoring conditional stability, and forgetting to convert between dB and linear magnitude.
- **Units always:** $GM$ in **dB**, $PM$ in **degrees**, frequencies in **rad/s**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, if a problem gives you a transfer function with an unknown $K$, the standard 14-mark approach is: (i) sketch Bode for a convenient $K$, (ii) find $\omega_{pc}$ in terms of $K$, (iii) impose $GM$ condition to solve for $K$, (iv) recompute $PM$, (v) discuss stability. Practice this template until it is automatic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nyquist Stability Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nyquist Stability Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Nyquist Stability Criterion** is a powerful **frequency-domain** graphical technique developed by **Harry Nyquist** (1932) at Bell Telephone Laboratories. It determines the **closed-loop stability** of a feedback control system by analyzing the **encirclements** of the critical point $(-1 + j0)$ in the complex plane by the **polar plot** (Nyquist plot) of the **open-loop transfer function** $G(s)H(s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The Nyquist stability criterion relates the closed-loop stability of a control system to the open-loop frequency response and the number of encirclements of the critical point $(-1, j0)$ by the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$."*

Mathematically, the criterion is expressed through **Cauchy's Argument Principle** applied to the complex function $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$:

$$N = Z - P$$

where every term represents a count of poles/zeros on the **right half of the s-plane (RHP)**.

## 1.2 Symbolic Breakdown of the Master Equation

| Symbol | Meaning | Stability Goal |
|:------:|:--------|:---------------|
| $N$ | Net **clockwise encirclements** of the $(-1, j0)$ point by the Nyquist plot of $G(s)H(s)$ | — |
| $Z$ | Number of **closed-loop poles** in the RHP | Must equal **0** |
| $P$ | Number of **open-loop poles** of $G(s)H(s)$ in the RHP | Found from open-loop TF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Requirement:** For a stable closed-loop system, $Z = 0$, which means the Nyquist plot must encircle the critical point $(-1, j0)$ exactly **$N = -P$ times** in the clockwise direction. If $P = 0$ (minimum phase open-loop), then $N = 0$ (no encirclements).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Lighthouse and the Island

Imagine you are piloting a small boat in the ocean at night. The **lighthouse** stands on a tiny island located precisely at the point $(-1, j0)$ on the map. As you sail along the Nyquist contour (a giant closed path surrounding the entire right half of the s-plane), you continuously observe the lighthouse through your compass.

- **Each time you see the lighthouse pass on your right side (port)**, you have made one **counterclockwise encirclement**.
- **Each time the lighthouse passes on your left side (starboard)**, you have made one **clockwise encirclement**.
- If at the end of your journey, the lighthouse has remained exactly where it started (no net circling), and you know there are no open-loop "sharks" ($P = 0$) in the right half-plane, then the waters are safe for ships — the closed-loop system is **stable**.

If, however, your boat circles the lighthouse without intending to, dangerous currents (instability) are pulling you, indicating that the closed-loop system has poles drifting into the RHP ($Z \neq 0$).

## 1.4 The Critical Point: Why $(-1, j0)$?

The characteristic equation of a unity feedback closed-loop system is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$G(s)H(s) = -1 = -1 + j0$$

Hence the **critical point** at which the Nyquist plot passes through determines whether $|G(j\omega_c)H(j\omega_c)| = 1$ and $\angle G(j\omega_c)H(j\omega_c) = -180°$, i.e., the **Barkhausen oscillation condition**. The closer the plot comes to this point, the closer the system is to instability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Mathematical Foundation: Cauchy's Argument Principle

The Nyquist criterion is fundamentally an application of the **Argument Principle** from complex analysis. For a closed contour $\Gamma_s$ in the s-plane, mapping through a rational function $F(s)$ produces a closed contour $\Gamma_F$ in the F-plane. The principle states:

$$N_F = Z_F - P_F$$

where $N_F$ is the **net clockwise encirclements** of the origin by $\Gamma_F$, $Z_F$ is the number of zeros of $F(s)$ inside $\Gamma_s$, and $P_F$ is the number of poles of $F(s)$ inside $\Gamma_s$.

By mapping $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$ instead of $G(s)H(s)$, we transform the problem into counting encirclements of the **origin**. By shifting the mapping to $G(s)H(s)$, we convert this into encirclements of the **$-1$ point**.

## 2.2 The Nyquist Contour ($\Gamma_s$)

The Nyquist contour is a closed path in the s-plane chosen to enclose the **entire right half-plane**:

1. Travels along the **imaginary axis** from $-j\infty$ to $+j\infty$.
2. A **semicircle of infinite radius** ($\mathcal{R} \to \infty$) closes the contour through the right half-plane.
3. For systems with poles on the imaginary axis (e.g., at origin for **Type 1** systems), small **detour semicircles of infinitesimal radius** bypass these poles to the right.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type-Specific Behavior at $\omega = 0$:**
> - **Type 0**: Plot starts at finite point on positive real axis.
> - **Type 1**: Plot starts at infinity along $-90°$ (negative imaginary axis).
> - **Type 2**: Plot starts at infinity along $-180°$ (negative real axis).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure for Applying the Nyquist Criterion

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ in $G(s)H(s)$ to obtain $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$.

**Step 2:** Sketch the polar/Nyquist plot by computing magnitude and phase for $\omega \in (0, +\infty)$.

**Step 3:** Reflect the plot across the real axis to obtain the complete Nyquist plot for $\omega \in (-\infty, +\infty)$.

**Step 4:** Count the net clockwise encirclements $N$ of the critical point $(-1, j0)$.

**Step 5:** Identify $P$, the number of open-loop poles in the RHP, from the open-loop transfer function.

**Step 6:** Compute $Z = N + P$. The system is stable **if and only if $Z = 0$**.

## 2.4 Special Case: Minimum Phase Systems

For **minimum phase systems** ($P = 0$), stability requires that the Nyquist plot does **NOT encircle** the point $(-1, j0)$:

$$N = 0 \quad \text{(for stability when } P = 0\text{)}$$

## 2.5 Gain Margin and Phase Margin from Nyquist Plot

The Nyquist plot directly yields two critical relative stability measures:

**Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$):** Frequency at which the Nyquist plot intersects the **negative real axis** (phase = $-180°$).

$$\omega_{pc} : \angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180°$$

**Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$):** Frequency at which the Nyquist plot crosses the **unit circle** (magnitude = 1).

$$\omega_{gc} : \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$$

**Gain Margin ($GM$):**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

**Phase Margin ($PM$):**

$$PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Margins Convention (KTU):**
> - $GM > 1$ (in linear scale) or $GM > 0$ dB → Stable with gain margin
> - $PM > 0°$ → Stable with phase margin
> - Typical design targets: $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM \geq 30°$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|:-:|:---------------|:------------|
| 1 | $N = Z - P$ | Master Nyquist relation (clockwise encirclements) |
| 2 | $Z = N + P$ | Closed-loop RHP poles calculation |
| 3 | Stability: $Z = 0$ | Mandatory for BIBO stable closed-loop |
| 4 | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | Linear gain margin |
| 5 | $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$ | Gain margin in decibels |
| 6 | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Phase margin in degrees |
| 7 | $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert_{\omega = \omega_{pc}} < 1$ | Gain margin condition (stable) |
| 8 | Type $n$ system: starts at $\dfrac{-n \cdot 90°}{}$ | Phase at $\omega = 0^+$ |
| 9 | Order $m$ system: ends at $\dfrac{-(m-n) \cdot 90°}{}$ | Phase at $\omega = +\infty$ |
| 10 | Conditional Stability | Occurs when plot encircles $(-1, j0)$ twice oppositely |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of the Nyquist Criterion

- **Rocket Guidance Systems:** Used by NASA to verify closed-loop stability of autopilot systems where RHP open-loop poles exist (non-minimum phase dynamics).
- **Oscillator Design:** Determines the onset of oscillation in feedback amplifiers and sinusoidal oscillators.
- **Power Electronics:** Analyzes DC-DC converters (e.g., boost converters) which often have RHP zeros.
- **Process Control:** Validates stability of chemical reactors with integrating dynamics (Type 1).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$

Using the Nyquist stability criterion:
**(a)** Sketch the Nyquist plot for $K = 1$.
**(b)** Find the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability.

---

### Part (a): Nyquist Plot Construction

**Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$**

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)}$$

**Step 2: Separate real and imaginary parts**

Expand the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate. First expand $(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)$:

$$(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2) = (j\omega)^2 + 3(j\omega) + 2 = -\omega^2 + 2 + 3j\omega$$

Then multiply by $j\omega$:

$$j\omega(-\omega^2 + 2 + 3j\omega) = -j\omega^3 + 2j\omega - 3\omega^2 = -3\omega^2 + j(2\omega - \omega^3)$$

Therefore:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{-3\omega^2 + j(2\omega - \omega^3)}$$

**Step 3: Rationalize by multiplying with complex conjugate**

Let $A = -3\omega^2$ and $B = 2\omega - \omega^3$. Then:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{A + jB} \cdot \frac{A - jB}{A - jB}$$

$$= \frac{K(A - jB)}{A^2 + B^2}$$

**Step 4: Write magnitude and phase explicitly**

Magnitude:

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{9\omega^4 + (2\omega - \omega^3)^2}}$$

Phase:

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{1}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Analyze low-frequency behavior ($\omega \to 0^+$)**

$$\lim_{\omega \to 0^+} G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \to \infty \quad \text{at angle} \quad -90°$$

The plot starts at infinity along the **negative imaginary axis**.

**Step 6: Analyze high-frequency behavior ($\omega \to +\infty$)**

$$\lim_{\omega \to +\infty} \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert \to 0 \quad \text{at angle} \quad -270°$$

The plot ends at origin with a phase of $-270°$ (or equivalently $+90°$).

**Step 7: Find phase crossover frequency (intersection with negative real axis)**

Setting the imaginary part equal to zero (i.e., $B = 0$):

$$2\omega - \omega^3 = 0 \implies \omega(2 - \omega^2) = 0$$

Non-zero solution: $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2}$ rad/s.

**Step 8: Compute magnitude at phase crossover**

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{2})H(j\sqrt{2}) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{9(2) + 0}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{18}} = \frac{K}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{K}{2\sqrt{6}}$$

**Step 9: Sketch the complete Nyquist plot**

The plot for $\omega: 0^+ \to +\infty$ starts at $-j\infty$, curves through quadrants III and II, and intersects the negative real axis at $-\dfrac{K}{2\sqrt{6}}$. The mirror image for negative $\omega$ completes the full Nyquist contour.

---

### Part (b): Range of K for Stability

**Step 10: Identify open-loop poles**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)} \implies \text{Poles at } s = 0, -1, -2$$

No poles lie in the RHP, therefore:

$$P = 0$$

**Step 11: Apply stability condition $Z = 0$**

From $Z = N + P$, with $P = 0$:

$$Z = N \quad \text{requires} \quad N = 0$$

The Nyquist plot must **NOT encircle** $(-1, j0)$.

**Step 12: Derive the critical condition**

The plot crosses the negative real axis at $-\dfrac{K}{2\sqrt{6}}$. For no encirclement of $-1$:

$$\left|\frac{-K}{2\sqrt{6}}\right| < 1 \implies \frac{K}{2\sqrt{6}} < 1$$

$$K < 2\sqrt{6} \approx 4.899$$

**Step 13: Final stability range**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 2\sqrt{6} \quad \text{(approximately } 0 < K < 4.899\text{)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Conditional Stability Case

**Problem Statement:**
For a system where the Nyquist plot encircles the $(-1, j0)$ point **twice in opposite directions**, determine the stability classification.

**Analysis:**
- Let $N_1$ = clockwise encirclements, $N_2$ = counterclockwise encirclements
- Net encirclement: $N = N_1 - N_2$
- If $N_1 = 2$ and $N_2 = 2$, then $N = 0$, and with $P = 0$, we get $Z = 0$ → **Stable**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Stability:** Such a system is stable for the current gain, but **destabilizes** if the gain is either decreased (loop becomes too weak) or increased (loop becomes too strong). This is common in aerospace control systems and is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.3 Derivative Trace: From Bode to Nyquist Equivalence

Starting from the open-loop frequency response in polar form:

$$\begin{aligned}
G(j\omega)H(j\omega) &= \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert \cdot e^{j\phi(\omega)} \\
\text{where } \phi(\omega) &= \angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)
\end{aligned}$$

In Cartesian form:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = X(\omega) + jY(\omega)$$

The locus traced by varying $\omega$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ is precisely the **Nyquist contour**. Any point $(X, Y)$ on this contour maps to a corresponding point on the **Nichols chart** via the transformation $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$ in dB vs. phase — demonstrating the **Bode-Nyquist duality**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nyquist Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete step-by-step process an engineer follows when applying the Nyquist Stability Criterion to a control system problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given Open Loop TF GsHs] --> stepB[Identify Type n and Order m of System]
    stepB --> stepC[Substitute s equals j omega to obtain Gj omega H j omega]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Magnitude and Phase Expressions]
    stepD --> stepE[Analyze Low Frequency Behavior as omega approaches 0 plus]
    stepE --> stepF[Analyze High Frequency Behavior as omega approaches infinity]
    stepF --> stepG[Find Phase Crossover by setting Imaginary part equals 0]
    stepG --> stepH[Compute Real Axis Intercept Magnitude]
    stepH --> stepI[Sketch Polar Plot for omega from 0 to plus infinity]
    stepI --> stepJ[Mirror Across Real Axis for Negative omega]
    stepJ --> stepK[Draw Complete Nyquist Contour]
    stepK --> stepL[Count Clockwise Encirclements N of point minus 1 plus j 0]
    stepL --> stepM[Determine Open Loop RHP Poles P from GsHs]
    stepM --> stepN[Calculate Z equals N plus P]
    stepN --> stepO{Is Z equal to 0?}
    stepO -->|Yes| resultP[System is STABLE]
    stepO -->|No| resultQ[System is UNSTABLE with Z RHP poles]
    stepP --> stepR[Compute Gain Margin and Phase Margin for Relative Stability]
    stepQ --> stepS[Adjust Gain K or Add Compensator to Stabilize]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Mapping: Nyquist Plot Reference Geometry

The following diagram shows the canonical geometry of a Type 1, Order 3 Nyquist plot (matching the example in Section 3.1) and identifies all critical points and regions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    originPoint[Origin 0 plus j 0] --- negReal[Negative Real Axis]
    criticalPoint[CRITICAL POINT: minus 1 plus j 0] --- negReal
    negImagAxis[Negative Imaginary Axis] --- originPoint
    posImagAxis[Positive Imaginary Axis] --- originPoint
    quadrant3[Quadrant III: omega near 0 plus] --- negImagAxis
    quadrant2[Quadrant II: Mid Frequency Range] --- quadrant3
    interceptPoint[REAL AXIS INTERCEPT: minus K over 2 sqrt 6] --- criticalPoint
    phaseCross[Phase Crossover Frequency omega pc equals sqrt 2 rad per sec] --- interceptPoint
    quadrant1[Quadrant I: Image for Negative omega] --- posImagAxis
    quadrant4[Quadrant IV: Image for Negative omega] --- quadrant1
    encircleNote[Encirclement Region for P greater than 0] --- criticalPoint
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Relationships Between Stability Tools in Module 4

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    module4[Module 4: Stability Analysis Tools] --> routh[Routh Hurwitz Criterion]
    module4 --> rootLocus[Root Locus Technique]
    module4 --> nyquist[Nyquist Stability Criterion]
    module4 --> bode[Bode Plot Analysis]
    nyquist --> gm[Gain Margin]
    nyquist --> pm[Phase Margin]
    nyquist --> relativeStab[Relative Stability Assessment]
    nyquist --> conditionalStab[Conditional Stability Detection]
    bode --> gm
    bode --> pm
    nyquist --> cauchyLink[Based on Cauchy Argument Principle]
    cauchyLink --> complexVar[Complex Variable Theory]
    routh --> absoluteStab[Absolute Stability Check]
    rootLocus --> poleTrajectory[Pole Migration Paths]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the Nyquist stability criterion. What is the significance of the critical point $(-1, j0)$?**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist stability criterion is a frequency-domain technique that determines closed-loop stability from the open-loop frequency response. It is based on **Cauchy's argument principle** and is expressed as:

$$N = Z - P$$

where $N$ = net clockwise encirclements of the critical point by the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$, $Z$ = closed-loop poles in the right half-plane, and $P$ = open-loop poles in the right half-plane. For stability, $Z = 0$.

The **critical point** $(-1, j0)$ corresponds to the magnitude = 1 and phase = $-180°$ condition, which is the **Barkhausen oscillation criterion**. If the Nyquist plot passes through this point, the system is marginally stable (sustained oscillation).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define gain margin and phase margin. How are they obtained from the Nyquist plot?**

**Model Answer:**

**Gain Margin (GM):** It is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the system becomes unstable. It is measured at the **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180°$):

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

**Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag required to bring the system to the verge of instability, measured at the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = 1):

$$PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

Both metrics are obtained directly from the Nyquist plot: GM from the negative real-axis intercept distance from origin, and PM from the angular distance of the unit circle intersection point from the negative real axis.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Nyquist plot and determine the phase crossover frequency. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable using the Nyquist criterion. (7 Marks)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+1)}{s(s-1)(s+4)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Nyquist plot. Comment on the type and stability characteristics of the system. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the value of $K$ for stable operation and compute the gain margin. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Nyquist Plot Construction (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify system type and order**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

Type = 1 (one pole at origin), Order = 3 (three poles total).

**Step 2: Compute $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$**

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+5)}$$

**Step 3: Find phase crossover frequency**

The phase of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$:

$$\phi(\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)$$

Setting $\phi(\omega_{pc}) = -180°$:

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90°$$

For two angles to sum to $90°$, the denominator of the tangent addition formula vanishes:

$$1 - \omega_{pc} \cdot \frac{\omega_{pc}}{5} = 0 \implies \omega_{pc}^2 = 5 \implies \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute the real-axis intercept**

The imaginary part becomes zero when $\omega = \omega_{pc}$. The magnitude at this point:

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{5})H(j\sqrt{5}) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{1+5} \cdot \sqrt{25+5}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{5 \cdot 6 \cdot 30}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{900}} = \frac{K}{30}$$

The Nyquist plot for $\omega: 0^+ \to +\infty$ starts at $-j\infty$, curves through Q3 → Q2, and intersects the negative real axis at $-\dfrac{K}{30}$.

**(b) Stability Range (7 Marks)**

**Step 5: Identify open-loop RHP poles**

Poles are at $s = 0, -1, -5$. All in the left half-plane (LHP) or on imaginary axis (origin).

$$P = 0$$

**Step 6: Apply stability condition**

For stability, $Z = 0$, so $N = -P = 0$. No encirclement of $(-1, j0)$ is allowed.

**Step 7: Compute critical K**

For the Nyquist plot to just touch $(-1, j0)$:

$$\frac{K}{30} = 1 \implies K_{critical} = 30$$

**Step 8: Final stability range**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 30}$$

For $K = 30$, the system is **marginally stable** with sustained oscillations at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$ rad/s.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Nyquist Plot and Stability Characteristics (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify poles**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+1)}{s(s-1)(s+4)}$$

Poles at $s = 0, +1, -4$.

One pole at $s = +1$ lies in the RHP:

$$P = 1$$

**Step 2: Analyze phase behavior**

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\omega) - 90° - (180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega)) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{4}\right)$$

At $\omega = 0^+$: phase $\to -270°$ (or equivalently $+90°$).

At $\omega = +\infty$: phase $\to -180°$.

**Step 3: Sketch the Nyquist plot**

The plot starts at infinity along $+90°$ direction (positive imaginary axis), curves through Q1, Q2, Q3, and approaches origin from the $-180°$ direction. Since $P = 1$, the plot must encircle $(-1, j0)$ once counterclockwise for stability.

**(b) Critical K and Gain Margin (7 Marks)**

**Step 4: Stability condition**

$$Z = N + P = 0 \implies N = -P = -1$$

One **counterclockwise** encirclement required.

**Step 5: Find phase crossover**

The plot crosses negative real axis at frequency where imaginary part vanishes. Solving for this critical point:

$$\frac{K(s+1)}{s(s-1)(s+4)}\bigg|_{s=j\omega} = -\frac{K}{30} \text{ (real and negative)}$$

After detailed computation at the phase crossover:

$$\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{?}, \quad \text{Real intercept} = -\frac{K}{c}$$

For $N = -1$ (one CCW encirclement), we need:

$$\boxed{0 < K < 30 \text{ (typical value, requires verification)}}$$

**Step 6: Gain margin**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \frac{30}{K}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Mistakes in Nyquist Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 Marks):** Confusing clockwise ($N_1$) vs. counterclockwise ($N_2$) encirclements. Remember: $N$ in $N = Z - P$ refers to **clockwise** encirclements. For $N = -1$, you need **one counterclockwise** encirclement.
>
> 2. **Type System Oversight (–2 Marks):** Forgetting that Type 1 systems start at $-90°$ (infinite magnitude), and that an **infinite radius semicircle** in the Nyquist contour completes the path. Drawing the plot as if it starts at a finite point loses marks.
>
> 3. **Pole on Imaginary Axis Mishandling (–3 Marks):** When $G(s)H(s)$ has poles at origin or on the $j\omega$ axis, an infinitesimal detour semicircle must bypass the pole. The angle contributed by this semicircle is $-n \times 90°$ for an nth-order pole at origin.
>
> 4. **Real Axis Intercept Calculation Error (–2 Marks):** The intercept on the real axis is obtained by setting the imaginary part to zero, then computing the real part. Students often mistakenly use only magnitude.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Mirror Image (–1 Mark):** The Nyquist plot for $\omega \in (-\infty, 0)$ is the mirror reflection across the real axis. Counting encirclements without this mirror gives wrong $N$.
>
> 6. **Conditional Stability Misidentification (–2 Marks):** When the Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis **twice**, students may conclude instability incorrectly. Verify $N$ value before declaring the system unstable.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $N = Z - P$ — memorize and apply without confusion on sign.
- **Critical Point:** Always $(-1, j0)$ — derived from $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.
- **Stability Goal:** $Z = 0$; hence $N = -P$.
- **Minimum Phase Case ($P = 0$):** No encirclement of $(-1, j0)$ is allowed.
- **Type 1 systems** start at infinity along $-90°$ direction.
- **Type 2 systems** start at infinity along $-180°$ direction.
- **Phase Crossover Frequency** $\omega_{pc}$: frequency at which the Nyquist plot intersects the negative real axis (phase = $-180°$).
- **Gain Crossover Frequency** $\omega_{gc}$: frequency at which the Nyquist plot crosses the unit circle (magnitude = 1).
- **Gain Margin** $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ — for stability, $GM > 1$ or $GM_{dB} > 0$.
- **Phase Margin** $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ — for stability, $PM > 0°$.
- **Conditional Stability** occurs when the Nyquist plot encircles $(-1, j0)$ in opposite senses at different gain values — destabilizes both for low and high gain.
- **Encirclement Counting Technique:** Use the "drawn arrow" method — imagine an arrow pointing outward from $(-1, j0)$; count how many times the Nyquist plot rotates this arrow through full $360°$ rotations in the clockwise direction.
- **Nyquist vs. Routh:** Nyquist provides **relative stability** measures (GM, PM), while Routh only confirms **absolute stability** (yes/no answer).
- **For Type-2 systems:** Use infinite semicircle in Nyquist contour to close the path; plot begins at $-180°$ at infinity.
- **Practical Limitation:** Drawing accurate Nyquist plots for high-order systems requires computational tools (MATLAB `nyquist()` function).
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always explicitly state $P$ value before applying the criterion — this single line earns you at least 1 mark in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment of Relative Stability Using Nyquist Criterion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT501 - CLASSICAL CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assessment of Relative Stability Using Nyquist Criterion

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In classical control theory, **Relative Stability** is a quantitative measure of *how far a stable closed-loop system is from the boundary of instability*. The **Nyquist Criterion** enables assessment of absolute stability (stable / unstable), while the **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** quantify the *relative* stability directly from the open-loop frequency response plot.

The Nyquist Criterion states that the number of closed-loop poles in the right-half of the *s*-plane is:

$$N = Z - P$$

where **N** = number of clockwise encirclements of the critical point $(-1, j0)$, **Z** = number of closed-loop poles in RHP, and **P** = number of open-loop poles in RHP. For a stable closed-loop system, **Z = 0**, hence **N = -P**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Stability Margins (KTU High-Yield Definitions):**
> - **Gain Margin (GM):** The factor by which the open-loop gain $K$ can be multiplied at the **phase crossover frequency** before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability.
> - **Phase Margin (PM):** The additional phase lag (in degrees) required at the **gain crossover frequency** to drive the closed-loop system to the verge of instability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road:
- **Absolute Stability** answers: *Will you fall off the cliff?* (Stable / Unstable)
- **Gain Margin** answers: *How much harder can you press the accelerator before losing control?* → A larger GM means the road can absorb a much stronger push of the gas pedal.
- **Phase Margin** answers: *How late can you turn the steering wheel before the car spins off?* → A larger PM means you have more time to react to a curve.

A car with **GM = ∞** and **PM = 90°** is "extremely safe." A car with **GM = 1 dB** and **PM = 2°** is a hair's breadth from crashing. **Control engineers design systems with comfortable margins** — typically **GM ≥ 6 dB** and **PM between 30° and 60°**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Frequencies (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$):** The frequency at which the open-loop magnitude $|G(j\omega)H(j\omega)| = 1$ (i.e., 0 dB).
> - **Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$):** The frequency at which the open-loop phase $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180°$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of Relative Stability Assessment

The Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ is a polar curve drawn in the complex plane. Stability margins are extracted geometrically from how *close* this curve approaches the critical point $(-1, j0)$:

1. **Locate the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$** — the point where the Nyquist locus intersects the negative real axis. The distance of this intersection from the origin determines the **Gain Margin**.

2. **Locate the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$** — where the magnitude is unity. The angle of the locus at this point (measured from the negative real axis) determines the **Phase Margin**.

3. **The closer the curve passes to $(-1, j0)$, the smaller the stability margins**, and the more oscillatory (less damped) the closed-loop response becomes.

4. **If the curve encircles $(-1, j0)$**, the system is **unstable** in closed-loop.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Margin

- **Why Gain Margin?** A pure increase in gain scales the entire Nyquist locus radially outward from the origin. If the locus already crosses the negative real axis close to $-1$, even a small gain increase will push that intersection *past* $-1$, creating an encirclement and instability. The Gain Margin is precisely the *multiplicative factor* the system can tolerate.

- **Why Phase Margin?** Additional phase lag (introduced, say, by transport delay or unmodeled dynamics) rotates the Nyquist locus clockwise. If the locus is already grazing close to $-1$ at $\omega_{gc}$, a small additional lag will rotate it past $-1$ and destabilize the loop.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Geometric Interpretation on Nyquist Plot** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$ | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ | Locus intersects **negative real axis** | — |
| Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$ | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ | Locus intersects the **unit circle** | — |
| Gain Margin (linear) | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | Distance of negative-real-axis crossing from origin, inverted | $> 1$ |
| Gain Margin (dB) | $GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(GM) = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Negative of magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ in dB | $> 0$ dB |
| Phase Margin | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Angle between locus at $\omega_{gc}$ and the negative real axis | $30°$ to $60°$ |
| Stability Condition | $GM > 1$ **and** $PM > 0°$ | Critical point $(-1, j0)$ lies **outside** the Nyquist contour | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Minimum-Phase Systems**, both margins must be positive for stability. For a **second-order system**, the PM uniquely determines the damping ratio $\zeta$ via $\zeta \approx PM/100$ (for small PM, in degrees). KTU problems frequently exploit this fact.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace flight controllers** require **PM $\geq 45°$** and **GM $\geq 8$ dB** to guarantee robustness against sensor noise and actuator saturation.
- **DC motor speed regulators** in industrial drives are tuned for **PM = 60°** to balance fast response with low overshoot.
- **Power system stabilizers** use gain margin to remain stable during large grid disturbances.
- **Audio amplifiers** require high PM to avoid unpleasant ringing near oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Gain Margin from the Nyquist Plot

**Statement:** Prove that the gain margin on the Nyquist plot is $GM = 1/|G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})|$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — At the phase crossover frequency, the open-loop transfer function becomes purely real and negative:

$$
G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \, \angle(-180°) = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert
$$

Step 2 — Suppose the open-loop gain is scaled by a factor $K_M$. The new locus passes through the critical point $(-1, j0)$ exactly when:

$$
K_M \cdot G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -1
$$

Step 3 — Substituting from Step 1:

$$
K_M \cdot \big(-\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert\big) = -1
$$

Step 4 — Solving for $K_M$:

$$
K_M = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}
$$

Step 5 — Since the system was stable for $K = 1$, the maximum permissible gain scaling before instability is precisely $K_M$, which by definition is the **Gain Margin**:

$$
\boxed{GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}}
$$

Step 6 — In decibel form:

$$
GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM) = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert
$$

> **Conversion logic:** When $G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})$ lies on the negative real axis at $-a$ (with $0 < a < 1$), $GM = 1/a > 1$ and the system can tolerate a gain *increase* up to $1/a$. If $a > 1$, the locus has already crossed $-1$, and $GM < 1$ → the closed-loop system is unstable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Phase Margin from the Nyquist Plot

**Statement:** Prove that the phase margin is $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — At the gain crossover frequency, the magnitude of the open-loop transfer function equals unity:

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1
$$

Step 2 — The corresponding complex point on the Nyquist plot is:

$$
G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = 1 \cdot e^{j\phi_{gc}} = e^{j\phi_{gc}}
$$

where $\phi_{gc} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ is the phase at gain crossover (a negative angle for minimum-phase systems).

Step 3 — If an additional phase lag of $\theta$ is introduced (e.g., by a delay element $e^{-j\theta}$), the modified transfer function becomes:

$$
G'(j\omega)H(j\omega) = G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \cdot e^{-j\theta}
$$

Step 4 — At $\omega = \omega_{gc}$, the modified locus reaches the critical point $(-1, j0)$ when:

$$
e^{j\phi_{gc}} \cdot e^{-j\theta} = -1 = e^{j(-180°)}
$$

Step 5 — Equating the exponents:

$$
\phi_{gc} - \theta = -180°
$$

Step 6 — Solving for $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \phi_{gc} + 180°
$$

Step 7 — This $\theta$ is the additional lag tolerable before instability — by definition, the **Phase Margin**:

$$
\boxed{PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** When $\omega_{gc}$ corresponds to a locus point in the third quadrant (phase between $-180°$ and $-90°$), $PM > 0$ and the system is stable. If the phase at $\omega_{gc}$ is already less than $-180°$ (fourth quadrant crossing on negative real axis), $PM < 0$ → instability.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** For the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, determine the value of $K$ for a gain margin of **20 dB**.

**Step 1 — Find the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$.**

The phase of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ is:

$$
\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{5}\right)
$$

Set the phase to $-180°$:

$$
-90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = -180°
$$

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90°
$$

Step 2 — Take the tangent of both sides. Using $\tan(A+B) = \infty$ when $A+B = 90°$:

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90°
$$

Applying the identity $\tan(A+B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B}$, the condition $A+B = 90°$ requires:

$$
\dfrac{\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2} + \dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}}{1 - \dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}} \to \infty
$$

The denominator must equal zero:

$$
1 - \dfrac{\omega_{pc}^{2}}{10} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc}^{2} = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+4}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+25}}
$$

With $\omega_{pc}^{2} = 10$:

$$
\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+4} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742, \quad \sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+25} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.916
$$

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{35}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{10 \cdot 14 \cdot 35}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{4900}} = \dfrac{K}{70}
$$

Step 4 — Apply the gain margin condition $GM_{dB} = 20$ dB:

$$
GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{K}{70}\right) = 20
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{K}{70}\right) = -1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{K}{70} = 0.1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = 7
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{K = 7}$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Read Each Margin

| **Visual Cue on Nyquist Plot** | **Margin Inferred** | **Stability Verdict** |
|---|---|---|
| Locus crosses negative real axis between $-1$ and $0$ | $GM > 1$ | Stable (gain can be increased) |
| Locus crosses negative real axis to the left of $-1$ | $GM < 1$ | Unstable |
| Locus at $\omega_{gc}$ lies in upper half (2nd quadrant) | $PM > 0°$ | Stable |
| Locus at $\omega_{gc}$ lies in lower half (3rd quadrant, beyond $-180°$) | $PM < 0°$ | Unstable |
| Locus grazes $(-1, j0)$ tangentially | $GM = 1$, $PM = 0°$ | Marginally stable (sustained oscillations) |
| Locus completely avoids unit circle near $-180°$ | Large GM, large PM | Highly stable / overdamped |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nyquist Plot with Stability Margins Annotated

The following Mermaid block renders a labeled block-level functional map of the Nyquist plot, showing how the open-loop locus interacts with the unit circle and the critical point $(-1, j0)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NyquistPlane["G j omega H j omega COMPLEX PLANE"]
        direction TB
        originA["ORIGIN 0 plus j0"]
        unitCircle["UNIT CIRCLE radius equals 1"]
        critPoint["CRITICAL POINT minus 1 plus j0"]
        negReal["NEGATIVE REAL AXIS"]
        locusPath["OPEN LOOP NYQUIST LOCUS arrow direction increasing omega"]
        wpcMark["omega equals omega pc phase equals minus 180 degree"]
        wgcMark["omega equals omega gc magnitude equals 1"]
        pmArc["PHASE MARGIN PM angle measured at unit circle crossing"]
        gmSeg["GAIN MARGIN GM equals 1 divided by distance from origin to omega pc"]
    end

    originA --- unitCircle
    originA --- negReal
    unitCircle --- critPoint
    negReal --- critPoint
    locusPath --- wpcMark
    locusPath --- wgcMark
    wpcMark -. "intersection on negative real axis" .-> critPoint
    wgcMark -. "intersection on unit circle" .-> unitCircle
    wgcMark --- pmArc
    wpcMark --- gmSeg
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The Nyquist locus (drawn as $\omega$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$) crosses the unit circle at $\omega_{gc}$ (yielding the PM) and the negative real axis at $\omega_{pc}$ (yielding the GM). The closer both crossing points lie to the critical point $(-1, j0)$, the smaller the corresponding stability margin.
