# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. State the stability conditions in terms of these margins.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ before driving the closed-loop system to the verge of instability. Mathematically:
>
> $$GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}, \qquad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$$
>
> **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be introduced at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. Mathematically:
>
> $$PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$
>
> **Stability Conditions:** For a minimum-phase system, both margins must be positive for closed-loop stability:
>
> $$GM > 1 \quad \text{(or } GM_{dB} > 0 \text{)} \quad \text{AND} \quad PM > 0°$$
>
> Typical *desirable* values are $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM$ between $30°$ and $60°$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the significance of the critical point $(-1, j0)$ in the Nyquist criterion? Explain how Gain Margin is measured on the Nyquist plot.**

**Model Answer:**

> The critical point $(-1, j0)$ represents the threshold of closed-loop instability. For a unity-feedback system, the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ requires the open-loop transfer function to equal $-1$ at the onset of instability.
>
> On the Nyquist plot, the **Gain Margin is measured as the reciprocal of the distance** from the origin to the point where the Nyquist locus intersects the **negative real axis** (i.e., at $\omega = \omega_{pc}$). If this intersection occurs at a point $-a$ on the real axis, then:
>
> $$GM = \dfrac{1}{a} \quad \text{and} \quad GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(a)$$
>
> - If $a < 1$ (intersection between $0$ and $-1$), then $GM > 1$ → stable.
> - If $a > 1$ (intersection to the left of $-1$), then $GM < 1$ → unstable.
> - If $a = 1$ (locus passes exactly through $-1$), then $GM = 1$ → marginally stable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
The open-loop transfer function of a unity-feedback system is given by:
$$
G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+4)(s+6)}
$$

**(a)** Find the value of $K$ such that the **Gain Margin** of the system is **12 dB**.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the value of $K$ obtained in part (a), determine the **Phase Margin** and comment on the stability of the closed-loop system.  **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A, Part (a)

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega\,(j\omega+4)\,(j\omega+6)}
$$

**Step 2:** Phase angle:

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{6}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** For phase crossover, set phase to $-180°$:

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{4}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{6}\right) = 90°
$$

Using the tangent addition identity, the condition reduces to the denominator vanishing:

$$
1 - \dfrac{\omega_{pc}^{2}}{24} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc}^{2} = 24 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{24} = 2\sqrt{6} \approx 4.899 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+16}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+36}}
$$

With $\omega_{pc}^{2} = 24$:

$$
\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+16} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10}, \quad \sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+36} = \sqrt{60} = 2\sqrt{15}
$$

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{24} \cdot 2\sqrt{10} \cdot 2\sqrt{15}} = \dfrac{K}{4\sqrt{24 \cdot 10 \cdot 15}} = \dfrac{K}{4\sqrt{3600}} = \dfrac{K}{4 \cdot 60} = \dfrac{K}{240}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply $GM_{dB} = 12$:

$$
GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{K}{240}\right) = 12
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{K}{240}\right) = -0.6 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{K}{240} = 10^{-0.6} \approx 0.2512
$$

$$
\boxed{K \approx 240 \times 0.2512 \approx 60.29}
$$

---

### Model Solution — Question A, Part (b)

**Step 1:** Find gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$
\dfrac{K}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^{2}+16}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^{2}+36}} = 1
$$

Substitute $K = 60.29$:

$$
\omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^{2}+16}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^{2}+36} = 60.29
$$

**Step 2:** Solve by numerical iteration (KTU-accepted method):

- Try $\omega_{gc} = 1$: $1 \cdot \sqrt{17}\sqrt{37} \approx 1 \cdot 4.123 \cdot 6.083 \approx 25.08 < 60.29$
- Try $\omega_{gc} = 2$: $2 \cdot \sqrt{20}\sqrt{40} \approx 2 \cdot 4.472 \cdot 6.325 \approx 56.58 < 60.29$
- Try $\omega_{gc} = 2.1$: $2.1 \cdot \sqrt{20.41}\sqrt{40.41} \approx 2.1 \cdot 4.518 \cdot 6.357 \approx 60.30 \approx 60.29$ ✓

Therefore, $\omega_{gc} \approx 2.1$ rad/s.

**Step 3:** Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:

$$
\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2.1}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2.1}{6}\right)
$$

$$
= -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.525) - \tan^{-1}(0.35) = -90° - 27.67° - 19.29° = -136.96°
$$

**Step 4:** Phase margin:

$$
PM = 180° + (-136.96°) = 43.04°
$$

**Stability Comment:** Since $GM_{dB} = 12 > 0$ dB and $PM \approx 43° > 0°$, the closed-loop system is **stable** with **good relative stability**. A PM of $\sim 43°$ implies moderately damped transient response with acceptable overshoot.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]`
For the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$:

**(a)** Sketch the Nyquist plot and identify the critical point.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **Gain Margin** and **Phase Margin**. Comment on the closed-loop stability.  **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B, Part (a)

**Step 1:** Convert to frequency response form with $s = j\omega$:

$$
G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega\,(1+j0.5\omega)\,(1+j0.2\omega)}
$$

**Step 2:** Magnitude:

$$
\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{10}{\omega\sqrt{1+0.25\omega^{2}}\sqrt{1+0.04\omega^{2}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Phase:

$$
\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at key frequencies:

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | $\vert GH \vert$ | $\angle GH$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 95.4 | $-95.7°$ |
| 0.5 | 17.89 | $-112.6°$ |
| 1.0 | 7.91 | $-131.7°$ |
| 2.0 | 3.27 | $-156.8°$ |
| 3.0 | 1.94 | $-171.0°$ |
| 3.5 | 1.58 | $-176.0°$ |
| 4.0 | 1.32 | $-179.7°$ |
| 5.0 | 0.97 | $-185.3°$ |
| 10.0 | 0.41 | $-200.0°$ |

**Step 5:** Sketch description for the answer script:
- The locus starts at $\infty$ on the negative imaginary axis ($\omega \to 0^+$).
- It moves clockwise, sweeping through the second and third quadrants.
- It crosses the negative real axis near $\omega \approx 3.85$ rad/s (this is $\omega_{pc}$).
- It crosses the unit circle near $\omega \approx 5.0$ rad/s (this is $\omega_{gc}$).
- The mirror image is drawn for negative $\omega$, completing the closed contour.

**Critical point:** $(-1, j0)$ — clearly shown on the negative real axis with the locus passing close to but not encircling it.

---

### Model Solution — Question B, Part (b)

**Step 1:** Find $\omega_{pc}$ more precisely. Set phase to $-180°$:

$$
\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) = 90°
$$

The denominator of the tangent addition identity vanishes:

$$
1 - (0.5\omega_{pc})(0.2\omega_{pc}) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0.1\omega_{pc}^{2} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:

$$
\vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{10}\sqrt{1+0.25 \cdot 10}\sqrt{1+0.04 \cdot 10}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{10}\sqrt{3.5}\sqrt{1.4}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{10}{3.162 \cdot 1.871 \cdot 1.183} \approx \dfrac{10}{6.998} \approx 1.429
$$

**Step 3:** Gain margin:

$$
GM = \dfrac{1}{1.429} \approx 0.70, \qquad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(0.70) \approx -3.10 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4:** Find $\omega_{gc}$ from $\vert GH \vert = 1$:

Solve $\dfrac{10}{\omega\sqrt{1+0.25\omega^{2}}\sqrt{1+0.04\omega^{2}}} = 1$. Try $\omega = 5$:

$$
\dfrac{10}{5\sqrt{7.25}\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{10}{5 \cdot 2.693 \cdot 1.414} = \dfrac{10}{19.04} \approx 0.525
$$

Try $\omega = 3$:

$$
\dfrac{10}{3\sqrt{3.25}\sqrt{1.36}} = \dfrac{10}{3 \cdot 1.803 \cdot 1.166} = \dfrac{10}{6.309} \approx 1.585
$$

By interpolation, $\omega_{gc} \approx 4.0$ rad/s.

**Step 5:** Phase at $\omega_{gc} = 4.0$:

$$
\angle GH = -90° - \tan^{-1}(2.0) - \tan^{-1}(0.8) = -90° - 63.43° - 38.66° = -192.09°
$$

**Step 6:** Phase margin:

$$
PM = 180° + (-192.09°) = -12.09°
$$

**Stability Comment:**
- $GM_{dB} \approx -3.1$ dB $< 0$
- $PM \approx -12.09° < 0°$
- **Both margins are negative → the closed-loop system is UNSTABLE.**

The Nyquist plot encircles the critical point $(-1, j0)$, confirming the unstable nature. To stabilize, the gain $K = 10$ must be reduced (i.e., the loop gain must be scaled down by at least a factor of $1/0.70 \approx 1.43$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (Module 4 — Relative Stability):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $GM$ to dB:** Many students compute the linear $GM$ correctly but then write $GM_{dB} = \log_{10}(GM)$ instead of $20\log_{10}(GM)$. This loses **1 full mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$:** $\omega_{gc}$ comes from $\vert GH \vert = 1$ (magnitude condition), $\omega_{pc}$ comes from $\angle GH = -180°$ (phase condition). Reversing these gives wrong GM/PM and triggers cascade marks loss.
>
> 3. **Skipping the stability comment:** Even when GM and PM are correctly calculated, students often forget to write "the system is stable/unstable because $GM > 1$ and $PM > 0°$." Examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for this concluding statement.
>
> 4. **Sign error in phase angle:** The phase of $\dfrac{1}{j\omega}$ is $-90°$, not $+90°$. A sign slip here propagates through $\omega_{pc}$ and ruins the entire solution.
>
> 5. **Not sketching the Nyquist plot:** When asked to "sketch," students who omit the plot (even a hand-drawn one) lose **2–3 marks** outright. Always plot at least 6–8 frequency points and clearly mark the critical point.
>
> 6. **Ignoring mirror image for negative $\omega$:** The full Nyquist contour requires the locus for $\omega: -\infty \to 0^-$. Missing this invalidates the encirclement count for absolute stability.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Relative Stability via Nyquist:**
>
> - **Two critical frequencies to memorize:** $\omega_{gc}$ (gain crossover, $\vert GH \vert = 1$) and $\omega_{pc}$ (phase crossover, $\angle GH = -180°$).
> - **Gain Margin formula (linear):** $GM = 1 / \vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ ; **In dB:** $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
> - **Phase Margin formula:** $PM = 180° + \angle GH(j\omega_{gc})$.
> - **Stability verdict:** Stable iff $GM > 1$ (i.e., $GM_{dB} > 0$) **AND** $PM > 0°$ (for minimum-phase systems).
> - **Typical design targets:** $GM \geq 6$ dB, $PM = 30°$–$60°$.
> - **Nyquist stability equation:** $N = Z - P$. For $Z = 0$: $N = -P$.
> - **Geometric meaning of GM:** Reciprocal of distance from origin to the negative-real-axis crossing on the Nyquist locus.
> - **Geometric meaning of PM:** Angle (measured from negative real axis) at the unit-circle crossing of the Nyquist locus.
> - **Conditional stability:** A system whose Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis *multiple times*. Stability then depends on the *operating gain*; KTU questions sometimes test this.
> - **Minimum-phase systems:** No RHP poles or zeros; PM and GM uniquely characterize stability.
> - **Non-minimum-phase systems:** May have $PM > 0°$ yet still be unstable; always verify using $N = Z - P$.
> - **Frequency-response substitution:** Always replace $s$ by $j\omega$ *before* computing magnitude and phase.
> - **Phase convention:** Each factor in the denominator contributes $-90°$ or $-\tan^{-1}(\omega/\text{constant})$; each numerator factor contributes $+90°$ or $+\tan^{-1}(\omega/\text{constant})$.
> - **Final stability comment is mandatory** — write one concluding sentence tying GM, PM, and stability together.

---
